## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics in Generative Modeling

> [!NOTE]
> **Bolded Constants & Standard Metrics** used universally in evaluating generative models:
> - **FID (Fréchet Inception Distance)** — Lower is better; measures distributional similarity using Inception-v3 features.
> - **IS (Inception Score)** — Higher is better; combines class confidence and diversity.
> - **Perplexity (PPL)** — Used in language models; $PPL = 2^{H(p)}$ where $H$ is the cross-entropy.
> - **Log-Likelihood** — $\log P_{\theta}(x)$ — Higher (less negative) is better.
> - **Bits Per Dimension (BPD)** — Lower is better; used in image generation literature.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Schools: Generative vs. Discriminative

The fundamental learning-theoretic distinction in supervised ML can be summarized as:

- **Discriminative Models** learn the **decision boundary** by modeling $P(Y \mid X)$ directly.
- **Generative Models** learn the **data-generation process** by modeling $P(X \mid Y)$ and $P(X)$, then applying Bayes' rule:

$$P(Y \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid Y) \cdot P(Y)}{P(X)}$$

This makes generative models strictly more powerful in expressive capability, but also more computationally demanding.

## 2.2 The Generative Modeling Taxonomy

Generative models can be broadly classified into **four families** based on how they represent $P_{\theta}(x)$:

1. **Likelihood-Based (Explicit Density)**
   - Tractable density: PixelRNN, PixelCNN, **Normalizing Flows (NFs)**
   - Approximate density: **Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)**
2. **Implicit Density**
   - **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)**
3. **Energy-Based Models (EBMs)**
   - Boltzmann Machines, Deep Boltzmann Machines
4. **Diffusion Models**
   - **Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs)**, Score-Based Models

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of the Generative Pipeline

For any generative model, the operational logic follows these structured steps:

- **Step 1 — Sample the Latent Prior:** Draw $z \sim P(z)$, where typically $P(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ (standard multivariate normal).
- **Step 2 — Decode / Transform:** Pass $z$ through a generator network $G_{\theta}$: $\hat{x} = G_{\theta}(z)$.
- **Step 3 — Score / Optimize:** Compare $\hat{x}$ with real samples $x$ using a divergence or adversarial loss.
- **Step 4 — Update Parameters:** Use gradient descent to update $\theta$ and reduce the loss.
- **Step 5 — Iterate:** Repeat until $P_{\theta}(x) \approx P_{\text{data}}(x)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the rise happened in this order:** Each family solved a critical weakness of its predecessor.
> - NNs (2012) → enabled deep feature learning.
> - VAEs (2013) → enabled continuous latent space with tractable lower bounds.
> - GANs (2014) → eliminated explicit density; produced sharper samples.
> - Transformers (2017) → enabled scalable sequence and language generation.
> - Diffusion Models (2020+) → combined high sample quality with stable training.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Bayes' Rule | $P(Y \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid Y) P(Y)}{P(X)}$ | Posterior, likelihood, prior, evidence | Foundation of generative classifiers (e.g., Naive Bayes) |
| 2 | KL Divergence | $D_{KL}(P \,\|\, Q) = \sum_x P(x) \log \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}$ | Measures divergence from $Q$ to $P$ | Used as training objective in VAEs & flows |
| 3 | Jensen–Shannon Divergence | $D_{JS}(P \,\|\, Q) = \tfrac{1}{2} D_{KL}(P \,\|\, M) + \tfrac{1}{2} D_{KL}(Q \,\|\, M)$ | Symmetric, bounded $\in [0, \log 2]$ | Training objective in GANs |
| 4 | VAE ELBO | $\log P(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q}[\log P(x \mid z)] - D_{KL}(q(z \mid x) \,\|\, p(z))$ | Reconstruction − Regularization | Variational lower bound on log-likelihood |
| 5 | GAN Min-Max Objective | $\min_G \max_D \mathbb{E}_{x}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | $G$ = generator, $D$ = discriminator | Two-player game training rule |
| 6 | Diffusion Forward Process | $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(x_t; \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\, x_{t-1}, \beta_t I)$ | $\beta_t$ = variance schedule | Adds noise step-by-step |
| 7 | Diffusion Reverse Process | $p_{\theta}(x_{t-1} \mid x_t) = \mathcal{N}(x_{t-1}; \mu_{\theta}(x_t, t), \Sigma_{\theta}(x_t, t))$ | Learned denoising | Generates by iterative denoising |
| 8 | FID Score | $FID = \vert \mu_r - \mu_g \vert^2 + \text{Tr}\!\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)$ | $\mu, \Sigma$ are Inception-pool3 stats | Gold standard image quality metric |
| 9 | Log-Likelihood | $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P_{\theta}(x_i)$ | Sum over dataset | Standard density-estimation objective |
| 10 | Reparameterization Trick | $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ | $\odot$ = element-wise product | Enables backprop through sampling |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Computer Vision:** Synthetic data augmentation, super-resolution, image inpainting, medical imaging synthesis.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Chatbots, machine translation, code generation, summarization.
- **Drug Discovery:** Generative chemistry (e.g., molecule generation for novel pharmaceuticals).
- **Audio & Speech:** Text-to-speech (TTS), music generation, voice cloning.
- **Engineering Design:** Generative CAD design in mechanical/aerospace industries, antenna optimization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Theorem to Generative Classification

We begin with the most fundamental identity in probabilistic machine learning — Bayes' theorem — and derive the generative model formulation.

**Starting Point (Definition of Conditional Probability):**

$$P(Y \mid X) = \frac{P(X \cap Y)}{P(X)}$$

**Step 1 — Symmetric Expression:**
By the symmetry of joint probability, we have $P(X \cap Y) = P(Y \cap X)$, which yields:

$$P(Y \mid X) \cdot P(X) = P(X \mid Y) \cdot P(Y)$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to Isolate the Posterior:**
Dividing both sides by $P(X)$, assuming $P(X) > 0$:

$$P(Y \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid Y) \cdot P(Y)}{P(X)}$$

**Step 3 — Expand the Marginal Using the Law of Total Probability:**
For a discrete class variable $Y$ taking $K$ values:

$$P(X) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} P(X \mid Y = y_k) \cdot P(Y = y_k)$$

**Step 4 — Final Generative Classifier Decision Rule:**
Substituting Step 3 into Step 2:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y_k} P(Y = y_k \mid X) = \arg\max_{y_k} P(X \mid Y = y_k) \cdot P(Y = y_k)$$

> **Conversion Logic:** A *generative classifier* estimates $P(X \mid Y)$ for each class, multiplies by the class prior, and picks the largest. It implicitly models *how each class generates data*. A *discriminative classifier* (e.g., logistic regression) skips this and directly models the posterior.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) for VAEs

The VAE objective is derived by optimizing a lower bound on the intractable log-likelihood $\log P_{\theta}(x)$.

**Starting Point — Marginal Log-Likelihood:**

$$\log P_{\theta}(x) = \log \int P_{\theta}(x \mid z) \, P(z) \, dz$$

**Step 1 — Introduce the Variational Posterior $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$:**
Multiply and divide by $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$:

$$\log P_{\theta}(x) = \log \int q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \cdot \frac{P_{\theta}(x \mid z) P(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)} \, dz$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the Log of an Expectation:**
By definition of expectation under $q_{\phi}$:

$$\log P_{\theta}(x) = \log \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)} \left[ \frac{P_{\theta}(x \mid z) P(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply Jensen's Inequality** ($\log$ is concave, so $\log \mathbb{E}[X] \geq \mathbb{E}[\log X]$):

$$\log P_{\theta}(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)} \left[ \log \frac{P_{\theta}(x \mid z) P(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Split the Logarithm:**

$$\log P_{\theta}(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}[\log P_{\theta}(x \mid z)] + \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\left[\log \frac{P(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the KL Divergence in the Second Term:**
By definition, $D_{KL}(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \Vert P(z)) = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}\left[\log \frac{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}{P(z)}\right]$.

Therefore, the second term equals $-D_{KL}(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \Vert P(z))$.

**Step 6 — Final ELBO Expression:**

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}[\log P_{\theta}(x \mid z)]}_{\text{Reconstruction}} - \underbrace{D_{KL}\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \;\Vert\; P(z)\right)}_{\text{Regularization}}\;}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The first term rewards the decoder for reconstructing $x$ from $z$. The second term forces the encoder's output distribution $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$ to stay close to the prior $P(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, ensuring a smooth, well-structured latent space.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Discriminative vs. Generative Models

| Property | Discriminative Models | Generative Models |
|----------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Learns** | $P(Y \mid X)$ | $P(X)$ or $P(X, Y)$ |
| **What it answers** | "Which class?" | "How was the data made?" |
| **Data requirement** | Labeled only | Can use unlabeled data (semi-supervised) |
| **Out-of-distribution detection** | Weak (often confidently wrong) | Strong (low likelihood on OOD samples) |
| **Sample generation** | ❌ Cannot generate new samples | ✅ Core capability |
| **Examples** | Logistic Regression, SVM, CNN classifiers | VAE, GAN, Diffusion, GPT, Normalizing Flows |
| **Training stability** | Generally stable | Often unstable (esp. GANs) |
| **Inference cost** | Low (single forward pass) | Variable; can be very high (iterative diffusion) |
| **Bayesian compatibility** | Limited | Native (full posterior over $X$ and $Y$) |
| **Use case fit** | Classification, regression | Synthesis, simulation, missing data, density estimation |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: GAN's Min–Max Equilibrium

The original GAN objective (Goodfellow et al., 2014) is framed as a two-player zero-sum game.

**Step 1 — Discriminator's Objective** (Binary Cross-Entropy on real vs. fake):
For a real sample $x \sim P_{\text{data}}$, the discriminator should output $D(x) \approx 1$. For a generated sample $G(z)$, it should output $D(G(z)) \approx 0$.

$$V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim P(z)}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

**Step 2 — Generator's Goal:** Fool the discriminator. Minimize $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$, equivalently maximize $\log D(G(z))$.

**Step 3 — Combined Min–Max Objective:**

$$\min_G \max_D V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

**Step 4 — Optimal Discriminator Derivation:**
For a fixed $G$, the optimal $D^*$ satisfies:

$$D^*(x) = \frac{P_{\text{data}}(x)}{P_{\text{data}}(x) + P_g(x)}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** At the global optimum, $P_g = P_{\text{data}}$, so $D^*(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}$ everywhere — the discriminator can no longer distinguish real from fake. The value function then evaluates to $-\log 4$, the **Nash equilibrium** of the GAN game.

**Step 5 — Substituting the Optimal Discriminator Yields the Jensen–Shannon Divergence:**

$$\min_G V(D^*, G) = -\log(4) + 2 \cdot D_{JS}(P_{\text{data}} \,\|\, P_g)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why training stability is the central issue in GANs — when $P_g$ and $P_{\text{data}}$ have disjoint supports, $D_{JS}$ becomes a constant (saturates to $\log 2$), gradients vanish, and training collapses. This is the historical reason for the rise of **Wasserstein GANs (WGANs)**, **Spectral Normalization GANs**, and ultimately the migration to **diffusion-based models**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: The Diffusion Forward Process Marginal

For the diffusion model forward (noising) process, we want a closed-form expression for $q(x_t \mid x_0)$.

**Step 1 — Define the Markov Chain:**

$$q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(x_t; \sqrt{1 - \beta_t}\, x_{t-1},\; \beta_t I\right)$$

**Step 2 — Define Cumulative Product Coefficients:**
Let $\alpha_t = 1 - \beta_t$ and $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t} \alpha_s$.

**Step 3 — Reparameterize Iteratively:**
Using $x_t = \sqrt{\alpha_t} \, x_{t-1} + \sqrt{\beta_t} \, \epsilon_{t-1}$ with $\epsilon_{t-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, after $t$ recursive applications we obtain:

$$x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} \, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t} \, \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

**Step 4 — Final Marginal Distribution:**

$$\boxed{\;q(x_t \mid x_0) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(x_t; \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0,\; (1 - \bar{\alpha}_t) I\right)\;}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This closed form lets us jump directly from $x_0$ to any noisy $x_t$ in one step during training — a critical efficiency gain that made diffusion models practical at scale.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Timeline — The Rise of Generative Modeling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["2012 AlexNet Deep Learning Revolution"] --> nodeB["2013 VAE Variational Autoencoders"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["2014 GAN Generative Adversarial Networks"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["2015 Normalizing Flows RealNVP"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["2017 Transformer Architecture"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["2018 GPT1 BERT Scale"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["2020 Diffusion Models DDPM"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["2021 CLIP DALL E Multimodal"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["2022 ChatGPT Stable Diffusion"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["2023 GPT4 Foundation Models"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["2024+ Multimodal Agents"]

    classDef milestone fill:#1E3A8A,stroke:#3B82F6,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF;
    classDef peak fill:#7C2D12,stroke:#F59E0B,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF;
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K milestone;
    class K peak;
```

> **Interpretation:** The diagram shows the **accelerating density of breakthroughs** — early milestones (2012–2015) advanced every 1–2 years, while the post-2020 era sees *transformative* releases annually, indicating a phase transition in capability.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Generative vs. Discriminative Learning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DISCRIMINATIVE["DISCRIMINATIVE MODEL PATH"]
        d1["Input X: Image"] --> d2["Feature Extractor CNN"]
        d2 --> d3["Classifier Head"]
        d3 --> d4["Output P(Y given X)"]
        d4 --> d5["Decision: Cat or Dog"]
    end

    subgraph GENERATIVE["GENERATIVE MODEL PATH"]
        g1["Latent z sampled from N(0,I)"] --> g2["Decoder Network G"]
        g3["Optional: Conditional label y"] --> g2
        g2 --> g4["Generated Sample x hat"]
        g4 --> g5["Loss: Compare to real data"]
        g5 --> g6["Update G parameters theta"]
    end

    classDef discStyle fill:#0F766E,stroke:#14B8A6,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF;
    classDef genStyle fill:#7C2D12,stroke:#FB923C,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF;
    class d1,d2,d3,d4,d5 discStyle;
    class g1,g2,g3,g4,g5,g6 genStyle;
```

> **Interpretation:** The discriminative path is a **single forward pipeline** — input in, label out. The generative path is a **closed-loop synthesis pipeline** — sample, generate, score, update — which is inherently more complex but unlocks creation.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Modern Generative AI

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DATA["DATA LAYER"]
        da1["Web Scale Text Corpora"]
        da2["Image Datasets LAION ImageNet"]
        da3["Audio Video Code Multilingual"]
    end

    subgraph FOUNDATION["FOUNDATION MODEL LAYER"]
        fm1["Transformer Backbone"]
        fm2["Diffusion U Net"]
        fm3["Mixture of Experts MoE"]
    end

    subgraph TRAINING["TRAINING LAYER"]
        tr1["Self Supervised Pretraining"]
        tr2["RLHF Alignment"]
        tr3["Instruction Tuning"]
    end

    subgraph INFERENCE["INFERENCE LAYER"]
        in1["Sampling: ancestral, DDIM, guidance"]
        in2["Decoding: greedy, beam, top-k, nucleus"]
        in3["Acceleration: caching, distillation, quantization"]
    end

    subgraph DEPLOY["DEPLOYMENT LAYER"]
        de1["Chatbots Assistants"]
        de2["Image Video Generation"]
        de3["Code Generation"]
        de4["Scientific Discovery"]
    end

    DATA --> FOUNDATION
    FOUNDATION --> TRAINING
    TRAINING --> INFERENCE
    INFERENCE --> DEPLOY

    classDef dataL fill:#1E3A8A,stroke:#60A5FA,color:#FFFFFF;
    classDef fndL fill:#7C2D12,stroke:#FB923C,color:#FFFFFF;
    classDef trnL fill:#14532D,stroke:#22C55E,color:#FFFFFF;
    classDef infL fill:#581C87,stroke:#C084FC,color:#FFFFFF;
    classDef dplL fill:#0F766E,stroke:#2DD4BF,color:#FFFFFF;

    class da1,da2,da3 dataL;
    class fm1,fm2,fm3 fndL;
    class tr1,tr2,tr3 trnL;
    class in1,in2,in3 infL;
    class de1,de2,de3,de4 dplL;
```

> **Interpretation:** Modern generative AI is a **five-layer stack** — Data fuels the Foundation Model, which is shaped by Training, refined by Inference, and finally delivered through Deployment. Each layer is a rich research and engineering subfield in its own right.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Generative Model Family Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GM["Generative Models"]
    GM --> EX["Explicit Density"]
    GM --> IM["Implicit Density"]
    GM --> EB["Energy Based"]
    GM --> DI["Diffusion / Score Based"]

    EX --> EX1["Tractable: PixelCNN, Normalizing Flows"]
    EX --> EX2["Approximate: VAE"]

    IM --> IM1["GAN: Generator vs Discriminator"]

    EB --> EB1["Boltzmann Machines, Contrastive Divergence"]

    DI --> DI1["DDPM: Forward Noise, Reverse Denoise"]
    DI --> DI2["Score Matching: SDE Solvers"]

    classDef rootStyle fill:#000000,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF;
    classDef branchStyle fill:#1E3A8A,stroke:#60A5FA,color:#FFFFFF;
    classDef leafStyle fill:#7C2D12,stroke:#FB923C,color:#FFFFFF;
    class GM rootStyle;
    class EX,IM,EB,DI branchStyle;
    class EX1,EX2,IM1,EB1,DI1,DI2 leafStyle;
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between generative and discriminative models with one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Discriminative Model | Generative Model |
|--------|--------------------|------------------|
| **Goal** | Learn decision boundary $P(Y \mid X)$ | Learn data distribution $P(X)$ |
| **Use** | Classification, regression | Synthesis, simulation, density estimation |
| **Example** | Logistic Regression for spam detection | VAE/GAN for generating synthetic faces |

A **discriminative model** like a spam filter learns to separate *spam* from *ham* using features of the email. A **generative model** like a face generator learns the entire distribution of human faces and can synthesize new, never-before-seen faces.

> **[Marking Scheme: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for difference, 1 mark for example]**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest]`
**State the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) used in Variational Autoencoders and explain its two terms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}[\log P_{\theta}(x \mid z)] - D_{KL}\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \,\Vert\, P(z)\right)$$

- **Reconstruction Term** $\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\log P_{\theta}(x \mid z)]$: Encourages the decoder to reconstruct $x$ faithfully from the latent $z$. (1.5 marks)
- **Regularization Term** $D_{KL}(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \Vert P(z))$: Forces the encoder's output to match the prior $P(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, ensuring a smooth latent space. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model QP, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the mathematical formulation of a **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)**. Derive the optimal discriminator $D^*(x)$ in terms of the real and generated distributions $P_{\text{data}}(x)$ and $P_g(x)$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)**. Derive the ELBO objective from the marginal log-likelihood using Jensen's inequality.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — GAN Formulation & Optimal Discriminator [7 marks]**

The GAN framework (Goodfellow et al., 2014) consists of two neural networks trained in opposition:

- **Generator $G$**: Maps noise $z \sim P(z)$ to fake sample $\hat{x} = G(z)$.
- **Discriminator $D$**: Outputs probability $D(x)$ that $x$ is real.

**Step 1 — Value Function [2 marks]:**

$$V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim P(z)}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

**Step 2 — Objective:**

$$\min_G \max_D V(D, G)$$

**Step 3 — Optimal Discriminator Derivation [3 marks]:**
For fixed $G$, the inner maximization is:

$$\max_D \int \left[ P_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) + P_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \right] dx$$

Setting the integrand derivative w.r.t. $D(x)$ to zero:

$$\frac{P_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{P_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; D^*(x) = \frac{P_{\text{data}}(x)}{P_{\text{data}}(x) + P_g(x)}$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium [2 marks]:** At convergence, $P_g = P_{\text{data}}$, so $D^*(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}$. The value function reaches the global minimum of $-\log 4$, corresponding to a **Nash equilibrium** where the discriminator is maximally confused.

---

#### **Part (b) — VAE Block Diagram & ELBO Derivation [7 marks]**

**Block Diagram [2 marks]:**

```
[Input x] --> [Encoder q_phi(z|x)] --> [Latent z (mu, sigma)] 
                                          |
                                          v
[Output x_hat] <-- [Decoder P_theta(x|z)] <-- [Sampled z = mu + sigma * epsilon]
```

**ELBO Derivation [5 marks]:**

Starting from the marginal log-likelihood:

$$\log P_{\theta}(x) = \log \int P_{\theta}(x \mid z) P(z) \, dz$$

**Step 1** — Multiply and divide by $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$:

$$\log P_{\theta}(x) = \log \int q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \frac{P_{\theta}(x \mid z) P(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)} dz$$

**Step 2** — Rewrite as expectation:

$$\log P_{\theta}(x) = \log \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\!\left[\frac{P_{\theta}(x \mid z) P(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\right]$$

**Step 3** — Apply Jensen's Inequality ($\log$ is concave):

$$\log P_{\theta}(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\!\left[\log \frac{P_{\theta}(x \mid z) P(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\right]$$

**Step 4** — Split into two terms:

$$\log P_{\theta}(x) \geq \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\log P_{\theta}(x \mid z)]}_{\text{Reconstruction}} - \underbrace{D_{KL}\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \,\Vert\, P(z)\right)}_{\text{Regularization}}$$

**Interpretation [from remaining 0 marks, considered in credit]:** This is the ELBO — a tractable lower bound on $\log P_{\theta}(x)$ that is differentiable and can be maximized via stochastic gradient descent. The **reparameterization trick** $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$ enables backpropagation through the sampling step.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Model QP, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **forward (noising) and reverse (denoising) processes** of Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs). Derive the closed-form marginal $q(x_t \mid x_0)$ in terms of cumulative noise schedule $\bar{\alpha}_t$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the **training stability and sample quality** of GANs, VAEs, and Diffusion Models. Use FID/IS as evaluation criteria.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — DDPM Forward & Reverse Processes [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Forward (Noising) Process [2 marks]:**
A fixed Markov chain gradually adds Gaussian noise to a clean image $x_0$ over $T$ steps:

$$q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(x_t;\; \sqrt{1 - \beta_t}\, x_{t-1},\; \beta_t I\right), \quad t = 1, \dots, T$$

The noise schedule $\{\beta_t\}$ is predefined (linear or cosine) and is **not** learned.

**Step 2 — Define Cumulative Coefficients [1 mark]:**
$\alpha_t = 1 - \beta_t$ and $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t} \alpha_s$.

**Step 3 — Derive Closed-Form Marginal [3 marks]:**
By recursive reparameterization:

$$x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\, \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

Hence:

$$q(x_t \mid x_0) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(x_t;\; \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0,\; (1 - \bar{\alpha}_t) I\right)$$

This lets us sample $x_t$ in **one step** rather than $t$ recursive steps.

**Step 4 — Reverse (Denoising) Process [1 mark]:**
A neural network $\epsilon_{\theta}(x_t, t)$ predicts the noise; the reverse process denoises step-by-step to recover $x_0$ from pure noise $x_T \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.

---

#### **Part (b) — Comparative Analysis [7 marks]**

| Criterion | GAN | VAE | Diffusion Model |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----------------|
| **Sample Sharpness** | ★★★★★ Excellent | ★★★ Blurry | ★★★★★ Excellent |
| **Training Stability** | ★★ Unstable, mode collapse | ★★★★ Stable | ★★★★★ Very stable |
| **Likelihood Estimation** | ❌ Implicit density | ✅ Lower bound | ✅ Exact ELBO-like |
| **Inference Speed** | ★★★★★ Fast (1 pass) | ★★★★★ Fast | ★★ Slow ($T$ steps, e.g., 1000) |
| **FID Score (typical)** | Low (good) | Moderate | Lowest (best) |
| **Mode Coverage** | Poor (mode collapse) | Good | Excellent |
| **Interpretability** | Low | Moderate (latent space) | Moderate (noise schedule) |

**Conclusion [2 marks]:** Diffusion Models have become the **gold standard for image synthesis** (e.g., Stable Diffusion, DALL·E 3, Imagen) because they combine the sample quality of GANs with the training stability of likelihood-based models. Their main weakness is **slow inference**, which is being actively addressed via techniques like DDIM sampling, DPM-Solver, and consistency models.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the assumption** $P(X) > 0$ in Bayes' theorem derivations. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Confusing $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q)$ with $D_{KL}(Q \Vert P)$** — they are *not* symmetric and the order matters in VAE regularization. **(−2 marks)**
> 3. **Skipping the Jensen's Inequality justification** in ELBO derivation — examiners expect the explicit statement "since $\log$ is concave." **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **In GAN questions, students often forget to mention the Nash Equilibrium** at the end of optimal-discriminator derivation. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **For diffusion models, students frequently omit the reparameterization** that yields the closed-form $q(x_t \mid x_0)$ — they write the iterative form instead, losing **2–3 marks**.
> 6. **Not labeling axes/curves in diagrams** (e.g., diffusion schedule $\beta_t$ vs. $t$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "The Rise of Generative Modeling"**

- ⭐ **Core Definition:** A generative model learns $P(X)$ or $P(X, Y)$, enabling it to *synthesize new samples* — not just classify existing ones.
- ⭐ **The Fundamental Trade-off:** Generative models are more expressive and data-efficient (can use unlabeled data) than discriminative models, but are **computationally more expensive and harder to train**.
- ⭐ **Bayes' Rule is the bedrock:** $P(Y \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid Y) P(Y)}{P(X)}$ — discriminative models skip this, generative models embrace it.
- ⭐ **VAE ELBO has two terms:** *Reconstruction* (fidelity) − *KL Regularization* (latent smoothness). Don't mix them up.
- ⭐ **GAN optimal discriminator:** $D^*(x) = \frac{P_{\text{data}}(x)}{P_{\text{data}}(x) + P_g(x)}$. At equilibrium, $D^* = \tfrac{1}{2}$.
- ⭐ **GANs minimize the Jensen–Shannon divergence** at equilibrium — this is why training can be unstable (vanishing gradients when supports don't overlap).
- ⭐ **Diffusion closed-form marginal:** $q(x_t \mid x_0) = \mathcal{N}(\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0,\; (1 - \bar{\alpha}_t) I)$ — memorize the $\bar{\alpha}_t$ formula.
- ⭐ **Reparameterization trick:** $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$ — essential for VAE training, enables backprop through random sampling.
- ⭐ **Four families of generative models:** Likelihood-based (VAE, NFs), Implicit (GAN), Energy-based (Boltzmann), Diffusion/Score-based.
- ⭐ **Standard metrics:** FID ↓ (lower better), IS ↑, Perplexity ↓, Log-likelihood ↑, BPD ↓.
- ⭐ **Historical catalysts:** AlexNet (2012) → VAE (2013) → GAN (2014) → Transformer (2017) → Diffusion (2020) → Foundation Models (2022+).
- ⭐ **Real-world impact:** Drug discovery, code generation, content creation, scientific simulation, design automation.
- ⭐ **KTU Hot Topic:** Expect derivations of ELBO, optimal $D^*$, and the diffusion forward marginal to be the most commonly asked long-answer questions in Module 1.
- ⭐ **Examiner's Pet Pitfall:** Always declare assumptions (concavity of log, non-zero denominators, prior $P(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$) — silence here costs marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Generative Modeling Framework
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Generative Modeling Framework

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Generative Modeling Framework** is a unified mathematical and statistical paradigm in machine learning whose central objective is to learn the **underlying data-generating distribution** $p_{\text{data}}(x)$ from a finite set of samples, and subsequently to draw *new* samples that are statistically indistinguishable from the original data. Formally, given an unknown true distribution $p_{\text{data}}(x)$ and a parametric family of distributions $p_{\theta}(x)$ (indexed by parameters $\theta$), the framework seeks parameters $\theta^{*}$ that bring $p_{\theta}(x)$ as close as possible to $p_{\text{data}}(x)$.

$$p_{\theta}(x) \;\approx\; p_{\text{data}}(x)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The framework explicitly distinguishes **Generative Models** (which model $p(x)$ or $p(x \vert y)$) from **Discriminative Models** (which model $p(y \vert x)$). The core training objective is to minimize the divergence between the model distribution and the true data distribution, typically using **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** as the foundational principle.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Forger and the Curator"

Imagine a museum that wishes to identify whether a submitted painting is an original Monet or a forgery:

* A **Discriminative Model** is the **museum curator**. It learns a *decision boundary* — it looks at a painting and asks: *"Is the brushstroke on the left side characteristic of a real Monet (label = 1) or a fake (label = 0)?"* It never has to understand *how* a Monet painting is created; it only needs to tell originals from fakes.
* A **Generative Model** is the **forger**. It must learn the entire creative process — the colour palette, the brush pressure, the composition, the lighting. To produce a convincing fake, the forger must internalize the **full joint distribution** of every pixel and pigment. Once learned, the forger can produce *new, never-before-seen* Monet-like paintings.

### 1.3 The Two Pillars of the Framework

Every generative model in the KTU syllabus — whether it is a Gaussian Mixture Model, a Variational Autoencoder (VAE), a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN), or an autoregressive Transformer — instantiates the same two-pillar structure:

1. **Density Estimation** — learn $p_{\theta}(x)$ (or $p_{\theta}(x \vert y)$ for conditional generation such as text-to-image).
2. **Sampling** — draw a fresh instance $x_{\text{new}} \sim p_{\theta}(x)$.

> [!NOTE]
> A useful diagnostic: if a model can answer *"How likely is this $x$?"* and can also draw new $x$'s, it is generative. If it can only answer *"Which class does this $x$ belong to?"* it is discriminative.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Discriminative vs. Generative — Operational Contrast

| Aspect | Discriminative Model | Generative Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Learn Target** | $p(y \mid x)$ | $p(x)$ or $p(x, y)$ |
| **Question Answered** | "What is the label?" | "How is the data created?" |
| **Boundary** | Decision boundary | Full distribution |
| **Sampling Ability** | None | Can draw new $x$ |
| **Examples** | Logistic Regression, SVM, CNN classifier | GMM, VAE, GAN, Diffusion Models |
| **Information Required** | Less (just the boundary) | More (full data structure) |

### 2.2 Bayes' Theorem — The Mathematical Bridge

Bayes' theorem connects the generative quantity $p(x, y)$ with the discriminative quantity $p(y \mid x)$, justifying why *modelling $p(x, y)$ is strictly more powerful than modelling $p(y \mid x)$ alone*.

$$p(y \mid x) \;=\; \frac{p(x \mid y)\,p(y)}{p(x)} \;=\; \frac{p(x \mid y)\,p(y)}{\sum_{y'} p(x \mid y')\,p(y')}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A common 3-mark question asks students to *derive* how a generative model can be converted into a discriminative classifier (by applying Bayes' rule). Memorize the formula — it is the bridge that makes Naive Bayes, GMM-classifiers, and even modern LLM-based classifiers work.

### 2.3 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) — The Canonical Training Rule

Given an i.i.d. dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(N)}\}$ drawn from $p_{\text{data}}(x)$, the MLE objective is:

$$\theta^{*} \;=\; \arg\max_{\theta} \prod_{i=1}^{N} p_{\theta}\!\left(x^{(i)}\right) \;=\; \arg\max_{\theta} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_{\theta}\!\left(x^{(i)}\right)$$

The second equality uses the **monotonicity of the logarithm**: the product is converted to a sum, which is numerically stable and mathematically convenient because it turns products of small probabilities into summable terms.

### 2.4 KL Divergence — The True Objective Hidden Inside MLE

The **Kullback–Leibler (KL) Divergence** measures how one probability distribution $Q$ diverges from a reference distribution $P$:

$$D_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\left(P \,\|\, Q\right) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P}\!\left[\log \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}\right] \;=\; \sum_{x} P(x) \log \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Property:** $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P \| Q) \geq 0$, with equality *if and only if* $P = Q$ almost everywhere. KL is **asymmetric**: $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P \| Q) \neq D_{\mathrm{KL}}(Q \| P)$. This asymmetry is the technical reason why two generative-model families (forward-KL vs. reverse-KL) produce visually different sample qualities.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Joint Probability | $p(x, y) = p(y \mid x)\,p(x)$ | Foundation of all generative models |
| Bayes' Rule | $p(y \mid x) = \dfrac{p(x \mid y)\,p(y)}{p(x)}$ | Bridge between generative & discriminative |
| MLE Objective | $\theta^{*} = \arg\max_{\theta} \sum_{i} \log p_{\theta}(x^{(i)})$ | Log-likelihood is unit-less (nats) |
| KL Divergence | $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P \| Q) = \mathbb{E}_{P}[\log P - \log Q]$ | Always $\geq 0$; units = nats or bits |
| MLE $\equiv$ Min Forward-KL | $\min_{\theta} D_{\mathrm{KL}}(p_{\text{data}} \| p_{\theta})$ | Equivalent to MLE under i.i.d. assumption |
| Cross-Entropy (MINE eq.) | $H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P \| Q)$ | Used as a practical loss |
| Independence | $p(x, y) = p(x)\,p(y)$ | Required for Naive Bayes assumption |
| Log-Likelihood | $\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i} \log p_{\theta}(x^{(i)})$ | Maximized during training |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The framework is the backbone of modern AI engineering: text generation (LLMs like GPT), image synthesis (Stable Diffusion, DALL·E), audio generation (WaveNet, MusicLM), drug discovery (molecular generation), and synthetic data augmentation in autonomous-driving simulations. In every case, the engineering pipeline is the same: **estimate $p_{\theta}(x)$ from data, then sample**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From MLE to KL-Divergence Minimization

**Goal:** Prove that maximizing the log-likelihood is *mathematically identical* to minimizing the forward KL divergence between the empirical data distribution and the model distribution.

**Step 1 — Define the empirical data distribution.**

The training set $\mathcal{D} = \{x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(N)}\}$ implicitly defines an **empirical distribution** that places a point mass of $\frac{1}{N}$ on each observed sample:

$$p_{\text{data}}(x) \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{I}\!\left[x = x^{(i)}\right]$$

where $\mathbb{I}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function ($\mathbb{I} = 1$ if the condition is true, else $0$).

**Step 2 — Write the KL divergence between $p_{\text{data}}$ and $p_{\theta}$.**

$$D_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_{\theta}\right) \;=\; \sum_{x} p_{\text{data}}(x)\, \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\theta}(x)}$$

**Step 3 — Split the logarithm.**

$$D_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_{\theta}\right) \;=\; \sum_{x} p_{\text{data}}(x)\,\log p_{\text{data}}(x) \;-\; \sum_{x} p_{\text{data}}(x)\,\log p_{\theta}(x)$$

**Step 4 — Observe the first term is constant w.r.t. $\theta$.**

The first sum, $\sum_{x} p_{\text{data}}(x)\log p_{\text{data}}(x)$, depends *only* on the dataset, not on the model parameters $\theta$. Therefore it plays no role in the optimization:

$$\text{Constant } C \;=\; \sum_{x} p_{\text{data}}(x)\,\log p_{\text{data}}(x)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the empirical distribution into the second term.**

Since $p_{\text{data}}(x) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i} \mathbb{I}[x = x^{(i)}]$, the second sum collapses — only the $N$ points where the indicator is $1$ contribute:

$$-\sum_{x} p_{\text{data}}(x)\,\log p_{\theta}(x) \;=\; -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_{\theta}\!\left(x^{(i)}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Combine and identify the log-likelihood.**

Putting it together, ignoring the constant and the factor of $\frac{1}{N}$ (which do not affect the argmax):

$$\min_{\theta}\, D_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \| p_{\theta}\right) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \max_{\theta}\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_{\theta}\!\left(x^{(i)}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This equivalence is the single most important theoretical result in the KTU Module-1 syllabus.** It tells you that *every* generative model trained with maximum likelihood is, *without knowing it*, performing forward-KL divergence minimization.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Non-negativity of KL Divergence

**Goal:** Prove $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P \| Q) \geq 0$.

**Step 1 — Recall Gibbs' inequality.** For any two probability distributions $P$ and $Q$:

$$\sum_{x} P(x)\,\log P(x) \;\geq\; \sum_{x} P(x)\,\log Q(x)$$

with equality iff $P = Q$.

**Step 2 — Apply Gibbs' inequality directly to the definition of KL.**

$$-D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P \| Q) \;=\; \sum_{x} P(x)\,\log \frac{Q(x)}{P(x)} \;\leq\; 0$$

Multiplying by $-1$ reverses the inequality:

$$D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P \| Q) \;\geq\; 0 \quad\blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Models to the Framework

| Model Family | Learns | Training Loss (≈ equivalent to) | Sampling Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Naive Bayes** | $p(x \mid y)\,p(y)$ | MLE on counts | Closed-form |
| **GMM** | $p(x) = \sum_{k} \pi_{k}\, \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_{k}, \Sigma_{k})$ | MLE via EM | Ancestral sampling |
| **VAE** | $p_{\theta}(x)$ via latent $z$ | Maximize ELBO $\approx$ reverse-KL | Reparameterization trick |
| **GAN** | Implicit $p_{\theta}(x)$ | Minimax game (not MLE) | Single forward pass $G(z)$ |
| **Diffusion** | Noise-prediction $\epsilon_{\theta}(x_t, t)$ | MLE on score function | Reverse SDE / DDPM |
| **Autoregressive LM** | $p(x) = \prod_{t} p(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ | Cross-entropy (MLE) | Token-by-token decoding |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — The Generative Modeling Pipeline

The high-level block diagram showing the complete flow from raw data to generated sample, applicable to *every* generative model in the syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[rawDataSet] --> B[empiricalDistPdata]
    B --> C{chooseModelFamily}
    C --> D1[parametricPthetaX]
    C --> D2[implicitPthetaX]
    C --> D3[latentVariablePthetaXZ]
    D1 --> E[trainViaMLEorEM]
    D2 --> F[trainViaAdversarial]
    D3 --> G[trainViaELBO]
    E --> H[learnedModel]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[newSampleXnew]
    H --> J[densityQueryPX]
    H --> K[conditionalSampleXY]

    classDef dataNode fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef modelNode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    class A,B dataNode
    class C,D1,D2,D3,E,F,G modelNode
    class H,I,J,K outputNode
```

### 4.2 Diagram B — Discriminative vs. Generative Conceptual Split

A block-level schematic contrasting the *information flow* of the two paradigms. Discriminative models cut straight to the decision boundary; generative models must learn the full joint distribution first.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph D [Discriminative Pathway]
        D1[inputX] --> D2[learnPYgivenX] --> D3[decisionBoundary] --> D4[predictedLabelY]
    end

    subgraph G [Generative Pathway]
        G1[inputXandY] --> G2[learnJointPXY] --> G3[factorizePXtimesPYgivenX] --> G4[fullDistributionPX]
        G4 --> G5[applyBayesRule] --> G4
        G4 --> G6[sampledXnew]
    end

    classDef disc fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
    classDef gen fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000
    class D1,D2,D3,D4 disc
    class G1,G2,G3,G4,G5,G6 gen
```

### 4.3 Diagram C — MLE to KL Equivalence (Block Schematic)

This block diagram visualizes the derivation in Section 3.1 — the constant term and the data-dependent term cleanly separated.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[KLdivergence] --> B[sumPdataLogPdata]
    A --> C[sumPdataLogPtheta]
    B --> D[constantWithRespectToTheta]
    C --> E[minusOneOverNtimesSumLogPthetaXi]
    D --> F[droppedInOptimization]
    E --> G[negativeAverageLogLikelihood]
    G --> H[maximizeLogLikelihood]
    F --> H

    classDef input fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#075985,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    class A,B,C input
    class D,E,F,G proc
    class H out
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
*Distinguish between generative and discriminative models with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **discriminative model** learns the conditional probability $p(y \mid x)$ — the boundary that separates classes — without modelling how the input $x$ is generated. Example: Logistic Regression, which directly estimates $P(y=1 \mid x)$.

A **generative model** learns the full distribution $p(x)$ (or the joint $p(x, y)$), from which new samples can be drawn. Example: Gaussian Mixture Model, which estimates $p(x) = \sum_{k} \pi_{k}\,\mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_{k}, \Sigma_{k})$ and can synthesize new points via ancestral sampling.

Key difference: discriminative models answer *"which class?"*; generative models answer *"how is data made?"* and additionally support sampling and density queries. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*State Bayes' theorem and explain its role in connecting generative and discriminative modelling.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$p(y \mid x) \;=\; \frac{p(x \mid y)\,p(y)}{p(x)}$$

**Role:** A generative model estimates $p(x \mid y)$ and $p(y)$. Once these are known, Bayes' theorem mechanically produces the discriminative quantity $p(y \mid x)$ — without ever training a separate classifier. This is why Naive Bayes, GMMs, and even LLM-based classifiers can perform classification *as a by-product* of having learned a generative model. The denominator $p(x) = \sum_{y'} p(x \mid y')\,p(y')$ is the **evidence**, a normalizing constant ensuring the result is a valid probability. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently (a) confuse *forward* KL $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P \| Q)$ with *reverse* KL $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(Q \| P)$ — the choice changes the qualitative behaviour of the model (mean-seeking vs. mode-seeking); (b) forget to *state* the non-negativity property of KL when asked to "discuss" it; and (c) write Bayes' theorem without identifying the role of the evidence term $p(x)$. **Each of these omissions costs 2 marks.**

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1, Set A]`

**(a) Derive the equivalence between Maximum Likelihood Estimation and the minimization of the forward KL divergence for a dataset of i.i.d. samples. Show every algebraic step. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Define the empirical data distribution* (1 mark)

$$p_{\text{data}}(x) \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{I}\!\left[x = x^{(i)}\right]$$

*Step 2 — Write the forward KL divergence* (1 mark)

$$D_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \| p_{\theta}\right) \;=\; \sum_{x} p_{\text{data}}(x)\, \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\theta}(x)}$$

*Step 3 — Split the log* (1 mark)

$$D_{\mathrm{KL}} \;=\; \underbrace{\sum_{x} p_{\text{data}}(x)\,\log p_{\text{data}}(x)}_{\text{Term A}} \;-\; \underbrace{\sum_{x} p_{\text{data}}(x)\,\log p_{\theta}(x)}_{\text{Term B}}$$

*Step 4 — Identify Term A as constant w.r.t. $\theta$* (1 mark)

Term A depends only on the data, so $\frac{\partial A}{\partial \theta} = 0$.

*Step 5 — Substitute the empirical distribution into Term B* (1 mark)

Only the $N$ training points contribute, giving

$$\text{Term B} \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_{\theta}\!\left(x^{(i)}\right)$$

*Step 6 — Conclude the equivalence* (2 marks)

$$\min_{\theta} D_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \| p_{\theta}\right) \quad\equiv\quad \max_{\theta} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_{\theta}\!\left(x^{(i)}\right)$$

Thus MLE = forward-KL minimization. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Explain why KL divergence is asymmetric and discuss the practical consequence of this asymmetry in mean-seeking (reverse-KL) versus mode-seeking (forward-KL) behaviour. Use a single-mode vs. multi-modal example. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Asymmetry (2 marks):* $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P \| Q) \neq D_{\mathrm{KL}}(Q \| P)$ in general. The expectation is taken over *different* distributions, so the two quantities weight probability mass differently.

*Forward-KL $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P_{\text{data}} \| P_{\theta})$ — "mean-seeking" (2 marks):* The expectation is over $P_{\text{data}}$, so wherever $P_{\text{data}}$ has mass, the model $P_{\theta}$ is *forced* to put mass too. If $P_{\text{data}}$ is multi-modal, the model must cover *all* modes — its samples will lie on the *mean* of the modes, sometimes producing blurry in-between outputs (a phenomenon called "mode-averaging").

*Reverse-KL $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P_{\theta} \| P_{\text{data}})$ — "mode-seeking" (2 marks):* The expectation is over $P_{\theta}$. The model is free to choose *one* mode and ignore the others, as long as the chosen mode has low reverse-KL. Samples are sharp but diversity is lost.

*Practical consequence (1 mark):* VAEs typically minimize reverse-KL (sharp but mode-collapsed samples), whereas GANs and diffusion models behave closer to forward-KL (more diverse but sometimes lower-quality individual samples). **(7 marks)**

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1, Set B]`

**(a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the Generative Modeling Framework. Identify the two primary objectives and the training principle that unifies them. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Framework description (3 marks):* The framework begins with a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$ assumed to be i.i.d. samples from an unknown distribution $p_{\text{data}}(x)$. A parametric family $p_{\theta}(x)$ is chosen, and the parameters are updated so that $p_{\theta}(x) \approx p_{\text{data}}(x)$. The diagram is reproduced here in textual form:

```
[Dataset] --> [Empirical p_data] --> [Choose p_theta family] --> [Train via MLE] --> [Learned model] --> [Sample / Query]
```

*Two primary objectives (2 marks):* **(1) Density estimation** — accurately compute or approximate $p_{\theta}(x)$ for any $x$. **(2) Sampling** — draw fresh $x_{\text{new}} \sim p_{\theta}(x)$ that resembles training data.

*Unifying training principle (2 marks):* **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** is the canonical training rule, equivalent to minimizing the forward KL divergence. It provides a single, principled loss that all generative models in the syllabus — GMM, VAE, diffusion, autoregressive — implicitly or explicitly optimize. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) A 1-D dataset consists of samples $\{-2.0,\,-1.0,\,0.0,\,1.0,\,2.0\}$. You fit a univariate Gaussian $p_{\theta}(x) = \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu, \sigma^{2})$ by maximum likelihood. Compute the MLE estimates $\hat{\mu}$ and $\hat{\sigma}^{2}$. Then write down the log-likelihood value at the optimum. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Compute the sample mean* (2 marks)

$$\hat{\mu} \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x^{(i)} \;=\; \frac{(-2)+(-1)+0+1+2}{5} \;=\; \frac{0}{5} \;=\; 0$$

*Compute the sample variance* (2 marks)

$$\hat{\sigma}^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(x^{(i)} - \hat{\mu}\right)^{2} \;=\; \frac{4+1+0+1+4}{5} \;=\; \frac{10}{5} \;=\; 2$$

*Write the log-likelihood at the optimum* (2 marks)

The Gaussian log-likelihood is

$$\ell(\mu, \sigma^{2}) \;=\; -\frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi) \;-\; \frac{N}{2}\log(\sigma^{2}) \;-\; \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu\right)^{2}$$

Substituting $N = 5$, $\hat{\mu} = 0$, $\hat{\sigma}^{2} = 2$:

$$\ell(\hat{\mu}, \hat{\sigma}^{2}) \;=\; -\frac{5}{2}\log(2\pi) \;-\; \frac{5}{2}\log(2) \;-\; \frac{10}{2 \cdot 2} \;=\; -\tfrac{5}{2}\log(4\pi) \;-\; 2.5$$

Numerically, $-\tfrac{5}{2}\log(4\pi) \approx -9.19$ nats, and subtracting $2.5$ gives $\ell \approx -11.69$ nats. **(7 marks)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Generative vs. Discriminative:** generative models learn $p(x)$ or $p(x, y)$ and can sample; discriminative models learn $p(y \mid x)$ and classify.
- **Bayes' Rule:** $p(y \mid x) = \dfrac{p(x \mid y)\,p(y)}{p(x)}$ is the bridge that converts a generative model into a classifier.
- **MLE objective:** $\theta^{*} = \arg\max_{\theta} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_{\theta}(x^{(i)})$ — always take the log for numerical stability.
- **KL Divergence:** $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P \| Q) = \mathbb{E}_{P}[\log P - \log Q] \geq 0$, asymmetric, zero iff $P = Q$.
- **Central theorem of Module 1:** MLE $\equiv$ minimization of forward KL divergence $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(p_{\text{data}} \| p_{\theta})$.
- **Forward-KL → mean-seeking / mode-covering; reverse-KL → mode-seeking** — the asymmetry has visible consequences on sample quality.
- **Cross-entropy** $H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P \| Q)$ — the practical loss used in classification is just KL plus a constant.
- **Empirical distribution:** $p_{\text{data}}(x) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i} \mathbb{I}[x = x^{(i)}]$ — this substitution is the *key step* in every MLE-to-KL derivation.
- **Mermaid/LaTeX hygiene to remember for paper-writing:** use `\vert` instead of `|` inside markdown tables; one blank line around every `$$` block; alphabet-prefixed node IDs in Mermaid.
- **High-yield exam fact:** all modern generative models (VAE, GAN, Diffusion, LLMs) are different *parameterizations* of $p_{\theta}(x)$ trained by the *same* MLE principle — recognizing this unifies the entire syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Challenges of Generative Modeling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Challenges of Generative Modeling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned):**
> *Challenges of Generative Modeling* refer to the set of mathematical, statistical, computational, and ethical obstacles encountered when training and deploying generative models — systems that attempt to learn an unknown data distribution $P_{data}(x)$ in order to synthesize new samples $x \sim P_{model}(x)$ that are statistically indistinguishable from the training distribution.

In formal probability terms, a generative model $G_\theta$ parameterized by $\theta$ aims to minimize a divergence measure $D(\cdot \Vert \cdot)$ between the true distribution $P_{data}$ and the model distribution $P_\theta$:

$$\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \; D\!\left(P_{data}(x) \,\Vert\, P_\theta(x)\right)$$

The **Challenges of Generative Modeling** encompass all theoretical and practical difficulties that arise when trying to solve, approximate, or optimize this objective.

## 1.2 Taxonomy of Generative Modeling Challenges (High-Level Overview)

| Challenge Family | Core Question | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Statistical | How do we measure distributional closeness? | Divergences are intractable in high dimensions |
| Optimization | How do we train a non-convex generator? | Loss landscapes are riddled with saddle points |
| Architectural | Can the model express $P_{data}$? | Capacity vs. tractability trade-off |
| Computational | Can we afford to train? | Memory, FLOPs, and wall-clock constraints |
| Evaluation | Is the output "good"? | No single metric captures quality + diversity |
| Societal | Is the output safe? | Bias, deepfakes, hallucination |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Forensic Sketch Artist"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine you hire a **forensic sketch artist** (the *generator*) who has never met a suspect but is shown 10,000 photographs of suspects (the *training data*). The artist's job is to draw a *new* face that looks like it could belong to the same "population" of suspects.
>
> The **challenges** arise because:
> 1. The artist might keep drawing the *same face* (Mode Collapse).
> 2. The feedback from a detective (the *discriminator*) might be too harsh or too vague (Vanishing/Exploding Gradients).
> 3. The artist might memorize the photos instead of learning patterns (Overfitting / Memorization).
> 4. You have no objective ruler to measure how "realistic" a sketch is (Evaluation Problem).
> 5. The artist's studio might catch fire because the GPU overheats (Computational Cost).
> 6. The sketches might inherit human biases from the photographs (Fairness / Bias).

## 1.4 The Central Mathematical Tension

Generative modeling is fundamentally a problem of **density estimation without explicit access to $P_{data}$**. The challenges are all downstream consequences of three unobservable quantities:

$$P_{data}(x) = \text{unknown}, \quad Z = \text{latent noise}, \quad G_\theta(Z) \xrightarrow{?} P_{data}(x)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test the difference between **implicit** generative models (GANs — no explicit likelihood) and **explicit** generative models (VAEs, Normalizing Flows, Autoregressive models — tractable likelihood). The challenges differ significantly between these two families.

---

## 1.5 Quick-Reference Glossary for This Module

- **Mode Collapse** — Generator produces limited varieties of outputs.
- **Posterior Collapse** — Latent variables become uninformative (VAE-specific).
- **Hallucination** — Fluent but factually incorrect generation (LLM-specific).
- **FID** — Fréchet Inception Distance; measures quality + diversity jointly.
- **KL Divergence** — $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \mathbb{E}_{P}[\log(P/Q)]$; non-symmetric measure.
- **Jensen–Shannon Divergence** — Symmetric, bounded version used in vanilla GANs.
- **Curse of Dimensionality** — Exponential growth of space with dimensions.
- **Adversarial Example** — Input crafted to fool the model with imperceptible changes.
- **Memorization** — Model reproduces training data verbatim instead of generalizing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Generative Modeling Challenges

### Pillar 1 — Statistical & Density Estimation Challenges

**Why it is hard:** The true data distribution $P_{data}(x)$ in high-dimensional spaces (e.g., $1024 \times 1024$ images = 786,432 dimensions) is an absolutely continuous, intractable object.

**Key sub-problems:**

- **Intractability of the Partition Function:**
  In energy-based models, the probability is defined as:
  
$$P(x) = \frac{e^{-E(x)}}{Z}, \quad Z = \int_{x} e^{-E(x)}\,dx$$
  
  Computing $Z$ requires integration over an exponentially large space — **infeasible for high-dimensional $x$**.

- **Choice of Divergence:** Different divergence choices lead to radically different optimization behaviors.

  | Divergence | Formula | Symmetric? | Used In |
  |---|---|---|---|
  | KL (forward) | $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \sum P \log(P/Q)$ | No | VAE |
  | KL (reverse) | $D_{KL}(Q \Vert P)$ | No | Wake-Sleep |
  | Jensen–Shannon | $JSD(P \Vert Q) = \tfrac{1}{2}D_{KL}(P \Vert M) + \tfrac{1}{2}D_{KL}(Q \Vert M)$ | Yes | Original GAN |
  | Wasserstein-1 | $W_1(P, Q) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\gamma}[\Vert x-y \Vert]$ | Yes | WGAN |

- **Curse of Dimensionality:** The volume of space grows exponentially; samples become sparse, and density estimation becomes ill-posed.

### Pillar 2 — Optimization & Training Instability

**Why it is hard:** Generative models are typically non-convex, with adversarial or variational objectives that produce chaotic loss landscapes.

- **Vanishing Gradients in GANs:** When the discriminator becomes near-perfect, the sigmoid cross-entropy saturates and $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_G \to 0$.
- **Exploding Gradients in RNN-based generators:** Multiplicative backpropagation through time causes gradient magnitudes to blow up.
- **Saddle Points:** In high dimensions, saddle points (not local minima) dominate the loss landscape, making second-order methods expensive.
- **Mode Hopping:** In GANs, the generator may oscillate between modes instead of converging.

### Pillar 3 — Mode Collapse & Mode Dropping

**Why it is hard:** The generator discovers a "shortcut" by producing a few high-confidence samples that fool the discriminator.

- **Mode Collapse** → All generated samples look alike.
- **Mode Dropping** → Some modes of $P_{data}$ are entirely ignored.

Mathematically, if $P_{data}$ has $k$ modes (peaks) but $P_\theta$ captures only $m < k$ of them, then **support mismatch** occurs.

### Pillar 4 — Evaluation Challenges (The "No Ground Truth" Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Question:** *"Why is it difficult to evaluate generative models?"*

Because there is **no single scalar metric** that captures:
1. **Sample Quality** — Are individual samples realistic?
2. **Sample Diversity** — Does the model cover the full data distribution?
3. **Mode Coverage** — Are all modes represented?
4. **Per-Sample Likelihood** — Often intractable for implicit models.

| Metric | What It Measures | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **Inception Score (IS)** | Quality + Diversity via classifier entropy | Biased toward ImageNet |
| **Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)** | Distance between Gaussians fitted to Inception features | Requires large sample size |
| **Perplexity** | Likelihood in language models | Not comparable across corpora |
| **Human Evaluation** | Subjective judgment | Expensive, non-reproducible |

### Pillar 5 — Computational & Scalability Challenges

- **Memory:** A single $1024 \times 1024$ image as a 3-channel float32 tensor = **12 MB**. Training batches of 64 = **768 MB just for one forward pass**.
- **FLOPs:** Diffusion models like Stable Diffusion require $\sim 10^{21}$ FLOPs for training.
- **Wall-Clock:** State-of-the-art models require thousands of GPU-hours on H100 clusters.
- **Carbon Footprint:** Training a large LLM can emit **hundreds of tons of CO₂**.

### Pillar 6 — Societal, Ethical & Safety Challenges

- **Bias Amplification:** Models inherit and amplify societal biases present in training data.
- **Deepfakes & Misinformation:** Synthetic media can be weaponized.
- **Hallucination in LLMs:** Fluent outputs that are factually incorrect.
- **Copyright & Memorization:** Models may regurgitate training data verbatim.
- **Adversarial Vulnerability:** Small input perturbations cause catastrophic output changes.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Where It Appears |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \int P(x) \log \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}\,dx$ | Asymmetric divergence | VAE objective |
| 2 | $JSD(P \Vert Q) = \tfrac{1}{2}D_{KL}(P \Vert M) + \tfrac{1}{2}D_{KL}(Q \Vert M)$ | Symmetric, bounded | GAN objective |
| 3 | $W_1(P,Q) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi(P,Q)} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\gamma}[\Vert x-y \Vert]$ | Earth Mover's Distance | WGAN |
| 4 | $\mathcal{L}_{GAN} = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim P_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | Min-max objective | Vanilla GAN |
| 5 | $\mathcal{L}_{VAE} = -D_{KL}(q_\phi(z \vert x) \,\Vert\, p(z)) + \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi}[\log p_\theta(x \vert z)]$ | ELBO | VAE training |
| 6 | $\text{FID} = \Vert \mu_r - \mu_g \Vert^2 + \text{Tr}\!\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)$ | Inception-feature Fréchet distance | Image evaluation |
| 7 | $\text{IS} = \exp\!\left(\mathbb{E}_x \, D_{KL}(p(y \vert x) \,\Vert\, p(y))\right)$ | Inception Score | Image evaluation |
| 8 | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$ | Gradient descent step | All training |
| 9 | $x_{t} = \sqrt{\alpha_t}\,x_{t-1} + \sqrt{1-\alpha_t}\,\epsilon_t$ | Diffusion forward process | Diffusion models |
| 10 | $\nabla_\theta \, J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p(z)}\!\left[\nabla_\theta \log p_\theta(x)\, R(x)\right]$ | REINFORCE / Score Function | Policy gradient style |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Notation:** Throughout this sheet, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes a delimiter using $\backslash$vert or $\backslash$mid to avoid breaking markdown tables. For example, $D_{KL}(P \,\backslash$vert$\, Q)$ in raw LaTeX.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Practice

| Application Domain | Which Challenge Dominates? |
|---|---|
| Medical Imaging Synthesis | Evaluation + Bias |
| Drug Discovery (molecule generation) | Mode collapse + Validity |
| Text-to-Image (DALL·E, Stable Diffusion) | Compute + Hallucination |
| Autonomous Driving (synthetic scenarios) | Mode coverage + Safety |
| LLM Chatbots | Hallucination + Memorization |
| Audio / Music Generation | Temporal coherence + Compute |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Why KL Divergence is Intractable for Generative Models

**Goal:** Show that directly minimizing $D_{KL}(P_{data} \Vert P_\theta)$ is infeasible when $P_{data}$ is unknown.

### Step 1 — Write the KL Divergence Definition

$$D_{KL}(P_{data} \Vert P_\theta) = \int_{x} P_{data}(x) \log \frac{P_{data}(x)}{P_\theta(x)}\,dx$$

### Step 2 — Expand Using Logarithm Algebra

$$D_{KL}(P_{data} \Vert P_\theta) = \underbrace{\int P_{data}(x) \log P_{data}(x)\,dx}_{H(P_{data})} - \int P_{data}(x) \log P_\theta(x)\,dx$$

### Step 3 — Observe the Entropy Term

The first term, $H(P_{data})$, is the **entropy of the true data distribution**. It is a constant with respect to $\theta$ and does not affect optimization.

### Step 4 — Reduce the Optimization Problem

Minimizing $D_{KL}(P_{data} \Vert P_\theta)$ over $\theta$ is equivalent to maximizing the **expected log-likelihood** of the model under the data:

$$\theta^* = \arg\min_\theta D_{KL}(P_{data} \Vert P_\theta) = \arg\max_\theta \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P_{data}}\!\left[\log P_\theta(x)\right]$$

### Step 5 — Replace Expectation with Empirical Mean

In practice, we only have $N$ i.i.d. samples $\{x^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$, so we approximate the expectation using a Monte Carlo sum:

$$\mathbb{E}_{x \sim P_{data}}\!\left[\log P_\theta(x)\right] \approx \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P_\theta(x^{(i)})$$

### Step 6 — Identify the Remaining Difficulty

The challenge is that $\log P_\theta(x)$ requires **explicit access to a normalized density**. For most model families, we can compute $\tilde{P}_\theta(x) = f_\theta(x)$ but **not** the partition function $Z_\theta$ required to normalize it. Therefore, $D_{KL}$ minimization is replaced by surrogate objectives (ELBO in VAEs, adversarial loss in GANs).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** This derivation is the conceptual bridge that motivates *every* generative model family. Examiners love to ask: *"Why can't we just minimize KL divergence directly?"* — the answer is this derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Vanishing Gradient in the Vanilla GAN Generator

**Goal:** Show mathematically why the generator stops learning when the discriminator becomes too strong.

### Step 1 — Recall the GAN Min-Max Objective

$$\min_G \max_D \; V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim P_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Optimal Discriminator

For a fixed $G$, the optimal $D^*$ that maximizes $V$ is given by:

$$D^*(x) = \frac{P_{data}(x)}{P_{data}(x) + P_g(x)}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Back to Obtain the Generator's Loss

Plugging $D^*$ back into $V$, the generator's objective becomes (up to an additive constant):

$$\mathcal{L}_G = 2\,D_{JS}(P_{data} \Vert P_g) - 2\log 2$$

### Step 4 — Examine the Gradient of the Generator

When $P_{data}$ and $P_g$ have **disjoint supports** (a common situation early in training), the **Jensen–Shannon Divergence saturates to its maximum value of $\log 2$**. This means:

$$D_{JS}(P_{data} \Vert P_g) = \log 2 \quad \text{(a constant)}$$

### Step 5 — Consequence: Vanishing Gradient

Because the loss is now a **constant**, its gradient with respect to $\theta$ is:

$$\nabla_\theta \, \mathcal{L}_G = \nabla_\theta \, \log 2 = 0$$

The generator receives **no learning signal**.

### Step 6 — Why the Sigmoid Saturates (Alternative View)

The non-saturating heuristic loss is:

$$\mathcal{L}_G^{\text{NS}} = -\mathbb{E}_{z \sim P_z}\!\left[\log D(G(z))\right]$$

When $D(G(z)) \to 0$, then $\log D(G(z)) \to -\infty$, but more importantly, the **gradient of the log-sigmoid** tends to zero:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial G(z)} \log D(G(z)) = \frac{1}{D(G(z))} \cdot \frac{\partial D}{\partial G(z)} \xrightarrow{D \to 0} 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** This is the standard textbook proof that motivates **Wasserstein GANs**, which use $W_1$ distance precisely because it has *non-vanishing gradients* even when supports are disjoint.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — The Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) Formula

**Goal:** Derive the FID as used in modern evaluation of generative models.

### Step 1 — Extract Features Using a Pre-trained Inception Network

For real images $\{x_i\}$ and generated images $\{y_j\}$, compute the Inception-v3 pool features:

$$f_r = \text{Inception}(x), \quad f_g = \text{Inception}(y)$$

### Step 2 — Assume Gaussianity of Feature Distributions

Model the empirical distributions of features as multivariate Gaussians:

$$f_r \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_r, \Sigma_r), \quad f_g \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_g, \Sigma_g)$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Fréchet Distance Between Two Gaussians

The Fréchet distance between two multivariate Gaussians is:

$$d^2\!\left(\mathcal{N}(\mu_r, \Sigma_r),\, \mathcal{N}(\mu_g, \Sigma_g)\right) = \Vert \mu_r - \mu_g \Vert^2 + \text{Tr}\!\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Interpret the Two Terms

- **$\Vert \mu_r - \mu_g \Vert^2$** → penalizes a shift in the **mean** of the feature distribution (quality).
- **$\text{Tr}(\ldots)$** → penalizes a mismatch in the **covariance** structure (diversity).

### Step 5 — Lower Bound Interpretation

$$\text{FID} = 0 \iff \mu_r = \mu_g \;\text{and}\; \Sigma_r = \Sigma_g$$

The minimum value is **0** (perfect match), and FID $\to \infty$ indicates total distributional mismatch.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that FID is **sensitive to sample size**. The standard recommendation is $N \geq 10{,}000$ generated samples for stable estimation. Examiners may deduct marks if you claim FID is reliable on small batches.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Severity of Challenges Across Model Families

| Challenge | GAN | VAE | Diffusion | Autoregressive LLM | Normalizing Flow |
|---|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Mode Collapse | **Severe** | Mild | Mild | None | None |
| Vanishing Gradients | **Severe** | Mild | Mild | Mild (exploding) | Mild |
| Computational Cost | Moderate | Low | **Severe** | High | Moderate |
| Evaluation Difficulty | **High** | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate (perplexity) | Low (exact log-lik) |
| Hallucination | N/A | N/A | Mild | **Severe** | N/A |
| Bias & Fairness | High | High | **High** | **Severe** | Moderate |
| Memorization | Moderate | Low | Moderate | **Severe** | Low |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-Loss Trap:** Do not write "GANs have vanishing gradients" without specifying it is the *generator's* gradient that vanishes, *not* the discriminator's. The discriminator continues to learn well — only the generator stalls.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define *Mode Collapse* in the context of Generative Adversarial Networks. Why is it considered a major challenge? `[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Mode Collapse** is a failure mode in GANs where the generator learns to produce a **limited variety of samples** — typically concentrating its output on one or a few modes of the data distribution, ignoring the rest. Although the generated samples may appear realistic, they lack the diversity of the true data distribution.
>
> It is considered a major challenge because:
> 1. The training objective (min-max) does not explicitly encourage diversity.
> 2. The generator discovers a "shortcut" that fools the discriminator with a single (or few) high-confidence outputs.
> 3. It is **hard to detect** using only a discriminator — one needs a separate diversity metric (e.g., FID).
> 4. It **limits real-world utility** (e.g., a generative model for drug discovery that only suggests one molecule is useless).

---

### Question 2: What is the *Curse of Dimensionality*, and how does it affect generative modeling? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

> The **Curse of Dimensionality** refers to the exponential growth of the volume of a space as its dimensionality increases. As dimensions grow, the data becomes increasingly sparse, distances become less meaningful, and density estimation becomes ill-posed.
>
> It affects generative modeling in three key ways:
> 1. **Sample Inefficiency:** Exponential volume means exponentially more data is required to cover the space.
> 2. **Evaluation Difficulty:** Distributional distances (KL, JSD, Wasserstein) become unreliable or vacuous in high dimensions.
> 3. **Training Instability:** Generators must navigate an enormous, mostly empty latent space, leading to mode collapse and gradient issues.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Discuss in detail the major challenges faced during the training of Generative Adversarial Networks. How do techniques like Wasserstein loss and mini-batch discrimination address these challenges? (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Expected]`

#### (a) Discuss the major training challenges in GANs. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

GANs face five major training challenges:

**1. Mode Collapse**
The generator learns to produce a narrow subset of outputs that fool the discriminator. Mathematically, the generated distribution $P_g$ fails to cover the support of $P_{data}$:

$$\text{Support}(P_g) \subsetneq \text{Support}(P_{data})$$

**2. Vanishing Gradients**
When the discriminator is near-optimal, the Jensen–Shannon Divergence between $P_{data}$ and $P_g$ saturates at $\log 2$. The generator's gradient vanishes:

$$\nabla_\theta \, \mathcal{L}_G \to 0$$

**3. Training Instability (Oscillation)**
The min-max objective leads to non-convergence — the generator and discriminator chase each other in a saddle-point landscape.

**4. Overfitting & Memorization**
The generator may memorize training samples rather than learning the distribution.

**5. Evaluation Difficulty**
There is no single metric to judge both quality and diversity of generated samples.

**Mitigations include:** feature matching, mini-batch discrimination, unrolled GANs, spectral normalization, and Wasserstein loss.

#### (b) Explain how Wasserstein loss and mini-batch discrimination address these challenges. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Wasserstein Loss (WGAN):**

The Wasserstein-1 distance between $P_{data}$ and $P_g$ is:

$$W_1(P_{data}, P_g) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi(P_{data}, P_g)} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim \gamma}\!\left[\Vert x - y \Vert\right]$$

By Kantorovich–Rubinstein duality, this can be estimated using a 1-Lipschitz critic $f$:

$$W_1(P_{data}, P_g) = \sup_{\Vert f \Vert_L \le 1} \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P_{data}}[f(x)] - \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P_g}[f(x)]$$

**Why it helps:**
- $W_1$ has **non-vanishing gradients** even when supports are disjoint.
- It correlates better with sample quality.
- It is **continuous everywhere**, unlike JSD.

**Mini-Batch Discrimination:**

Instead of the discriminator scoring individual samples, it scores **mini-batches** and explicitly models the similarity between samples within a batch.

- Compute a tensor $M_i = f(x_i)$ for each sample.
- Combine via a minibatch layer: $c(x_i, X) = \sum_j \exp(-\Vert M_i - M_j \Vert)$.
- The discriminator's output includes $c(x_i, X)$.

**Why it helps:**
- The discriminator can detect when the generator produces **similar samples** within a batch.
- It forces the generator to diversify outputs, directly combating **mode collapse**.

**Comparison Summary Table:**

| Technique | Addresses | Key Idea |
|---|---|---|
| Wasserstein Loss | Vanishing Gradients | Earth Mover's distance has smooth gradients |
| Mini-Batch Discrimination | Mode Collapse | Discriminator sees batch-level similarity |
| Spectral Normalization | Instability | Constrains Lipschitz constant of discriminator |

---

### Question B: Evaluate the challenges of evaluating generative models. Compare Inception Score (IS) and Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) as evaluation metrics. Discuss their limitations. (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Expected]`

#### (a) Why is it difficult to evaluate generative models? Discuss the requirements an ideal metric should satisfy. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Evaluating generative models is hard because there is **no direct access to $P_{data}$** for comparison. An ideal evaluation metric should:

1. **Measure sample quality** — Are individual samples realistic?
2. **Measure sample diversity** — Is the full distribution covered?
3. **Detect mode collapse / mode dropping.**
4. **Be computationally cheap** and reproducible.
5. **Correlate with human judgment.**
6. **Work for any modality** (images, text, audio, molecules).

**Additional challenges:**
- For **implicit models** (GANs), the likelihood $P_\theta(x)$ is intractable.
- Human evaluation is expensive and subjective.
- Different metrics favor different model families.

#### (b) Derive and compare IS and FID. Discuss their limitations. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Inception Score (IS):**

For a generated sample $x$, let $p(y \vert x)$ be the softmax output of an Inception-v3 classifier. Then:

$$\text{IS} = \exp\!\left(\mathbb{E}_{x \sim P_g}\!\left[D_{KL}\!\left(p(y \vert x) \,\Vert\, p(y)\right)\right]\right)$$

where $p(y) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P_g}[p(y \vert x)]$ is the marginal class distribution.

- **High IS** requires $p(y \vert x)$ to be *peaked* (quality) and $p(y)$ to be *uniform* (diversity).
- **Limitation:** IS does not compare to real data. A model that produces ImageNet-realistic images of *one class* scores high.

**Fréchet Inception Distance (FID):**

As derived in Section 3.3, FID is:

$$\text{FID} = \Vert \mu_r - \mu_g \Vert^2 + \text{Tr}\!\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)$$

- FID **does** compare to real data (via Gaussian fit of Inception features).
- **Lower FID** = better (closer match).
- **Limitation:** Sensitive to sample size; biased toward Inception-v3 features; assumes Gaussianity.

**Comparative Table:**

| Property | Inception Score | FID |
|---|---|---|
| Compares to real data | No | Yes |
| Detects mode collapse | No (weakly) | Yes |
| Sample-size sensitive | Low | High |
| Modality-specific | ImageNet only | ImageNet only |
| Theoretical basis | KL divergence | Fréchet distance |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> A common error is to state "**IS is always better than FID**" or vice-versa. They measure **different things**: IS measures discriminability and diversity *within generated samples*; FID measures distributional closeness *to real data*. The correct answer is to state the **trade-off** and the **use case** (e.g., FID is preferred when real data is available; IS is preferred for quick sanity checks). Marks are deducted for ignoring limitations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Six challenge families:** Statistical, Optimization, Evaluation, Computational, Architectural, Ethical.
- **Mode collapse** → generator lacks diversity; mitigated by mini-batch discrimination, unrolled GANs, Wasserstein loss.
- **Vanishing gradients** → caused by JSD saturation when supports are disjoint; solved by Wasserstein distance (smooth gradients).
- **Posterior collapse** → VAE-specific; latent variables become uninformative.
- **Hallucination** → LLM-specific; fluent but factually wrong outputs.
- **Curse of dimensionality** → exponential sample sparsity; makes density estimation ill-posed.
- **Memorization** → verbatim regurgitation of training data; mitigated by differential privacy, deduplication.
- **Evaluation** → IS (quality + diversity, no real-data comparison), FID (real-data comparison, sample-size sensitive).
- **KL Divergence** is non-symmetric and intractable; JSD is symmetric and bounded; Wasserstein is continuous and has non-vanishing gradients.
- **Computational cost** → diffusion models and large LLMs require massive GPU clusters; significant carbon footprint.
- **Bias amplification** → models inherit and exaggerate societal biases in training data.
- **Adversarial vulnerability** → small input perturbations cause catastrophic failure; a major deployment challenge.
- **KTU key takeaway:** Every generative model family has *unique* challenges; examiners reward answers that identify the **specific** challenge for a given architecture.

---

### **End of Module 1 Notes — Challenges of Generative Modeling**
