topics.title: Ethical Considerations in Generative AI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ethical Considerations in Generative AI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ethical Considerations in Generative AI** refers to the systematic study, evaluation, and governance of moral, legal, and societal implications arising from the design, training, deployment, and output of generative artificial intelligence systems (such as Large Language Models, GANs, VAEs, and Diffusion Models). It encompasses principles of **fairness**, **accountability**, **transparency**, **privacy**, **safety**, and **human oversight**, evaluated through the lens of established AI ethics frameworks including the **OECD AI Principles**, **EU AI Act (2024)**, **NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)**, and **UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of AI (2021)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Ethical considerations form a foundational pillar of Generative AI literacy. KTU examiners frequently test a student's ability to identify, classify, and propose mitigation strategies for ethical risks. Expect 14-mark analytical questions that connect technical mechanisms (e.g., RLHF, dataset curation) to ethical outcomes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Think of Generative AI as a **gigantic public printing press** that anyone can use to produce any text, image, audio, or video. Now imagine that the press:

1. **Learned everything it knows by secretly reading billions of private letters** (privacy & consent).
2. **Copied the writing styles of famous authors without asking them** (intellectual property).
3. **Can be tricked into printing convincing lies that look like real news** (misinformation & deepfakes).
4. **Sometimes produces text that subtly favors one political group over another** (bias & fairness).
5. **No one is officially held responsible when the press causes harm** (accountability gap).
6. **Operates as a "black box" — even its own engineers cannot fully explain how it decides what to print** (opacity).

Just as a society creates **laws, oversight committees, and ethical codes for mass media**, the field of AI Ethics builds governance structures to ensure Generative AI benefits humanity without causing systematic harm.

### 1.3 Core Pillars of Generative AI Ethics (The "Six Pillars" Framework)

| Pillar | One-line Definition | KTU-Relevant Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| **Fairness** | Equitable treatment across demographic groups | Statistical Parity |
| **Accountability** | Clear ownership of AI-generated outcomes | Chain of Custody |
| **Transparency** | Understandable model behavior | Explainable AI (XAI) |
| **Privacy** | Protection of personal data in training sets | Differential Privacy |
| **Safety** | Prevention of harmful or malicious outputs | Red-Teaming |
| **Human Oversight** | Meaningful human control in the loop | Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Exams:** **"FAST-PH"** → **F**airness, **A**ccountability, **S**afety, **T**ransparency, **P**rivacy, **H**uman Oversight.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Definitions (KTU High-Yield Terms)

- **Bias**: A systematic skew in model outputs that disadvantages certain groups; measurable via the **Statistical Parity Difference (SPD)**. **Reference threshold:** $\vert \text{SPD} \vert \leq 0.1$ is often considered acceptable in industry (e.g., **IBM AI Fairness 360** toolkit).
- **Hallucination**: A confident but factually incorrect or fabricated output. **Industry standard metric:** **HHEM-2.1** (HHEM Leaderboard, Hugging Face 2024).
- **Toxicity Score**: Probability that an output is harmful, hateful, or offensive. Standard threshold (Perspective API): **> 0.8** → high toxicity.
- **Model Card**: A structured document (proposed by **Mitchell et al., 2019**) reporting intended use, performance metrics, and ethical considerations.
- **Datasheet for Datasets** (Gebru et al., 2021): Standardized documentation of dataset provenance, composition, and ethical review.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to "list ethical concerns," do not just enumerate. Always pair each concern with (a) a technical root cause, and (b) a mitigation technique. This is the difference between a 6-mark and a 12-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight Major Ethical Risk Categories in Generative AI

#### 2.1.1 Bias and Fairness
- **Root Cause**: Generative models inherit statistical skews from training data (e.g., underrepresentation of women in STEM images in LAION-5B).
- **Manifestations**: Gender bias, racial bias, cultural bias, occupational stereotyping, dialect prejudice.
- **Technical Mitigation**: Dataset balancing, adversarial debiasing, RLHF with fairness rewards, counterfactual data augmentation.
- **Regulatory Backdrop**: **EU AI Act (2024), Article 10** mandates bias detection and mitigation for high-risk systems.

#### 2.1.2 Hallucinations and Misinformation
- **Root Cause**: LLMs are next-token predictors, not knowledge bases; they have no ground-truth access.
- **Manifestations**: Fabricated citations, invented legal cases (e.g., *Mata v. Avianca* — 2023 NY lawyer sanctioned), false medical advice.
- **Technical Mitigation**: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), grounding, citation forcing, confidence calibration.
- **Industry Threshold**: Google Search Generative Experience targets **< 5% hallucination rate** for health queries (2024 policy).

#### 2.1.3 Privacy and Data Leakage
- **Root Cause**: Training on web-scraped data containing PII (Personally Identifiable Information).
- **Manifestations**: Memorization of training data, prompt injection leaking system prompts, membership inference attacks.
- **Technical Mitigation**: **Differential Privacy** (ε-budget), federated learning, PII redaction, on-device inference.
- **Legal Backdrop**: **GDPR (EU), DPDP Act 2023 (India), CCPA (California)**.

#### 2.1.4 Intellectual Property (IP) and Copyright
- **Root Cause**: Generative models trained on copyrighted text, art, and code without explicit licensing.
- **Active Litigation (2024-2025)**: *NYT v. OpenAI*, *Getty Images v. Stability AI*, *Authors Guild v. Google*, *UMG v. Suno/Udio*.
- **Mitigation**: Licensed datasets (e.g., Common Crawl licensed corpora), output watermarking, opt-out registries (e.g., robots.txt, ai.txt).

#### 2.1.5 Deepfakes and Synthetic Media
- **Root Cause**: Diffusion models and GANs can produce photorealistic but fabricated content.
- **Threat Vectors**: Non-consensual intimate imagery (NCII), political deepfakes, voice cloning fraud (e.g., $25M CEO voice scam, 2024).
- **Mitigation**: **C2PA Content Credentials** standard (Adobe-led, 2024), SynthID (Google DeepMind watermark), detection models.

#### 2.1.6 Environmental Impact
- **Root Cause**: Training and inference consume massive energy and water (for cooling).
- **Empirical Data** (per **Strubell et al., 2019**; updated **Bender et al., 2021**):
  - GPT-3 training: estimated **1,287 MWh** → ~552 tonnes CO₂e.
  - GPT-4 inference: estimated **~18x** the carbon of GPT-3 inference.
  - A single 20-query ChatGPT session: ~**500 mL** of fresh water (Washington Post, 2023 estimate).
- **Mitigation**: Model distillation, quantization, green data centers, carbon-aware scheduling.

#### 2.1.7 Job Displacement and Economic Impact
- **Manifestations**: Automation of copywriting, junior coding, illustration, customer support, translation.
- **Projections**: **Goldman Sachs (March 2023)** estimated **300M** jobs exposed; **~7%** global workforce displacement; **WEF Future of Jobs Report 2023** projects 85M displaced vs. 97M new roles by 2027.
- **Mitigation**: Reskilling programs, universal basic income pilots, human-AI collaboration design.

#### 2.1.8 Safety, Security, and Misuse
- **Threats**: CBRN (Chemical, Biological, Radiological, Nuclear) risk amplification, cyber-offensive code generation, social engineering.
- **Mitigation**: Red-teaming (e.g., OpenAI's **Preparedness Framework**, 2024), refusal training, capability evals, sandbagging detection.

### 2.2 The Responsible AI Lifecycle (Operational Stages)

1. **Problem Formulation** → Define use case, stakeholders, and ethical risk surface.
2. **Data Collection & Curation** → Consent, licensing, bias auditing, datasheets.
3. **Model Training** → Compute documentation, energy tracking, RLHF with safety constraints.
4. **Evaluation & Red-Teaming** → Toxicity evals, jailbreak testing, fairness audits.
5. **Deployment** → Model cards, usage policies, API access tiers.
6. **Monitoring & Incident Response** → Drift detection, abuse reporting, rollback plans.
7. **Decommissioning** → Data deletion, model retirement, knowledge transfer.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Interpretation | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Statistical Parity Difference (SPD)** | $\text{SPD} = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1)$ | Difference in positive prediction rates between groups $A=0$ and $A=1$. Ideal: $\text{SPD} = 0$ | Dwork et al., 2012 |
| **Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)** | $\text{DIR} = \dfrac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1)}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0)}$ | Ratio of favorable outcomes. Threshold: $\text{DIR} \in [0.8, 1.25]$ (the "**four-fifths rule**") | EEOC, 1978 |
| **Equalized Odds Difference** | $\text{EOD} = \max_{y \in \{0,1\}} \vert TPR_{A=0,y} - TPR_{A=1,y} \vert$ | Difference in True Positive Rates across groups | Hardt et al., 2016 |
| **Perplexity (PPL)** | $\text{PPL}(X) = \exp\!\left( -\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_{\theta}(x_i \mid x_{<i}) \right)$ | Lower is better; measures generative fluency | Standard NLP metric |
| **Rényi Differential Privacy** | $D_{\alpha}(P \Vert Q) \leq \varepsilon$ | $(\alpha, \varepsilon)$-RDP guarantee; composed via Gaussian mechanism | Mironov, 2017 |
| **Energy per Token (Wh)** | $E_{\text{tok}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{GPU}} \cdot t_{\text{inference}}}{N_{\text{tokens}}}$ | Energy efficiency proxy for inference | Strubell et al., 2019 |
| **Carbon Footprint (kgCO₂e)** | $C = P_{\text{GPU}} \cdot t \cdot \rho_{\text{grid}} \cdot 0.001$ | $\rho_{\text{grid}}$ = grid carbon intensity (kgCO₂e/kWh) | Strubell et al., 2019 |
| **Watermark Detectability (Z-score)** | $Z = \dfrac{S_{\text{text}} - \mu_{0}}{\sigma_{0}}$ | Statistical test for SynthID-style watermarks | Kirchenbauer et al., 2023 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Table Syntax:** The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ and $\mid$ are used inside mathematical expressions to avoid breaking markdown table parsing. **Never** write $P(\hat{Y} \mid X)$ inside a table row using `|...|` — always use `\vert` or `\mid`.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare**: Bias detection in radiology AI (e.g., **CheXNet** performed worse on non-white patients) → now required for FDA 510(k) submissions.
- **Finance**: Credit scoring models must pass **SR 11-7** (US Federal Reserve) governance; SPD monitoring is regulator-mandated.
- **Hiring**: Amazon scrapped an internal resume screening AI (2018) due to gender bias.
- **Legal**: EU AI Act (2024) classifies "AI in judicial decision-making" as **high-risk** → requires full compliance with bias, transparency, and human oversight articles.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Statistical Parity Difference (SPD) from First Principles

We start from the **Bayesian decision-theoretic framework**. Let $Y \in \{0,1\}$ be the true label, $\hat{Y} \in \{0,1\}$ the predicted label, and $A \in \{0,1\}$ a protected attribute (e.g., gender).

**Step 1**: Define the *positive prediction rate* for group $A=a$:

$$P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=a) = \sum_{y \in \{0,1\}} P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=y, A=a) \cdot P(Y=y \mid A=a)$$

**Step 2**: The Statistical Parity Difference is the absolute difference in positive prediction rates:

$$\text{SPD} \;=\; \big\vert \, P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) \;-\; P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1) \,\big\vert$$

**Step 3**: Interpretation. If $\text{SPD} = 0$, the model grants the favorable outcome $\hat{Y}=1$ at *equal rates* across groups. If $\text{SPD} > 0.1$, most enterprise fairness toolkits flag the model as **biased**.

**Step 4**: Worked Numerical Example.

Suppose a loan approval model produces the following:

| Group $A$ | Approved ($\hat{Y}=1$) | Rejected ($\hat{Y}=0$) | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A=0$ (Group 1) | 45 | 55 | 100 |
| $A=1$ (Group 2) | 70 | 30 | 100 |

Compute positive prediction rates:

$$P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) = \dfrac{45}{100} = 0.45$$

$$P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1) = \dfrac{70}{100} = 0.70$$

Compute SPD:

$$\text{SPD} = \vert 0.45 - 0.70 \vert = 0.25$$

Since $\text{SPD} = 0.25 > 0.1$, the model is **biased** against group $A=0$.

Check the Disparate Impact Ratio:

$$\text{DIR} = \dfrac{0.45}{0.70} \approx 0.643$$

Since $\text{DIR} = 0.643 < 0.8$, the model **fails the four-fifths rule** and is legally suspect under US EEOC guidelines.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Rényi Differential Privacy Guarantee for the Gaussian Mechanism

The Gaussian Mechanism adds noise $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2} I)$ to a function $f$ with sensitivity $\Delta f$.

**Step 1**: The L2-sensitivity of $f$ is:

$$\Delta_{2} f = \max_{D, D'} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_{2}$$

where $D$ and $D'$ differ in exactly one record.

**Step 2**: For the Gaussian Mechanism $M(D) = f(D) + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2} I)$, the Rényi divergence of order $\alpha > 1$ between the output distributions on neighboring datasets is:

$$D_{\alpha}\!\left( M(D) \,\Vert\, M(D') \right) = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot (\Delta_{2} f)^{2}}{2 \sigma^{2}}$$

**Step 3**: The mechanism satisfies $(\alpha, \varepsilon)$-RDP if:

$$\dfrac{\alpha \cdot (\Delta_{2} f)^{2}}{2 \sigma^{2}} \leq \varepsilon$$

**Step 4**: Solve for the required noise standard deviation:

$$\sigma \geq \dfrac{\Delta_{2} f}{\sqrt{2\varepsilon / \alpha}}$$

**Step 5**: Conversion to standard $(\varepsilon', \delta)$-DP. For any $0 < \delta < 1$, define:

$$\varepsilon'(\delta) = \varepsilon + \dfrac{\log(1/\delta)}{\alpha - 1}$$

**Worked Example**: Let $\Delta_{2} f = 1$, $\alpha = 2$, target $\varepsilon = 1$.

$$\sigma \geq \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 1 / 2}} = 1$$

So adding Gaussian noise with $\sigma = 1$ yields a $(2, 1)$-RDP guarantee, which converts to roughly $(1 + \log(1/\delta), \delta)$-DP.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Perplexity-Based Hallucination Proxy

For a generative model producing a sequence $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N)$:

**Step 1**: The average negative log-likelihood is:

$$\mathcal{L}(X) = -\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_{\theta}(x_i \mid x_{<i})$$

**Step 2**: Perplexity is the exponentiated loss:

$$\text{PPL}(X) = \exp\!\big( \mathcal{L}(X) \big)$$

**Step 3**: A model with $\text{PPL} = 1$ perfectly predicts the next token (impossible in practice). $\text{PPL} = V$ (vocab size) means random guessing. GPT-4-class models on standard benchmarks: $\text{PPL} \approx 10\text{–}30$.

**Step 4**: Hallucination Proxy Score (HPS) used internally by some teams:

$$\text{HPS} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + \text{PPL}(X)/\text{PPL}_{\text{ref}}}$$

Higher PPL (relative to a reference) suggests the model is "uncertain" → higher hallucination risk.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Ethical Risks to Technical & Regulatory Mitigations

| Ethical Risk | Technical Root Cause | Technical Mitigation | Regulatory / Governance Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bias | Skewed training data | Reweighting, RAG, RLHF | EU AI Act Art. 10; NIST AI RMF |
| Hallucination | Autoregressive sampling | Retrieval grounding, log-prob thresholds | EU AI Act Art. 13 (Transparency) |
| Privacy Leakage | Memorization | Differential privacy, federated learning | GDPR, India DPDP Act 2023 |
| Copyright Infringement | Web-scraped training data | Licensed corpora, opt-out registries | US Copyright Office rulings 2023 |
| Deepfakes | High-fidelity generative models | C2PA, SynthID, detection classifiers | EU AI Act Art. 50; US DEFIANCE Act |
| Environmental | Compute-intensive training | Distillation, quantization, green scheduling | SEC Climate Disclosure Rules 2024 |
| Job Displacement | Task automation | Reskilling, HITL workflows | OECD AI Principles |
| Misuse (CBRN/cyber) | Capability generalization | Red-teaming, refusal training | US EO 14110 (2023), Bletchley Declaration |

---

### 3.5 The Incompatibility Theorem (Chouldechova 2017; Kleinberg et al. 2016)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept: Impossibility of Simultaneous Fairness.**

It is mathematically impossible (under base-rate disparity between groups) to satisfy **all three** of the following fairness criteria simultaneously:

1. **Calibration**: $P(Y=1 \mid \hat{P}=p, A=0) = P(Y=1 \mid \hat{P}=p, A=1)$ for all $p$.
2. **Equal False Positive Rates**: $P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=0, A=0) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=0, A=1)$.
3. **Equal False Negative Rates**: $P(\hat{Y}=0 \mid Y=1, A=0) = P(\hat{Y}=0 \mid Y=1, A=1)$.

This forces practitioners to make **explicit normative trade-offs** — a frequent KTU exam theme.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define "AI Hallucination" in the context of Generative AI. List two technical strategies to mitigate it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **hallucination** is a generated output that is syntactically fluent but semantically false or unfaithful to any real-world source. It arises because Large Language Models are next-token predictors trained on statistical patterns, not grounded in verified knowledge bases.

**Mitigation strategies:**
1. **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)**: Augment the model input with verified external documents retrieved at inference time, grounding the response in factual sources.
2. **Logit-level uncertainty calibration**: Reject or flag outputs whose top-token probabilities fall below a calibrated confidence threshold (e.g., $p_{\max} < 0.6$).

*(Examiner allocates ~1 mark for definition, 1 mark per mitigation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between "Model Card" and "Datasheet for Datasets". Why are both essential for Responsible AI governance?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Model Card | Datasheet for Datasets |
|---|---|---|
| **Subject** | The trained model | The training data |
| **Proponent** | Mitchell et al., 2019 | Gebru et al., 2021 |
| **Content** | Intended use, performance across demographic slices, ethical considerations, limitations | Provenance, composition, collection process, ethical review, intended use |
| **Purpose** | Inform downstream deployers | Inform model developers and auditors |

**Why both are essential:** A model cannot be evaluated responsibly without knowing the biases of its training data (Datasheet), and a dataset cannot be safely deployed without knowing the model's downstream behavior (Model Card). Together they form the **provenance chain** required by the **EU AI Act (2024)** and **NIST AI RMF**.

*(1 mark for differentiation table, 2 marks for justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **eight major ethical risk categories** in Generative AI. For each category, provide (i) the technical root cause, (ii) one real-world example, and (iii) one mitigation strategy.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **Statistical Parity Difference (SPD)** and **Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)** from first principles. A hiring model approves 30 out of 100 male applicants and 60 out of 100 female applicants. Compute the SPD and DIR, and state whether the model passes the four-fifths rule.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

| Risk | Root Cause | Real-World Example | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bias** | Skewed training corpora | Amazon's scrapped resume screener (2018) | Adversarial debiasing |
| **Hallucination** | Next-token prediction without grounding | *Mata v. Avianca* (2023) fabricated cases | RAG + citation |
| **Privacy Leakage** | Web-scraped PII | GPT-2 memorizing real addresses | Differential privacy (ε-budget) |
| **Copyright** | Unlicensed training data | *NYT v. OpenAI* (2023) | Licensed corpora, opt-out |
| **Deepfakes** | Photorealistic synthesis | Taylor Swift NCII images (2024) | C2PA, SynthID |
| **Environmental** | Massive compute | GPT-3 ~552 tCO₂e | Distillation, green grids |
| **Job Displacement** | Task automation | ~300M jobs exposed (Goldman, 2023) | Reskilling, HITL |
| **Misuse** | Capability generalization | Jailbreak → CBRN instructions | Red-teaming, refusal training |

*(1 mark per row for a clean four-column table, with each column justified in 1–2 lines. Allocate ~1 mark for concluding synthesis paragraph linking the risks to the Responsible AI Lifecycle.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Step 1: Derive SPD.**

For protected attribute $A \in \{0, 1\}$ and binary outcome $\hat{Y} \in \{0, 1\}$:

$$P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=a) = \sum_{y \in \{0,1\}} P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=y, A=a) \cdot P(Y=y \mid A=a)$$

$$\text{SPD} = \big\vert P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1) \big\vert$$

**Step 2: Derive DIR.**

$$\text{DIR} = \dfrac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0)}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1)}$$

*(Here $A=0$ is the historically disadvantaged group per EEOC convention; some texts invert the ratio. State the convention explicitly.)*

**Step 3: Apply to the hiring data.**

Let $A=0$ = male, $A=1$ = female:

$$P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid \text{male}) = \dfrac{30}{100} = 0.30$$

$$P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid \text{female}) = \dfrac{60}{100} = 0.60$$

**Step 4: Compute SPD.**

$$\text{SPD} = \vert 0.30 - 0.60 \vert = 0.30$$

Since $\text{SPD} = 0.30 > 0.1$, the model exhibits **substantial bias**.

**Step 5: Compute DIR.**

$$\text{DIR} = \dfrac{0.30}{0.60} = 0.50$$

Since $\text{DIR} = 0.50 < 0.80$, the model **violates** the four-fifths rule and would be legally suspect under EEOC guidelines.

**Step 6: Conclusion (synthesis).** The model is structurally biased against male applicants in this snapshot and would fail an enterprise fairness audit (e.g., **IBM AI Fairness 360**). The organization would need to (a) audit training data for proxies, (b) recalibrate decision thresholds, and (c) document the trade-off using a Model Card.

*(Allocate: 1 mark for SPD derivation, 1 mark for DIR derivation, 1 mark for plugging values, 1 mark for SPD numeric, 1 mark for DIR numeric, 1 mark for four-fifths judgment, 1 mark for synthesis conclusion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **Fairness Incompatibility Theorem** (Chouldechova 2017; Kleinberg et al. 2016). Why is it impossible to satisfy calibration, equal false positive rate, and equal false negative rate simultaneously when group base rates differ? What is the practical implication for AI product teams?

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Rényi Differential Privacy (RDP)** framework. For a query function with L2-sensitivity $\Delta_{2} f = 2$ and a target RDP guarantee of $(\alpha = 3, \varepsilon = 1.5)$, compute the minimum noise standard deviation $\sigma$ required for the Gaussian Mechanism. Convert this to a standard $(\varepsilon', \delta)$-DP bound using $\delta = 10^{-5}$.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**Step 1: State the three criteria.**

Let $\hat{P} = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid X)$ be the model's predicted probability. The three criteria are:

- **Calibration**: $P(Y=1 \mid \hat{P}=p, A=a)$ is identical for all $a$.
- **Equal FPR**: $P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=0, A=0) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=0, A=1)$.
- **Equal FNR**: $P(\hat{Y}=0 \mid Y=1, A=0) = P(\hat{Y}=0 \mid Y=1, A=1)$.

**Step 2: Show the conflict.**

By the Bayes' rule decomposition of accuracy:

$$\text{Accuracy} = P(Y=1) \cdot (1 - \text{FNR}) + P(Y=0) \cdot (1 - \text{FPR})$$

If the base rates $P(Y=1 \mid A=0) \neq P(Y=1 \mid A=1)$, then enforcing equal FPR **and** equal FNR **forces** the model to be *mis-calibrated* on at least one group. Conversely, perfect calibration on both groups, given a single decision threshold, mathematically precludes both equal FPR and equal FNR.

**Step 3: Practical implication.**

AI product teams must make an **explicit normative choice**: which group is being protected, and which error type is the lesser harm? Examples:
- **Criminal recidivism (COMPAS)**: Stakeholders chose to prioritize **equal FPR** to avoid falsely flagging innocent Black defendants.
- **Medical screening**: Stakeholders often prioritize **equal FNR** to avoid missing cancer in any demographic.
- **Credit lending**: Calibration is favored because of legal requirements under **ECOA / Reg B**.

**Step 4: Conclusion.** Responsible AI is not a purely technical exercise — it is a **socio-technical negotiation** between stakeholders, lawyers, and engineers. Document the choice in a Model Card.

*(Allocate: 1 mark for stating the three criteria, 2 marks for the mathematical conflict argument, 2 marks for the practical implication, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for the conclusion.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Step 1: Recall the Gaussian Mechanism under RDP.**

For a function $f$ with L2-sensitivity $\Delta_{2} f$, the Gaussian Mechanism $M(D) = f(D) + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2} I)$ satisfies $(\alpha, \varepsilon)$-RDP iff:

$$\varepsilon \;\geq\; \dfrac{\alpha \cdot (\Delta_{2} f)^{2}}{2 \sigma^{2}}$$

**Step 2: Solve for $\sigma$.**

$$\sigma \;\geq\; \Delta_{2} f \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\alpha}{2\varepsilon}}$$

**Step 3: Plug in the given values.**

Given $\Delta_{2} f = 2$, $\alpha = 3$, $\varepsilon = 1.5$:

$$\sigma \;\geq\; 2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2 \cdot 1.5}} \;=\; 2 \cdot \sqrt{1} \;=\; 2$$

So the **minimum noise standard deviation is $\sigma = 2$**.

**Step 4: Convert to $(\varepsilon', \delta)$-DP.**

The standard conversion formula (Mironov, 2017) is:

$$\varepsilon'(\delta) = \varepsilon + \dfrac{\log(1/\delta)}{\alpha - 1}$$

For $\delta = 10^{-5}$:

$$\varepsilon'(10^{-5}) = 1.5 + \dfrac{\log(10^{5})}{3 - 1} = 1.5 + \dfrac{5 \cdot \ln(10)}{2}$$

Using $\ln(10) \approx 2.3026$:

$$\varepsilon'(10^{-5}) = 1.5 + \dfrac{5 \cdot 2.3026}{2} = 1.5 + 5.7565 \approx 7.26$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.** The mechanism satisfies $(3, 1.5)$-RDP, which translates to a $(7.26, 10^{-5})$-DP guarantee. In production systems, this is a **strong** privacy budget; running many such queries would require tracking the **composition budget** across the full pipeline.

*(Allocate: 1 mark for the Gaussian RDP formula, 2 marks for solving $\sigma$, 1 mark for the conversion formula, 1 mark for plugging $\delta = 10^{-5}$, 1 mark for arithmetic, 1 mark for interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing SPD and DIR:** SPD is a *difference*; DIR is a *ratio*. Writing "SPD = 0.5" instead of "DIR = 0.5" costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the absolute value** in SPD: $\text{SPD} = \vert \cdot \vert$. Many students drop the absolute value and lose a mark.
> 3. **Inverting the DIR convention**: Always explicitly state which group is the *reference* (disadvantaged) group before plugging values.
> 4. **Mis-stating the Chouldechova condition**: The theorem requires **unequal base rates**. Students who write "it is always impossible" lose a mark.
> 5. **Skipping units in carbon/energy calculations**: Always state **kgCO₂e**, **Wh**, or **MWh** explicitly.
> 6. **Mixing up DP variants:** $(\varepsilon, 0)$-DP is **pure** DP; $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP is **approximate**; $(\alpha, \varepsilon)$-RDP is **Rényi**. Examiners deduct marks for misuse.
> 7. **Ignoring the EU AI Act context:** KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes regulatory awareness. Citing *at least one* concrete regulation (EU AI Act, GDPR, NIST AI RMF, India DPDP Act 2023) typically elevates a 10-mark answer to a 12-mark answer.

---

- [ ] **Exam Mantra:** Always pair an **ethical risk** with (i) its **technical root cause** and (ii) a **mitigation strategy**. Always cite a **concrete regulation or standard** to earn full marks.

topics.title: Bias and Fairness in Generative AI Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bias and Fairness in Generative AI Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bias in Generative AI** refers to systematic, repeatable errors in the output of a generative model (LLM, GAN, VAE, Diffusion Model) that produce outputs unfairly favoring or disadvantaging certain groups, attributes, or perspectives. **Fairness** is the property of a generative system that ensures equitable treatment, representation, and performance across demographic, social, or contextual categories defined by sensitive/protected attributes (e.g., gender, race, age, religion, geography, dialect).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework**, bias and fairness in Generative AI are evaluated as critical ethical and engineering concerns, directly tied to the course outcome: *"Identify and mitigate ethical, social, and legal risks including bias, fairness, and hallucination in generative AI systems."*

### 1.1.1 Taxonomy of Bias (Formally Recognized Categories)

| Bias Type | Formal Definition | KTU Syllabus Anchor |
|---|---|---|
| **Historical Bias** | Pre-existing societal inequities encoded in training data before model construction. | Module 1.4 – Data Provenance |
| **Representation Bias** | Under- or over-representation of certain groups in training corpora. | Module 1.4 – Dataset Auditing |
| **Measurement Bias** | Inconsistencies in how labels/features are recorded or annotated. | Module 1.4 – Annotation Pipelines |
| **Aggregation Bias** | False assumption that one model fits all subgroups uniformly. | Module 1.4 – Subgroup Analysis |
| **Learning Bias** | Bias introduced by model architecture, objective function, or training dynamics. | Module 1.4 – Algorithmic Design |
| **Generation Bias** | Bias that surfaces specifically in the *output space* (text/image/audio). | Module 1.4 – Output Auditing |
| **Evaluation Bias** | Benchmark datasets that under-represent marginalized dialects/groups. | Module 1.4 – Benchmark Design |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🪞 The "Funhouse Mirror" Analogy**
> Imagine a funhouse mirror in an old carnival. The mirror doesn't *create* your ugliness or beauty — it *reflects* you, but in a distorted way. Generative AI is exactly this funhouse mirror. If the carnival has been visited mostly by tall people, the mirror's frame (training data) is calibrated to them. When a short person walks in front, the reflection is either squished or stretched.
> - **Bias** = the systematic distortion built into the mirror.
> - **Fairness** = the engineering effort to *recalibrate* the mirror so every person sees a faithful reflection.
> - **Hallucination** = the mirror inventing features that were never there (a separate but related issue).
>
> Just as a responsible carnival owner would periodically re-align their mirrors, a responsible GenAI engineer must periodically audit and debias their models.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is Critical in KTU 2024 Scheme

The **NEP 2020** curriculum mandates ethics-integrated engineering education. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly lists *bias mitigation* as a graduate attribute under **GA5 – Ethical Responsibility**, and as a **CO (Course Outcome)** in this course. Failing to address bias is no longer a "nice-to-have" — it is a **board-examinable core competency**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams**: Always remember the **3 R's of Fair GenAI**:
> 1. **R**ecognize (audit the data + outputs)
> 2. **R**eport (publish bias cards / model cards)
> 3. **R**emediate (debias via preprocessing, in-training, or post-processing techniques)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Bias-Fairness Pipeline

The bias-fairness pipeline in a Generative AI system is best understood as a **5-stage causal chain**. A bias introduced at any stage propagates to the next, compounding in magnitude.

### Stage 1: Data Acquisition
- Training corpora are scraped from the internet, books, and licensed databases.
- These corpora mirror the *demographic, geographic, and ideological distribution* of the web.
- The **statistical frequency** of protected attributes (e.g., male pronouns vs. female pronouns) becomes the **base rate** that the model learns.

### Stage 2: Tokenization & Representation
- Text is broken into tokens; the **embedding space** assigns vector coordinates to each token.
- If the corpus is biased, the *geometry* of the embedding space becomes biased — words like "doctor" cluster closer to "man", while "nurse" clusters closer to "woman".
- This is known as **semantic bias** or **stereotypical association bias** in the latent space.

### Stage 3: Training Objective
- Models are trained using **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** or variants (RLHF, DPO).
- The objective function does not penalize the model for being biased — it only penalizes it for being *wrong* on the training distribution.
- Result: the model optimizes for **statistical fidelity**, not **social fairness**.

### Stage 4: Decoding / Generation
- During inference, the model samples from a probability distribution.
- **Sampling parameters** (temperature, top-k, top-p) can either amplify or suppress bias.
- A higher **temperature** increases diversity but can also increase the probability of generating stereotypical or harmful completions.

### Stage 5: Output & Societal Feedback
- Generated outputs enter society, influence opinions, and may *re-enter* future training datasets, creating a **feedback loop of bias amplification**.

## 2.2 Formal Fairness Metrics (KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are **board-exam relevant**. The three primary formal fairness criteria in machine learning are **Demographic Parity**, **Equalized Odds**, and **Counterfactual Fairness**. For Generative AI, additional **distribution-based metrics** are used.

### 2.2.1 Demographic Parity (Statistical Parity)

A model satisfies **Demographic Parity** if the probability of a positive (or specific) outcome is independent of the protected attribute $A$.

$$P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a) = P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a') \quad \forall \, a, a' \in A$$

**Demographic Parity Difference (DPD):**

$$\text{DPD} = \max_{a, a'} \vert P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a) - P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a') \vert$$

A model is considered **fair under demographic parity** if $\text{DPD} \le \epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 0.05$ or $0.1$).

### 2.2.2 Equalized Odds

A model satisfies **Equalized Odds** if the true positive and false positive rates are equal across groups.

$$P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a, Y = y) = P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a', Y = y)$$

This is **stricter** than demographic parity because it conditions on the true label $Y$.

### 2.2.3 Counterfactual Fairness

A model is **counterfactually fair** if its prediction for an individual remains the same in the **counterfactual world** where their protected attribute is changed.

$$P(\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a}(U) = y \mid X = x, A = a) = P(\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a'}(U) = y \mid X = x, A = a)$$

where $U$ denotes the unobserved background variables.

### 2.2.4 Distribution-Based Fairness Metrics (Generative AI Specific)

| Metric | Formula (Conceptual) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)** | $\text{DIR} = \dfrac{\min_a P(\hat{Y}=y \mid A=a)}{\max_a P(\hat{Y}=y \mid A=a)}$ | Measures ratio of selection rates. Fair if $\ge 0.8$ (the "80% rule"). |
| **KL Divergence Between Subgroups** | $D_{KL}(P_a \Vert P_{a'}) = \sum_i P_a(i) \log \dfrac{P_a(i)}{P_{a'}(i)}$ | Compares output distributions across demographic groups. |
| **Wasserstein Distance (Earth Mover's)** | $W_1(P_a, P_{a'}) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Gamma} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \gamma}[\Vert x - y \Vert]$ | Measures cost of transforming one group's output distribution into another. |
| **Embedding Association Test (EAT)** | Cosine similarity differential between target and attribute embeddings. | Detects semantic bias in embedding space (analogous to IAT in psychology). |
| **SEAT (Sentence Encoder Association Test)** | Variant of EAT applied to sentence-level embeddings from LLMs. | Used for BERT/GPT-style model auditing. |
| **StereoSet / CrowS-Pairs Score** | Likelihood differential between stereotypical and anti-stereotypical sentence pairs. | Measures stereotypical bias in language models. |

### 2.2.5 The "80% Rule" (Four-Fifths Rule)

A model passes the **Four-Fifths Rule** if the selection rate for any protected group is at least **80%** of the rate for the highest-selected group.

$$\frac{P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a_{\text{lowest}})}{P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a_{\text{highest}})} \ge 0.8$$

This is the **de facto industry standard** for hiring, lending, and content recommendation systems.

## 2.3 Bias Mitigation Strategies (The Three-Layered Approach)

| Layer | Stage | Techniques |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-processing** | Before Training | Re-sampling, re-weighting, data augmentation, counterfactual data generation, bias-aware embedding neutralization. |
| **In-processing** | During Training | Adversarial debiasing, fairness-constrained loss functions, RLHF with fairness rewards, DRO (Distributionally Robust Optimization). |
| **Post-processing** | After Training | Output filtering, calibrated equalized odds post-processing, prompt engineering, retrieval-augmented debiasing. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: When asked "Compare the three layers of bias mitigation," always mention the **trade-off triangle**:
> - **Pre-processing** → highest data-level control, but may reduce model utility.
> - **In-processing** → best fairness-accuracy balance, but requires architectural changes.
> - **Post-processing** → most flexible, but only addresses *symptoms* not *causes*.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hiring AI (LinkedIn, HireVue)**: Uses demographic parity metrics to comply with EEOC guidelines.
- **Medical Image Generation**: Bias mitigation prevents the GAN from generating only light-skinned skin lesions, which would harm diagnostic equity.
- **Text-to-Image Models (DALL·E, Stable Diffusion, Midjourney)**: Companies use CLIP-based bias audits to ensure diverse occupational portrayals (e.g., CEO ≠ only male).
- **Voice Assistants (Siri, Alexa)**: Fairness in accent recognition across Indian, African-American, and regional English dialects.
- **LLM Chatbots (ChatGPT, Gemini)**: RLHF + safety fine-tuning to suppress toxic, biased, or harmful completions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Bias Amplification Coefficient

A core analytical question: *Does bias in a generative model grow or shrink compared to the training data?* Research by **Zhao et al. (2017)** introduced the concept of **Bias Amplification**.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Notation

Let:
- $Y$ = the *training* label (e.g., the ground-truth profession in an image).
- $\hat{Y}$ = the *predicted* label from the model.
- $G$ = a binary gender attribute (e.g., $G = 1$ for male, $G = 0$ for female).
- $a_g$ = the proportion of samples with gender $g$ and label $y$ in the **training set**.
- $p_g$ = the proportion of samples predicted with label $y$ and gender $g$ by the **model**.

**Training Distribution Statistic:**

$$a_g = P(Y = y, G = g)$$

**Model Prediction Statistic:**

$$p_g = P(\hat{Y} = y, G = g)$$

### 3.1.2 Definition of Bias Amplification

$$\text{BA}_g = \frac{p_g / \sum_{g'} p_{g'}}{a_g / \sum_{g'} a_{g'}}$$

> **Interpretation**: A $\text{BA}_g > 1$ means the model has *amplified* the bias; a $\text{BA}_g < 1$ means the model has *attenuated* it.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose we have a model trained to classify 1000 images into "Doctor" or "Nurse" with the following training distribution:

- **Training Data**: 700 Doctors (600 male, 100 female), 300 Nurses (50 male, 250 female).
- **Model Predictions**: 720 Doctors (650 male, 70 female), 280 Nurses (40 male, 240 female).

We want to compute bias amplification for the "Doctor" class.

**Step 1**: Compute training proportions $a_g$.

$$a_{\text{male}} = \frac{600}{1000} = 0.60$$

$$a_{\text{female}} = \frac{100}{1000} = 0.10$$

**Step 2**: Compute model-predicted proportions $p_g$.

$$p_{\text{male}} = \frac{650}{1000} = 0.65$$

$$p_{\text{female}} = \frac{70}{1000} = 0.07$$

**Step 3**: Compute the denominator (sum of training proportions for "Doctor").

$$\sum_{g'} a_{g'} = 0.60 + 0.10 = 0.70$$

**Step 4**: Compute the numerator (sum of predicted proportions for "Doctor").

$$\sum_{g'} p_{g'} = 0.65 + 0.07 = 0.72$$

**Step 5**: Compute the ratio for males.

$$\text{BA}_{\text{male}} = \frac{0.65 / 0.72}{0.60 / 0.70} = \frac{0.9028}{0.8571} \approx 1.0533$$

**Step 6**: Compute the ratio for females.

$$\text{BA}_{\text{female}} = \frac{0.07 / 0.72}{0.10 / 0.70} = \frac{0.0972}{0.1429} \approx 0.6803$$

**Step 7**: Interpretation.

$$\text{BA}_{\text{male}} = 1.0533 > 1 \implies \text{Bias Amplified for males}$$

$$\text{BA}_{\text{female}} = 0.6803 < 1 \implies \text{Bias Attenuated for females (but still heavily skewed)}$$

> **Conclusion**: The model has *amplified* the existing gender bias by approximately 5.3% for males in the "Doctor" class. This is a measurable, board-examinable result that justifies post-training debiasing.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Bias Type × Mitigation Strategy

This matrix helps a student answer "Which mitigation strategy should be applied to which bias type?" — a high-frequency KTU exam question.

| Bias Type | Best Pre-processing | Best In-processing | Best Post-processing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Historical Bias** | Counterfactual data augmentation, synthetic minority oversampling. | Fairness-constrained loss with explicit group priors. | Output rewriting, prompt-based debiasing. |
| **Representation Bias** | Re-sampling, data weighting, focused crawling. | DRO, group-aware batch sampling. | Calibration adjustments at decoding time. |
| **Measurement Bias** | Inter-annotator agreement checks, label smoothing. | Confidence calibration loss. | Confidence thresholding per group. |
| **Aggregation Bias** | Subgroup-aware data collection. | Multi-task learning with subgroup heads. | Conditional decoding per subgroup. |
| **Learning Bias** | Embedding debiasing (e.g., Hard-Debias, Double-Hard-Debias). | Adversarial debiasing. | Token-level bias re-ranking. |
| **Generation Bias** | Diverse prompt engineering datasets. | RLHF with diversity rewards. | Nucleus sampling tuning, safe-completion filters. |
| **Evaluation Bias** | Curated, demographically balanced benchmark design. | N/A (evaluation is post-hoc). | Stratified evaluation reporting. |

## 3.3 The Impossibility Theorem (Board-Relevant Caveat)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ The Fairness Impossibility Theorem (Chouldechova 2017; Kleinberg et al. 2016)** states that it is **mathematically impossible** to simultaneously satisfy all three of the following fairness criteria, except in degenerate cases:
> 1. **Calibration** (Predicted probabilities match true frequencies across groups.)
> 2. **Balance for Positive Class** (Average predicted probability equals average true probability among positives, for every group.)
> 3. **Balance for Negative Class** (Same as above, among negatives.)
>
> For the KTU board exam, remember: **No single fairness metric is universally correct; the choice depends on the application context.**

## 3.4 Decision Framework: Choosing a Fairness Metric

A practicing GenAI engineer asks: *"Which fairness metric should I optimize?"* The answer is **context-dependent**.

| Application Domain | Recommended Fairness Metric | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Hiring / College Admissions** | Demographic Parity (80% Rule) | Legal compliance with anti-discrimination statutes. |
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Equalized Odds | False negative rates must be equal across ethnic groups. |
| **Loan / Credit Scoring** | Calibration | Probabilistic predictions must be accurate within each group. |
| **Generative Content Creation** | Output Distribution Divergence (KL / Wasserstein) | Diversity of representation in generated images/text. |
| **Criminal Justice Risk Scoring** | Counterfactual Fairness | Decisions should not change under counterfactual demographic flips. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Bias-Fairness Lifecycle in Generative AI

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Data Acquisition] --> B[Stage 2: Tokenization and Embedding]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Training Objective MLE or RLHF]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Decoding and Generation]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Output and Societal Feedback]
    E --> A

    A -. Bias Injection .-> A1[Historical Bias]
    A -. Bias Injection .-> A2[Representation Bias]
    B -. Bias Injection .-> B1[Measurement Bias]
    C -. Bias Injection .-> C1[Learning Bias]
    D -. Bias Injection .-> D1[Generation Bias]
    E -. Bias Injection .-> E1[Evaluation Bias]

    F[Layer 1: Pre-processing Mitigation] -. acts on .-> A
    G[Layer 2: In-processing Mitigation] -. acts on .-> C
    H[Layer 3: Post-processing Mitigation] -. acts on .-> D

    I[Fairness Audit and Reporting] -. continuous loop .-> A
    I -. continuous loop .-> C
    I -. continuous loop .-> D
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Three-Layered Bias Mitigation Framework

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PRE[Pre-processing Layer]
        P1[Re-sampling]
        P2[Re-weighting]
        P3[Counterfactual Augmentation]
        P4[Embedding Neutralization]
    end

    subgraph INP[In-processing Layer]
        I1[Adversarial Debiasing]
        I2[Fairness-constrained Loss]
        I3[RLHF with Fairness Reward]
        I4[Distributionally Robust Optimization]
    end

    subgraph POS[Post-processing Layer]
        Q1[Output Filtering]
        Q2[Calibrated Equalized Odds Adjustment]
        Q3[Prompt Engineering and Re-ranking]
        Q4[Safety Fine-tuning]
    end

    P1 --> I1
    P2 --> I2
    P3 --> I3
    P4 --> I4
    I1 --> Q1
    I2 --> Q2
    I3 --> Q3
    I4 --> Q4
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Causal Chain of Bias Amplification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Web Scraping] --> S2[Demographic Skewed Corpus]
    S2 --> S3[Biased Embedding Geometry]
    S3 --> S4[MLE Objective Optimizes for Skew]
    S4 --> S5[Stereotypical Generations]
    S5 --> S6[User Consumption and Sharing]
    S6 --> S7[Re-ingestion into Future Web Data]
    S7 --> S2

    M1[Counterfactual Data Augmentation] -. breaks .-> S2
    M2[Hard-Debias or Double-Hard-Debias] -. breaks .-> S3
    M3[Fairness Regularizer in Loss] -. breaks .-> S4
    M4[Decoding Re-ranking] -. breaks .-> S5
    M5[Output Watermarking and Provenance] -. breaks .-> S7
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Fairness Metric Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the application high-stakes legal?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Use Demographic Parity or 80% Rule]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is calibration critical?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Use Equalized Odds or Calibration]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Are decisions about individuals?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Use Counterfactual Fairness]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is the system generative?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> R4[Use KL Divergence or Wasserstein Distance]
    Q4 -- No --> R5[Use Disparate Impact Ratio]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fair GenAI System

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        IN1[User Prompt]
        IN2[System Prompt with Fairness Constraints]
    end

    subgraph AUDIT[Pre-generation Audit]
        AU1[Prompt Classifier: Detects Sensitive Attributes]
        AU2[Retrieval Module: Fetches Counterfactual Examples]
    end

    subgraph MODEL[Generative Core]
        MC1[Safety-Fine-Tuned LLM or Diffusion Model]
        MC2[Constitutional AI Principles Embedded]
    end

    subgraph POST[Post-generation Stage]
        PO1[Output Bias Classifier]
        PO2[Re-ranking Module for Diversity]
        PO3[Hallucination and Toxicity Filter]
    end

    subgraph REPORT[Reporting Stage]
        RE1[Model Card Generator]
        RE2[Bias Card Generator]
        RE3[Audit Log]
    end

    IN1 --> AU1
    IN2 --> AU1
    AU1 --> AU2
    AU2 --> MC1
    MC1 --> MC2
    MC2 --> PO1
    PO1 --> PO2
    PO2 --> PO3
    PO3 --> RE1
    RE3 --> PO1
    RE2 --> MC1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Define "bias" in Generative AI. List any four common types of bias encountered in generative systems.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Bias in Generative AI refers to systematic and repeatable errors in the output of generative models that unfairly favor or disadvantage certain groups, attributes, or perspectives, often arising from skewed training data, model architecture, or sampling strategies.

**Four Types of Bias (2 Marks – 0.5 each):**
1. **Historical Bias** – Pre-existing societal inequities reflected in training data.
2. **Representation Bias** – Under- or over-representation of certain demographic groups in the training corpus.
3. **Learning Bias** – Bias introduced by the model's training objective or optimization procedure.
4. **Generation Bias** – Bias that appears in the *output space* of the model during inference.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is the "80% Rule" (Four-Fifths Rule) in AI fairness? Why is it significant?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** The 80% Rule, also called the Four-Fifths Rule, states that the selection rate for any protected demographic group must be **at least 80% of the selection rate of the highest-selected group**. Mathematically:

$$\frac{P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a_{\text{lowest}})}{P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a_{\text{highest}})} \ge 0.8$$

**Significance (1 Mark):** It is the *de facto* industry and legal standard for detecting **disparate impact** in AI systems, especially in hiring, lending, and admissions, originating from the *Uniform Guidelines on Employee Selection Procedures (1978)* of the U.S. EEOC.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the three primary fairness criteria — Demographic Parity, Equalized Odds, and Counterfactual Fairness — with mathematical formulations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Bias Amplification phenomenon** in generative models with a suitable worked example. Propose two debiasing strategies for a text-to-image model. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**(1) Demographic Parity (Statistical Parity) — 2 Marks:**
Demographic Parity requires that the predicted outcome $\hat{Y}$ be statistically independent of the protected attribute $A$.

$$P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a) = P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a') \quad \forall \, a, a' \in A$$

In other words, the *positive prediction rate* must be the same across all demographic groups. The **Demographic Parity Difference (DPD)** is defined as:

$$\text{DPD} = \max_{a, a'} \vert P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a) - P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a') \vert$$

A model is fair under demographic parity if $\text{DPD} \le \epsilon$ (typically $0.05$).

**(2) Equalized Odds — 2 Marks:**
Equalized Odds requires that the true positive rate (TPR) and false positive rate (FPR) be equal across groups, *conditioned* on the true label $Y$.

$$P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a, Y = y) = P(\hat{Y} = y \mid A = a', Y = y)$$

This is **stricter** than demographic parity because it does not allow the model to trade off errors between groups.

**(3) Counterfactual Fairness — 2 Marks:**
A model is counterfactually fair if its prediction for an individual remains the same when we *hypothetically* change the protected attribute $A$ to a different value, holding other causally relevant factors constant.

$$P(\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a}(U) = y \mid X = x, A = a) = P(\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a'}(U) = y \mid X = x, A = a)$$

where $U$ represents the unobserved background variables and $X$ represents legitimate, non-protected features.

**Synthesis (1 Mark):** These three criteria are **mutually exclusive in non-trivial settings** (Chouldechova 2017, Kleinberg et al. 2016). The choice depends on the application context.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(1) Bias Amplification Concept — 2 Marks:**
**Bias Amplification** refers to the phenomenon where a generative model not only *learns* the statistical skew of the training data but *worsens* it in its predictions. Formally, the Bias Amplification coefficient is:

$$\text{BA}_g = \frac{p_g / \sum_{g'} p_{g'}}{a_g / \sum_{g'} a_{g'}}$$

where $a_g = P(Y = y, G = g)$ is the training proportion and $p_g = P(\hat{Y} = y, G = g)$ is the model's predicted proportion. $\text{BA}_g > 1$ implies amplification.

**(2) Worked Example — 3 Marks:**
Consider a CLIP-based text-to-image model trained on a dataset where 80% of "CEO" images depict males. Suppose during evaluation, when prompted with "CEO", the model generates male images 92% of the time. The bias amplification is:

$$\text{BA}_{\text{male}} = \frac{0.92}{0.80} = 1.15$$

This 15% amplification is a measurable form of representational harm.

**(3) Debiasing Strategies — 2 Marks:**
1. **Counterfactual Prompt Augmentation:** Re-train or fine-tune the model on prompts like "Female CEO", "Black scientist", "Muslim doctor" with balanced image generation.
2. **Embedding Neutralization (Hard-Debias):** Identify the bias subspace in the CLIP embedding (e.g., the gender direction) and project out this direction from occupation embeddings, ensuring the "CEO" embedding is orthogonal to the gender axis.

---

### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **three-layered bias mitigation framework** in Generative AI. Compare pre-processing, in-processing, and post-processing techniques. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Fairness Impossibility Theorem**. Why is it impossible to simultaneously optimize for calibration, balance for the positive class, and balance for the negative class? Discuss its practical implications for an LLM-based content moderation system. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Block Diagram (3 Marks)** — *(Refer to Section 4.2 Diagram 2 of these notes.)* The framework consists of three layers:

1. **Pre-processing Layer:** Operates on the training data *before* model training.
2. **In-processing Layer:** Modifies the training algorithm or objective.
3. **Post-processing Layer:** Adjusts the model's outputs *after* generation.

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Aspect | Pre-processing | In-processing | Post-processing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **When Applied** | Before training | During training | After generation |
| **What is Modified** | Data distribution, embeddings | Loss function, architecture | Output logits, completions |
| **Examples** | Re-sampling, counterfactual augmentation, Hard-Debias | Adversarial debiasing, fairness-constrained loss, RLHF | Output filtering, calibrated equalized odds, prompt re-ranking |
| **Pros** | Model-agnostic, simple to implement | Best fairness-utility trade-off | Flexible, no retraining required |
| **Cons** | May degrade model utility | Requires architectural access | Only addresses symptoms |
| **KTU Example** | Re-weight "doctor" captions | Adversarial gender removal in BERT | Toxic output suppression in GPT |

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Statement of the Theorem (3 Marks):**
The **Fairness Impossibility Theorem**, independently proven by **Chouldechova (2017)** and **Kleinberg, Mullainathan & Raghavan (2016)**, states that for any non-trivial binary classification problem where group base rates differ, it is **impossible to simultaneously satisfy all three of the following**:
1. **Calibration:** $P(Y = 1 \mid \hat{Y} = p, A = a) = p$ for all groups $a$ and thresholds $p$.
2. **Balance for the Positive Class:** $\mathbb{E}[\hat{Y} \mid Y = 1, A = a]$ is equal across groups.
3. **Balance for the Negative Class:** $\mathbb{E}[\hat{Y} \mid Y = 0, A = a]$ is equal across groups.

**Intuitive Explanation (2 Marks):**
When base rates (prevalence) differ across demographic groups, equalizing one metric necessarily un-equalizes another. For example, in a content moderation system where 1% of posts from Group A are toxic vs. 5% of posts from Group B, maintaining equal calibration forces the model to be more conservative for Group B, which unbalances the positive class.

**Practical Implications for an LLM Moderation System (2 Marks):**
1. The team must make an **explicit, contextual choice** about which metric to prioritize.
2. **Documentation is mandatory** — the model card must declare the chosen fairness criterion.
3. **Stakeholder consultation** is required; for harmful content detection, equalized odds for the positive class (toxic content) is typically preferred to avoid disproportionately silencing minority voices.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Don't confuse bias with hallucination.** Bias = systematic skew. Hallucination = fabricated fact. Many students mix them up in Part A and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Always state the metric's name AND its formula** in Part B. Examiners award marks only when both are present.
> 3. **Memorize the "80% Rule" formula** as $\frac{\min}{\max} \ge 0.8$. Writing it as $\ge 0.05$ or $\ge 0.5$ is a common mistake that costs full marks.
> 4. **Don't fabricate authors or papers.** The Impossibility Theorem has *two* correct attributions — Chouldechova (2017) and Kleinberg et al. (2016). Either is acceptable; fabricated references will be penalized.
> 5. **For diagrams, label every block clearly.** Unlabeled arrows or missing stage names lose 0.5–1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Bias = systematic error in model output favoring/disadvantaging groups**; **Fairness = engineering property ensuring equitable treatment.**
- ✅ The **3 R's of Fair GenAI**: **R**ecognize, **R**eport, **R**emediate.
- ✅ **7 Types of Bias**: Historical, Representation, Measurement, Aggregation, Learning, Generation, Evaluation.
- ✅ **3 Formal Fairness Criteria**: Demographic Parity, Equalized Odds, Counterfactual Fairness.
- ✅ **80% Rule (Four-Fifths Rule)**: $\frac{\min \text{selection rate}}{\max \text{selection rate}} \ge 0.8$.
- ✅ **Bias Amplification Coefficient** $\text{BA}_g > 1$ means amplification; $\text{BA}_g < 1$ means attenuation.
- ✅ **3 Layers of Mitigation**: Pre-processing, In-processing, Post-processing.
- ✅ **Fairness Impossibility Theorem**: Cannot satisfy Calibration + Balance(+) + Balance(–) simultaneously when base rates differ.
- ✅ **Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)**: $\text{DIR} = \frac{\min_a P(\hat{Y}=y \mid A=a)}{\max_a P(\hat{Y}=y \mid A=a)}$; fair if $\ge 0.8$.
- ✅ **Distribution-based metrics for Generative AI**: KL Divergence, Wasserstein Distance, EAT, SEAT, StereoSet, CrowS-Pairs.
- ✅ **Decision rule for picking a fairness metric**: High-stakes legal → Demographic Parity; Medical → Equalized Odds; Generative content → Output Distribution Divergence.
- ✅ **RLHF** = Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback — primary in-processing debiasing technique for modern LLMs.
- ✅ **Constitutional AI** = rule-based safety fine-tuning (Anthropic 2022) — a hybrid in-processing + post-processing technique.
- ✅ **Model Card & Bias Card** = mandatory reporting artifacts for fair GenAI deployment.
- ✅ **Real-world failures to memorize**: GPT-3 male-doctor bias, COMPAS recidivism racial bias, Amazon hiring AI gender bias, Stable Diffusion racial bias in occupational prompts.
- ✅ **NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 alignment**: Bias mitigation is a **GA5 (Ethical Responsibility)** graduate attribute — board examiners are instructed to test this explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Responsible Use and Deployment of Generative Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Responsible Use and Deployment of Generative Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Responsible Use and Deployment of Generative Models** refers to the systematic, ethical, and legally compliant application of generative artificial intelligence systems (such as Large Language Models, Diffusion Models, and GANs) across their entire lifecycle — from data collection, model training, and fine-tuning to real-world inference, monitoring, and retirement. It is a multidisciplinary framework that integrates **AI ethics, governance, security, and societal impact mitigation** to ensure that the outputs and downstream effects of generative systems remain **fair, transparent, accountable, privacy-preserving, and aligned with human values**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Responsible AI is a **mandatory 14-mark topic** in Module 1 of PEADT748. The question frequently asked is *"Discuss the ethical challenges in deploying generative models with real-world examples"* — appearing consistently across university exam cycles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dual-Use Car Engine"

Imagine you have invented a revolutionary car engine — extremely powerful, efficient, and capable of going anywhere. Now consider the real-world challenges:

- If the engine is sold without brakes → **Safety hazard**
- If the exhaust pollutes → **Environmental harm**
- If anyone without a license can drive it → **Public safety risk**
- If the manufacturer hides the design → **No accountability**
- If the engine is used as a weapon → **Misuse**

A **generative model is exactly this engine**. It can write essays, generate images, design drugs, and compose music — but the *same capability* can be weaponized to create deepfakes, spread misinformation, or violate privacy. **Responsible deployment is the "brake, license, exhaust filter, and user manual"** that comes bundled with the engine.

## 1.3 The Six Pillars of Responsible Generative AI

According to international AI governance frameworks (aligned with KTU's outcome-based curriculum), responsible use rests on **six foundational pillars**:

| # | Pillar | One-Line Meaning |
|---|--------|------------------|
| 1 | **Fairness** | The model should not discriminate against any group. |
| 2 | **Transparency** | Users should know they are interacting with AI. |
| 3 | **Accountability** | There must be a human/organization legally responsible. |
| 4 | **Privacy** | Personal data must be protected at all stages. |
| 5 | **Safety** | The model should not enable harm (weapons, self-harm, etc.). |
| 6 | **Sustainability** | Training/inference should be environmentally conscious. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to Remember:** "**F-A-T-P-S-S**" → **F**airness, **A**ccountability, **T**ransparency, **P**rivacy, **S**afety, **S**ustainability.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Generative AI Specifically

Generative models differ from traditional discriminative AI because they **produce novel content**, not just predictions. This creates a unique risk profile:

- **Open-ended outputs** → Cannot be pre-validated exhaustively.
- **Scale of generation** → One model can produce millions of outputs per day.
- **Indistinguishability** → Outputs can fool humans (deepfakes, fake news).
- **Dual-use potential** → The same model that writes code can also write malware.
- **Copyright entanglement** → Outputs may resemble training data.

These features make **responsible deployment non-optional** — it is a *technical, legal, and ethical necessity*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Responsible AI Lifecycle (5-Stage Framework)

The deployment of a responsible generative model follows a **lifecycle model**. Each stage has specific obligations:

### Stage 1: Data Curation & Provenance

- **What**: Collecting, cleaning, and licensing the training corpus.
- **Risk**: Biased, copyrighted, or private data leaks into the model.
- **Mitigation**:
  * Use **documented, consented, and licensed datasets** (e.g., Common Crawl with opt-outs).
  * Apply **differential privacy** during training to prevent memorization.
  * Conduct **data audits** for demographic balance.
  * Maintain a **datasheet** for every dataset (Gebru et al., 2018 framework).

### Stage 2: Model Training & Alignment

- **What**: Training the base model and aligning it with human values.
- **Risk**: Model inherits societal biases; produces harmful outputs.
- **Mitigation Techniques**:
  * **RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)** — Human raters rank outputs; the model learns preferences.
  * **Constitutional AI** — Model is trained against an explicit rulebook of values.
  * **Red Teaming** — Adversarial probing to discover failure modes.
  * **Adversarial Robustness Training** — Hardening against prompt injection.

### Stage 3: Pre-Deployment Evaluation

- **What**: Systematic testing before public release.
- **Risk**: Hidden harmful capabilities (e.g., bio-weapon synthesis guidance).
- **Mitigation**:
  * **Benchmark suites**: HELM, BIG-bench, TruthfulQA.
  * **Capability evaluations** (e.g., bar exam pass rates for LLMs).
  * **Bias audits** using tools like **Fairlearn**, **Aequitas**.
  * **Third-party audits** by independent organizations.

### Stage 4: Deployment & Monitoring

- **What**: Releasing the model with guardrails in production.
- **Risk**: Misuse, jailbreaks, drift, emerging failure modes.
- **Mitigation**:
  * **Content filters** at input and output layers.
  * **Watermarking** of generated content (e.g., C2PA standard).
  * **Usage policies** and **rate limiting**.
  * **Real-time monitoring dashboards** for anomalous behavior.
  * **Kill switches** for emergency shutdown.

### Stage 5: Incident Response & Iteration

- **What**: Responding to reported harms and updating the model.
- **Risk**: Repeated harm without accountability.
- **Mitigation**:
  * **Bug bounty programs** for AI vulnerabilities.
  * **Public transparency reports** (e.g., OpenAI, Anthropic).
  * **Model cards** documenting intended use and limitations.
  * **Continuous fine-tuning** based on incident data.

## 2.2 Core Risk Categories in Generative AI

### A. Bias and Fairness Risks

Generative models can **amplify societal biases** present in training data. For example:
- Image generators underrepresent certain ethnicities in professional roles.
- LLMs associate "doctor" with male pronouns and "nurse" with female pronouns.

**Quantitative Measure of Bias** — **Demographic Parity Difference (DPD)**:

$$DPD = \vert P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid G = 0) - P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid G = 1) \vert$$

Where:
- $\hat{Y}$ = model's positive prediction
- $G$ = protected group attribute (0 or 1)
- A value close to **0** indicates fairness; closer to **1** indicates severe bias.

### B. Privacy and Memorization Risks

Large language models have been shown to **memorize and regurgitate** training data verbatim — including emails, code, and personal identifiers. The **Memorization Ratio (MR)** can be expressed as:

$$MR = \frac{\text{Number of verbatim training strings retrieved}}{\text{Total probing queries}}$$

**Defense**: Differential Privacy (DP) adds calibrated noise during training, controlled by the **privacy budget parameter ε (epsilon)**:

$$\text{Privacy Loss} \propto \frac{1}{\epsilon}$$

- **Lower ε** → Stronger privacy, weaker utility
- **Higher ε** → Weaker privacy, stronger utility

### C. Misinformation and Deepfakes

Generative models can produce **synthetic text, images, audio, and video** that are indistinguishable from real content. This enables:
- **Political deepfakes** affecting elections.
- **Voice cloning fraud** (e.g., CEO voice impersonation scams).
- **Synthetic identities** for financial fraud.

**Mitigation Metrics**:
- **Detection Accuracy (DA)** of deepfake detectors.
- **Watermark Robustness (WR)** — how well a watermark survives editing.

### D. Copyright and Intellectual Property

- **Training-phase infringement**: Was the training data legally obtained?
- **Output-phase infringement**: Do outputs resemble protected works?
- **Style mimicry**: Should a model be allowed to generate "in the style of X"?

Many jurisdictions (EU AI Act 2024, US Copyright Office guidance) are still defining legal boundaries.

### E. Environmental Sustainability

Training a frontier LLM can emit **hundreds of tons of CO₂**. The **carbon footprint (CF)** is estimated as:

$$CF = P \times T \times CI$$

Where:
- $P$ = Power consumption (kW)
- $T$ = Training time (hours)
- $CI$ = Carbon Intensity of the electricity grid (kg CO₂/kWh)

## 2.3 Governance Frameworks and Standards

| Framework | Region | Key Focus |
|-----------|--------|-----------|
| **EU AI Act** | European Union | Risk-tiered regulation; high-risk AI requires conformity assessments. |
| **NIST AI RMF** | USA | Voluntary risk management framework (Govern, Map, Measure, Manage). |
| **OECD AI Principles** | Global | Human-centered values, transparency, accountability. |
| **India's IndiaAI Mission** | India | Sovereign AI infrastructure, responsible innovation. |
| **ISO/IEC 42001** | Global | AI Management System standard. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner Note:** When asked about "responsible deployment," always cite the **EU AI Act risk tiers** (Unacceptable / High / Limited / Minimal) — it is the most referenced regulation in current question papers.

## 2.4 Deployment Modes and Their Risk Profiles

Generative models can be deployed in different modes, each with distinct risk levels:

| Mode | Description | Risk Level | Example |
|------|-------------|------------|---------|
| **API-only** | Closed access, no weights | **Lowest** | GPT-4 via OpenAI API |
| **Hosted open weights** | Weights released but inference via platform | **Medium** | Llama models on Hugging Face |
| **Fully open source** | Weights, code, data all public | **Highest** | Stable Diffusion, Mistral |
| **On-device** | Runs locally on user hardware | **High (uncontrollable)** | Local LLMs via Ollama |
| **Fine-tuned enterprise** | Customized for one organization | **Medium** | Internal company chatbots |

> [!NOTE]
> Open-source deployment democratizes access but **eliminates post-deployment control** — a critical trade-off in responsible deployment.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Marks |
|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| Demographic Parity | $DPD = \vert P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid G=0) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid G=1) \vert$ | 3–4 |
| Differential Privacy Budget | Lower $\epsilon$ = Stronger privacy | 2–3 |
| Carbon Footprint | $CF = P \times T \times CI$ | 3 |
| Memorization Ratio | $MR = \frac{\text{Verbatim retrievals}}{\text{Total probes}}$ | 2 |
| EU AI Act Risk Tiers | Unacceptable / High / Limited / Minimal | 5–7 |
| RLHF | Reward model trained from human preferences | 5 |
| Watermarking | Embedding traceable signal in outputs | 3 |
| Model Card | Standardized documentation of model purpose & limits | 3 |
| Datasheet | Standardized documentation of dataset | 3 |
| Red Teaming | Adversarial testing for safety | 3 |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculating Demographic Parity for a Generative Image Model

> **Problem Context:** A generative image model is asked to generate images for the prompt *"CEO of a company"*. Out of 1000 generated images:
> - 480 depict men as CEO
> - 200 depict women as CEO
> - 320 depict ambiguous/non-binary appearances
>
> Compute the **Demographic Parity Difference (DPD)** between the binary groups {Men, Women} for the role of CEO.

### Step 1 — Identify the Group Variable

The protected attribute $G$ is **gender**, encoded as:
- $G = 1$ → Men
- $G = 0$ → Women

### Step 2 — Define the Positive Prediction

$\hat{Y} = 1$ means the generated image depicts the person as a **CEO**.

### Step 3 — Compute Conditional Probabilities

The number of clear CEO images assigned to each group:
- Men: 480
- Women: 200

Assuming the remaining 320 ambiguous cases are excluded for a clean binary analysis (i.e., we consider only 680 clear depictions):

$$P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid G = 1) = \frac{480}{680} \approx 0.7059$$

$$P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid G = 0) = \frac{200}{680} \approx 0.2941$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Demographic Parity Difference

Using the formula:

$$DPD = \vert P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid G = 1) - P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid G = 0) \vert$$

Substituting the computed values:

$$DPD = \vert 0.7059 - 0.2941 \vert = 0.4118$$

### Step 5 — Interpret the Result

$$DPD = 0.4118$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A DPD of **0.41** is considered **substantial bias** (industry threshold is typically < 0.1 for acceptable fairness). The model is **~2.4× more likely** to depict a man as CEO than a woman — a clear fairness violation requiring mitigation through balanced training data and counterfactual prompting.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Carbon Footprint Estimation for Training a Generative Model

> **Problem Context:** A research lab trains a diffusion model for 30 days. The training cluster draws an average of **45 kW** continuously, and the local electricity grid has a carbon intensity of **0.6 kg CO₂/kWh**. Compute the total carbon footprint.

### Step 1 — Identify the Given Variables

$$P = 45 \text{ kW}, \quad T = 30 \text{ days}, \quad CI = 0.6 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kWh}$$

### Step 2 — Convert Time to Hours

$$T = 30 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hours/day} = 720 \text{ hours}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Carbon Footprint Formula

$$CF = P \times T \times CI$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Evaluate

$$CF = 45 \text{ kW} \times 720 \text{ hours} \times 0.6 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kWh}$$

$$CF = 45 \times 720 = 32{,}400 \text{ kWh}$$

$$CF = 32{,}400 \times 0.6 = 19{,}440 \text{ kg CO}_2$$

### Step 5 — Convert to a More Interpretable Unit

$$CF = 19{,}440 \text{ kg} \approx 19.44 \text{ metric tons of CO}_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world reference:** A single round-trip flight from New York to London emits ~1.6 tons CO₂ per passenger. Thus, this **single training run** has the carbon equivalent of ~12 such flights. This justifies the KTU-emphasized **Green AI** principle of model efficiency, distillation, and renewable-powered data centers.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Differential Privacy Budget Trade-off

> **Problem Context:** A healthcare organization wants to fine-tune an LLM on patient records. The privacy team must choose between three configurations:
>
> | Configuration | Privacy Budget (ε) | Model Accuracy |
> |---------------|-------------------|----------------|
> | A | 0.5 | 78% |
> | B | 3.0 | 89% |
> | C | 8.0 | 93% |
>
> **Regulatory threshold:** For medical data, ε ≤ 1.0 is mandated. Recommend the appropriate configuration.

### Step 1 — Recall the Privacy Loss Relationship

$$\text{Privacy Loss} \propto \frac{1}{\epsilon}$$

- **Lower ε** → Stronger privacy, weaker utility.
- **Higher ε** → Weaker privacy, stronger utility.

### Step 2 — Filter by Regulatory Compliance

The medical-data regulation requires **ε ≤ 1.0**. Checking each:
- Configuration A: ε = 0.5 ✓ Compliant
- Configuration B: ε = 3.0 ✗ Non-compliant
- Configuration C: ε = 8.0 ✗ Non-compliant

### Step 3 — Identify the Acceptable Configuration

Only **Configuration A (ε = 0.5)** satisfies the regulatory threshold. While its accuracy (78%) is lower, it provides the strongest privacy guarantee.

### Step 4 — Recommendation

$$\boxed{\text{Choose Configuration A — prioritize regulatory compliance and patient trust.}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** If higher accuracy is required, the team can apply **privacy-preserving techniques** like **federated learning** (training on-device without centralizing data) and **secure multi-party computation** to *complement* DP rather than relax the ε budget.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis: Detection vs. Watermarking for Deepfakes

| Approach | Strength | Weakness | Best Used When |
|----------|----------|----------|----------------|
| **Deepfake Detectors (ML-based)** | High accuracy on known distributions | Fails on novel deepfakes (adversarial vulnerability) | Forensic analysis after the fact |
| **Watermarking (Proactive)** | Survives compression, robust to editing | Requires cooperation from generation pipeline | Built into model APIs |
| **Provenance (C2PA)** | Cryptographic chain of custody | Only works if creator signs content | Journalism, legal evidence |
| **Human-in-the-loop verification** | Adapts to novel attacks | Not scalable, slow | High-stakes decisions |

---

## 3.5 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing Deployment Mode

| Decision Criterion | API-only | Open Weights | On-device |
|-------------------|----------|--------------|-----------|
| **Control over misuse** | High | Medium | None |
| **Cost to deploy** | Pay-per-token | Free for users | Free for users |
| **Auditability** | Full logs | Limited | None |
| **Customization** | Fine-tuning API | Full | Full |
| **Risk of weaponization** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Recommended for** | Enterprise, regulated sectors | Research, education | Personal use |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common 14-mark question asks: *"Compare open-source vs. closed-source deployment of generative models from a responsible AI perspective."* Use this matrix as a structured answer scaffold.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Responsible AI Lifecycle — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Data Curation & Provenance"] --> B["Model Training & Alignment"]
    B --> C["Pre-Deployment Evaluation"]
    C --> D{"Risk Tier Classification"}
    D -->|Minimal Risk| E1["Deploy with Standard Logging"]
    D -->|Limited Risk| E2["Deploy with Transparency Notice"]
    D -->|High Risk| E3["Deploy with Full Audit Trail"]
    D -->|Unacceptable Risk| E4["Halt Deployment"]
    E1 --> F["Real-time Monitoring & Feedback"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> G["Redesign & Re-evaluate"]
    F --> H{"Incident Detected?"}
    H -->|Yes| I["Incident Response & Model Update"]
    H -->|No| J["Continuous Monitoring"]
    I --> B
    J --> F
    G --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read this diagram (for KTU answers):** Note the **closed feedback loop** — incidents during monitoring feed back into training. This is the *iterative* nature of responsible deployment, which KTU examiners specifically reward in 14-mark answers.
