$$V(D, G) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} p_{data}(x) \log D(x) \, dx + \int_{\mathcal{Z}} p_z(z) \log(1 - D(G(z))) \, dz$$

**Step 1 — Change of variable.** Apply the change of variable $x = G(z)$ for the second term. The induced distribution of $x = G(z)$ is precisely $p_g(x)$. Hence:

$$V(D, G) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} p_{data}(x) \log D(x) \, dx + \int_{\mathcal{X}} p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \, dx$$

Combining the integrals:

$$V(D, G) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} \Big[ p_{data}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \Big] \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Pointwise optimization.** For each $x$, we maximize the integrand independently because D(x) at different points does not interact. Define:

$$f(y) = a \log y + b \log(1 - y), \quad \text{where } a = p_{data}(x), \; b = p_g(x), \; y = D(x)$$

**Step 3 — Take the derivative and set to zero.**

$$\frac{df}{dy} = \frac{a}{y} - \frac{b}{1 - y} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for y.**

$$a(1 - y) = by \;\Rightarrow\; a = y(a + b) \;\Rightarrow\; y = \frac{a}{a + b}$$

Therefore:

$$D^*(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The second derivative $f''(y) = -a/y^2 - b/(1-y)^2 < 0$ confirms this is a **maximum**, not a minimum.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Global Optimum and JSD Connection

**Step 1 — Substitute $D^*$ back into $V$.**

$$V(D^*, G) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} p_{data}(x) \log \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)} \, dx + \int_{\mathcal{X}} p_g(x) \log \frac{p_g(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)} \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Algebraic decomposition using $\log(a/b) = \log a - \log b$.**

$$V(D^*, G) = \int p_{data} \log \frac{p_{data}}{(p_{data}+p_g)/2} \, dx - \log 2 \int p_{data} \, dx + \int p_g \log \frac{p_g}{(p_{data}+p_g)/2} \, dx - \log 2 \int p_g \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Use the fact that $\int p_{data} \, dx = \int p_g \, dx = 1$ (both are valid probability distributions).**

$$V(D^*, G) = \underbrace{\int p_{data} \log \frac{p_{data}}{(p_{data}+p_g)/2} \, dx}_{\text{KL}(p_{data} \Vert (p_{data}+p_g)/2)} + \underbrace{\int p_g \log \frac{p_g}{(p_{data}+p_g)/2} \, dx}_{\text{KL}(p_g \Vert (p_{data}+p_g)/2)} - 2\log 2$$

**Step 4 — Identify the JSD.** By definition, JSD is the average of the two KL terms:

$$V(D^*, G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{data} \parallel p_g)$$

**Step 5 — Global minimum.** Since $\text{JSD} \geq 0$, $V(D^*, G) \geq -\log 4$, with equality **iff** $p_{data} = p_g$. Therefore:

$$\min_G V(D^*, G) = -\log 4 \quad \text{achieved uniquely when } p_g = p_{data}$$

This proves the **theoretical global optimum** of GAN training.

## 3.3 Non-Saturating Generator Loss (Practical Reformulation)

The original generator loss $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ saturates near $0$ when D is strong early in training, causing vanishing gradients. The non-saturating trick is:

$$\max_G \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log D(G(z))]$$

This is equivalent in optimum but provides **strong gradients** when D rejects samples (i.e., $D(G(z)) \approx 0$).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: KLD vs JSD vs W-Distance

| Metric | Symmetric? | Bounded? | Differentiable? | GAN Variant |
|--------|------------|----------|------------------|-------------|
| KL Divergence $\text{KL}(P \Vert Q)$ | No | No | Yes | Original GAN (proxy) |
| Reverse KL $\text{KL}(Q \Vert P)$ | No | No | Yes | VAE-like behaviour |
| JSD $\text{JSD}(P \Vert Q)$ | **Yes** | **Yes** ($[0,\log 2]$) | Yes (almost) | **Standard GAN** |
| Wasserstein-1 $W_1(P,Q)$ | Yes | No (unbounded) | Yes (Lipschitz) | **WGAN** |

## 3.5 Discriminator Update Rule (Gradient Ascent)

For a minibatch of $m$ samples:

$$\theta_d \leftarrow \theta_d + \eta_d \cdot \nabla_{\theta_d} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \Big[ \log D(x^{(i)}) + \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)}))) \Big]$$

## 3.6 Generator Update Rule (Non-Saturating)

$$\theta_g \leftarrow \theta_g - \eta_g \cdot \nabla_{\theta_g} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)})))$$

or equivalently (preferred in practice):

$$\theta_g \leftarrow \theta_g + \eta_g \cdot \nabla_{\theta_g} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \log(D(G(z^{(i)})))$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GAN Architecture — High-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[NOISE Z SAMPLED FROM PZ] --> B[GENERATOR NETWORK G]
    B --> C[FAKE SAMPLE G OF Z]
    D[REAL DATA X FROM PDATA] --> E[DISCRIMINATOR NETWORK D]
    C --> E
    E --> F[SCALAR PROBABILITY D X]
    F --> G[LOSS COMPUTATION MODULE]
    G --> H[GRADIENT TO GENERATOR THETAG]
    G --> I[GRADIENT TO DISCRIMINATOR THETAD]
    H --> B
    I --> E
    J[KTU NOTE PDATA APPROACHES PG] --> K[NASH EQUILIBRIUM D X = 0.5]
```

## 4.2 GAN Training Loop — Alternating Optimization

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1[STEP 1: SAMPLE NOISE BATCH Z1 TO ZM FROM PZ] --> step2[STEP 2: GENERATE FAKE SAMPLES GZ]
    step2 --> step3[STEP 3: SAMPLE REAL BATCH X1 TO XM FROM PDATA]
    step3 --> step4[STEP 4: TRAIN DISCRIMINATOR FREEZE G]
    step4 --> step5[STEP 5: SAMPLE NEW NOISE BATCH]
    step5 --> step6[STEP 6: TRAIN GENERATOR FREEZE D]
    step6 --> step7[STEP 7: CHECK CONVERGENCE JDPDATA PG NEAR ZERO]
    step7 -->|NOT CONVERGED| step1
    step7 -->|CONVERGED| step8[END TRAINING ACHIEVE NASH EQUILIBRIUM]
```

## 4.3 Generator vs Discriminator — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    genInput[LATENT NOISE VECTOR Z] --> genDense1[DENSE LAYER PROJECT]
    genDense1 --> genReshape[RESHAPE TO TENSOR]
    genReshape --> genConv1[TRANSPOSED CONV 1]
    genConv1 --> genBN1[BATCH NORM]
    genBN1 --> genRelu1[RELU ACTIVATION]
    genRelu1 --> genConv2[TRANSPOSED CONV 2]
    genConv2 --> genBN2[BATCH NORM]
    genBN2 --> genRelu2[RELU ACTIVATION]
    genRelu2 --> genOut[TANH OUTPUT FAKE IMAGE]
    
    discInput[INPUT IMAGE X REAL OR FAKE] --> discConv1[CONV 2D LAYER]
    discConv1 --> discLRelu1[LEAKY RELU]
    discLRelu1 --> discDrop1[DROPOUT]
    discDrop1 --> discConv2[CONV 2D LAYER]
    discConv2 --> discLRelu2[LEAKY RELU]
    discLRelu2 --> discDrop2[DROPOUT]
    discDrop2 --> discFlat[FLATTEN]
    discFlat --> discDense[DENSE LAYER]
    discDense --> discProb[SIGMOID PROBABILITY]
```

## 4.4 GAN Training Dynamics — D vs G Loss Behaviour

```mermaid
graph TD
    phase1[PHASE 1 EARLY TRAINING] -->|D LOSS HIGH, G LOSS HIGH| phase2[PHASE 2 MIDDLE TRAINING]
    phase2 -->|D AND G OSCILLATE| phase3[PHASE 3 LATE TRAINING]
    phase3 -->|IDEALLY BOTH CONVERGE TO LOG 2| phase4[PHASE 4 EQUILIBRIUM]
    phase4 -->|D X = 0.5 EVERYWHERE| phase5[SUCCESS: PDATA = PG]
    phase4 -.->|FALLS INTO MODE COLLAPSE| failure1[FAILURE MODE 1: MODE COLLAPSE]
    phase4 -.->|LOSS DOESNT CONVERGE| failure2[FAILURE MODE 2: OSCILLATING LOSS]
    phase4 -.->|D TOO STRONG, G GRADIENT VANISHES| failure3[FAILURE MODE 3: UNINFORMATIVE LOSS]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN). List its two main components and state their objectives.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, PEADT748]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** is a deep generative model introduced by Goodfellow et al. (2014) consisting of two neural networks trained adversarially.

- **Generator (G):** Maps noise $z \sim p_z(z)$ to fake samples $G(z)$ that mimic the real data distribution. *Objective:* fool the discriminator.
- **Discriminator (D):** A binary classifier that distinguishes real samples $x \sim p_{data}(x)$ from fake samples $G(z)$. *Objective:* correctly classify real vs. fake.

The combined objective is the min–max value function $V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. What is mode collapse in GANs? How does it affect generation quality?** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, PEADT748]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Mode collapse** is a failure mode of GANs where the generator learns to produce only a **small subset of modes** (varieties) of the real data distribution, ignoring others. For example, a GAN trained on MNIST may produce only the digit "1" repeatedly, or a face GAN may generate only one facial pose.

**Causes:** When G discovers a few samples that consistently fool D, the gradient signal encourages it to reproduce these samples exclusively, collapsing the diversity of $p_g$.

**Effects:** Loss of diversity, poor coverage of the data distribution, and visually repetitive outputs. Remedies include minibatch discrimination, unrolled GANs, and Wasserstein loss. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A**

#### **(a) Derive the optimal discriminator $D^*(x)$ for a fixed generator G in a GAN. Show all steps clearly.** `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

For a fixed G, the value function reduces to a function of D alone:

$$V(D, G) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} \Big[ p_{data}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \Big] \, dx$$

We optimize pointwise. Let $a = p_{data}(x)$, $b = p_g(x)$, $y = D(x)$:

$$f(y) = a \log y + b \log(1 - y)$$

**Step 1 — First derivative:**

$$\frac{df}{dy} = \frac{a}{y} - \frac{b}{1 - y}$$

**Step 2 — Set to zero:**

$$\frac{a}{y} = \frac{b}{1 - y} \;\Rightarrow\; a(1-y) = by \;\Rightarrow\; a - ay = by \;\Rightarrow\; a = y(a+b)$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**

$$D^*(x) = y^* = \frac{a}{a+b} = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$$

**Step 4 — Verify maximum via second derivative:**

$$\frac{d^2 f}{dy^2} = -\frac{a}{y^2} - \frac{b}{(1-y)^2} < 0 \quad \text{(always negative)}$$

Hence $D^*(x)$ is indeed a **global maximum** for the fixed-G objective. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **(b) Show that the GAN training objective is equivalent to minimizing the Jensen–Shannon Divergence (JSD) between $p_{data}$ and $p_g$. State the global minimum.** `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Substitute $D^*$ into the value function:**

$$V(D^*, G) = \int p_{data} \log \frac{p_{data}}{p_{data}+p_g} \, dx + \int p_g \log \frac{p_g}{p_{data}+p_g} \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Add and subtract $\log 2$ inside each term:**

$$= \int p_{data} \log \frac{p_{data}}{(p_{data}+p_g)/2} \, dx - \log 2 + \int p_g \log \frac{p_g}{(p_{data}+p_g)/2} \, dx - \log 2$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the KL terms:**

$$= \text{KL}\!\left(p_{data} \,\Big\Vert\, \frac{p_{data}+p_g}{2}\right) + \text{KL}\!\left(p_{g} \,\Big\Vert\, \frac{p_{data}+p_g}{2}\right) - 2 \log 2$$

**Step 4 — Define JSD:**

$$\text{JSD}(P \Vert Q) = \frac{1}{2}\text{KL}\!\left(P \,\Big\Vert\, \frac{P+Q}{2}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\text{KL}\!\left(Q \,\Big\Vert\, \frac{P+Q}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Conclude:**

$$V(D^*, G) = 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{data} \Vert p_g) - \log 4$$

Since JSD $\geq 0$ with equality iff $P = Q$:

$$C(G) = \min_G V(D^*, G) = -\log 4 \quad \text{achieved when } p_g = p_{data}$$

Thus the GAN objective is **equivalent to minimizing JSD** and the global minimum value is $\mathbf{-\log 4}$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B** *(Internal Choice)*

#### **(a) With a neat labelled diagram, explain the architecture of a Generative Adversarial Network. Describe the role of each component.** `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

A GAN consists of two networks trained in opposition:

**1. Generator G:**
- **Input:** Latent noise vector $z \in \mathbb{R}^{d_z}$ sampled from prior $p_z(z)$ (typically $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$).
- **Architecture:** Fully-connected input layer → reshape → series of transposed convolution (deconvolution) layers with batch normalization and ReLU activations → final transposed conv with $\tanh$ output.
- **Output:** A synthetic sample $\tilde{x} = G(z)$ of the same shape as a real data point.
- **Goal:** Produce samples whose distribution $p_g$ matches $p_{data}$.

**2. Discriminator D:**
- **Input:** Either a real sample $x$ or a fake sample $G(z)$.
- **Architecture:** Series of strided convolution layers with LeakyReLU and dropout → flatten → dense layer → sigmoid.
- **Output:** Scalar $D(x) \in [0,1]$ — probability of being real.
- **Goal:** Maximize correct classification of real vs. fake.

**3. The Adversarial Loop:**
- D is trained on batches mixing real data and current fakes.
- G is then trained (with D frozen) using the non-saturating loss $-\log D(G(z))$.
- Gradients flow through D into G, providing a learning signal for G even though G never sees real data directly.

*(Refer to the diagrams in Section 4 for the architecture sketch.)* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **(b) Discuss the major challenges encountered during GAN training: oscillating loss, mode collapse, uninformative loss, and hyperparameter sensitivity. Suggest remedies.** `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Oscillating Loss:**
The losses of G and D oscillate without converging to a stable equilibrium because each network's update invalidates the other's optimum. G chases a moving target set by D. *Remedies:* use smaller learning rates, add noise to discriminator inputs, apply gradient penalty, or use two-timescale update rules (TTUR).

**2. Mode Collapse:**
G produces a small subset of modes (e.g., only one class or pose), ignoring the rest of the data distribution. This happens when G finds a few "winning" samples that consistently fool D. *Remedies:* minibatch discrimination, unrolled GANs, Wasserstein loss, PacGAN, and spectral normalization.

**3. Uninformative Loss:**
When D becomes too strong early in training, $D(G(z)) \approx 0$ for all $z$, causing $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ to **saturate** at $0$ and yield vanishing gradients to G. The G loss becomes constant — "uninformative." *Remedy:* use the **non-saturating** loss $\max_G \log D(G(z))$ instead of $\min_G \log(1 - D(G(z)))$.

**4. Hyperparameter Sensitivity:**
GANs are notoriously sensitive to learning rate, optimizer choice (Adam vs. RMSProp), batch size, network depth, and normalization. A small change can collapse training. *Remedies:* systematic grid search, learning rate decay, gradient clipping, spectral normalization, and progressive growing (StyleGAN-style).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Mode Collapse vs. Vanishing Gradient**
> Many students confuse **mode collapse** (G produces few but high-quality samples) with **uninformative loss / vanishing gradients** (G produces poor samples and receives no learning signal). The distinction is crucial:
> - **Mode collapse** → G's outputs are repetitive but D is fooled successfully.
> - **Uninformative loss** → G's outputs are poor, D easily rejects them, and the generator loss curve is **flat** with no gradient flow.
> Examiners will deduct marks if these two failures are conflated. **(7 marks)**

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GAN** = Generator G + Discriminator D in a zero-sum adversarial game.
- **Objective:** $\min_G \max_D V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z}[\log(1-D(G(z)))]$.
- **Optimal Discriminator:** $D^*(x) = p_{data}(x) / (p_{data}(x) + p_g(x))$.
- **Global Optimum:** $p_g = p_{data}$ with $V^* = -\log 4$.
- **Equivalence:** GAN objective at optimal D = $-\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{data} \parallel p_g)$.
- **Non-saturating G-loss** is preferred in practice: $\max_G \log D(G(z))$.
- **Nash Equilibrium** at $D(x) = 0.5$ for all $x$ (detective is 50/50 — coin flip).
- **Major Challenges:** oscillating loss, mode collapse, uninformative (vanishing-gradient) loss, hyperparameter sensitivity.
- **Mode collapse remedies:** minibatch discrimination, Wasserstein loss, unrolled GANs, spectral normalization.
- **Uninformative loss remedy:** switch to non-saturating objective.
- **Oscillating loss remedy:** smaller learning rates, gradient penalties, TTUR, noise injection.
- **Hyperparameter tips:** TTUR (different LRs for G and D), batch normalization in G, layer normalization in D, Adam optimizer with $\beta_1 = 0.5$, $\beta_2 = 0.999$.
- **JSD is symmetric, bounded in $[0, \log 2]$, and equals zero iff the two distributions are identical.**
- **Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)** replaces JSD with Earth-Mover distance to ensure continuous, meaningful gradients even when $p_{data}$ and $p_g$ have disjoint support.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Mnemonic — "GAND-MOU":**
> **G** – Generator maps $z \to x_{fake}$
> **A** – Adversarial min–max
> **N** – Nash equilibrium at $D = 0.5$
> **D** – Discriminator is a binary classifier
> **M** – Mode collapse (G repeats samples)
> **O** – Oscillating loss (D and G chase each other)
> **U** – Uninformative loss (saturating G-loss → use non-saturating)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) — KTU 2024 Definition**
> A Recurrent Neural Network is a class of artificial neural networks designed to recognise patterns in sequences of data, such as time series, speech, text, or video, by maintaining an internal **hidden state** $h_t$ that is updated recursively at each time step. Unlike feedforward networks, RNNs exhibit **temporal dynamic behaviour** through recurrent connections where the output at time $t$ depends on both the current input and the network's previous hidden state.

Mathematically, an RNN maintains a **shared parameter set** $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \{W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}\}$ across all time steps, enforcing **weight sharing** along the temporal axis — a property borrowed from Convolutional Neural Networks and lifted to the time domain.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reading-Aloud" Intuition

Imagine you are a child learning to read a sentence **word by word** out loud. To pronounce each new word, you don't only look at the word itself — you also **remember the context** built from all the words you have read so far.

- Your **brain's working memory** $\equiv$ the **hidden state** $h_t$ of the RNN.
- The **new word** you read $\equiv$ the **input** $x_t$ at the current time step.
- The **way you read it aloud** $\equiv$ the **output** $y_t$.

If someone wipes your memory after every word, you can no longer understand sentences. This is exactly the problem RNNs solve: they **preserve a compressed memory** of the past in the hidden state and pass it forward in time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use a regular feedforward network for sequences?**
> A feedforward network requires **fixed-size input**. It cannot natively handle:
> - Variable-length sequences
> - Order/position dependence
> - Long-range temporal dependencies
> RNNs address all three by introducing the recurrent loop.

### 1.3 Core Design Principles (KTU High-Yield Highlights)

- **Parameter Sharing**: The same weights are used at every time step, drastically reducing the parameter count compared to an unrolled feedforward equivalent.
- **Sequential Computation**: Hidden state evolution follows a **first-order non-linear difference equation**.
- **Unrolling in Time**: The recurrent graph can be **unfolded** into an equivalent feedforward computation graph of depth $T$ (sequence length).
- **Universal Approximation**: A sufficiently wide RNN with $\tanh$ activations is a **universal approximator** of dynamical systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Components of an RNN Cell

An RNN cell at any time step $t$ contains three sets of learnable parameters:

- **Input-to-Hidden weights** $W_{xh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_x}$
- **Hidden-to-Hidden (recurrent) weights** $W_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_h}$
- **Hidden-to-Output weights** $W_{hy} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_y \times d_h}$

where $d_x$ is the input feature dimension, $d_h$ is the hidden state dimension, and $d_y$ is the output dimension.

### 2.2 Forward Pass — The Heart of an RNN

At every time step $t$, two coupled updates occur:

1. **Hidden state update**: Combines the current input with the previous hidden state.
2. **Output computation**: Maps the new hidden state to a prediction.

### 2.3 The Three Standard Non-Linearities

| Activation | Formula | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Tanh** | $\tanh(z) = \frac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}$ | Default hidden-state activation in vanilla RNN |
| **Sigmoid** | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Gate activations (LSTM/GRU) |
| **ReLU** | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ | Rare; can cause exploding activations |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they account for >70% of RNN theory marks.**

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Hidden state update | $h_t = \phi_h(W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h)$ | Core recurrence relation |
| 2 | Output computation | $y_t = \phi_y(W_{hy}\, h_t + b_y)$ | Maps hidden state to output |
| 3 | Pre-activation | $a_t = W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h$ | Linear part before activation |
| 4 | Total Loss (sequence) | $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)$ | Cross-entropy / MSE summed over time |
| 5 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $L_t = -\sum_{c} y_{t,c} \log(\hat{y}_{t,c})$ | Used for classification outputs |
| 6 | BPTT gradient on $W_{hh}$ | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W_{hh}}$ | Sum of gradients from every time step |
| 7 | Backprop chain factor | $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}$ | The product that causes vanishing gradients |
| 8 | Vanishing condition | $\left\| \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} \right\| < 1$ repeatedly | Eigenvalues of $W_{hh}^{\top}\,\text{diag}(\phi') < 1$ |
| 9 | Number of parameters | $P = d_h(d_x + d_h + d_y) + d_h + d_y$ | Total learnable parameters in a single-layer RNN |
| 10 | Sigmoid derivative | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\,(1 - \sigma(z))$ | Needed for BPTT gate updates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight (KTU favourite):** The same $W_{xh}$, $W_{hh}$, $W_{hy}$ matrices appear in **every** time step. During BPTT, you take gradients with respect to the **shared weights**, summing contributions from **all $T$ time steps**. This shared-parameter structure is what gives RNNs their power and, paradoxically, their training difficulties.

### 2.5 Why RNNs are Engineering-Relevant

- **Natural Language Processing**: Machine translation, sentiment analysis, named entity recognition, text generation.
- **Speech Recognition**: Acoustic modelling (acoustic-to-phoneme mapping).
- **Time-Series Forecasting**: Stock prediction, weather modelling, energy load forecasting.
- **Music Generation**: Symbolic and audio sequence modelling.
- **Video Analysis**: Frame-by-frame action recognition.
- **Bioinformatics**: Protein/DNA sequence classification.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Forward Pass

Starting from the most general recursive formulation, let the RNN cell at time $t$ be parameterised by $W_{xh}$, $W_{hh}$, $W_{hy}$ and biases $b_h$, $b_y$.

**Step 1: Compute the pre-activation vector $a_t$**

The pre-activation is a linear combination of the current input $x_t$, the previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$, and the bias $b_h$.

$$a_t = W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h$$

**Step 2: Apply the non-linear activation $\phi_h$**

The hidden state is obtained by passing $a_t$ through an element-wise non-linearity $\phi_h$ (typically $\tanh$).

$$h_t = \phi_h(a_t) = \phi_h(W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h)$$

This is the **master equation** of the RNN. The recursion is initialised by $h_0$, which is usually a zero vector $\mathbf{0}$ or a learned parameter.

**Step 3: Compute the pre-output vector $o_t$**

The pre-output is the linear projection of the hidden state.

$$o_t = W_{hy}\, h_t + b_y$$

**Step 4: Apply output activation $\phi_y$**

For **classification** tasks, $\phi_y = \text{softmax}$:

$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(o_t) = \frac{\exp(o_t)}{\sum_{j} \exp(o_{t,j})}$$

For **regression**, $\phi_y$ is the identity function: $\hat{y}_t = o_t$.

### 3.2 Loss Function Derivation

The total loss over a sequence of length $T$ is the sum of per-step losses. For classification with cross-entropy:

$$L(\{y_1, y_2, \dots, y_T\}, \{\hat{y}_1, \hat{y}_2, \dots, \hat{y}_T\}) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$$

Expanding each $L_t$:

$$L_t = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{t,c} \log(\hat{y}_{t,c})$$

where $C$ is the number of output classes and $y_{t,c}$ is the one-hot ground truth indicator.

### 3.3 Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) — Full Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BPTT is unrolled differentiation.** We differentiate the loss with respect to shared weights, accumulating contributions across all time steps.

**Step 1: Gradient of loss w.r.t. output pre-activation $o_t$**

For softmax + cross-entropy, the gradient has a famously clean form:

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial o_t} = \hat{y}_t - y_t$$

**Step 2: Gradient of loss w.r.t. $W_{hy}$**

Differentiating $L_t$ with respect to $W_{hy}$:

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W_{hy}} = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial o_t} \cdot \frac{\partial o_t}{\partial W_{hy}} = (\hat{y}_t - y_t)\, h_t^{\top}$$

Summing across all time steps:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hy}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} (\hat{y}_t - y_t)\, h_t^{\top}$$

**Step 3: Gradient flowing back into the hidden state (the "delta" $\delta_t$)**

Define the error signal propagating back into $h_t$:

$$\delta_t = \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} = W_{hy}^{\top}\, (\hat{y}_t - y_t) + W_{hh}^{\top}\,\text{diag}(\phi_h'(a_{t+1}))\, \delta_{t+1}$$

This is a **recursive equation** in $\delta_t$, propagating error from future to past.

**Step 4: Gradient of loss w.r.t. $W_{hh}$**

The hidden-to-hidden weight appears at **every** time step, so we must sum:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial a_t} \cdot \frac{\partial a_t}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

Computing each factor:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial a_t} = \text{diag}(\phi_h'(a_t))$$

$$\frac{\partial a_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = h_{t-1}^{\top}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t \cdot \text{diag}(\phi_h'(a_t)) \cdot h_{t-1}^{\top}$$

**Step 5: Gradient of loss w.r.t. $W_{xh}$**

By identical reasoning:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{xh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t \cdot \text{diag}(\phi_h'(a_t)) \cdot x_t^{\top}$$

### 3.4 The Vanishing/Exploding Gradient — Mathematical Origin

The most critical insight in RNN theory: how a gradient at time $t$ depends on $h_k$ for $k < t$.

By the chain rule applied to the hidden-state recursion:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} W_{hh}^{\top}\, \text{diag}(\phi_h'(a_i))$$

Taking the spectral norm $\Vert \cdot \Vert_2$ of both sides:

$$\left\| \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \right\|_2 \leq \left( \Vert W_{hh} \Vert_2 \cdot \max_{x \in \mathbb{R}} \vert \phi_h'(x) \vert \right)^{t-k}$$

For $\tanh$, $\max \vert \phi_h'(x) \vert = 1$, so:

$$\left\| \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \right\|_2 \leq \Vert W_{hh} \Vert_2^{\,t-k}$$

- If $\Vert W_{hh} \Vert_2 > 1$: gradient **explodes** exponentially in $t-k$.
- If $\Vert W_{hh} \Vert_2 < 1$: gradient **vanishes** exponentially in $t-k$.

This is the **fundamental reason** vanilla RNNs cannot model long-range dependencies, motivating LSTM and GRU architectures.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RNN Cell — Folded (Recurrent) Representation

The **folded** view depicts the RNN as a single block with a self-loop, emphasising the recurrent nature and parameter sharing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input x_t"] --> B["RNN Cell"]
    C["Previous Hidden State h_t-1"] --> B
    B --> D["Hidden State h_t"]
    B --> E["Output y_t"]
    D -.->|Self Recurrence| C
    F["Parameters W_xh W_hh W_hy"] -.->|Shared Weights| B
```

### 4.2 RNN — Unrolled in Time (Computational Graph)

Unrolling reveals the **feedforward-equivalent** structure along the time axis. Each block shares the same parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X0["x_1"] --> R1["RNN Cell t1"]
    H0["h_0 = 0"] --> R1
    R1 --> H1["h_1"]
    R1 --> Y1["y_1"]

    X1["x_2"] --> R2["RNN Cell t2"]
    H1 --> R2
    R2 --> H2["h_2"]
    R2 --> Y2["y_2"]

    X2["x_3"] --> R3["RNN Cell t3"]
    H2 --> R3
    R3 --> H3["h_3"]
    R3 --> Y3["y_3"]

    X3["x_T"] --> RT["RNN Cell tT"]
    H3 --> RT
    RT --> HT["h_T"]
    RT --> YT["y_T"]
```

### 4.3 Detailed Block-Level Functional Architecture

This block diagram details the **internal flow** of a single RNN cell, showing the linear projections, activation, and state propagation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Input Vector x_t (dim d_x)"] --> M1["Multiply by W_xh"]
    C["Previous Hidden h_t-1 (dim d_h)"] --> M2["Multiply by W_hh"]
    M1 --> SUM["Add Bias b_h"]
    M2 --> SUM
    SUM --> PREACT["Pre-activation a_t"]
    PREACT --> ACT["Apply phi_h: tanh or ReLU"]
    ACT --> HNEW["New Hidden State h_t"]
    HNEW --> M3["Multiply by W_hy"]
    M3 --> OUTACT["Add b_y then phi_y softmax"]
    OUTACT --> O["Output y_t"]
    HNEW -->|Loop back| C
```

### 4.4 RNN Architectural Variants — Input/Output Cardinality

KTU often asks students to **classify a problem** by its I/O mapping. The four canonical RNN architectures are:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ONE_TO_ONE["One to One: Standard Feedforward"]
        A1["x"] --> B1["RNN"] --> C1["y"]
    end
    subgraph ONE_TO_MANY["One to Many: Image Captioning"]
        A2["x"] --> B2["RNN"] --> C2["y_1"]
        B2 --> C3["y_2"]
        B2 --> C4["y_T"]
    end
    subgraph MANY_TO_ONE["Many to One: Sentiment Classification"]
        A3["x_1"] --> B3["RNN"] --> C5["y"]
        A4["x_2"] --> B3
        AT["x_T"] --> B3
    end
    subgraph MANY_TO_MANY["Many to Many: Machine Translation"]
        A5["x_1"] --> B4["RNN1"] --> C6["h1_1"]
        A6["x_2"] --> B4 --> C7["h1_2"]
        AT2["x_T"] --> B4 --> C8["h1_T"]
        C6 --> B5["RNN2"] --> C9["y_1"]
        C7 --> B5
        C8 --> B5
    end
```

| Architecture | Example Application | Input Length | Output Length |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **One-to-One** | Vanilla classification | 1 | 1 |
| **One-to-Many** | Image captioning, music generation | 1 | $T$ |
| **Many-to-One** | Sentiment analysis, ECG classification | $T$ | 1 |
| **Many-to-Many (sync)** | Video frame labelling, POS tagging | $T$ | $T$ |
| **Many-to-Many (async)** | Machine translation, speech-to-text | $T_x$ | $T_y$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define a Recurrent Neural Network. How does it differ from a standard feedforward neural network when processing sequential data?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **RNN** is a neural network architecture containing recurrent connections that allow information to persist across time steps via a **hidden state** $h_t$, which is recursively updated as $h_t = \phi_h(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$.

**Differences from feedforward networks (3 key points):**
1. **Recurrent connections**: RNNs have feedback loops from $h_{t-1}$ to $h_t$; feedforward networks have no cycles.
2. **Parameter sharing across time**: RNNs use the same $W_{xh}$, $W_{hh}$, $W_{hy}$ at every time step; feedforward networks have independent parameters per layer.
3. **Variable-length input handling**: RNNs can natively process sequences of arbitrary length; feedforward networks require fixed input dimensionality.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs? State the mathematical condition that causes it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **vanishing gradient problem** in RNNs refers to the exponential decay of gradients during backpropagation through time, which prevents the network from learning **long-range dependencies** in sequences.

**Mathematical condition:** The gradient of $h_t$ with respect to $h_k$ (for $k \ll t$) is:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} W_{hh}^{\top}\, \text{diag}(\phi_h'(a_i))$$

Taking the spectral norm:

$$\left\| \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \right\|_2 \leq \Vert W_{hh} \Vert_2^{\,t-k} \cdot \max \vert \phi_h' \vert^{\,t-k}$$

When $\Vert W_{hh} \Vert_2 \cdot \max \vert \phi_h' \vert < 1$, the product shrinks **exponentially** with the time gap $t-k$, making early-time-step gradients negligibly small. As a result, weights $W_{xh}$ and $W_{hh}$ receive virtually no learning signal from distant past inputs, and the RNN fails to capture long-term context.

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the forward pass equations for a simple Recurrent Neural Network with input $x_t$, hidden state $h_t$, and output $y_t$. Clearly state the role of weight matrices $W_{xh}$, $W_{hh}$, and $W_{hy}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) for RNNs. Derive the expression for $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}}$ in terms of the hidden states and error signals. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Forward Pass Derivation (7 marks):**

The RNN at each time step $t$ takes two inputs:
- The current input vector $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_x}$
- The previous hidden state $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$

**Step 1:** Compute the pre-activation $a_t$ as a linear combination (1 mark):

$$a_t = W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h$$

**Step 2:** Apply non-linearity $\phi_h$ (typically $\tanh$) to get the new hidden state (2 marks):

$$h_t = \phi_h(a_t) = \phi_h(W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the output via the hidden-to-output projection (2 marks):

$$o_t = W_{hy}\, h_t + b_y, \qquad \hat{y}_t = \phi_y(o_t)$$

**Step 4:** Role of weight matrices (2 marks):
- $W_{xh}$: projects the current input into hidden-space.
- $W_{hh}$: projects the previous hidden state into the current hidden-space — this is the **recurrent** weight.
- $W_{hy}$: projects the hidden state into output-space.

Initialisation: $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$ (commonly).

---

**(b) BPTT Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total loss over sequence of length $T$ (1 mark):**

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)$$

**Step 2 — Define the back-propagated error $\delta_t$ at the hidden state (2 marks):**

$$\delta_t^{(h)} = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} = W_{hy}^{\top}\, \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial o_t} + W_{hh}^{\top}\, \text{diag}(\phi_h'(a_{t+1}))\, \delta_{t+1}^{(h)}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $W_{hh}$ (2 marks):**

Since $W_{hh}$ appears in $a_t$ at **every** time step, the chain rule gives:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial a_t} \cdot \frac{\partial a_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^{(h)} \cdot \text{diag}(\phi_h'(a_t)) \cdot h_{t-1}^{\top}$$

**Step 4 — Final BPTT update rule and intuition (2 marks):**

$$W_{hh} \leftarrow W_{hh} - \eta \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t^{(h)} \cdot \text{diag}(\phi_h'(a_t)) \cdot h_{t-1}^{\top}$$

The recursion in $\delta_t$ is what causes vanishing/exploding gradients: $\delta_t$ depends on $\delta_{t+1}$, which depends on $\delta_{t+2}$, … ad infinitum, with each step multiplied by $W_{hh}^{\top}$ and the activation derivative.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **folded** and **unfolded** representations of an RNN. State the advantages of unrolling. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **vanishing and exploding gradient** problems in deep RNNs. Show mathematically how the magnitude of the gradient $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k}$ behaves as a function of the time gap $t-k$. List two architectural solutions. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Folded vs Unfolded RNN (7 marks):**

**Folded Representation (3 marks):**
- A single RNN cell is drawn as one block with a **self-loop** arrow.
- The self-loop represents the recurrence: $h_t$ depends on $h_{t-1}$ via the same weight matrix $W_{hh}$.
- Emphasises **parameter sharing** and the **recurrent** nature.

**Unfolded Representation (2 marks):**
- The RNN is "unrolled" along the time axis, creating one cell per time step $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$.
- Each cell is connected to the next via a horizontal arrow carrying the hidden state.
- Emphasises the **feedforward-equivalent** computation graph used in BPTT.

**Advantages of Unrolling (2 marks):**
1. Makes the **dependency structure** of BPTT explicit, allowing gradient flow to be analysed.
2. Reveals the **shared-parameter** structure — the same $W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ are reused at every unrolled cell, which is the key to generalisation across sequence positions.

---

**(b) Vanishing & Exploding Gradients (7 marks):**

**Mathematical Derivation (4 marks):**

By the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} W_{hh}^{\top}\, \text{diag}(\phi_h'(a_i))$$

Taking the spectral norm (assuming $\Vert W_{hh} \Vert_2 = \rho$ and $\max \vert \phi_h' \vert = \gamma$):

$$\left\| \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \right\|_2 \leq (\rho \cdot \gamma)^{t-k}$$

**Behaviour Analysis (2 marks):**
- **Exploding gradient**: If $\rho \cdot \gamma > 1$, the gradient grows exponentially with $t-k$, leading to numerical overflow and unstable training.
- **Vanishing gradient**: If $\rho \cdot \gamma < 1$, the gradient shrinks exponentially, making early-time-step weights essentially untrainable. Long-range dependencies become unlearnable.

**Two Architectural Solutions (1 mark):**
1. **LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)**: Uses gating mechanisms (input, forget, output gates) and a separate cell state to preserve long-term gradients.
2. **GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)**: Simplified gating with reset and update gates, mitigating vanishing gradients with fewer parameters.
3. *(Bonus: gradient clipping for exploding gradients, orthogonal weight initialisation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to **sum** gradients across all time steps when writing BPTT — the weight $W_{hh}$ is *shared*, so every time step contributes. Writing $\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W_{hh}}$ alone loses **2–3 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Conflating the **vanishing gradient** problem (vanishing in $W_{hh}$ gradient) with vanishing activations. They are distinct. The gradient vanishes because of the **product of Jacobian matrices** along the chain, not because activations are small.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing the **folded** and **unfolded** views and drawing the same number of weight matrices in both. The unrolled view must show **shared** weights — explicitly state "same $W$" with an annotation.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting the **bias terms** $b_h$ and $b_y$ in the forward pass derivation. Examiners often allocate 1 mark specifically for bias terms.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **RNN Definition**: A neural network with recurrent connections maintaining a hidden state $h_t$ that evolves as $h_t = \phi_h(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$.
- [x] **Three weight matrices**: $W_{xh}$ (input-to-hidden), $W_{hh}$ (hidden-to-hidden, the recurrent one), $W_{hy}$ (hidden-to-output).
- [x] **Parameter sharing**: The same $W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ are used at every time step — a key reason RNNs generalise across sequence positions.
- [x] **Folded vs Unfolded**: Folded = single cell with self-loop. Unfolded = cells replicated for each time step with shared weights.
- [x] **BPTT**: Differentiate the total loss $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$ through the unrolled graph; sum gradients from all time steps for shared weights.
- [x] **Vanishing gradient cause**: $\left\| \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \right\| \leq (\Vert W_{hh} \Vert \cdot \max \vert \phi_h' \vert)^{t-k}$ — exponential decay in the time gap.
- [x] **Exploding gradient fix**: Gradient clipping (rescale gradient when norm exceeds a threshold).
- [x] **Vanishing gradient fix**: LSTM, GRU, orthogonal weight initialisation, layer normalisation.
- [x] **Architectural variants**: One-to-One, One-to-Many, Many-to-One, Many-to-Many (sync/async) — match I/O cardinality to the task.
- [x] **KTU 2024 hot topics**: Forward pass derivation, BPTT derivation with explicit summation, vanishing gradient proof, I/O variant diagrams.
- [x] **Activation defaults**: $\tanh$ for hidden state, $\text{softmax}$ for classification output, identity for regression output.
- [x] **Common exam trap**: Forgetting to sum across time steps in BPTT. Always write $\sum_{t=1}^{T}$ explicitly.
- [x] **Number of parameters**: $P = d_h(d_x + d_h + d_y) + d_h + d_y$ for a single-layer vanilla RNN.
- [x] **Initial hidden state**: $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$ is standard, but can also be a learnable parameter.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** is a specialized type of Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) architecture introduced by *Hochreiter and Schmidhuber* capable of learning **long-term dependencies** in sequential data. It achieves this by introducing a **memory cell** regulated by three multiplicative **gating mechanisms** (Forget, Input, Output) that control the flow of information across time steps.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **Generative AI (PEADT748)**, LSTM is classified as a **sequential generative / conditional modeling primitive** that underpins many modern architectures such as sequence-to-sequence translation, music generation, and early text generation systems (precursors to Transformer-based LLMs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** LSTMs are taught as a remedy to the **vanishing/exploding gradient problem** in vanilla RNNs, and as the foundational concept for understanding gated recurrent units (GRUs) and the encoder–decoder paradigm.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Courier with Three Locks

Imagine a **reliable courier** (the LSTM cell) entrusted with carrying a sealed **diary** (the **cell state**, $C_t$) across many days. The diary contains long-term information, but not every page is relevant today. The courier has **three locked compartments (gates)** that decide, at each new day (time step $t$):

| Gate | Courier's Decision | Mathematical Role |
|------|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Forget Gate ($f_t$)** | Which old diary pages to rip out? | Erases irrelevant past memory |
| **Input Gate ($i_t$)** | Which new notes to paste in? | Writes new relevant information |
| **Output Gate ($o_t$)** | What to read aloud from today's diary? | Exposes filtered memory to next layer |

This selective, **learned** memory management is precisely what allows LSTMs to remember information across **hundreds of time steps** — something vanilla RNNs cannot do.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core takeaway:** The LSTM cell maintains a separate **cell state highway** $C_t$ that runs across time steps with minimal interference, while the gates act as **continuous, differentiable valves** (values in $[0,1]$ via the **sigmoid function**).

## 1.3 Why LSTM Exists — The Motivation

Vanilla RNNs update their hidden state using:
$$h_t = \tanh(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t + b)$$

When backpropagating through time (BPTT), gradients are repeatedly multiplied by $W_h$. If $\vert W_h \vert < 1$, gradients **vanish** exponentially; if $\vert W_h \vert > 1$, they **explode**. LSTMs solve vanishing gradients via an **additive cell-state update** (the $+$ operator below), which preserves gradient magnitude over long sequences.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Expectation:** Always mention "LSTM mitigates vanishing gradient via additive cell-state updates and gated control" in any 14-mark LSTM question. This single sentence guarantees partial credit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of an LSTM Cell

At every time step $t$, the LSTM receives two inputs:
- $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ — current input vector
- $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ — previous hidden state
- $C_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ — previous cell state

It produces two outputs:
- $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ — current hidden state
- $C_t \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ — current cell state

The computation proceeds in **four sequential logical stages**:

### Stage 1 — Forget Gate: Decide What to Erase
- Computes a vector $f_t$ of values in $[0,1]$.
- $f_t \approx 1$ → "keep this memory"; $f_t \approx 0$ → "forget it completely".
- Operates element-wise on the previous cell state $C_{t-1}$.

### Stage 2 — Input Gate: Decide What New Info to Store
- A **sigmoid** layer $i_t$ decides *which values to update*.
- A **tanh** layer $\tilde{C}_t$ creates a vector of *candidate new values* in $[-1,1]$.
- Their element-wise product $i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ is the *new information payload*.

### Stage 3 — Cell State Update: The Memory Highway
- The previous cell state is **forgotten selectively**: $f_t \odot C_{t-1}$.
- New information is **added selectively**: $+ \; i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$.
- The **additive** structure (rather than multiplicative) is the *crucial* vanishing-gradient fix.

### Stage 4 — Output Gate: Decide What to Expose
- A sigmoid $o_t$ filters which parts of the (tanh-compressed) cell state become the visible hidden state $h_t$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for Exams:** Memorize the **FICO** mnemonic — **F**orget, **I**nput, **C**ell update, **O**utput.

| # | Component | Equation | Activation | Output Range |
|---|-----------|----------|------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Forget Gate** | $f_t = \sigma\!\left(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f\right)$ | Sigmoid ($\sigma$) | $[0,1]$ |
| 2 | **Input Gate** | $i_t = \sigma\!\left(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i\right)$ | Sigmoid ($\sigma$) | $[0,1]$ |
| 3 | **Candidate Cell** | $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh\!\left(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C\right)$ | Tanh | $[-1,1]$ |
| 4 | **Cell State Update** | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} \;+\; i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | Linear (additive) | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| 5 | **Output Gate** | $o_t = \sigma\!\left(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o\right)$ | Sigmoid ($\sigma$) | $[0,1]$ |
| 6 | **Hidden State** | $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | Tanh $\odot$ Sigmoid | $\mathbb{R}$ |

**Notation conventions used in the KTU Formula Sheet:**

- $[h_{t-1}, x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{h+d}$ denotes row-wise **concatenation** of the two vectors.
- $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times (h+d)}$ are the **learnable weight matrices**.
- $b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ are **bias vectors** (initialized to zero; the forget-gate bias is sometimes initialized to **1** to encourage retention early in training).
- $\odot$ denotes the **Hadamard (element-wise) product**.
- $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ is the logistic sigmoid; $\tanh(z) = \dfrac{e^{z}-e^{-z}}{e^{z}+e^{-z}}$.
- The **standard sigmoid and tanh** are: $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ and $\tanh(z) = \frac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}$.

## 2.3 Total Parameter Count (Frequently Asked in KTU)

For an LSTM with input dimension $d$ and hidden dimension $h$, the total number of **trainable parameters** is:

$$\text{Params} \;=\; 4 \times \left[(h + d) \times h + h\right] \;=\; 4h^{2} + 4hd + 4h$$

> The factor of **4** arises because LSTM has 4 weight matrices (one per gate + candidate) and 4 bias vectors. This is **4× more parameters** than a vanilla RNN of the same hidden size — a frequent KTU Part-A question.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

LSTMs are deployed wherever **temporal context** matters:

- **Time-series forecasting** — energy load prediction, stock price modeling, weather nowcasting.
- **Natural Language Processing** — machine translation (pre-Transformer era), named-entity recognition, sentiment analysis.
- **Speech and audio** — speech recognition acoustic models, music generation (used in early Magenta/Google projects).
- **Industrial anomaly detection** — sensor stream monitoring in IoT pipelines.
- **Generative AI precursors** — text generation models (char-RNN, word-LSTM) and image captioning, all of which are direct KTU syllabus ancestors of modern Transformer-based generators.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the LSTM Forward Pass

We derive the six equations of the LSTM from the principle of **gated additive memory**. The cell at time $t$ must:
1. Combine inputs from $x_t$ and $h_{t-1}$ into a single linear projection.
2. Apply nonlinear activations to produce gating values.
3. Use element-wise products to enforce selective memory.

### Step 1 — Concatenate Previous Hidden State and Current Input
We form a single vector by horizontal concatenation:

$$z_t \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} h_{t-1} \\ x_t \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{h+d}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Four Pre-Activations
A single concatenated projection is most efficiently implemented as two separate matrix multiplications added together:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{net}_f &= W_{fh}\, h_{t-1} \;+\; W_{fx}\, x_t \;+\; b_f \\
\text{net}_i &= W_{ih}\, h_{t-1} \;+\; W_{ix}\, x_t \;+\; b_i \\
\text{net}_C &= W_{Ch}\, h_{t-1} \;+\; W_{Cx}\, x_t \;+\; b_C \\
\text{net}_o &= W_{oh}\, h_{t-1} \;+\; W_{ox}\, x_t \;+\; b_o
\end{aligned}
$$

Each weight matrix $W_{(\cdot)h} \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times h}$ and $W_{(\cdot)x} \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times d}$. Equivalently, we may stack them as a single matrix $W_f = [W_{fh} \mid W_{fx}] \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times (h+d)}$, recovering the compact form used in the formula sheet:

$$\text{net}_f = W_f\,[h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f$$

### Step 3 — Apply Nonlinearities to Produce the Gates
The **sigmoid** squashes the pre-activations into the gate space $[0,1]$, and **tanh** produces the candidate cell values in $[-1,1]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_t &= \sigma(\text{net}_f) \\
i_t &= \sigma(\text{net}_i) \\
\tilde{C}_t &= \tanh(\text{net}_C) \\
o_t &= \sigma(\text{net}_o)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic row:** Sigmoid is the "gate" function because it answers *"what fraction should pass through?"* with a value in $(0,1)$. Tanh is used for the candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ because, unlike sigmoid, it is **zero-centered**, which keeps the cell state distribution balanced during training.

### Step 4 — Update the Cell State (The Core LSTM Innovation)
The previous cell state is **forgotten** by element-wise multiplication with $f_t$, and new information is **added** via element-wise multiplication of $i_t$ and $\tilde{C}_t$:

$$C_t \;=\; f_t \odot C_{t-1} \;+\; i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

**Logic row:** The **additive** term is the key innovation. When we differentiate $C_t$ with respect to $C_{t-1}$ during backpropagation, we get:

$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} \;=\; f_t$$

Because $f_t = \sigma(\text{net}_f)$ and $\sigma$ is bounded in $(0,1)$, this gradient is **bounded and non-zero** — preventing the vanishing-gradient pathology of vanilla RNNs. This is the **gradient highway**.

### Step 5 — Compute the New Hidden State
The cell state is first squashed via $\tanh$ to lie in $[-1,1]$, then filtered element-wise by the output gate:

$$h_t \;=\; o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

**Logic row:** $\tanh(C_t)$ produces the "raw memory content," and $o_t$ decides *which slices* of that content are exposed to the rest of the network (the next time step and the next layer).

### Step 6 — Summary of the Forward Pass
Collecting all six equations, the **complete LSTM forward pass** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_t &= \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f) \\
i_t &= \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i) \\
\tilde{C}_t &= \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C) \\
C_t &= f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \\
o_t &= \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o) \\
h_t &= o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — LSTM vs Vanilla RNN

| Property | Vanilla RNN | LSTM |
|----------|-------------|------|
| Hidden state update | $h_t = \tanh(W h_{t-1} + U x_t + b)$ | $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ |
| Memory mechanism | Single multiplicative $W$ on $h_{t-1}$ | Additive cell-state update $C_t = f_t C_{t-1} + i_t \tilde{C}_t$ |
| Gradient behavior | Vanishes/explodes over long sequences | Bounded by forget gate, retains long-term signal |
| Parameters per layer | $h(h+d) + h$ | $4[h(h+d) + h]$ |
| Effective memory horizon | $\approx 5$–$10$ steps | Hundreds of steps |
| Information filtering | None (all info passes through) | Three learned gates |
| Computational cost (per step) | $1$ matmul | $4$ matmuls (≈ 4× slower) |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Mini-Example (Hand-Computable)

Let hidden size $h=1$, input size $d=1$. Suppose at time $t$ we have:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{t-1} &= 0.5, \quad x_t = 0.8, \quad C_{t-1} = 1.2 \\
W_f &= 0.3, \quad W_i = 0.2, \quad W_C = 0.4, \quad W_o = 0.25 \\
b_f &= 0.1, \quad b_i = 0.0, \quad b_C = 0.0, \quad b_o = 0.05
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step A — Forget gate pre-activation:**
$$\text{net}_f = 0.3(0.5) + 0.3(0.8) + 0.1 = 0.15 + 0.24 + 0.1 = 0.49$$

**Step B — Forget gate activation:**
$$f_t = \sigma(0.49) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.49}} \approx 0.620$$

**Step C — Input gate pre-activation:**
$$\text{net}_i = 0.2(0.5) + 0.2(0.8) + 0.0 = 0.10 + 0.16 = 0.26$$

**Step D — Input gate activation:**
$$i_t = \sigma(0.26) \approx 0.565$$

**Step E — Candidate cell value:**
$$\text{net}_C = 0.4(0.5) + 0.4(0.8) + 0.0 = 0.52$$

$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(0.52) \approx 0.482$$

**Step F — New cell state:**
$$C_t = (0.620)(1.2) + (0.565)(0.482) = 0.744 + 0.272 = 1.016$$

**Step G — Output gate:**
$$\text{net}_o = 0.25(0.5) + 0.25(0.8) + 0.05 = 0.325 + 0.05 = 0.375$$

$$o_t = \sigma(0.375) \approx 0.593$$

**Step H — New hidden state:**
$$h_t = 0.593 \cdot \tanh(1.016) = 0.593 \cdot 0.770 \approx 0.457$$

> This numerical sketch is the *type* of computation students should be able to do for short KTU Part-A numerical tie-ins. The key insight: **forget gate retained $\approx 62\%$ of past memory**, and the cell state moved from $1.2 \to 1.016$, demonstrating controlled update.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Single LSTM Cell

The diagram below is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing the data path through the three gates and the cell-state highway. This is the *exact* figure students are expected to draw or describe in KTU 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xt["Input x sub t"]:::input
    hprev["Previous Hidden h sub t-1"]:::input
    cprev["Previous Cell State C sub t-1"]:::input

    concat["Concatenation h sub t-1 , x sub t"]:::merge
    fgate["Forget Gate Sigma Net f"]:::gate
    igate["Input Gate Sigma Net i"]:::gate
    cgate["Candidate Tanh Net C"]:::gate
    ogate["Output Gate Sigma Net o"]:::gate

    fmul["Hadamard Product f t x C sub t-1"]:::merge
    imul["Hadamard Product i t x C tilde sub t"]:::merge
    csum["Additive Sum plus"]:::merge
    cnew["New Cell State C sub t"]:::output

    tanhC["Tanh of C sub t"]:::merge
    omul["Hadamard Product o t x tanh C t"]:::merge
    hnew["New Hidden State h sub t"]:::output

    xt --> concat
    hprev --> concat
    concat --> fgate
    concat --> igate
    concat --> cgate
    concat --> ogate

    cprev --> fmul
    fgate --> fmul
    fmul --> csum

    igate --> imul
    cgate --> imul
    imul --> csum

    csum --> cnew
    cnew --> tanhC
    tanhC --> omul
    ogate --> omul
    omul --> hnew
    cnew --> hprevNext["Cell State to Next Step"]:::output

    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef gate fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef merge fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Unrolled LSTM Through Time

This shows how the **cell-state highway** propagates almost unchanged across time steps, while the hidden state changes more rapidly. This visual captures *why* LSTMs handle long sequences well.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x0["x sub 0"]:::input --> cellA["LSTM Cell t=0"]:::gate
    cellA --> h0["h sub 0"]:::output
    cellA --> c0["C sub 0"]:::output

    x1["x sub 1"]:::input --> cellB["LSTM Cell t=1"]:::gate
    h0 --> cellB
    c0 --> cellB
    cellB --> h1["h sub 1"]:::output
    cellB --> c1["C sub 1"]:::output

    x2["x sub 2"]:::input --> cellC["LSTM Cell t=2"]:::gate
    h1 --> cellC
    c1 --> cellC
    cellC --> h2["h sub 2"]:::output
    cellC --> c2["C sub 2"]:::output

    x3["x sub 3"]:::input --> cellD["LSTM Cell t=3"]:::gate
    h2 --> cellD
    c2 --> cellD
    cellD --> h3["h sub 3"]:::output
    cellD --> c3["C sub 3"]:::output

    c0 -. Highway .-> c1
    c1 -. Highway .-> c2
    c2 -. Highway .-> c3

    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef gate fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Description (for textual answers)

When describing the architecture in writing (which is what KTU examiners prefer when diagrams are not allowed):

1. The **bottom horizontal line** carrying $C_{t-1} \to C_t$ is the *cell-state highway*, regulated by the forget gate.
2. The **three sigmoid gates** (forget, input, output) sit above the highway and act as learned valves.
3. The **tanh candidate layer** generates new memory content.
4. The **output** $h_t$ branches upward to the next layer and rightward to the next time step.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — 3 Marks
**Q: Define LSTM. Why is it preferred over a vanilla RNN for sequence modeling?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) is a type of recurrent neural network introduced by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber that uses **gating mechanisms** to control information flow. **(1 mark)**

It maintains a separate **cell state** $C_t$ updated additively as $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$, providing a **gradient highway** that mitigates the **vanishing-gradient problem** in vanilla RNNs. **(1 mark)**

Because of this additive memory structure and the three learned gates (forget, input, output), LSTM can capture **long-term dependencies** spanning hundreds of time steps, making it suitable for tasks such as language modeling, speech recognition, and time-series forecasting. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2 — 3 Marks
**Q: List and briefly state the function of the three gates in an LSTM cell.**
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Forget Gate ($f_t$):** Decides what information to discard from the previous cell state $C_{t-1}$. Output in $[0,1]$; values near 0 erase, values near 1 retain. **(1 mark)**
2. **Input Gate ($i_t$):** Decides which new candidate values $\tilde{C}_t$ should be added to the cell state. Controls *what new information* is stored. **(1 mark)**
3. **Output Gate ($o_t$):** Decides what part of the (tanh-compressed) cell state becomes the next hidden state $h_t$. Controls *what is exposed* to subsequent layers/time steps. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question is 14 marks with two sub-parts: typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. **Internal choice** means students answer *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — LSTM Architecture & Forward Pass

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
**Explain the architecture of an LSTM cell with a neat diagram. How does the cell state differ from the hidden state?**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (5 marks):**
An LSTM cell receives $x_t$ (current input), $h_{t-1}$ (previous hidden state), and $C_{t-1}$ (previous cell state). It contains:

- A **forget gate** sigmoid layer producing $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$.
- An **input gate** sigmoid layer producing $i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$.
- A **candidate** tanh layer producing $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$.
- An **output gate** sigmoid layer producing $o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$.

The cell state is updated as $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ (additive, gradient-preserving). The hidden state is $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ (filtered exposure).

A block-level schematic showing the cell-state highway running horizontally with three gates sitting above it and the $h_t$ output branching off is the standard diagram. (Refer to Section 4.1 above.)

**Difference between cell state and hidden state (2 marks):**
- **Cell state $C_t$**: Internal long-term memory, runs across time with additive updates; not directly exposed to other layers.
- **Hidden state $h_t$**: Filtered, exposed representation used by subsequent layers and time steps; recomputed at every step from the cell state.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**
**Derive the forward-pass equations of an LSTM. Show how the cell-state update addresses the vanishing-gradient problem.**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from concatenated input $z_t = [h_{t-1}, x_t]$, the four pre-activations are:
$\text{net}_f = W_f z_t + b_f$, $\text{net}_i = W_i z_t + b_i$, $\text{net}_C = W_C z_t + b_C$, $\text{net}_o = W_o z_t + b_o$. **(2 marks)**

Applying activations:
$f_t = \sigma(\text{net}_f)$, $i_t = \sigma(\text{net}_i)$, $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(\text{net}_C)$, $o_t = \sigma(\text{net}_o)$. **(1 mark)**

Cell state and hidden state:
$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ and $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$. **(1 mark)**

**Vanishing-gradient analysis (3 marks):**
During BPTT, the gradient of the loss $L$ at time $t$ with respect to $C_{t-1}$ is:

$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = f_t = \sigma(\text{net}_f)$$

Since $f_t \in (0, 1)$ but bounded away from zero in expectation, the gradient is **not repeatedly multiplied by a small number** as in vanilla RNNs (where $\partial h_t / \partial h_{t-1} = \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2) \cdot W_h$, and $\vert W_h \vert < 1$ leads to exponential decay). The **additive** term $i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ creates a residual-like connection analogous to ResNet, allowing gradients to flow through hundreds of steps.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — LSTM Mathematical Rigor & Variants

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
**For an LSTM with input dimension $d=2$ and hidden dimension $h=3$, compute the total number of trainable parameters. Show all calculations.**

**Model Solution:**

The LSTM has **four** weight matrices, each of size $h \times (h+d) = 3 \times 5 = 15$, and **four** bias vectors, each of size $h = 3$. **(2 marks)**

Parameters per gate:
- Forget: $15 + 3 = 18$
- Input: $15 + 3 = 18$
- Candidate: $15 + 3 = 18$
- Output: $15 + 3 = 18$

Total per-cell: $4 \times 18 = 72$ parameters. **(3 marks)**

Verification using the formula: $4h^2 + 4hd + 4h = 4(9) + 4(6) + 4(3) = 36 + 24 + 12 = 72$ parameters. **(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A *very* common error is forgetting the bias terms or counting only one weight matrix. KTU examiners often set 1 mark aside for *correctly including* the biases $b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o$. Always state "weights + biases" explicitly.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**
**Compare LSTM with GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit). Discuss the trade-offs in terms of parameters, gates, and typical use cases.**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | LSTM | GRU |
|--------|------|-----|
| Gates | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (reset, update) |
| Cell state | Separate $C_t$ and $h_t$ | Single hidden state $h_t$ |
| Parameters per cell | $4h(h+d) + 4h$ | $3h(h+d) + 3h$ |
| Computational cost | Higher | ~25% lower |
| Long-term memory | Stronger (dedicated cell state) | Slightly weaker |
| When to prefer | Long sequences, fine-grained memory | Short sequences, faster training |

**LSTM** has a dedicated cell-state highway making it better for tasks needing **fine-grained memory control** (e.g., machine translation, music generation, document-level NLP). **(3 marks)**

**GRU** merges cell state and hidden state, using an **update gate** to interpolate between previous and candidate hidden states, and a **reset gate** to control how much past information to ignore. It is faster and sometimes preferable on small datasets or short sequences. **(3 marks)**

For Generative AI applications like **text generation and speech synthesis**, LSTMs remain the gated recurrent benchmark; GRUs are favored when **latency** or **model size** is critical (e.g., on-device inference). **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Warning:**
> - **Don't confuse** the GRU update gate with the LSTM forget gate — they are *not* equivalent.
> - **Don't** claim LSTM is "always better" — GRU often matches LSTM on shorter tasks with less compute.
> - **Always** state the parameter formula and compute it numerically for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **LSTM = RNN + Cell State + 3 Gates.** It is a *gated* recurrent network.
- [x] **FICO Mnemonic** — Forget → Input → Cell update → Output.
- [x] **Six core equations** must be memorized verbatim (see Formula Sheet in Section 2.2).
- [x] **Vanishing gradient fix** comes from the **additive** cell-state update and the forget-gate gradient highway.
- [x] **Sigmoid** is for gates (values in $[0,1]$); **tanh** is for cell-state candidates and the hidden-state squashing.
- [x] **Parameters:** $4h(h+d) + 4h$ for one LSTM cell — always state *weights + biases* explicitly.
- [x] **Cell state $C_t$** is the *internal* long-term memory; **hidden state $h_t$** is the *exposed* representation.
- [x] **Forget-gate bias initialization trick:** $b_f = 1$ at initialization encourages retention early in training.
- [x] **GRU vs LSTM:** GRU is simpler (2 gates, single state), ~25% fewer parameters, comparable performance on short tasks.
- [x] **In Generative AI context:** LSTM is the *precursor* to Transformer-based generators; many early text-to-text, music, and caption-generation systems used stacked LSTMs.
- [x] **KTU 14-Mark Strategy:** Always include the diagram, all six equations, parameter count formula, and the vanishing-gradient analysis in your answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture of LSTM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Architecture of LSTM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** is a specialized variant of the **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** architecture, introduced by **Sepp Hochreiter and Jürgen Schmidhuber (1997)**, engineered specifically to mitigate the **vanishing and exploding gradient problems** that plague conventional RNNs when learning long-range temporal dependencies in sequential data.

LSTM introduces a **memory cell** governed by a **Constant Error Carousel (CEC)** mechanism, regulated by three multiplicative **gating units** — the **Forget Gate**, **Input Gate**, and **Output Gate** — that collectively control the flow, retention, and emission of information across arbitrary time intervals.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, LSTM is treated as a foundational generative/sequence-modeling block that underpins downstream architectures such as **Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq)** models, **Encoder-Decoder** frameworks, and early **Transformer precursors**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Notebook" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Imagine you are a student preparing for exams over a six-month period.**

- You carry a **notebook (Cell State)** that stores the entire syllabus. Each chapter is either **kept, rewritten, or removed** based on how important it is for the final exam.
- Before every study session, you read a **summary of what you studied yesterday (Hidden State, hₜ₋₁)** and the **new topic of the day (Input, xₜ)**.
- A **Forget Gate** acts like a strict editor: *"Drop the chapters on Newtonian Mechanics; they won't appear on the Modern Physics exam."*
- An **Input Gate** acts like a careful writer: *"Add Quantum Tunneling notes — they are new and important."*
- An **Output Gate** acts like a presentable teacher: *"Today, only explain Photoelectric Effect to the class; keep the rest stored in the notebook."*

This is **exactly** how LSTM operates at every time step: selectively **forgetting**, **writing**, and **reading** from its long-term memory.

## 1.3 Key Architectural Highlights

> [!TIP]
> **Core Components of a Single LSTM Cell**
> - **Cell State (Cₜ)** — the long-term memory highway running through the entire sequence.
> - **Hidden State (hₜ)** — the short-term working output emitted at each time step.
> - **Forget Gate (fₜ)** — controls what to discard from the cell state.
> - **Input Gate (iₜ)** — controls what new information to write into the cell state.
> - **Candidate Cell (C̃ₜ)** — the new candidate values proposed for addition.
> - **Output Gate (oₜ)** — controls what portion of the cell state becomes the hidden output.

| Symbol | Component | Activation | Range |
|--------|-----------|------------|-------|
| σ | Sigmoid | Gate activations | **[0, 1]** |
| tanh | Hyperbolic tangent | Candidate/Output squashing | **[−1, 1]** |
| ⊙ | Element-wise (Hadamard) multiplication | Information gating | **N/A** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic Flow

The LSTM cell executes **four sequential sub-operations** at every time step *t*. Each step is logically isolated to allow fine-grained control over the memory pipeline.

### Step 1 — Forget Gate Operation
- **Why?** Decide which legacy information from the previous cell state is no longer relevant.
- **How?** Apply a sigmoid layer to the concatenation of the previous hidden state and current input.
- **Output Range:** A value between **0** (completely forget) and **1** (completely retain).

### Step 2 — Input Gate Operation + Candidate Generation
- **Why?** Determine which new candidate values should be considered for addition to the cell state.
- **How?** Two parallel layers — a sigmoid (**iₜ**) for the "importance" filter and a **tanh** (**C̃ₜ**) for the "value" proposal.

### Step 3 — Cell State Update (The Core Memory Write)
- **Why?** Combine the **retained** old memory with the **filtered** new memory.
- **How?** A linear interpolation governed by two gates.

### Step 4 — Output Gate Operation
- **Why?** Determine which parts of the updated cell state should be exposed as the next hidden state.
- **How?** A sigmoid filter applied to the squashed cell state.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **canonical LSTM forward-pass equation set** as per the original Hochreiter & Schmidhuber formulation. These are **board-exam mandatory**.

| Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Output Range | Purpose |
|---------------|-------------------|--------------|---------|
| Forget Gate | $f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | [0, 1] | Discard irrelevant memory |
| Input Gate | $i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ | [0, 1] | Permit new memory intake |
| Candidate Cell | $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ | [−1, 1] | Propose new memory values |
| Cell State Update | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | [−1, 1] | Write to long-term memory |
| Output Gate | $o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ | [0, 1] | Filter visible output |
| Hidden State Output | $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | [−1, 1] | Emit short-term output |

### Notation Legend

- $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ — Input vector at time step *t*
- $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ — Previous hidden state (dimension **h**)
- $C_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ — Previous cell state
- $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times (h+d)}$ — Learnable weight matrices
- $b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ — Learnable bias vectors
- $\sigma$ — Logistic sigmoid function: $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$
- $\tanh$ — Hyperbolic tangent function: $\tanh(z) = \frac{e^z - e^{-z}}{e^z + e^{-z}}$
- $\odot$ — Hadamard (element-wise) product

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where LSTMs are deployed in industry and research:**
> - **Natural Language Processing (NLP):** Machine translation, sentiment analysis, text generation.
> - **Speech Recognition:** Acoustic modeling in systems like Google Speech-to-Text.
> - **Time-Series Forecasting:** Stock price prediction, weather modeling, energy load forecasting.
> - **Music & Generative AI:** Melody generation, MIDI sequence modeling (pre-Transformer era).
> - **Medical Signal Processing:** ECG/EEG anomaly detection, patient vital sign prediction.

The KTU 2024 Generative AI syllabus places LSTM as the **conceptual predecessor** to modern Transformer-based generative models, making its mastery critical for understanding why attention mechanisms eventually replaced recurrence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Forward-Pass Equations

The LSTM forward pass is a **composite gating mechanism**. We now derive each component explicitly, showing how the cell state and hidden state evolve over time.

### 3.1.1 Concatenation Step (Pre-Gate)

At the entry of every gate, the previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ and the current input $x_t$ are concatenated into a single vector.

$$z_t = [h_{t-1}, x_t]$$

This concatenation produces a vector of dimension $(h + d)$, which is then linearly transformed and squashed by the appropriate activation.

### 3.1.2 Forget Gate Derivation

**Step 1 — Linear transformation:**
$$z^f_t = W_f \cdot z_t + b_f$$

**Step 2 — Sigmoid squashing (maps to [0, 1] probability):**
$$f_t = \sigma(z^f_t) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)}}$$

**Interpretation:** $f_t$ is element-wise; a value of **0** erases that memory component, a value of **1** preserves it entirely.

### 3.1.3 Input Gate & Candidate Cell Derivation

**Step 1 — Input gate linear transform + sigmoid:**
$$i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$$

**Step 2 — Candidate cell linear transform + tanh:**
$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$$

**Interpretation:** $i_t$ acts as a **filter** that decides *how much* of each candidate to admit, while $\tilde{C}_t$ provides the *raw candidate values* bounded within [−1, 1].

### 3.1.4 Cell State Update Derivation (The Memory Write)

The new cell state is computed as a **gated linear interpolation** between the previous cell state and the candidate cell:

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**

- $f_t \odot C_{t-1}$ — **Forgetting term**: scaled old memory.
- $i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ — **Writing term**: gated new memory.
- Sum — The combined new cell state.

This equation is the **mathematical heart of LSTM** — it provides an **additive gradient path** that prevents the vanishing gradient issue, because the gradient of $C_t$ with respect to $C_{t-1}$ reduces to $f_t$, which is bounded but **non-vanishing** when properly initialized.

### 3.1.5 Output Gate & Hidden State Derivation

**Step 1 — Output gate linear transform + sigmoid:**
$$o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$$

**Step 2 — Hidden state output:**
$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

**Interpretation:** The cell state is squashed via $\tanh$ to bound it within [−1, 1], and then filtered by the output gate to produce the visible hidden state.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Gradient Flow Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix demonstrates **why LSTM solves vanishing gradients**, which is a frequent 14-mark question topic in KTU exams.

| Time Step Range | Gradient Path for Cell State | Behavior in Vanilla RNN | Behavior in LSTM |
|------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| $t$ to $t+1$ | $\frac{\partial C_{t+1}}{\partial C_t} = f_{t+1}$ | Scales by $\sigma'(W \cdot z) \cdot W$ (vanishes/explodes) | Scales by $f_{t+1} \in [0,1]$ (controlled decay) |
| $t$ to $t+k$ | Product $\prod_{j=1}^{k} f_{t+j}$ | Repeated matrix multiplications → vanishing/exploding | Forget gates can remain near 1 → gradient preserved |
| Long-range ($k \gg 1$) | Cumulative product | **Vanishing** (most likely) | **Preserved** if forget gates stay open |

**Key Insight:** If the forget gate activations $f_t$ are close to **1** for relevant memory paths, the gradient flows **unattenuated** across hundreds of time steps — solving the long-term dependency problem.

## 3.3 Parameter Count Derivation (Exam-Oriented)

For an LSTM cell with input dimension $d$ and hidden dimension $h$:

- **Each gate** has a weight matrix of shape $(h \times (h + d))$ and a bias of shape $h$.
- **Total gates:** 4 (forget, input, candidate, output).
- **Total parameters per cell:**

$$\text{Params} = 4 \times [h \times (h + d) + h] = 4h(h + d + 1)$$

For example, with $d = 128$ and $h = 256$:

$$\text{Params} = 4 \times 256 \times (256 + 128 + 1) = 4 \times 256 \times 385 = 394{,}240 \text{ parameters}$$

This 4× parameter overhead over vanilla RNN is the **price paid for memory persistence**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LSTM Cell Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional block-level architecture** of a single LSTM cell, showing data flow from input gates through to the hidden state output. All node identifiers are alphanumeric-prefixed to comply with Mermaid v10+ syntax safeguards.

```mermaid
graph LR
    Xt[INPUT XT]
    Ht1[HIDDEN STATE HT-1]
    Ct1[CELL STATE CT-1]
    
    Fgate[FORGET GATE FT]
    Igate[INPUT GATE IT]
    Ctilde[CANDIDATE CTILDE]
    Ogate[OUTPUT GATE OT]
    
    Mult1[FORGET MULTIPLY]
    Mult2[INPUT MULTIPLY]
    AddUp[CELL STATE ADDITION]
    Squash[TANH SQUASH]
    OutMult[OUTPUT MULTIPLY]
    
    Ct[CELL STATE CT]
    Ht[HIDDEN STATE HT]
    
    Xt --> Fgate
    Ht1 --> Fgate
    Xt --> Igate
    Ht1 --> Igate
    Xt --> Ctilde
    Ht1 --> Ctilde
    
    Ct1 --> Mult1
    Fgate --> Mult1
    
    Igate --> Mult2
    Ctilde --> Mult2
    
    Mult1 --> AddUp
    Mult2 --> AddUp
    
    AddUp --> Ct
    AddUp --> Squash
    
    Xt --> Ogate
    Ht1 --> Ogate
    
    Squash --> OutMult
    Ogate --> OutMult
    
    OutMult --> Ht
    Ht --> Ht1
    Ct --> Ct1
```

## 4.2 Architecture Description (Reading the Diagram)

> [!IMPORTANT]
