> **Reading Guide for the KTU Board Examiner:**

1. **Triple-Input Entry (Top-Left):** The cell receives three vectors at every time step — current input $x_t$, previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$, and previous cell state $C_{t-1}$.

2. **Gate Cluster (Top-Middle):** The three gating sigmoid units ($f_t$, $i_t$, $o_t$) and the candidate tanh ($\tilde{C}_t$) all consume the **same** concatenated input $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ but produce **different** outputs via their distinct weight matrices.

3. **Multiplicative Control (Middle):** The two Hadamard product nodes $\odot$ represent the **forget multiplication** and **input multiplication** — the only operations in LSTM that suppress or admit information.

4. **Cell State Pathway (Bottom Horizontal):** The cell state $C_t$ flows **horizontally** across time, modified only by additive and multiplicative gates — this is the **Constant Error Carousel (CEC)**.

5. **Hidden State Pathway (Bottom-Right):** The hidden state $h_t$ branches off to the next time step as the **short-term visible output**.

## 4.3 Peephole LSTM Variant (Advanced Note)

> [!TIP]
> **Beyond the Standard LSTM:** A common KTU advanced question involves **Peephole Connections**, introduced by Gers & Schmidhuber (2000), which allow gates to inspect the cell state directly:

- Forget Gate with peephole: $f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + v_f \odot C_{t-1} + b_f)$
- Input Gate with peephole: $i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + v_i \odot C_{t-1} + b_i)$
- Output Gate with peephole: $o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + v_o \odot C_t + b_o)$

This enables gates to make decisions based on the **current cell content**, not just the hidden state and input.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE) test **direct conceptual recall** and **precise definitions**. Answers must be concise, accurate, and avoid unnecessary elaboration.

### Question 1: Define the vanishing gradient problem. How does LSTM overcome it? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **vanishing gradient problem** occurs in deep and recurrent neural networks when error gradients propagated via backpropagation shrink exponentially through repeated multiplication of Jacobian matrices, causing weights in early layers/time steps to receive negligible updates and preventing learning of long-range dependencies.

LSTM overcomes this through its **additive cell state update equation** $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$. The gradient of $C_t$ with respect to $C_{t-1}$ equals $f_t$, which is bounded in [0, 1] but does not vanish as catastrophically as repeated matrix multiplications. Furthermore, the forget gate can be trained to output values near 1, **preserving the gradient flow** across hundreds of time steps.

---

### Question 2: What is the role of the cell state in an LSTM network? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **cell state $C_t$** is the **long-term memory component** of an LSTM network, carrying information across the entire sequence. It is regulated by the forget and input gates and updated via an additive mechanism. Mathematically, $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$, where $f_t$ controls retention of past information and $i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ introduces new information. This additive update enables stable gradient flow and makes the cell state the primary vehicle for **preserving long-range temporal dependencies**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts typically split as **7 + 7**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice). Each sub-part must be answered with full derivations, diagrams, and explanations.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the complete architecture of an LSTM cell with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each gate in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

An LSTM cell consists of three gates (forget, input, output), a candidate cell layer, the cell state $C_t$, and the hidden state $h_t$.

**1. Forget Gate (fₜ):** Uses a sigmoid activation to decide which information to discard from the cell state. Output range [0, 1]: 0 = completely forget, 1 = completely retain. Equation: $f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$.

**2. Input Gate (iₜ):** Uses a sigmoid activation to decide which new values to update. It works in tandem with the candidate cell layer. Equation: $i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$.

**3. Candidate Cell ($\tilde{C}_t$):** Creates a vector of new candidate values using tanh activation bounded in [−1, 1]. Equation: $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$.

**4. Cell State Update:** Combines old memory (scaled by forget gate) with new candidate (scaled by input gate): $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$.

**5. Output Gate (oₜ):** Decides which parts of the cell state to output. Equation: $o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$.

**6. Hidden State:** Final output: $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$.

**Block Diagram (7 marks requires the full mermaid-style block diagram from Section 4 to be reproduced by hand on the answer sheet).**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Derive the forward-pass equations of LSTM and explain why the cell state update uses an additive (rather than multiplicative) structure.**

**Model Solution:**

The forward pass is derived by:

**Step 1:** Concatenate $h_{t-1}$ and $x_t$ to form the gate input $z_t = [h_{t-1}, x_t]$.

**Step 2:** Apply the four parallel transformations:

$$f_t = \sigma(W_f z_t + b_f)$$

$$i_t = \sigma(W_i z_t + b_i)$$

$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C z_t + b_C)$$

$$o_t = \sigma(W_o z_t + b_o)$$

**Step 3:** Update the cell state via additive gating:

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

**Step 4:** Compute the hidden state:

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

**Why Additive Structure for Cell State:**

The cell state uses an **additive** structure (rather than purely multiplicative as in vanilla RNNs) for the following critical reasons:

1. **Gradient Preservation:** $\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = f_t$ (element-wise, no matrix multiplication), preventing gradient explosion/vanishing.

2. **Constant Error Carousel (CEC):** When $f_t = 1$ and $i_t = 0$, the cell state remains unchanged across time, allowing error signals to flow back through time without attenuation.

3. **Linear Information Highway:** The cell state acts as a "highway" with only linear interactions (scaling and addition), making backpropagation-through-time (BPTT) numerically stable.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Compare and contrast LSTM with standard RNN and GRU architectures. Use a comparison table to highlight key differences.**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Vanilla RNN | LSTM | GRU |
|---------|-------------|------|-----|
| **Memory Mechanism** | Single hidden state $h_t$ | Cell state + hidden state | Single hidden state (combined) |
| **Gates** | None | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (reset, update) |
| **Vanishing Gradient** | Severe issue | Solved via cell state | Partially mitigated |
| **Parameter Count** | $h(h+d+1)$ | $4h(h+d+1)$ | $3h(h+d+1)$ |
| **Long-Term Dependency** | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | High (4× RNN) | Moderate (3× RNN) |
| **Cell State** | None | Explicit $C_t$ | Implicit (in $h_t$) |
| **Output Mechanism** | $h_t = \tanh(Wh_{t-1} + Ux_t)$ | $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | $h_t = (1-z_t)h_{t-1} + z_t \tilde{h}_t$ |

**Key Contrasts:**

- **LSTM vs RNN:** LSTM adds gated memory cells, eliminating vanishing gradients; RNN cannot retain information over long sequences.
- **LSTM vs GRU:** GRU merges cell state and hidden state into a single vector, reducing parameters by ~25%, but at the cost of less fine-grained memory control.
- **GRU vs RNN:** GRU uses reset and update gates, providing partial mitigation of vanishing gradients with fewer parameters than LSTM.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain how LSTM solves the long-term dependency problem with mathematical justification. Discuss the role of the forget gate bias initialization.**

**Model Solution:**

**Long-Term Dependency Problem:** In vanilla RNNs, when the gradient is backpropagated through $k$ time steps, it gets multiplied by the same weight matrix $W$ raised to the power $k$. As $k$ grows, $\|W^k\|$ either shrinks to zero (vanishing) or grows unboundedly (exploding), preventing learning of long-range patterns.

**LSTM's Mathematical Solution:**

The cell state gradient over $k$ time steps is:

$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-k}} = \prod_{j=1}^{k} f_{t-k+j}$$

Since $f_t \in [0, 1]$ is element-wise and bounded:

- **If $f_t \approx 1$** for all intermediate steps, the product remains near 1, preserving gradient flow.
- **If $f_t \approx 0$**, the gradient is intentionally suppressed for irrelevant memory components.

This **selective** gradient preservation is mathematically superior to vanilla RNNs' uniform matrix multiplication.

**Forget Gate Bias Initialization:**

A critical implementation detail (often asked in KTU exams) is that the **forget gate bias $b_f$ should be initialized to a positive value (typically 1 or 2)** rather than 0. This ensures that at the start of training:

$$f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f) \approx \sigma(b_f) \approx 1$$

This causes the cell to **retain information by default**, preventing premature loss of memory at training onset. Jozefowicz et al. (2015) showed that this initialization is crucial for LSTM to learn long-term dependencies effectively.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Missing the Additive Cell State Update (–2 marks):** Students often forget to write the full $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ equation. The cell state update is the **core innovation** of LSTM and must be explicitly derived.
>
> 2. **Confusing Gate Activations (–1 mark):** Sigmoid ($\sigma$) is used for **gates** (range [0, 1]), tanh is used for **candidate cells and hidden squashing** (range [−1, 1]). Mixing these up is a frequent error.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Bias Term (–1 mark):** Every gate equation must include its corresponding bias vector $b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o$.
>
> 4. **No Diagram in 7-Mark Sub-Parts (–2 marks):** KTU examiners mandate a **neat block diagram** of the LSTM cell. Skipping the diagram results in direct mark deduction.
>
> 5. **Omitting the Hadamard Product Symbol (–1 mark):** Use $\odot$ explicitly to indicate element-wise multiplication, not regular multiplication $\times$.
>
> 6. **Not Explaining "Why" (–1 to –2 marks):** Simply writing equations without explaining the **intuition** behind each gate (e.g., "forget gate decides what to discard") loses conceptual marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for LSTM Architecture:**

- ☐ LSTM = **Long Short-Term Memory**, introduced by **Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997)**.
- ☐ Solves the **vanishing gradient problem** of vanilla RNNs.
- ☐ Three gates: **Forget ($f_t$), Input ($i_t$), Output ($o_t$)** — all use **sigmoid** activation.
- ☐ **Candidate cell** $\tilde{C}_t$ uses **tanh** activation.
- ☐ **Cell state update** is **additive**: $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$.
- ☐ **Hidden state output**: $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$.
- ☐ **Hadamard product** ($\odot$) is element-wise multiplication.
- ☐ Total parameters per cell: $4h(h + d + 1)$.
- ☐ **Forget gate bias** should be initialized to **positive value (≈ 1–2)**.
- ☐ **Peephole connections** allow gates to inspect cell state directly (advanced variant).
- ☐ LSTM is a **precursor** to modern Transformer architectures in generative AI.
- ☐ Applications: NLP, speech recognition, time-series forecasting, generative music, medical signal processing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)** is a sophisticated variant of the standard Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) architecture, introduced by **Cho et al. (2014)**. It is engineered to overcome the critical *vanishing and exploding gradient* problem that plagues conventional RNNs during back-propagation through time (BPTT). GRU achieves this by incorporating a **gating mechanism** that adaptively controls the flow of information through the network.

A GRU cell contains exactly **two gates**:
1. **Update Gate ($z_t$)** — Decides how much past information to carry forward.
2. **Reset Gate ($r_t$)** — Decides how much past information to forget.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** GRU is a streamlined alternative to LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory). Unlike LSTM which has *three* gates and a *separate cell state*, GRU merges the cell state and hidden state into a single vector, making it computationally more efficient while retaining comparable performance on many sequence-modelling tasks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Identity:** At every time step $t$, a GRU processes an input vector $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ along with the previous hidden state $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ to produce a new hidden state $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Selective Librarian

Imagine a **librarian** (the GRU cell) who manages a continuously arriving stream of news articles (sequential data).

- The **Update Gate** acts like the librarian's *filing decision* — when a new article arrives, the librarian asks: *"Should I keep my old notes or replace them with this new article's information?"* If the new article is more relevant, the update gate fires high and old memory is overwritten.
- The **Reset Gate** acts like the librarian's *forgetfulness switch* — when processing a new article, the librarian asks: *"Should I ignore everything I knew from previous articles and start fresh, or should I refer back to my old notes?"* If context from the past is irrelevant, the reset gate fires high and past memory is wiped out before computing the new candidate.

Just as a smart librarian selectively retains and forgets information to tell a coherent story, the GRU selectively retains and forgets past hidden states to model long-range dependencies.

## 1.3 Key Architectural Properties

- **Hidden State Vector Dimension:** $h$ (user-defined hyperparameter).
- **Number of Trainable Parameter Matrices per Layer:** **3 weight matrices** ($W_z, W_r, W_h$) and **3 bias vectors** ($b_z, b_r, b_h$).
- **Gating Activation Function:** Sigmoid $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$, output range $(0, 1)$.
- **Candidate Activation Function:** $\tanh(x) = \frac{e^{x}-e^{-x}}{e^{x}+e^{-x}}$, output range $(-1, +1)$.
- **Information Flow:** Fully recurrent — output $h_t$ is fed back as input to the next time step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Total trainable parameters for a single GRU layer with input dim $d$ and hidden dim $h$ is $\mathbf{3 \times (d \times h + h \times h + h)} = \mathbf{3h(d+h+1)}$.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Walkthrough — The Four Core Computations

A GRU cell at time step $t$ executes exactly **four sequential vector operations**.

### Step 1 — Update Gate Computation
The update gate $z_t$ looks at the current input $x_t$ and the previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ to decide what fraction of past information to retain.

$$z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot x_t + U_z \cdot h_{t-1} + b_z)$$

- $\sigma$ is the **sigmoid** activation, forcing each element of $z_t$ into $(0, 1)$.
- $W_z \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times d}$ and $U_z \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times h}$ are trainable weight matrices.
- $b_z \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ is the trainable bias vector.
- When $z_t \rightarrow 1$: the past hidden state is **preserved**.
- When $z_t \rightarrow 0$: the past hidden state is **replaced** by new candidate content.

### Step 2 — Reset Gate Computation
The reset gate $r_t$ controls how much of the previous hidden state contributes to the computation of the new candidate state.

$$r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot x_t + U_r \cdot h_{t-1} + b_r)$$

- When $r_t \rightarrow 1$: the previous hidden state is used **fully**.
- When $r_t \rightarrow 0$: the previous hidden state is **ignored** — the candidate is computed purely from the current input.

### Step 3 — Candidate Hidden State Computation
The candidate state $\tilde{h}_t$ represents a *proposal* for the new hidden state, modulated by the reset gate.

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h \cdot x_t + U_h \cdot (r_t \odot h_{t-1}) + b_h)$$

- $\odot$ denotes the **Hadamard (element-wise) product**.
- The element-wise multiplication $r_t \odot h_{t-1}$ selectively "resets" parts of the previous hidden state.
- The activation $\tanh$ squashes values into $(-1, +1)$ for stable gradient flow.

### Step 4 — Final Hidden State (Linear Interpolation)
The final hidden state $h_t$ is a **convex combination** of the previous hidden state and the new candidate, gated by $z_t$.

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

- This is the defining linear-interpolation identity of the GRU.
- It ensures the hidden state always lies in a bounded region of $\mathbb{R}^h$, mitigating gradient explosion.
- Note: $1$ here denotes a vector of ones of the same dimension as $z_t$.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

| Step | Mathematical Object | Why It Exists | How It Achieves It |
|------|--------------------|-----|-----|
| 1 | Update Gate $z_t$ | Solves *catastrophic forgetting* of long-term dependencies | Learns a soft mask in $(0,1)$ to scale retention of $h_{t-1}$ |
| 2 | Reset Gate $r_t$ | Solves *context-irrelevant noise* problem | Learns a soft mask in $(0,1)$ to scale contribution of $h_{t-1}$ to candidate |
| 3 | Candidate $\tilde{h}_t$ | Generates *new content* to potentially inject | $\tanh$ ensures bounded, zero-centred output |
| 4 | Final $h_t$ | Combines old and new content smoothly | Element-wise linear interpolation controlled by $z_t$ |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Definition | Shape / Range |
|--------|-----------|---------------|
| $x_t$ | Input vector at time $t$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| $h_{t-1}$ | Previous hidden state | $\mathbb{R}^{h}$, range depends on activation |
| $h_t$ | Current hidden state (output) | $\mathbb{R}^{h}$ |
| $z_t$ | Update gate | $\mathbb{R}^{h}$, each entry in $(0,1)$ |
| $r_t$ | Reset gate | $\mathbb{R}^{h}$, each entry in $(0,1)$ |
| $\tilde{h}_t$ | Candidate hidden state | $\mathbb{R}^{h}$, each entry in $(-1,1)$ |
| $W_z, W_r, W_h$ | Input-to-hidden weight matrices | $\mathbb{R}^{h \times d}$ |
| $U_z, U_r, U_h$ | Hidden-to-hidden weight matrices | $\mathbb{R}^{h \times h}$ |
| $b_z, b_r, b_h$ | Bias vectors | $\mathbb{R}^{h}$ |
| $\sigma(\cdot)$ | Sigmoid activation | $\frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$, range $(0,1)$ |
| $\tanh(\cdot)$ | Hyperbolic tangent activation | $\frac{e^{x}-e^{-x}}{e^{x}+e^{-x}}$, range $(-1,1)$ |
| $\odot$ | Hadamard (element-wise) product | Same shape as operands |
| $\vert \cdot \vert$ | Cardinality (size) operator | Scalar |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Time-Series Forecasting:** Stock price prediction, weather modelling, sensor data analytics.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Machine translation, sentiment classification, text generation.
- **Speech Recognition:** Acoustic modelling in ASR pipelines (acoustic-to-phoneme conversion).
- **Music Generation:** Used as a building block in generative models for sequential audio.
- **Generative AI Foundation:** GRU-based decoders are deployed in encoder-decoder architectures for image captioning, code generation, and chatbot systems.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Industrial IoT streams where point anomalies depend on temporal context.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** The following derivations are exhaustively laid out — every algebraic transition and vector operation is explicitly justified, as required by the KTU 2024 Scheme **Apply / Analyse** level questions in Module 3.

## 3.1 Derivation: Convex Combination Identity of the Final Hidden State

The defining equation of a GRU is:

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

We prove that $h_t$ is a **convex combination** of $h_{t-1}$ and $\tilde{h}_t$ at every component $i$.

**Step 1 — Expand the right-hand side component-wise.**

Let $h_t^{(i)}$ denote the $i$-th component of the hidden state. Then:

$$h_t^{(i)} = (1 - z_t^{(i)}) \cdot h_{t-1}^{(i)} + z_t^{(i)} \cdot \tilde{h}_t^{(i)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the sigmoid output range.**

Since $z_t = \sigma(\cdot)$, each component satisfies:

$$0 < z_t^{(i)} < 1$$

**Step 3 — Verify the convex combination coefficients sum to 1.**

The coefficient of $h_{t-1}^{(i)}$ is $(1 - z_t^{(i)})$ and the coefficient of $\tilde{h}_t^{(i)}$ is $z_t^{(i)}$. Their sum is:

$$(1 - z_t^{(i)}) + z_t^{(i)} = 1$$

Since both coefficients are strictly between 0 and 1, $h_t^{(i)}$ lies strictly **inside** the interval $(\min(h_{t-1}^{(i)}, \tilde{h}_t^{(i)}),\; \max(h_{t-1}^{(i)}, \tilde{h}_t^{(i)}))$.

**Step 4 — Conclude the convex combination property.**

Therefore, $h_t$ is a component-wise convex combination of $h_{t-1}$ and $\tilde{h}_t$, which guarantees the output remains bounded and gradient magnitudes are preserved. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Trainable Parameter Count for a Single GRU Layer

A single GRU layer has three gating units (update, reset, candidate) and three bias vectors.

**Step 1 — Count weights for the update gate $z_t$.**

$$z_t = \sigma(W_z x_t + U_z h_{t-1} + b_z)$$

- $W_z \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times d}$ contributes $h \cdot d$ parameters.
- $U_z \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times h}$ contributes $h \cdot h$ parameters.
- $b_z \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ contributes $h$ parameters.
- Subtotal: $h \cdot d + h^{2} + h = h(d + h + 1)$.

**Step 2 — Count weights for the reset gate $r_t$.**

By symmetry with the update gate:

$$\text{Subtotal: } h(d + h + 1)$$

**Step 3 — Count weights for the candidate state $\tilde{h}_t$.**

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h x_t + U_h (r_t \odot h_{t-1}) + b_h)$$

The Hadamard product $r_t \odot h_{t-1}$ is **element-wise and has no learnable parameters**; the multiplication is performed using the current values of $r_t$.

- $W_h \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times d}$: $h \cdot d$ parameters.
- $U_h \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times h}$: $h^{2}$ parameters.
- $b_h \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$: $h$ parameters.
- Subtotal: $h(d + h + 1)$.

**Step 4 — Sum all three subtotals.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= 3 \times h(d + h + 1) \\
&= 3hd + 3h^{2} + 3h \\
&= 3h(d + h + 1)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.**

Let $d = 4$ (input dimension) and $h = 3$ (hidden dimension):

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= 3 \times 3 \times (4 + 3 + 1) \\
&= 9 \times 8 \\
&= 72 \text{ parameters}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check with LSTM:** A comparable single-layer LSTM has **four** gating units and a separate cell state, giving a parameter count of $4h(d + h + 1)$. Thus GRU has exactly **25% fewer parameters** than LSTM for the same hidden size.

## 3.3 Derivation: Gradient Flow Through Time (Vanishing-Gradient Mitigation)

We analyse the gradient of a generic loss $L$ with respect to $h_{t-k}$ for a chain of GRU cells.

**Step 1 — Express the recurrent relationship.**

From the convex combination identity:

$$h_t = z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t + (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivative $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$.**

By the product rule on element-wise products:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(1 - z_t) + \text{diag}(z_t) \cdot \frac{\partial \tilde{h}_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} + \text{other cross terms}$$

The dominant term (when most elements of $z_t$ are near 1) is the diagonal matrix:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} \approx \text{diag}(1 - z_t)$$

**Step 3 — Chain the gradient over $k$ time steps.**

For the loss at time step $T$:

$$\frac{\partial L_T}{\partial h_{t-k}} = \frac{\partial L_T}{\partial h_T} \cdot \prod_{i=t-k+1}^{T} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the diagonal Jacobian.**

$$\frac{\partial h_T}{\partial h_{t-k}} \approx \prod_{i=t-k+1}^{T} \text{diag}(1 - z_i)$$

Each diagonal entry is bounded:

$$0 \le 1 - z_i^{(j)} \le 1$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

- If the network learns $z_i \approx 0$ (replace mode), then $1 - z_i \approx 1$, and the gradient **flows unchanged** over long ranges — solving the vanishing-gradient problem.
- If the network learns $z_i \approx 1$ (retain mode), then $1 - z_i \approx 0$, and the gradient decays — appropriate when past context is irrelevant.
- The product is **bounded** by 1 in absolute value, preventing the gradient from exploding in well-trained networks.

$\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: GRU vs. Vanilla RNN vs. LSTM

| Property | Vanilla RNN | LSTM | GRU |
|----------|------------|------|-----|
| Number of gates | 0 | 3 (input, forget, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| Cell state | Absent | Present ($c_t$) | Absent (merged into $h_t$) |
| Hidden state | $h_t$ | $h_t$ | $h_t$ |
| Activation for gates | None | Sigmoid | Sigmoid |
| Candidate activation | $\tanh$ | $\tanh$ | $\tanh$ |
| Parameters per layer | $h(d+h+1)$ | $4h(d+h+1)$ | $3h(d+h+1)$ |
| Vanishing gradient | Severe risk | Mitigated | Mitigated |
| Computational cost | Lowest | Highest | Medium |
| Memory footprint | Smallest | Largest | Medium |
| Typical KTU exam weight | 2 marks | 5 marks | 5 marks |
