<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Schematic: Single GRU Cell Internal Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xt[Input xt] --> Merge1
    hm1[Previous Hidden hm1] --> Merge1
    Merge1{Linear Wz dot xt + Uz dot hm1 + bz} --> Sigmoid1[Sigmoid sigma]
    Sigmoid1 --> zt[Update Gate zt]
    xt --> Merge2
    hm1 --> Merge2
    Merge2{Linear Wr dot xt + Ur dot hm1 + br} --> Sigmoid2[Sigmoid sigma]
    Sigmoid2 --> rt[Reset Gate rt]
    xt --> Merge3
    hm1 --> ResetMul((x))
    rt --> ResetMul
    ResetMul --> Merge3
    Merge3{Linear Wh dot xt + Uh dot rt o hm1 + bh} --> Tanh1[tanh]
    Tanh1 --> htt[Candidate Hidden htt]
    zt --> FinalInterp((o))
    hm1 --> FinalInterp
    FinalInterp --> FinalSum((+))
    zt --> FinalMul((o))
    htt --> FinalMul
    FinalMul --> FinalSum
    FinalSum --> ht[Current Hidden ht]
    ht --> OutArrow[Output and next step input]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Unrolled GRU Through Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x1[Input x1] --> G1[GRU Cell 1]
    G1 --> h1[Hidden h1]
    h1 --> G2[GRU Cell 2]
    x2[Input x2] --> G2
    G2 --> h2[Hidden h2]
    h2 --> G3[GRU Cell 3]
    x3[Input x3] --> G3
    G3 --> h3[Hidden h3]
    h3 --> G4[GRU Cell 4]
    x4[Input x4] --> G4
    G4 --> h4[Hidden h4]
    h1 --> y1[Output y1]
    h2 --> y2[Output y2]
    h3 --> y3[Output y3]
    h4 --> y4[Output y4]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputStage[Input Stage]
        i1[Input Vector xt]
        i2[Previous Hidden hm1]
    end
    subgraph GatingStage[Gating Computation Stage]
        g1[Update Gate zt equals sigma Wz xt + Uz hm1 + bz]
        g2[Reset Gate rt equals sigma Wr xt + Ur hm1 + br]
    end
    subgraph CandidateStage[Candidate Computation Stage]
        c1[Reset Modulated Memory rt o hm1]
        c2[Linear Projection Wh xt + Uh rt o hm1 + bh]
        c3[tanh Activation]
        c4[Candidate Hidden htt]
    end
    subgraph FusionStage[Fusion Stage]
        f1[Linear Interpolation 1 minus zt o hm1 + zt o htt]
        f2[Current Hidden ht]
    end
    subgraph OutputStage[Output Stage]
        o1[Feed ht to Next Time Step]
        o2[Optional Dense Layer for Task Output]
    end
    i1 --> g1
    i2 --> g1
    i1 --> g2
    i2 --> g2
    g2 --> c1
    i2 --> c1
    c1 --> c2
    i1 --> c2
    c2 --> c3
    c3 --> c4
    g1 --> f1
    i2 --> f1
    c4 --> f1
    f1 --> f2
    f2 --> o1
    f2 --> o2
```

## 4.4 Diagram Legend & Interpretation

- **$o$** inside a circle denotes the **Hadamard (element-wise) product** operator.
- **$+$** inside a circle denotes **element-wise vector addition**.
- **$x$** inside a circle denotes element-wise multiplication used for the reset-gate modulation.
- Each **GRU cell** receives two inputs: the current input $x_t$ and the previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$.
- The final hidden state $h_t$ is simultaneously the **output** of the current cell and the **input to the next cell**.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**
**Q:** Differentiate between the **Update Gate** and the **Reset Gate** in a GRU. What activation function is used for each?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Update Gate ($z_t$) | Reset Gate ($r_t$) |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| **Mathematical role** | $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ | $\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h x_t + U_h (r_t \odot h_{t-1}) + b_h)$ |
| **Function** | Controls how much of the past hidden state is carried forward | Controls how much of the past hidden state influences the candidate |
| **Activation** | Sigmoid $\sigma(\cdot)$ | Sigmoid $\sigma(\cdot)$ |
| **Output range** | $(0, 1)$ | $(0, 1)$ |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Both gates use the **sigmoid** activation to produce a soft mask, but they act on different terms — the update gate acts on the **final** convex combination, while the reset gate acts on the **candidate** computation.

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Q:** State any **three advantages** of GRU over a standard (vanilla) RNN for sequence modelling.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Mitigation of Vanishing Gradients:** The convex-combination identity $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ allows gradients to flow unchanged over long time horizons when $z_t \approx 0$.
2. **Adaptive Memory via Gating:** The update gate learns **data-driven** retention of past information, unlike vanilla RNNs that always fully overwrite $h_{t-1}$.
3. **Selective Forgetting via Reset Gate:** The reset gate allows the network to **discard irrelevant context** before computing the candidate state, which is impossible in vanilla RNNs.
4. *(Bonus point — 1 mark only if asked)* **Bounded Hidden State:** Since the final state is a convex combination of $\tanh$-bounded values, it remains in a controlled numerical range, reducing the risk of gradient explosion.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) **[KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Scheme]**
**Q:** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **architecture of a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)**. Derive the four governing equations and explain how the gating mechanism mitigates the vanishing gradient problem.

#### Part (a) — Architecture & Equations (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Architectural overview (2 marks):**
A GRU cell consists of two gates — the **update gate** $z_t$ and the **reset gate** $r_t$ — along with a **candidate hidden state** $\tilde{h}_t$. The architecture merges the cell state and hidden state of an LSTM into a single hidden state vector $h_t$, reducing parameter count.

**Step 2 — Four governing equations (4 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
z_t &= \sigma(W_z x_t + U_z h_{t-1} + b_z) \\
r_t &= \sigma(W_r x_t + U_r h_{t-1} + b_r) \\
\tilde{h}_t &= \tanh(W_h x_t + U_h (r_t \odot h_{t-1}) + b_h) \\
h_t &= (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Architectural diagram (1 mark):**
Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid schematic. Students are expected to draw a similar block diagram showing the two gating branches, the candidate computation, and the final linear interpolation.

#### Part (b) — Vanishing Gradient Mitigation (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the problem in vanilla RNNs (2 marks):**
In a vanilla RNN, the gradient of loss $L$ w.r.t. $h_{t-k}$ involves a product of Jacobian matrices $\prod_{i} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}$. Each Jacobian is the recurrent weight matrix $W_{hh}$, whose spectral radius $\rho < 1$ causes exponential gradient decay — the **vanishing gradient problem**.

**Step 2 — Jacobian structure of GRU (2 marks):**
For the GRU, the dominant component of $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$ is the diagonal matrix $\text{diag}(1 - z_t)$. Each diagonal entry is bounded in $(0, 1)$, but unlike a fixed weight matrix, it is **input-dependent and learned**.

**Step 3 — Adaptive gradient preservation (2 marks):**
When the network learns $z_t \approx 0$ for early time steps, the product $\prod_i (1 - z_i) \approx 1$, allowing gradients to flow **unchanged** across hundreds of time steps. This is the central mechanism by which GRU mitigates vanishing gradients.

**Step 4 — Practical implication (1 mark):**
This makes GRU suitable for tasks with long-range dependencies, such as document-level language modelling, multi-sentence translation, and time-series forecasting with seasonal patterns.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> - **Do NOT confuse the update gate with the LSTM forget gate.** They play structurally similar roles but operate on different terms. Update gate modulates the *final interpolation*; LSTM forget gate modulates the *cell state* directly.
> - **Do NOT claim GRU completely solves vanishing gradients.** It only *mitigates* the problem — extreme sequences (length > 500) still benefit from Transformer-based attention.
> - **Always include the convex-combination equation** $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ in any 7-mark descriptive answer — omitting it loses at least 2 marks.
> - **Reset gate is applied to $h_{t-1}$ BEFORE the linear projection by $U_h$**, not after. Students frequently misplace this Hadamard product.

---

### Question B (Option 2) **[KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Scheme]**
**Q:** Compare GRU with LSTM in terms of (i) architecture, (ii) parameter count, and (iii) computational cost. Derive the parameter-count formula for a single-layer GRU and justify the design choice of using $\tanh$ in the candidate activation and $\sigma$ in the gating activations.

#### Part (a) — Comparative Analysis (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Architectural comparison (2 marks):**
- **LSTM:** Three gates (input, forget, output), a separate cell state $c_t$, and a hidden state $h_t$.
- **GRU:** Two gates (update, reset), no separate cell state — $h_t$ serves both roles.

**Step 2 — Parameter count comparison (3 marks):**
- **LSTM** per layer: $4h(d + h + 1)$ — see derivation in the analytical matrix.
- **GRU** per layer: $3h(d + h + 1)$ — derived in Section 3.2 of these notes.
- **Reduction factor:** $\frac{3}{4} = 75\%$, i.e., GRU has 25% fewer parameters.

**Step 3 — Computational cost comparison (2 marks):**
- **LSTM** performs **4** matrix multiplications and **4** element-wise activation passes per time step.
- **GRU** performs **3** matrix multiplications and **3** element-wise activation passes per time step.
- **Wall-clock speedup:** ~25% faster per time step on identical hardware.

#### Part (b) — Activation Function Justification & Parameter Derivation (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Justification of $\sigma$ for gates (2 marks):**
The sigmoid function $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ produces values in $(0, 1)$, which is mathematically ideal for a **soft mask**:
- It is **differentiable everywhere**, enabling back-propagation.
- It is **monotonically increasing**, allowing clear "on/off" behaviour at the extremes.
- It is **bounded**, preventing the gate from amplifying or attenuating information unboundedly.

**Step 2 — Justification of $\tanh$ for candidate state (2 marks):**
The $\tanh$ function produces values in $(-1, +1)$, which is mathematically ideal for the **candidate activation**:
- It is **zero-centred**, ensuring the candidate state has balanced positive and negative contributions.
- It has a **stronger gradient** near the origin than $\sigma$, accelerating learning of the candidate values.
- It is **odd-symmetric**, providing natural regularization against drift.

**Step 3 — Parameter derivation for a single GRU layer (3 marks):**
From Section 3.2:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{GRU}} &= \underbrace{h \cdot d}_{W_z, W_r, W_h} \times 3 + \underbrace{h^{2}}_{U_z, U_r, U_h} \times 3 + \underbrace{h}_{b_z, b_r, b_h} \times 3 \\
&= 3h(d + h + 1)
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Example:** For $d = 8$, $h = 5$:

$$P_{\text{GRU}} = 3 \times 5 \times (8 + 5 + 1) = 15 \times 14 = 210 \text{ parameters}$$

The corresponding LSTM would have $\frac{4}{3} \times 210 = 280$ parameters.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks on this question:**
> - **Do NOT write "GRU has 2 gates and LSTM has 3 gates" without specifying the gate names.** Examiners expect: update/reset vs. input/forget/output.
> - **Do NOT confuse the parameter derivation logic.** Many students forget to include the bias vectors $b_z, b_r, b_h$ in the count. A bias vector of shape $\mathbb{R}^{h}$ has exactly $h$ parameters.
> - **Do NOT claim $\tanh$ is used in LSTM gates.** LSTM gates use **sigmoid**; $\tanh$ is used only for the candidate cell state and the output hidden state.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must memorize for KTU exams**

- **GRU has 2 gates:** Update gate $z_t$ and reset gate $r_t$. Both use **sigmoid** activation.
- **Candidate state** $\tilde{h}_t$ uses **$\tanh$** activation.
- **Final hidden state identity:** $\boxed{h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t}$ — this is a *convex combination*.
- **Reset gate is applied BEFORE the candidate linear projection:** $\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h x_t + U_h(r_t \odot h_{t-1}) + b_h)$.
- **Parameter count per layer:** $P_{\text{GRU}} = 3h(d + h + 1)$.
- **GRU parameter reduction vs. LSTM:** GRU uses **75%** of LSTM's parameters.
- **GRU merges cell state and hidden state** into a single vector $h_t$ — this is the *key architectural difference* from LSTM.
- **Vanishing-gradient mitigation mechanism:** The Jacobian $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} \approx \text{diag}(1 - z_t)$ allows learned, input-dependent gradient preservation.
- **Convex combination property:** $h_t$ always lies in the bounded interval between $h_{t-1}$ and $\tilde{h}_t$, preventing gradient explosion.
- **Element-wise product** symbol: $\odot$ — this is **Hadamard**, not matrix multiplication.
- **GRU was proposed by Cho et al. (2014)** — a standard textbook reference for KTU viva questions.
- **Hadamard product on $r_t \odot h_{t-1}$ has NO learnable parameters** — it is a runtime operation only.
- **Sigmoid range:** $(0, 1)$. **$\tanh$ range:** $(-1, +1)$. Memorize these — they are tested directly.
- **GRU's typical use cases:** NLP translation, time-series forecasting, speech recognition, music generation.
- **When NOT to use GRU:** Ultra-long sequences (>1000 tokens) where Transformer attention is empirically superior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always (a) draw a labelled diagram, (b) write all four governing equations, (c) derive one key property (parameter count OR convex combination), and (d) compare with LSTM in a small table. This four-part structure typically covers all CO mapping expectations in the KTU 2024 Scheme rubrics.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture of GRU
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Architecture of GRU

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)** is a streamlined variant of the Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) gating architecture, introduced by **Cho et al. (2014)**, that addresses the **vanishing gradient problem** in standard RNNs by using a pair of adaptive gating mechanisms — the **Update Gate** and the **Reset Gate** — to selectively regulate the flow of information through the recurrent hidden state.

The GRU is structurally simpler than the **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** cell because it merges the **input gate** and **forget gate** into a single **update gate**, and it fuses the **cell state** and **hidden state** into one unified vector **hₜ**. This results in a more computationally efficient gating model that is widely used in **sequence-to-sequence generation**, **machine translation**, and **text generation** tasks under the Generative AI umbrella.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** GRU is treated as a *foundational generative building block* — a precursor to modern Transformer architectures. Students are expected to derive gate equations, explain gradient flow, and contrast GRU vs. LSTM architectures.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a busy Chief Editor (the GRU cell) reading a long news feed:**
>
> 1. The **Update Gate** acts like a *curator* — it decides **how much of yesterday's headline notes** (`h_{t-1}`) should be carried forward into today's notebook and **how much new incoming news** (`x_t`) should overwrite them.
> 2. The **Reset Gate** acts like a *filter* — it decides **how much of the past context** should be *forgotten* when computing the new candidate memory. If the past is irrelevant (e.g., topic has shifted), the reset gate clears the slate.
> 3. The **Candidate Hidden State** is the *draft note* the editor writes based on the filtered past + the new input.
> 4. The **Final Hidden State** is the *published, official note* of the day, blended from the old note and the new draft.

This selective memory and forgetting is what allows GRU to retain **long-term dependencies** without the explosive or vanishing gradient issues of vanilla RNNs.

---

### 1.3 Key Architectural Distinctions

| Property | Vanilla RNN | LSTM | **GRU** |
|----------|-------------|------|---------|
| Number of gates | 0 | 3 (input, forget, output) | **2 (update, reset)** |
| State vectors | 1 (hidden) | 2 (cell + hidden) | **1 (unified hidden)** |
| Parameters | Low | High | **Moderate** |
| Training speed | Fast | Slow | **Faster than LSTM** |
| Long-range memory | Poor | Excellent | **Very Good** |

---

### 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics Used

- **Default activation:** $\tanh$ for candidate state, **sigmoid ($\sigma$)** for both gates.
- **Gate output range:** $[0, 1]$ (because of sigmoid).
- **Hidden state range:** $[-1, 1]$ (because of $\tanh$).
- **Parameter count per GRU cell:** $\mathbf{3 \times (n_h^2 + n_h \times n_x + n_h)}$ where $n_h$ = hidden size, $n_x$ = input size.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> A GRU cell processes one time-step `t` and produces an output hidden state `h_t`. Below is the **ordered information flow** every student must memorize for KTU valuation.

**Step 1 — Receive Inputs**
The cell receives two vectors at time `t`:
- Current input vector: $\mathbf{x}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n_x}$
- Previous hidden state: $\mathbf{h}_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h}$

**Step 2 — Compute the Update Gate ($z_t$)**
The update gate decides *what to keep* from the past. It looks at both the previous hidden state and the current input, applies learned weights, and squashes via sigmoid:

$$z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$$

- If $z_t \to 1$: copy the old state forward (ignore current input).
- If $z_t \to 0$: discard the old state (use only the new candidate).

**Step 3 — Compute the Reset Gate ($r_t$)**
The reset gate decides *how much past information to forget* when computing the new candidate:

$$r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$$

- If $r_t \to 0$: erase the past completely.
- If $r_t \to 1$: keep the full past context.

**Step 4 — Compute the Candidate Hidden State ($\tilde{h}_t$)**
This is the *proposed new memory*, computed using a reset-modulated previous state:

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h)$$

The Hadamard product $\mathbf{r_t \odot h_{t-1}}$ *element-wise multiplies* the reset gate with the previous hidden state, effectively erasing components of the past that are deemed irrelevant.

**Step 5 — Compute the Final Hidden State ($h_t$)**
The final hidden state is a **convex interpolation** between the old state and the new candidate, controlled by the update gate:

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

This is the key GRU innovation: **a single gate controls both forgetting and remembering** in one linear interpolation.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the table below. These four equations cover 80% of KTU questions on GRU.

| Symbol | Equation | Meaning | Activation |
|--------|----------|---------|------------|
| $z_t$ | $\sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$ | Update Gate | Sigmoid |
| $r_t$ | $\sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$ | Reset Gate | Sigmoid |
| $\tilde{h}_t$ | $\tanh(W_h [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h)$ | Candidate Hidden State | Tanh |
| $h_t$ | $(1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ | Final Hidden State | None (linear blend) |

**Variable / Dimension Reference:**

| Variable | Shape | Purpose |
|----------|-------|---------|
| $x_t$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n_x}$ | Input at time `t` |
| $h_{t-1}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n_h}$ | Previous hidden state |
| $W_z, W_r, W_h$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n_h \times (n_h + n_x)}$ | Learnable weight matrices |
| $b_z, b_r, b_h$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n_h}$ | Learnable bias vectors |
| $\odot$ | Element-wise | Hadamard product operator |
| $\sigma$ | $(0,1)$ | Logistic sigmoid |
| $\tanh$ | $(-1,1)$ | Hyperbolic tangent |

> [!TIP]
> **Watch the absolute-value symbol:** In KTU answer sheets, write $\lvert x \rvert$ (with `\lvert` `\rvert`) — never the raw keyboard pipe `|x|` — to avoid table-rendering and parsing issues.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

GRUs are the workhorse behind:
- **Google Translate (early seq2seq models)** — used as encoder/decoder RNNs.
- **Speech Synthesis (Tacotron-1)** — generating mel-spectrograms.
- **Stock Price Forecasting** — capturing temporal market patterns.
- **Music Generation (early Magenta/DeepJazz)** — sequence modeling of MIDI notes.
- **Text Autocompletion** — predicting next tokens in mobile keyboards.
- **Chatbot Intent Recognition** — classifying user utterances in conversational AI.

In **Generative AI pipelines**, GRUs often serve as the **decoder backbone** in pre-Transformer architectures and remain popular in **edge-AI deployments** where model size matters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below trace the complete forward-pass algebra of a single GRU cell, including the explicit dimension-level justification that KTU board examiners expect in 14-mark derivations.

---

### 3.1 Concatenation Step (Pre-Gate Computation)

We start with the input $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n_x}$ and previous hidden state $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h}$. To process them jointly, we **concatenate** along the feature axis:

$$[h_{t-1}, x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h + n_x}$$

This concatenated vector is then linearly transformed by the gate-specific weight matrices.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Update Gate $z_t$

**Step 1** — Apply a linear (affine) transformation:

$$a^{(z)}_t = W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z$$

Here $W_z \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h \times (n_h + n_x)}$ and $b_z \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h}$.

**Step 2** — Apply the sigmoid squashing function element-wise:

$$z_t = \sigma(a^{(z)}_t) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-a^{(z)}_t}}$$

**Why sigmoid?** Because we want a *gate value* between 0 and 1 to act as a soft mask on information flow.

**Output range:**

$$z_{t,j} \in (0, 1), \quad \forall j \in \{1, \dots, n_h\}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Reset Gate $r_t$

The derivation mirrors that of the update gate but uses *independent* weights $W_r$ and bias $b_r$:

**Step 1** — Linear transformation:

$$a^{(r)}_t = W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r$$

**Step 2** — Sigmoid activation:

$$r_t = \sigma(a^{(r)}_t) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-a^{(r)}_t}}$$

**Role:** Acts as an *element-wise forget filter* applied to $h_{t-1}$ before computing the candidate state.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Candidate Hidden State $\tilde{h}_t$

**Step 1** — Apply the reset gate to the previous hidden state via Hadamard product:

$$r_t \odot h_{t-1}$$

This zeros out dimensions of $h_{t-1}$ where $r_t$ is close to 0.

**Step 2** — Concatenate the reset-modulated past with the current input:

$$[r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h + n_x}$$

**Step 3** — Linear transformation using a *third* independent weight matrix $W_h$:

$$a^{(h)}_t = W_h \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h$$

**Step 4** — Apply $\tanh$ to produce the candidate:

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(a^{(h)}_t) = \frac{e^{a^{(h)}_t} - e^{-a^{(h)}_t}}{e^{a^{(h)}_t} + e^{-a^{(h)}_t}}$$

**Output range:**

$$\tilde{h}_{t,j} \in (-1, 1), \quad \forall j$$

**Why tanh here (not sigmoid)?** Because the candidate represents a *value* (not a gate), and tanh allows both positive and negative activations, giving the network richer representational capacity.

---

### 3.5 Derivation of the Final Hidden State $h_t$

**Step 1** — Interpret the update gate as a *blending coefficient*. Since $z_t \in (0,1)$, we can form its complement:

$$1 - z_t \in (0, 1)$$

**Step 2** — Compute a linear interpolation between the *retained past* and the *new candidate*:

$$h_t = \underbrace{(1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1}}_{\text{retained past}} + \underbrace{z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t}_{\text{new contribution}}$$

**Step 3** — Verify the convex-combination property:

$$h_{t,j} = (1 - z_{t,j}) \cdot h_{t-1,j} + z_{t,j} \cdot \tilde{h}_{t,j}$$

For any scalar $z_{t,j} \in [0,1]$, this is a convex combination of $h_{t-1,j}$ and $\tilde{h}_{t,j}$ — meaning $h_t$ is always a *smooth interpolation*, never a hard switch.

**Limiting cases:**

- If $z_t = \mathbf{0}$: $\quad h_t = h_{t-1}$ (pure copy of past).
- If $z_t = \mathbf{1}$: $\quad h_t = \tilde{h}_t$ (pure candidate, ignoring past).
- If $z_t = \mathbf{0.5}$: $\quad h_t = 0.5 \odot h_{t-1} + 0.5 \odot \tilde{h}_t$ (equal blend).

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Backpropagation-Through-Time (BPTT) Gradient Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask how GRU mitigates vanishing gradients. The key is the *linear interpolation* in $h_t$.

The gradient of the loss $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to $h_{t-1}$ flows through two pathways:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_{t-1}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$$

Expanding the partial derivative:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = (1 - z_t) + \text{gate-derivative terms}$$

The dominant term is the additive constant $\mathbf{(1 - z_t)}$, which:
- **Avoids repeated multiplication** (the cause of vanishing gradients in vanilla RNNs).
- Allows gradients to flow unimpeded when $z_t$ is small.
- Creates a *highway* for backpropagated signals — analogous to residual connections.

**Conclusion:** GRU solves vanishing gradients *not* by adding memory cells (like LSTM), but by introducing a *linear self-loop* via the update gate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GRU Cell — Internal Block Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below maps the **internal data flow** of a single GRU cell. Every arrow corresponds to a tensor operation defined in Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Vector Xt] --> C[Concatenation Buffer]
    B[Previous Hidden State Htm1] --> C
    C --> D1[Update Gate Linear Layer Wz]
    C --> D2[Reset Gate Linear Layer Wr]
    D1 --> E1[Sigmoid Activation]
    D2 --> E2[Sigmoid Activation]
    E2 --> F[Hadamard Product with Htm1]
    B --> F
    A --> G[Concatenation with Reset Htm1]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Hidden Linear Layer Wh]
    H --> I[Tanh Activation]
    I --> J[Final Hidden State Computation]
    E1 --> J
    B --> J
    J --> K[Output Hidden State Ht]
```

---

### 4.2 GRU Information-Flow Flowchart (Conceptual View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Time Step t Begins] --> S2[Receive Xt and Htm1]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Update Gate Zt]
    S2 --> S4[Compute Reset Gate Rt]
    S4 --> S5[Apply Reset Mask to Htm1]
    S5 --> S6[Compute Candidate Ht Tilde using Tanh]
    S3 --> S7[Blend Old State and Candidate]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Produce Final Hidden State Ht]
    S8 --> S9[Pass Ht to Next Time Step]
    S9 --> S1
```

---

### 4.3 Unrolled GRU Across Time Steps (Sequence View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1x[X1] --> T1c[GRU Cell t1]
    T1h[H0 initial] --> T1c
    T1c --> T1o[H1]
    T2x[X2] --> T2c[GRU Cell t2]
    T1o --> T2c
    T2c --> T2o[H2]
    T3x[X3] --> T3c[GRU Cell t3]
    T2o --> T3c
    T3c --> T3o[H3]
    T4x[X4] --> T4c[GRU Cell t4]
    T3o --> T4c
    T4c --> T4o[H4]
```

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (GRU vs. LSTM)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LSTM[LSTM Cell]
        L1[Forget Gate fg] --> L4[Cell State Ct]
        L2[Input Gate ig] --> L4
        L3[Output Gate og] --> L5[Hidden State Ht]
        L4 --> L5
    end
    subgraph GRU[GRU Cell]
        G1[Update Gate zt] --> G3[Hidden State Ht]
        G2[Reset Gate rt] --> G3
    end
    LSTM -.Uses 3 Gates and 2 States.-> NOTE1[Higher Parameter Count]
    GRU -.Uses 2 Gates and 1 Unified State.-> NOTE2[Faster Training and Compact]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Define Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU). List its two gates and state the role of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)** is a simplified variant of the LSTM architecture that uses gating mechanisms to control information flow in recurrent neural networks, mitigating the vanishing gradient problem.

The two gates are:

1. **Update Gate ($z_t$):** Decides how much of the past hidden state should be passed to the future. Acts as a blend coefficient between the old state and the new candidate.

2. **Reset Gate ($r_t$):** Decides how much of the past information to forget when computing the new candidate hidden state. Implemented via element-wise multiplication with $h_{t-1}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** Definition (1) + Update Gate explanation (1) + Reset Gate explanation (1).

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Why is $\tanh$ used in the candidate state computation of GRU instead of sigmoid?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The candidate hidden state $\tilde{h}_t$ must represent a *value* (not a gating decision), so it requires activations in the range $(-1, 1)$ to allow both positive and negative contributions. Sigmoid outputs are restricted to $(0, 1)$, which would force all candidate values to be strictly positive and limit the network's representational expressiveness. $\tanh$ is zero-centered, enabling better gradient flow and faster convergence during backpropagation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** Range limitation of sigmoid (1) + zero-centered property of tanh (1) + gradient flow benefit (1).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, students answer **ONE** of two internal-choice questions. Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Derive the complete forward-pass equations of a GRU cell, clearly stating the role of the **update gate**, **reset gate**, and **candidate hidden state**. Show all four governing equations with appropriate activation functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how the GRU architecture solves the **vanishing gradient problem** in vanilla RNNs. Use the gradient of $h_t$ with respect to $h_{t-1}$ in your reasoning. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Forward-Pass Derivation (7 marks):**

The GRU cell receives input $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n_x}$ and previous hidden state $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h}$.

**Step 1 — Update Gate (1.5 marks):**
The update gate $z_t$ controls the interpolation between old and new states.

$$z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$$

Sigmoid ensures $z_t \in (0,1)$, where values near 1 retain the past and values near 0 overwrite with the new candidate.

**Step 2 — Reset Gate (1.5 marks):**
The reset gate $r_t$ controls how much past context influences the new candidate.

$$r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$$

**Step 3 — Candidate Hidden State (2 marks):**

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h)$$

The Hadamard product $r_t \odot h_{t-1}$ acts as an element-wise mask, zeroing out past dimensions deemed irrelevant.

**Step 4 — Final Hidden State (2 marks):**
The final state is a linear interpolation between the retained past and the candidate:

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

**Summary:** The update gate is the *master controller*, while the reset gate is the *local modifier* — together they regulate both retention and forgetting in a single unified state vector.

---

**(b) Vanishing Gradient Analysis (7 marks):**

In a vanilla RNN, the recurrent update is $h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t)$. During backpropagation through time, the gradient becomes a product of Jacobians:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_{t-k}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_t} \prod_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{\partial h_{t-i}}{\partial h_{t-i-1}}$$

For the GRU, the derivative of $h_t$ with respect to $h_{t-1}$ contains the term:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = (1 - z_t) + (\text{gate-derivative terms})$$

The dominant term $\mathbf{(1 - z_t)}$ is an **additive constant**, not a multiplicative factor. When $z_t$ is small (the network has decided to retain the past), this term approaches $\mathbf{1}$, allowing gradients to **flow unimpeded** through the recurrent loop. This breaks the chain of repeated multiplications that causes gradients to vanish in vanilla RNNs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** Vanilla RNN gradient chain (2) + GRU derivative derivation (3) + interpretation of $(1-z_t)$ as highway (2).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **GRU** and **LSTM** architectures in terms of: (i) number of gates, (ii) number of state vectors, (iii) parameter count, and (iv) computational efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GRU network with hidden size $n_h = 4$ receives an input $x_t$ of dimension $n_x = 3$. Compute the **total number of learnable parameters** in a single GRU cell. Show your work. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) GRU vs. LSTM Comparison (7 marks):**

| Criterion | **LSTM** | **GRU** | Verdict |
|-----------|----------|---------|---------|
| (i) Number of gates | 3 (input, forget, output) | **2 (update, reset)** | GRU is simpler |
| (ii) State vectors | 2 (cell state $C_t$ + hidden $h_t$) | **1 (unified $h_t$)** | GRU is more compact |
| (iii) Parameter count | $4 \times (n_h^2 + n_h \cdot n_x + n_h)$ | $3 \times (n_h^2 + n_h \cdot n_x + n_h)$ | GRU has ~25% fewer parameters |
| (iv) Computational efficiency | Slower per step | **Faster per step** | GRU preferred for edge/real-time |

**Verdict:** GRU offers comparable performance to LSTM on most sequence tasks with fewer parameters and faster training, making it preferable for resource-constrained deployments. LSTM may still win on tasks requiring very long-range memory (e.g., document-level language modeling). **(1 mark for synthesis)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** 4 criteria × 1.5 marks = 6 marks + 1 mark synthesis = 7 marks.

---

**(b) Parameter Count Computation (7 marks):**

For each gate, the weight matrix has shape $n_h \times (n_h + n_x)$ and the bias has shape $n_h$.

**Per-gate parameter count:**

$$P_{\text{gate}} = n_h \cdot (n_h + n_x) + n_h$$

$$P_{\text{gate}} = n_h^2 + n_h \cdot n_x + n_h$$

**GRU has 3 such parameter groups** (Update gate, Reset gate, Candidate hidden state):

$$P_{\text{GRU}} = 3 \cdot \left( n_h^2 + n_h \cdot n_x + n_h \right)$$

**Substituting $n_h = 4$ and $n_x = 3$:**

$$P_{\text{GRU}} = 3 \cdot \left( 4^2 + 4 \cdot 3 + 4 \right)$$

$$P_{\text{GRU}} = 3 \cdot \left( 16 + 12 + 4 \right)$$

$$P_{\text{GRU}} = 3 \cdot 32$$

$$P_{\text{GRU}} = 96 \text{ learnable parameters}$$

**Final Answer:** A single GRU cell with $n_h=4$ and $n_x=3$ has **96 learnable parameters**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** Formula derivation (3) + substitution (2) + arithmetic (1) + final answer (1) = 7 marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing LSTM and GRU gates:** Students often call the GRU's "update gate" a "forget gate" or claim GRU has a "cell state." It does NOT. GRU has a *unified hidden state* only. (−1 mark)
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Hadamard product:** The expression $r_t \odot h_{t-1}$ inside the candidate state is *element-wise multiplication*, not matrix multiplication. Writing $r_t \cdot h_{t-1}$ (plain dot) is wrong and loses a mark. (−1 mark)
>
> 3. **Wrong activation in candidate state:** Some students apply sigmoid to $\tilde{h}_t$. This is incorrect — it must be **tanh** to allow negative activations. (−1 mark)
>
> 4. **Skipping dimensions in derivation:** KTU board examiners award marks for stating the *shape* of weight matrices (e.g., $W_z \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h \times (n_h+n_x)}$). Omitting this loses 1–2 marks per sub-part.
>
> 5. **Misstating vanishing-gradient fix:** The fix is the *additive* $(1-z_t)$ term, not "GRU uses gates." Saying "gates solve vanishing gradients" without mathematical justification is too vague for a 7-mark sub-part. (−2 marks)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — GRU Architecture**

- **GRU** = Gated Recurrent Unit, a streamlined RNN cell with **2 gates** and **1 unified state**.
- **Update Gate ($z_t$):** Uses **sigmoid**; controls interpolation between old state and candidate.
- **Reset Gate ($r_t$):** Uses **sigmoid**; element-wise multiplies the past hidden state before candidate computation.
- **Candidate Hidden State ($\tilde{h}_t$):** Uses **tanh**; the *proposed* new memory.
- **Final Hidden State ($h_t$):** Linear interpolation $(1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ — **no activation function** here.
- **Parameter count per cell:** $\mathbf{3 \times (n_h^2 + n_h \cdot n_x + n_h)}$ — remember the factor of 3, not 4 (unlike LSTM).
- **Vanishing gradient fix:** The additive $(1 - z_t)$ term creates a *gradient highway* — no repeated multiplication in BPTT.
- **GRU vs. LSTM:** GRU is faster, smaller, and often equally accurate; LSTM is better for very long-range dependencies.
- **Limiting behaviors:**
  - $z_t = 0 \Rightarrow h_t = h_{t-1}$ (pure memory)
  - $z_t = 1 \Rightarrow h_t = \tilde{h}_t$ (pure overwrite)
  - $r_t = 0 \Rightarrow$ past is completely erased when computing candidate
- **Hadamard product $\odot$** is **always element-wise** — never confused with dot product or matrix multiplication.
- **KTU 2024 trend:** Expect 14-mark derivation questions that combine forward-pass equations with vanishing-gradient justification in a single problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Encoder-Decoder Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Encoder-Decoder Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)**
> An **Encoder-Decoder Model** is a neural network architecture designed to map a variable-length input sequence to a variable-length output sequence by first compressing the input into a fixed-dimensional latent representation (called the **context vector** or **thought vector**) using an *encoder*, and then generating the output sequence from this representation using a *decoder*.

The encoder $\mathcal{E}$ transforms an input sequence $\mathbf{X} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{T_x})$ into a context vector $\mathbf{c}$, while the decoder $\mathcal{D}$ generates an output sequence $\mathbf{Y} = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_{T_y})$ conditioned on $\mathbf{c}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PEADT748 - Module 3)**
> Encoder-Decoder Models form the foundational architecture for **Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq)** learning and are the conceptual backbone of modern generative systems such as machine translation, abstractive summarization, image captioning, and neural chatbots. KTU examiners frequently test this topic under *CO2: Apply generative modelling concepts to design sequence generation pipelines*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **multilingual interpreter at the United Nations**:

1. The **Encoder** is like your *ear and brain's comprehension system* — you listen to a French diplomat speak an entire sentence (input sequence), and inside your head, you form a *mental gist* of the meaning (the **context vector**). You do not translate word-by-word as you listen; you wait until the whole idea is absorbed.
2. The **context vector** is the *condensed semantic idea* sitting in your working memory.
3. The **Decoder** is like your *mouth and speech-production system* — you take that mental gist and, word by word, produce an English translation (output sequence). The decoder uses both the gist **and** what it has just said (autoregression) to decide the next word.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** The encoder **reads and summarizes**, while the decoder **writes and expands**. Everything in the architecture is built around faithfully passing that summary from reader to writer.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold-Defined)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Context Vector** | $\mathbf{c}$ | Fixed-size latent representation summarizing the entire input. |
| **Hidden State** | $\mathbf{h}_t$ | Time-step-wise internal memory of the encoder/decoder RNN. |
| **Sequence Length (input)** | $T_x$ | Number of tokens in the source sequence. |
| **Sequence Length (output)** | $T_y$ | Number of tokens in the target sequence. |
| **Latent Dimensionality** | $d$ | Size of the context vector (e.g., **256**, **512**, **1024**). |
| **Teacher Forcing** | — | Training trick where ground-truth tokens are fed as decoder inputs. |

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Hyperparameters (Bold-Defined)

- **BLEU Score (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy):** range $\mathbf{0.0}$ to $\mathbf{1.0}$, measures n-gram overlap with reference translations.
- **Perplexity (PPL):** $PPL = e^{\mathcal{L}}$, lower is better.
- **Embedding Dimension:** typically **128**, **256**, or **512**.
- **Hidden State Dimension:** typically **256** to **1024**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

The Encoder-Decoder model operates in **two clearly demarcated phases**:

### 2.1.1 Phase A — Encoding
- **Step 1:** Tokenize the input sequence $\mathbf{X}$ into discrete units (words, subwords, characters).
- **Step 2:** Convert each token $x_t$ into a dense vector via an **embedding layer** $\mathbf{e}_t = E(x_t) \in \mathbb{R}^{d_e}$.
- **Step 3:** Feed the embeddings sequentially into a recurrent unit (RNN/LSTM/GRU) updating the hidden state:
$$\mathbf{h}_t^{enc} = f_{enc}(\mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{enc}, \mathbf{e}_t)$$
- **Step 4:** The final hidden state $\mathbf{h}_{T_x}^{enc}$ is passed through a non-linear projection to produce the **context vector** $\mathbf{c} = \tanh(\mathbf{V}_c \mathbf{h}_{T_x}^{enc})$.
- **Why this works:** The recurrence forces the model to compress *all* relevant information about the input into a single fixed-size vector, regardless of input length.

### 2.1.2 Phase B — Decoding
- **Step 1:** Initialize the decoder hidden state with $\mathbf{h}_0^{dec} = \mathbf{c}$.
- **Step 2:** At each time step $t$, the decoder computes:
$$\mathbf{h}_t^{dec} = f_{dec}(\mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{dec}, y_{t-1}, \mathbf{c})$$
- **Step 3:** A **softmax layer** converts $\mathbf{h}_t^{dec}$ into a probability distribution over the vocabulary:
$$P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{X}) = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_o \mathbf{h}_t^{dec} + \mathbf{b}_o)$$
- **Step 4:** The token with the highest probability is selected (greedy) or sampled (beam search / nucleus sampling) and fed back as $y_{t-1}$ for the next step (autoregression).
- **Why this works:** The decoder treats generation as a probabilistic chain rule decomposition:
$$P(\mathbf{Y} \mid \mathbf{X}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T_y} P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$$

## 2.2 Training Objective (Negative Log-Likelihood)

The model is trained to minimize the **cross-entropy loss**:
$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log P(y_t^{*} \mid y_{<t}^{*}, \mathbf{c}; \theta)$$
where $y_t^{*}$ is the ground-truth token. Gradients are back-propagated **Through Time (BPTT)** through both decoder and encoder.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Encoder Hidden Update | $\mathbf{h}_t^{enc} = f(\mathbf{W}_{hh} \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{W}_{xh} \mathbf{x}_t)$ | $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ |
| 2 | Context Vector | $\mathbf{c} = \tanh(\mathbf{V}_c \mathbf{h}_{T_x}^{enc})$ | $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| 3 | Decoder Hidden Update | $\mathbf{h}_t^{dec} = f(\mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{dec}, y_{t-1}, \mathbf{c})$ | Autoregressive |
| 4 | Output Probability | $P(y_t) = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_o \mathbf{h}_t^{dec})$ | Sum to **1.0** |
| 5 | Joint Likelihood | $P(\mathbf{Y}\vert\mathbf{X}) = \prod_t P(y_t \vert y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$ | Chain rule |
| 6 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_t \log P(y_t^{*}\vert y_{<t}^{*}, \mathbf{c})$ | Minimized via SGD/Adam |
| 7 | LSTM Forget Gate | $\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_f [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f)$ | Range $\mathbf{0}$ to $\mathbf{1}$ |
| 8 | LSTM Input Gate | $\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_i [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i)$ | Range $\mathbf{0}$ to $\mathbf{1}$ |
| 9 | LSTM Cell State | $\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$ | Long-term memory |
| 10 | Attention Score (dot) | $e_{t,s} = \mathbf{h}_t^{dec \top} \mathbf{h}_s^{enc}$ | Pre-softmax alignment |
| 11 | Attention Weights | $\alpha_{t,s} = \dfrac{\exp(e_{t,s})}{\sum_{k} \exp(e_{t,k})}$ | Sum to **1.0** |
| 12 | Attention Context | $\mathbf{c}_t = \sum_s \alpha_{t,s} \mathbf{h}_s^{enc}$ | Dynamic per step |

## 2.4 The Information Bottleneck Problem

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Concept — The Bottleneck**
> The classic encoder-decoder forces an entire input sequence (which can be **100+ tokens**) into a *single fixed-size vector* of dimension $d$ (often **256** or **512**). For long sequences, this causes **information loss**, **forgetting of early tokens**, and **gradient vanishing**. This is precisely why **Bahdanau Attention (2014)** and later **Luong Attention (2015)** were introduced — and eventually led to the **Transformer** architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Machine Translation** (Google Translate pre-Transformer era).
- **Speech Recognition** (DeepSpeech 1 & 2).
- **Image Captioning** (CNN encoder + RNN decoder — *Show and Tell*, Vinyals et al.).
- **Chatbots & Dialogue Systems** (Vinyals & Le, 2015 — *Neural Conversational Model*).
- **Code Generation** (latent sequence-to-sequence models).
- **Time-Series Forecasting** in industrial IoT pipelines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Pass of a Vanilla Seq2Seq Model

We start with an input sentence tokenized into $T_x$ tokens: $\mathbf{X} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{T_x})$.

### Step 1: Embedding Lookup
Each token is converted into a dense vector using an embedding matrix $\mathbf{E} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times d_e}$, where $\vert V \vert$ is the vocabulary size.

$$\mathbf{e}_t = \mathbf{E} \cdot \text{one\_hot}(x_t)$$

This produces a sequence of embeddings $(\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{e}_{T_x})$.

### Step 2: Encoder Recurrence
For an RNN, the hidden state at time $t$ is updated using the previous hidden state and the current embedding:

$$\mathbf{h}_t^{enc} = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{hh} \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{enc} + \mathbf{W}_{xh} \mathbf{e}_t + \mathbf{b}_h)$$

The matrices are shared across all time steps (parameter tying), which allows the model to handle variable-length inputs efficiently.

### Step 3: Context Vector Formation
The final hidden state $\mathbf{h}_{T_x}^{enc}$ is the natural summary of the input. It is linearly projected and squashed through $\tanh$:

$$\mathbf{c} = \tanh(\mathbf{V}_c \mathbf{h}_{T_x}^{enc} + \mathbf{b}_c)$$

The $\tanh$ non-linearity centres the vector in $[-1, 1]^d$, stabilizing the initial decoder state.

### Step 4: Decoder Initialization
The decoder's first hidden state is set to the context vector:

$$\mathbf{h}_0^{dec} = \mathbf{c}$$

The decoder's first input is conventionally a special start token $\langle \text{BOS} \rangle$.

### Step 5: Decoder Recurrence (Autoregressive)
For $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T_y$, the decoder computes:

$$\mathbf{h}_t^{dec} = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{hh}' \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{dec} + \mathbf{W}_{yh} \mathbf{E}(y_{t-1}) + \mathbf{W}_{ch} \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{b}_h')$$

Note how $\mathbf{c}$ is fed at *every* time step — this is the standard *conditional* decoder.

### Step 6: Output Probability Distribution
The decoder hidden state is projected to vocabulary size and softmaxed:

$$\mathbf{o}_t = \mathbf{W}_{o} \mathbf{h}_t^{dec} + \mathbf{b}_o$$

$$P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{X}) = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{o}_t^{(k)})}{\sum_{j=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp(\mathbf{o}_t^{(j)})}, \quad \forall k \in \{1, \ldots, \vert V \vert\}$$

This gives a valid probability distribution over the entire vocabulary.

### Step 7: Joint Probability of Output Sequence
By the chain rule of probability:

$$P(\mathbf{Y} \mid \mathbf{X}; \theta) = \prod_{t=1}^{T_y} P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c}; \theta)$$

### Step 8: Loss Computation
Using the negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy):

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\log P(\mathbf{Y}^* \mid \mathbf{X}; \theta) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log P(y_t^* \mid y_{<t}^*, \mathbf{c}; \theta)$$

### Step 9: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)
Gradients are computed with respect to all parameters $\{\mathbf{W}_{hh}, \mathbf{W}_{xh}, \mathbf{W}_{o}, \mathbf{V}_c, \mathbf{E}, \ldots\}$ and updated via Adam or SGD with learning rate $\eta$:

$$\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bahdanau Additive Attention

The attention mechanism addresses the bottleneck by computing a *dynamic* context $\mathbf{c}_t$ at every decoder step.

### Step 1: Alignment Score
For each encoder hidden state $\mathbf{h}_s^{enc}$ and current decoder state $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{dec}$, compute a scalar alignment score:

$$e_{t,s} = \mathbf{v}_a^{\top} \tanh(\mathbf{W}_a \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{dec} + \mathbf{U}_a \mathbf{h}_s^{enc})$$

where $\mathbf{v}_a \in \mathbb{R}^{d_a}$, $\mathbf{W}_a \in \mathbb{R}^{d_a \times d}$, and $\mathbf{U}_a \in \mathbb{R}^{d_a \times d}$ are learnable parameters.

### Step 2: Softmax Normalization
Convert raw scores into a probability distribution over source positions:

$$\alpha_{t,s} = \frac{\exp(e_{t,s})}{\sum_{k=1}^{T_x} \exp(e_{t,k})}$$

### Step 3: Weighted Sum
The dynamic context vector is a weighted sum of encoder states:

$$\mathbf{c}_t = \sum_{s=1}^{T_x} \alpha_{t,s} \mathbf{h}_s^{enc}$$

### Step 4: Augmented Decoder State
The decoder now conditions on $\mathbf{c}_t$ rather than a single static $\mathbf{c}$:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t^{dec} = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_c [\mathbf{c}_t ; \mathbf{h}_t^{dec}])$$

This is the analytical essence of the attention-augmented encoder-decoder — the model learns *where to look* in the source sequence at every generation step.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Vanilla vs. Attention-Based

| Feature | Vanilla Encoder-Decoder | Attention-Based Encoder-Decoder |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Context vector | Single, static $\mathbf{c}$ | Dynamic $\mathbf{c}_t$ per step |
| Long-sequence performance | Poor (forgets early tokens) | Strong (selective focus) |
| Computational cost | $O(T_x + T_y)$ | $O(T_x \cdot T_y)$ |
| Interpretability | Low (black-box gist) | High (attention weights visualizable) |
| Gradient flow | Vanishing risk on long input | Direct short-cut paths |
| BLEU on long sentences | Degrades rapidly | Robust |
| Architecture | RNN/LSTM/GRU base | RNN/LSTM/GRU + attention layer |
| Foundation for | Early NMT | Transformer (multi-head attn) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Vanilla Encoder-Decoder Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Tokens x1, x2, ..., xTx] --> B[Embedding Layer E]
    B --> C[Encoder RNN/LSTM]
    C --> D[Context Vector c]
    D --> E[Decoder RNN/LSTM Init h0 = c]
    E --> F[Decoder Step t=1]
    F --> G[Decoder Step t=2]
    G --> H[Decoder Step t=Ty]
    H --> I[Softmax over Vocabulary]
    I --> J[Output Tokens y1, y2, ..., yTy]
    F -. autoregressive feedback .-> F
    G -. autoregressive feedback .-> G
    H -. autoregressive feedback .-> H
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Encoder-Decoder with Bahdanau Attention

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENC[Encoder Side]
        A1[Input Embedding e1] --> A2[LSTM Cell h1enc]
        A2 --> A3[LSTM Cell h2enc]
        A3 --> A4[LSTM Cell hTxenc]
        A2 --> S1[Stacked Encoder States]
        A3 --> S1
        A4 --> S1
    end
    subgraph ATT[Attention Mechanism]
        S1 --> AT[Alignment Score e_t_s]
        AT --> SM[Softmax alphas]
        SM --> CT[Dynamic Context c_t]
    end
    subgraph DEC[Decoder Side]
        Y0[Previous Token y_t-1] --> D1[Decoder LSTM]
        D1 --> DH[Decoder Hidden h_tdec]
        CT --> D1
        DH --> CONC[Concat h_t and c_t]
        CONC --> OUT[Softmax Output]
        OUT --> YT[Predicted Token y_t]
        YT -. next step input .-> Y0
    end
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Training vs. Inference (Teacher Forcing)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TR[Training Phase]
        T1[Decoder Predicts y_t] --> T2[Compare with Ground Truth y_star_t]
        T2 --> T3[Compute Cross-Entropy Loss]
        T3 --> T4[Backprop Through Time]
        T4 --> T5[Update Weights theta]
        T6[Ground Truth y_star_t-1] -. teacher forcing .-> T1
    end
    subgraph IN[Inference Phase]
        I1[Decoder Predicts y_t] --> I2[Argmax / Beam Search]
        I2 --> I3[Predicted Token y_t]
        I3 -. autoregressive .-> I1
    end
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Information Flow in the Bottleneck (Failure Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[x1] --> H1[h1]
    X2[x2] --> H2[h2]
    X3[x3] --> H3[h3]
    X4[x4] --> H4[h4]
    X5[x5] --> H5[h5]
    H1 --> S((c single fixed vector))
    H2 --> S
    H3 --> S
    H4 --> S
    H5 --> S
    S --> D1[y1]
    D1 --> D2[y2]
    D2 --> D3[y3]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation Aid:** All five hidden states must collapse into the single circular node $\mathbf{c}$. This visualizes the bottleneck. The attention-augmented version replaces $\mathbf{c}$ with a *distribution* over all encoder states.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, PEADT748, Module 3]**
**Q: Define the *Encoder-Decoder* architecture. What is the role of the *context vector* in a sequence-to-sequence model?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Encoder-Decoder is a neural architecture that maps a variable-length input sequence to a variable-length output sequence via two networks. The **encoder** reads the input tokens one by one and compresses them into a fixed-dimensional **context vector** $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$. The **decoder** uses $\mathbf{c}$ as its initial hidden state and generates the output sequence one token at a time in an autoregressive manner. The context vector acts as the *bottleneck information bottleneck* — it must encode all semantic information from the source sequence for the decoder to translate, summarize, or generate from. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for encoder role, 1 for context vector purpose.)*

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, PEADT748, Module 3]**
**Q: List any two limitations of the vanilla Encoder-Decoder model and briefly state how attention mechanisms overcome them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Limitation 1 — Information Bottleneck:** A single fixed-size context vector cannot retain all details of long input sequences. **Limitation 2 — Vanishing Gradients:** Long-range dependencies in the source are hard to learn via BPTT. **Attention overcomes these** by computing a *dynamic, per-step* context vector as a weighted sum of *all* encoder hidden states, allowing the decoder to directly access any source position and providing shorter gradient paths. *(3 marks — 1 mark per limitation + 1 mark for attention's role.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, PEADT748, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq) Encoder-Decoder model** used in machine translation. Clearly label the encoder, context vector, and decoder. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the forward-pass equations for the encoder and decoder of a vanilla Seq2Seq model with LSTM cells. Write the joint probability $P(\mathbf{Y} \vert \mathbf{X})$ using the chain rule and state the loss function. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks):**
The Seq2Seq architecture consists of two recurrent networks coupled by a context vector.
- **Encoder:** A stack of LSTM cells that reads the source tokens $x_1, \ldots, x_{T_x}$ and updates its hidden state and cell state at each time step. The final hidden state $\mathbf{h}_{T_x}^{enc}$ is projected through $\tanh$ to form $\mathbf{c}$.
- **Context Vector $\mathbf{c}$:** A fixed-dimensional latent representation that summarizes the entire source sentence.
- **Decoder:** An LSTM initialized with $\mathbf{h}_0^{dec} = \mathbf{c}$ that generates target tokens $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_{T_y}$ autoregressively. At each step, it produces a probability distribution over the target vocabulary via a softmax layer.
- **Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete schematic. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for encoder, 2 for decoder, 1 for context vector description.)*

**Part (b) — Derivations (7 marks):**

**Encoder forward pass:**

$$\mathbf{i}_t^{enc} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_i [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{enc}, \mathbf{e}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i)$$
$$\mathbf{f}_t^{enc} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_f [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{enc}, \mathbf{e}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f)$$
$$\mathbf{o}_t^{enc} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_o [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{enc}, \mathbf{e}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o)$$
$$\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t^{enc} = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_C [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{enc}, \mathbf{e}_t] + \mathbf{b}_C)$$
$$\mathbf{C}_t^{enc} = \mathbf{f}_t^{enc} \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1}^{enc} + \mathbf{i}_t^{enc} \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t^{enc}$$
$$\mathbf{h}_t^{enc} = \mathbf{o}_t^{enc} \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t^{enc})$$

**Context vector:**

$$\mathbf{c} = \tanh(\mathbf{V}_c \mathbf{h}_{T_x}^{enc} + \mathbf{b}_c)$$

**Decoder forward pass (initialized with $\mathbf{c}$):**

$$\mathbf{h}_0^{dec} = \mathbf{c}, \quad y_0 = \langle \text{BOS} \rangle$$

For $t = 1, \ldots, T_y$:

$$\mathbf{h}_t^{dec} = \text{LSTM}(y_{t-1}, \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{dec}, \mathbf{c})$$

$$P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{X}) = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_o \mathbf{h}_t^{dec} + \mathbf{b}_o)$$

**Joint probability (chain rule):**

$$P(\mathbf{Y} \mid \mathbf{X}; \theta) = \prod_{t=1}^{T_y} P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c}; \theta)$$

**Loss function (cross-entropy):**

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log P(y_t^{*} \mid y_{<t}^{*}, \mathbf{c}; \theta)$$

*(7 marks: 2 for encoder equations, 2 for context + decoder equations, 2 for joint probability, 1 for loss formulation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, PEADT748, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **information bottleneck problem** in vanilla Encoder-Decoder models. Why does this degrade translation quality on long sentences? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable equations, derive the working of the **Bahdanau (additive) attention mechanism**. Show how the dynamic context vector $\mathbf{c}_t$ is computed at each decoder step. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Bottleneck Problem (7 marks):**
The vanilla Seq2Seq model compresses an arbitrarily long source sequence (e.g., 50-word sentence) into a *single fixed-dimensional context vector* of size $d$ (typically 256 or 512). This creates three issues:
1. **Capacity Limit:** $d$ is much smaller than the information content of the source.
2. **Long-Range Forgetting:** Early tokens must be propagated through many recurrent steps, suffering gradient decay.
3. **Uniform Conditioning:** The decoder is forced to use the *same* $\mathbf{c}$ for every output token, even when different output words depend on different parts of the source.
Consequently, on long sentences, the BLEU score drops sharply. Empirical results from **Cho et al. (2014)** and **Sutskever et al. (2014)** show performance degradation when source length exceeds ~20 words. *(7 marks: 2 for defining the bottleneck, 2 for explaining information loss, 2 for empirical/qualitative evidence, 1 for consequences.)*

**Part (b) — Bahdanau Attention Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Alignment Score (additive form):**

$$e_{t,s} = \mathbf{v}_a^{\top} \tanh(\mathbf{W}_a \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{dec} + \mathbf{U}_a \mathbf{h}_s^{enc})$$

This is a learnable function that scores "how relevant is source position $s$ when generating target position $t$?"

**Step 2 — Softmax Normalization:**

$$\alpha_{t,s} = \frac{\exp(e_{t,s})}{\sum_{k=1}^{T_x} \exp(e_{t,k})}$$

The $\alpha_{t,s}$ values form a probability distribution over source positions at each decoding step.

**Step 3 — Dynamic Context Vector:**

$$\mathbf{c}_t = \sum_{s=1}^{T_x} \alpha_{t,s} \mathbf{h}_s^{enc}$$

This is a content-aware, soft alignment — a weighted average of all encoder states.

**Step 4 — Decoder Update with Attention:**

$$\mathbf{h}_t^{dec} = \text{LSTM}(y_{t-1}, \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{dec}, \mathbf{c}_t)$$

**Step 5 — Output:**

$$\tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_c [\mathbf{c}_t ; \mathbf{h}_t^{dec}])$$
$$P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{X}) = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_o \tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t)$$

This dynamic per-step $\mathbf{c}_t$ solves the bottleneck by allowing the decoder to *attend* to the most relevant source tokens for each output word. *(7 marks: 2 for alignment score, 2 for softmax weights, 2 for dynamic context, 1 for decoder integration.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing encoder output with context vector:** The encoder's *final hidden state* is one common choice for $\mathbf{c}$, but it is *not* identical — there is usually a learnable projection $\mathbf{V}_c$ applied. Students often skip this distinction. (**−1 mark**)
> 2. **Forgetting the chain rule:** When asked for the joint probability, students often write $P(y_t)$ instead of $P(y_t \vert y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$. The autoregressive conditioning is essential. (**−1 mark**)
> 3. **Mixing up Bahdanau and Luong attention:** Bahdanau is *additive* (uses a feed-forward network with $\tanh$), Luong is *multiplicative* (uses dot-product or general/bilinear forms). Examiners check this distinction. (**−2 marks** if swapped)
> 4. **Ignoring the $\langle \text{BOS} \rangle$ and $\langle \text{EOS} \rangle$ tokens:** These special tokens are required for proper start/stop signalling in autoregressive decoders.
> 5. **Writing the loss as MSE:** For sequence generation, it is **categorical cross-entropy**, not mean-squared error. (**−2 marks**)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Recap — Encoder-Decoder Models**

- **Core idea:** Compress input $\rightarrow$ generate output. Encoder reads; decoder writes.
- **Context vector $\mathbf{c}$** is the *bottleneck* — fixed-dimensional latent summary of the input.
- **Forward pass equations** use tanh/softmax activations and recurrence (RNN/LSTM/GRU).
- **Joint probability** of output sequence uses the *chain rule* across all decoding time steps.
- **Loss function** is **categorical cross-entropy** of ground-truth tokens; optimized via BPTT.
- **Teacher forcing** feeds ground-truth $y_{t-1}^*$ during training (not inference).
- **Inference** is autoregressive: predicted $y_t$ is fed as $y_{t-1}$ for the next step.
- **Beam search / nucleus sampling** are used at inference (not greedy) for better generation quality.
- **Bottleneck problem** arises because a single $\mathbf{c}$ cannot summarize arbitrarily long inputs.
- **Attention mechanism** solves the bottleneck by producing a *dynamic* per-step context $\mathbf{c}_t$.
- **Bahdanau attention** is *additive*; **Luong attention** is *multiplicative* — remember the distinction.
- **Attention weights $\alpha_{t,s}$** are softmax-normalized alignment scores; they are interpretable.
- **LSTM** uses three gates: forget $\mathbf{f}_t$, input $\mathbf{i}_t$, output $\mathbf{o}_t$ + cell state $\mathbf{C}_t$.
- **GRU** is a lighter alternative with only reset and update gates.
- **Applications:** Machine translation, summarization, image captioning, speech recognition, chatbots.
- **Historical significance:** This architecture is the *direct precursor* to the **Transformer** (Vaswani et al., 2017), which replaces recurrence entirely with multi-head self-attention.
- **BLEU score** range is $\mathbf{0.0}$ to $\mathbf{1.0}$; **Perplexity** is $e^{\mathcal{L}}$; lower PPL is better.
- **Common hidden dimensions** in practice: $\mathbf{256}$, $\mathbf{512}$, or $\mathbf{1024}$.
- **Gradient issues:** BPTT on long sequences suffers from vanishing/exploding gradients — hence the rise of attention and Transformers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Question-Answer Generator using RNN and Encoder-Decoder

# Question-Answer Generator using RNN and Encoder-Decoder

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Question-Answer (QA) Generator** built on the **RNN-based Encoder–Decoder (Seq2Seq) paradigm** is a generative neural architecture that maps a variable-length input sequence (the *context* passage and/or the *question*) into a variable-length output sequence (the natural-language *answer*). Formally, it learns a conditional probability distribution:

$$P(Y \mid X) = \prod_{t=1}^{T_y} P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X; \theta)$$

where $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{T_x})$ is the encoded input sequence, $Y = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{T_y})$ is the generated answer, and $\theta$ denotes the learnable parameters of the network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The *Encoder* compresses the input question + context into a fixed-dimensional **thought vector** (context vector $c$), and the *Decoder* unrolls this vector token-by-token to produce the answer. This is the canonical **Sutskever–Vinyals–Le (2014) Seq2Seq** framework adapted for QA.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator" Mental Model

Imagine a **bilingual interpreter at a United Nations press conference**:
- The **encoder** is the interpreter *listening* to a long speech in English. While listening, they mentally summarize the entire speech into a single, dense "mental note" (the context vector).
- The **decoder** is the same interpreter now *speaking* the answer in fluent, grammatically correct sentences — word by word — referring back to that mental note.
- If the speech is very long, the interpreter may *forget* early parts — this is the famous **information bottleneck** of vanilla Seq2Seq, which later motivated the **Attention Mechanism** (Bahdanau et al., 2015).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for QA:** Real questions and contexts can span hundreds of words. Pure RNN encoders struggle to retain everything, which is why modern systems stack **LSTM/GRU cells** and add **attention** so the decoder can "look back" at relevant input tokens at every generation step.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Token** | Atomic unit of text (word, sub-word, or character) |
| **Embedding** | Dense vector representation of a token (e.g., 256-D) |
| **Hidden State** $h_t$ | RNN's running memory at step $t$ |
| **Context Vector** $c$ | Final encoder state summarizing the entire input |
| **Teacher Forcing** | Training trick where ground-truth tokens are fed as next decoder input |
| **Beam Width** $k$ | Number of candidate sequences kept during inference |
| **BLEU / ROUGE** | Standard n-gram overlap evaluation metrics for generated text |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The RNN Building Block

A vanilla **Recurrent Neural Network** maintains a hidden state that is updated at every time step as new tokens arrive.

### 2.1.1 Vanilla RNN Update Rule

$$h_t = \tanh\bigl(W_{hh}\,h_{t-1} + W_{xh}\,x_t + b_h\bigr)$$
$$y_t = \mathrm{softmax}\bigl(W_{hy}\,h_t + b_y\bigr)$$

where:
- $W_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_h}$ — recurrent weight matrix
- $W_{xh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_e}$ — input-to-hidden weight matrix
- $W_{hy} \in \mathbb{R}^{|V| \times d_h}$ — hidden-to-output weight matrix
- $d_h$ = hidden dimension, $d_e$ = embedding dimension, $|V|$ = vocabulary size

### 2.1.2 The Vanishing Gradient Problem in Vanilla RNNs

Through back-propagation through time (BPTT), gradients scale as:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_1} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_T} \prod_{t=2}^{T} \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$$

For long sequences, the Jacobian product collapses to near-zero (when $|\lambda_{\max} \cdot W_{hh}| < 1$), causing **long-range dependencies to be forgotten**. This is the primary motivation for **LSTM** and **GRU**.

### 2.1.3 LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) Equations

The LSTM introduces three gates and a cell state $C_t$ to preserve long-range information.

$$f_t = \sigma\bigl(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f\bigr) \quad \text{(forget gate)}$$
$$i_t = \sigma\bigl(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i\bigr) \quad \text{(input gate)}$$
$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh\bigl(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C\bigr) \quad \text{(candidate cell state)}$$
$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \quad \text{(cell-state update)}$$
$$o_t = \sigma\bigl(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o\bigr) \quad \text{(output gate)}$$
$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t) \quad \text{(hidden-state output)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\odot$ denotes **element-wise (Hadamard) product** and $\sigma$ is the logistic sigmoid $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$. For QA tasks, **Bi-directional LSTMs (BiLSTM)** are typically used in the encoder so that each token sees both left and right context.

### 2.1.4 GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit) — Lighter Alternative

$$z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t]) \quad \text{(update gate)}$$
$$r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t]) \quad \text{(reset gate)}$$
$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t])$$
$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

GRU uses only **2 gates** instead of 3, making it ~25% faster to train while delivering comparable accuracy on most QA benchmarks.

## 2.2 The Encoder–Decoder (Seq2Seq) Architecture

### 2.2.1 Encoder Phase

The encoder reads the input token stream $X = (x_1, \dots, x_{T_x})$ and produces a sequence of hidden states. The **final hidden state** is treated as the context vector $c$:

$$c = h_{T_x}^{\,\text{encoder}}$$

For an LSTM encoder, $c$ is typically the concatenation (or sum) of the final forward and backward cell states:

$$c = \bigl[\,\overrightarrow{h}_{T_x}\,;\,\overleftarrow{h}_{T_x}\,\bigr]$$

### 2.2.2 Decoder Phase

The decoder is also an RNN (often LSTM) initialized with $c$. At each step $t$ it predicts the next answer token $y_t$:

$$s_t = f(s_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, c)$$
$$P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X) = \mathrm{softmax}\bigl(W_s\,s_t + b_s\bigr)$$

where $s_t$ is the decoder hidden state and $f$ is an LSTM/GRU cell. Generation continues until an `<EOS>` (end-of-sentence) token is sampled.

### 2.2.3 The Information Bottleneck

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Hot-Spot:** A very common 7-mark question asks *why plain Seq2Seq fails on long contexts*. The answer: the entire input must be compressed into a **single fixed-size vector $c$**. When $T_x$ is large, $c$ becomes a lossy summary, hurting answer quality. The **Attention Mechanism** (Section 2.3) is the canonical fix.

## 2.3 Attention Mechanism (Bahdanau / Additive Attention)

Instead of forcing the decoder to look at one vector $c$, attention lets it re-weigh the **entire encoder state sequence** $(h_1^e, h_2^e, \dots, h_{T_x}^e)$ at every decoding step.

### 2.3.1 Alignment Score

For decoder state $s_{t-1}$ and encoder state $h_i^e$:

$$e_{t,i} = v_a^{\top}\,\tanh\bigl(W_a\,s_{t-1} + U_a\,h_i^e\bigr) \quad \text{(Bahdanau)}$$

$$e_{t,i} = s_{t-1}^{\top}\,W_a\,h_i^e \quad \text{(Luong — dot / general / concat)}$$

### 2.3.2 Attention Weights (Softmax over inputs)

$$\alpha_{t,i} = \frac{\exp(e_{t,i})}{\sum_{j=1}^{T_x} \exp(e_{t,j})}$$

### 2.3.3 Dynamic Context Vector

$$c_t = \sum_{i=1}^{T_x} \alpha_{t,i}\,h_i^e$$

The decoder then uses $c_t$ (instead of a static $c$) to update its state and emit $y_t$:

$$s_t = f(s_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, c_t)$$
$$y_t \sim \mathrm{softmax}\bigl(W_o\,[s_t; c_t]\bigr)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $\alpha_{t,i}$ can be **visualized as a heat-map** showing which input words the model "looked at" while generating each output word. This is a frequent KTU exam topic and a key interpretability tool in NLP.

## 2.4 Training Objective — Cross-Entropy Loss

The QA Generator is trained to **minimize the negative log-likelihood** of the gold answer:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\sum_{t=1}^{T_y^{(n)}} \log P_\theta\bigl(y_t^{(n)} \mid y_{<t}^{(n)}, X^{(n)}\bigr)$$

Optimized via **Adam** or **RMSProp** with mini-batch stochastic gradient descent.

## 2.5 Inference Strategies

| Strategy | Procedure | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Greedy Decoding** | Pick $\arg\max$ token at each step | Fast, but sub-optimal (no backtracking) |
| **Beam Search** | Keep top-$k$ partial hypotheses | Higher BLEU, $k\times$ slower |
| **Sampling + Temperature** | Sample with $\mathrm{softmax}(\cdot/\tau)$ | Diverse answers, $\tau<1$ = sharper |
| **Top-$k$ / Nucleus** | Sample from smallest set covering $p\%$ mass | Balanced quality & diversity |

## 2.6 Evaluation Metrics

### 2.6.1 BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy)

$$\mathrm{BLEU}_N = \mathrm{BP} \cdot \exp\Bigl(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\Bigr)$$

where $p_n$ is the modified $n$-gram precision and $\mathrm{BP}$ is the **brevity penalty**:

$$\mathrm{BP} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ e^{1 - r/c} & \text{if } c \le r \end{cases}$$

with $c$ = candidate length, $r$ = reference length.

### 2.6.2 ROUGE-L (Longest Common Subsequence based)

$$R_{\mathrm{lcs}} = \frac{\mathrm{LCS}(X, Y)}{m}, \quad P_{\mathrm{lcs}} = \frac{\mathrm{LCS}(X, Y)}{n}$$
$$F_{\mathrm{lcs}} = \frac{(1+\beta^2)\,R_{\mathrm{lcs}}\,P_{\mathrm{lcs}}}{R_{\mathrm{lcs}} + \beta^2\,P_{\mathrm{lcs}}}$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Use-Case in QA |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h_t = \tanh(W_{hh}h_{t-1} + W_{xh}x_t + b)$ | Vanilla RNN update |
| 2 | $f_t = \sigma(W_f[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_f)$ | LSTM forget gate |
| 3 | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | LSTM cell state |
| 4 | $z_t = \sigma(W_z[h_{t-1},x_t])$ | GRU update gate |
| 5 | $c = h_{T_x}^{\text{enc}}$ | Context vector (vanilla) |
| 6 | $e_{t,i} = v_a^\top\tanh(W_a s_{t-1} + U_a h_i^e)$ | Bahdanau attention score |
| 7 | $\alpha_{t,i} = \mathrm{softmax}_i(e_{t,i})$ | Attention weight |
| 8 | $c_t = \sum_i \alpha_{t,i} h_i^e$ | Dynamic context |
| 9 | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_t \log P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X)$ | Cross-entropy loss |
| 10 | $\mathrm{BLEU} = \mathrm{BP}\cdot\exp(\sum_n w_n \log p_n)$ | Generation quality |
| 11 | $F_{\mathrm{lcs}}$ | ROUGE-L F1 |
| 12 | $\mathrm{BP} = e^{1-r/c}$ for $c\le r$ | Brevity penalty |

> [!TIP]
> Memorize equations 5–8 (the **attention block**) and equation 9 (**loss function**) — these appear in almost every KTU 14-mark question on Seq2Seq QA.
