# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From RNN to Encoder–Decoder

### Step 1 — Conditional Probability of an Answer

We want the model to assign high probability to the *correct* answer $Y^*$ given the input $X$. By the **chain rule of probability**:

$$P(Y \mid X) = P(y_1 \mid X)\,P(y_2 \mid y_1, X)\,P(y_3 \mid y_{1:2}, X) \cdots P(y_{T_y} \mid y_{<T_y}, X)$$

$$\therefore \; P(Y \mid X) = \prod_{t=1}^{T_y} P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X)$$

### Step 2 — Parameterize Each Factor with an RNN Decoder

At decoding step $t$, the decoder RNN consumes the previous output $y_{t-1}$ and the context $c$ to update its hidden state $s_t$:

$$s_t = \phi\bigl(s_{t-1},\,y_{t-1},\,c\bigr)$$

where $\phi$ is a non-linear RNN cell (LSTM/GRU). The probability factor is then:

$$P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X) = \mathrm{softmax}_t\bigl(W_s\,s_t + b_s\bigr) = \frac{\exp\bigl((W_s s_t + b_s)_{y_t}\bigr)}{\sum_{v \in V} \exp\bigl((W_s s_t + b_s)_v\bigr)}$$

### Step 3 — Encode the Input into a Single Vector

The encoder runs from $t = 1$ to $T_x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_1^e &= \phi_e(x_1,\,0) \\
h_2^e &= \phi_e(x_2,\,h_1^e) \\
&\;\vdots \\
h_{T_x}^e &= \phi_e(x_{T_x},\,h_{T_x-1}^e)
\end{aligned}
$$

The **context vector** is the final hidden state:

$$c \;=\; h_{T_x}^e$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **BiLSTM encoder**, $c = [\overrightarrow{h}_{T_x}^e ; \overleftarrow{h}_{T_x}^e]$, doubling the dimensionality but capturing information from both directions.

### Step 4 — Initialization of the Decoder

The decoder's first hidden state is set to a transformed version of $c$:

$$s_0 = \tanh\bigl(W_{\text{init}}\,c + b_{\text{init}}\bigr)$$

This is a learnable projection that conditions the decoder's "starting mood" on the encoded input.

## 3.2 Derivation: Bahdanau Attention Step-by-Step

**Problem:** The static vector $c$ is a bottleneck. Let's derive a *dynamic* context $c_t$ for each decoder step $t$.

### Step 1 — Compute Alignment Scores

For every encoder position $i \in \{1, \dots, T_x\}$ and decoder step $t$:

$$e_{t,i} = v_a^{\top}\,\tanh\bigl(W_a\,s_{t-1} + U_a\,h_i^e\bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{R}$$

Each $e_{t,i}$ is a **scalar similarity** between the previous decoder state and the $i$-th encoder state.

### Step 2 — Softmax Normalization

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{t,i} &= \frac{\exp(e_{t,i})}{\sum_{j=1}^{T_x} \exp(e_{t,j})} \\
\text{such that} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{T_x} \alpha_{t,i} &= 1, \quad \alpha_{t,i} \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Weighted Sum → Dynamic Context

$$c_t = \sum_{i=1}^{T_x} \alpha_{t,i}\,h_i^e \;\in\; \mathbb{R}^{2d_h}$$

### Step 4 — Decoder Update with Dynamic Context

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_t &= \phi\bigl(s_{t-1},\,y_{t-1},\,c_t\bigr) \\
\tilde{s}_t &= \tanh\bigl(W_c\,[s_t; c_t] + b_c\bigr) \\
P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X) &= \mathrm{softmax}\bigl(W_s\,\tilde{s}_t + b_s\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the input is single-vector (vanilla Seq2Seq), $c_t = c$ for all $t$, recovering the original formulation. Attention is therefore a strict **generalization** of vanilla encoder-decoder.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cross-Entropy Loss for QA

Suppose we have $N$ training pairs $\{(X^{(n)}, Y^{(n)})\}_{n=1}^{N}$. The **maximum-likelihood estimate** of $\theta$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta^{*} &= \arg\max_\theta \prod_{n=1}^{N} P_\theta\bigl(Y^{(n)} \mid X^{(n)}\bigr) \\
&= \arg\max_\theta \prod_{n=1}^{N}\prod_{t=1}^{T_y^{(n)}} P_\theta\bigl(y_t^{(n)} \mid y_{<t}^{(n)}, X^{(n)}\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the **negative log** (and the average over $N$) to get a minimization objective:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\sum_{t=1}^{T_y^{(n)}} \log P_\theta\bigl(y_t^{(n)} \mid y_{<t}^{(n)}, X^{(n)}\bigr)$$

Substituting the softmax:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\sum_{t=1}^{T_y^{(n)}} \Bigl[(W_s s_t^{(n)} + b_s)_{y_t^{(n)}} - \log\sum_{v} \exp(W_s s_t^{(n)} + b_s)_v\Bigr]$$

This is the **exact loss** optimized by Adam in any standard Seq2Seq QA trainer.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Vanilla vs Attention Seq2Seq for QA

| Property | Vanilla Seq2Seq | Attention Seq2Seq |
|---|---|---|
| **Context vector** | One static $c$ | One dynamic $c_t$ per step |
| **Input length $T_x$** | Performance degrades sharply | Robust for $T_x > 200$ |
| **Interpretability** | Opaque | Attention heat-maps |
| **Parameters** | $\mathcal{O}(d^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(d^2 + d \cdot T_x)$ |
| **Train speed** | Faster per step | ~10–15% slower per step |
| **BLEU on SQuAD-style** | Lower | +3 to +8 BLEU points |
| **Best for** | Short Q-A ( $<30$ tokens) | Long-context QA |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Toy)

**Setup:** Vocab $V = \{a, b, c, \langle eos\rangle\}$, $d_h = 2$, single-layer LSTM encoder + decoder with attention. Input question tokens = $(a, b)$.

**Step 1** — Encoder reads $x_1 = a$:
$$
h_1^e = \mathrm{LSTM}(x_1, 0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.21 \\ -0.15 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2** — Encoder reads $x_2 = b$:
$$
h_2^e = \mathrm{LSTM}(x_2, h_1^e) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.47 \\ 0.33 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3** — Static context $c = h_2^e = [0.47,\;0.33]^{\top}$.

**Step 4** — Decoder step 1, $s_0 = [0,0]^{\top}$, no previous token. Compute alignment scores (assume $v_a = [1,1]^{\top},\;W_a = U_a = I$):

$$
e_{1,1} = v_a^{\top}\tanh(s_0 + h_1^e) = [1,1]\begin{bmatrix} 0.21 \\ -0.15 \end{bmatrix}_{\tanh} \approx 0.06
$$

$$
e_{1,2} = v_a^{\top}\tanh(s_0 + h_2^e) = [1,1]\begin{bmatrix} 0.47 \\ 0.33 \end{bmatrix}_{\tanh} \approx 0.67
$$

**Step 5** — Softmax:
$$
\alpha_{1,1} = \frac{e^{0.06}}{e^{0.06}+e^{0.67}} \approx 0.35, \quad \alpha_{1,2} \approx 0.65
$$

**Step 6** — Dynamic context:
$$
c_1 = 0.35\,h_1^e + 0.65\,h_2^e = 0.35\begin{bmatrix}0.21\\-0.15\end{bmatrix} + 0.65\begin{bmatrix}0.47\\0.33\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.379\\0.162\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7** — Decoder state update gives $s_1$; softmax over vocabulary emits the first answer token, e.g., $\hat{y}_1 = c$.

This is then fed back as $y_0$ for the next decoding step until `<eos>` is produced.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level QA Generator Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Input Question Tokens] --> EMB_Q[Question Embedding Layer]
    C[Input Context Passage] --> EMB_C[Context Embedding Layer]
    EMB_Q --> CONCAT[Concatenation / Fusion]
    EMB_C --> CONCAT
    CONCAT --> ENC[Encoder BiLSTM Stack]
    ENC --> H_E[Encoder Hidden States H1 to HTx]
    H_E --> ATT[Attention Layer]
    DEC[Decoder LSTM Initialized with Context c] --> ATT
    ATT --> CT[Dynamic Context Vector c_t]
    CT --> DEC
    DEC --> OUT[Softmax over Vocabulary]
    OUT --> ANS[Generated Answer Tokens y1 to yTy]
```

## 4.2 Encoder–Decoder Unrolled in Time

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENC_BLOCK[ENCODER BiLSTM]
        direction LR
        x1[x1] --> e1[h1e]
        x2[x2] --> e2[h2e]
        x3[x3] --> e3[h3e]
        xT[xTx] --> eT[hTx e]
    end
    e1 --> ATT_LAYER[Attention]
    e2 --> ATT_LAYER
    e3 --> ATT_LAYER
    eT --> ATT_LAYER
    ATT_LAYER --> C0[c0 c1 ... cT]
    subgraph DEC_BLOCK[DECODER LSTM]
        direction LR
        d0[s0] --> d1[s1]
        d1 --> d2[s2]
        d2 --> d3[s3]
    end
    C0 --> d0
    C0 --> d1
    C0 --> d2
    C0 --> d3
    d1 --> y1[y1]
    d2 --> y2[y2]
    d3 --> y3[y3 EOS]
```

## 4.3 Attention Mechanism Internals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ST[s sub t-1 decoder state] --> PROJ1[W_a s sub t-1]
    HI[h sub i encoder state] --> PROJ2[U_a h sub i]
    PROJ1 --> TANH[tanh and v_a transposed]
    PROJ2 --> TANH
    TANH --> E[e sub t,i scalar score]
    E --> SOFT[Softmax over all i from 1 to Tx]
    SOFT --> ALPHA[alpha sub t,i attention weights]
    HI --> WSUM[Weighted Sum over i]
    ALPHA --> WSUM
    WSUM --> CT[c sub t dynamic context vector]
```

## 4.4 Training vs Inference Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRAIN_PHASE[TRAINING PHASE Teacher Forcing]
        Q1[Gold Answer y star t-1] --> DEC1[Decoder]
        C1[Encoder Context c] --> DEC1
        DEC1 --> P1[Predicted y hat t]
        P1 --> LOSS[Cross Entropy vs Gold y star t]
        Q1 --> LOSS
    end
    subgraph INFER_PHASE[INFERENCE PHASE Autoregressive]
        Q2[Previously Sampled y hat t-1] --> DEC2[Decoder]
        C2[Encoder Context c] --> DEC2
        DEC2 --> P2[Logits over Vocabulary]
        P2 --> SEL[Greedy or Beam Search Selector]
        SEL --> Q2
        SEL --> FINAL[Output Sequence y hat 1 ... T]
    end
```

## 4.5 Data Pipeline for QA Training

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RAW[Raw Text Corpora SQuAD / WikiQA] --> CLEAN[Cleaning Tokenization Lowercasing]
    CLEAN --> VOCAB[Build Vocabulary min freq 2]
    VOCAB --> PAIR[Create Q Context A Triples]
    PAIR --> PAD[Pad Sequences to Fixed Length]
    PAD --> BATCH[Batch Size 32 Shuffle]
    BATCH --> MODEL[Encoder Decoder Seq2Seq with Attention]
    MODEL --> CKPT[Save Checkpoint Every Epoch]
    CKPT --> EVAL[Compute BLEU and ROUGE on Dev Set]
    EVAL --> DEC{Converged}
    DEC -- Yes --> STOP[Stop Training]
    DEC -- No --> BATCH
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> All questions are mapped to **Course Outcomes (CO3 / CO4)** and **Bloom's Levels (L3–L5)** as per the 2024 Scheme.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, CO3, L2]`
**Define the Encoder-Decoder architecture for a Question-Answer generation system. What is the role of the context vector?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **Encoder** is an RNN (typically BiLSTM) that processes the input question + context token sequence and produces a fixed-dimensional **context vector** $c$ (often the final hidden state). **(1 mark)**
- The **Decoder** is another RNN initialized with $c$ that generates the answer token-by-token, conditioning each step on the previous generated token and the context. **(1 mark)**
- The **context vector** acts as a compressed summary of the input, serving as the decoder's only bridge to the question and passage. Its limitation motivates **attention mechanisms**. **(1 mark)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, CO3, L2]`
**List and briefly explain any three gates of an LSTM cell. Why are they preferred over vanilla RNN cells for QA?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Forget gate** $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ — decides what to discard from the previous cell state. **(1 mark)**
- **Input gate** $i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ — controls what new information to write into the cell state. **(1 mark)**
- **Output gate** $o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ — regulates what parts of the cell state become the next hidden state. **(1 mark)**
- LSTMs are preferred because their **additive cell-state updates** mitigate the vanishing-gradient problem, enabling retention of long-range dependencies in long passages.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, CO3 + CO4, L4]`

**Question A (a):** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Encoder-Decoder architecture for a Question-Answer generation system using RNN/LSTM**. Discuss the role of the context vector and highlight its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** Show a left-to-right flow — *Input Tokens → Embedding → Encoder (BiLSTM stack) → Context Vector $c$ → Decoder (LSTM) → Softmax → Output Token → [loop back]*.
2. **Encoder phase (2 marks):** The encoder reads the question-context pair token by token, updating hidden states $h_t^e$ using LSTM equations. The final state (or its bidirectional concatenation) forms the context vector $c$.
3. **Decoder phase (2 marks):** The decoder is initialized with $s_0 = \tanh(W_{\text{init}}c + b_{\text{init}})$. At step $t$, it computes $s_t = \mathrm{LSTM}(s_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, c)$ and outputs $P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X) = \mathrm{softmax}(W_s s_t + b_s)$.
4. **Limitations (1 mark):** Single fixed-size $c$ is a **bottleneck**; long contexts lose information, motivating attention.

**Question A (b):** Derive the **Bahdanau attention mechanism** mathematically. Show how the dynamic context vector $c_t$ is computed at each decoder step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Motivation (1 mark):** Replace static $c$ with a position-aware dynamic $c_t$.
2. **Alignment score (2 marks):**
$$e_{t,i} = v_a^{\top}\,\tanh(W_a s_{t-1} + U_a h_i^e)$$
3. **Attention weights via softmax (2 marks):**
$$\alpha_{t,i} = \frac{\exp(e_{t,i})}{\sum_{j=1}^{T_x} \exp(e_{t,j})}, \quad \sum_i \alpha_{t,i} = 1$$
4. **Dynamic context (1 mark):**
$$c_t = \sum_{i=1}^{T_x} \alpha_{t,i}\,h_i^e$$
5. **Decoder integration (1 mark):** $s_t = \mathrm{LSTM}(s_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, c_t)$; output $y_t \sim \mathrm{softmax}(W_s [s_t; c_t] + b_s)$.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, CO3 + CO4, L4]`

**Question B (a):** Explain how **teacher forcing** is used to train a Seq2Seq QA generator. Compare **greedy decoding** and **beam search** for inference, stating their pros and cons. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Teacher Forcing — concept (2 marks):** During training, the ground-truth token $y_{t-1}^*$ is fed as input to the decoder at step $t$ (instead of the model's own previous prediction $y_{t-1}$). This stabilizes training and accelerates convergence.
2. **Teacher Forcing — caveat (1 mark):** Creates a **train–inference mismatch** (*exposure bias*) — fixed by **scheduled sampling** where ground-truth is replaced by the model's own output with probability $\epsilon$ that decays over epochs.
3. **Greedy Decoding (2 marks):** At each step, pick $\hat{y}_t = \arg\max_v P(v \mid y_{<t}, X)$. **Pros:** $\mathcal{O}(T_y \cdot |V|)$ — extremely fast. **Cons:** No backtracking; can fall into suboptimal sequences.
4. **Beam Search (2 marks):** Maintain top-$k$ partial hypotheses; expand each by one token; keep the best $k$. **Pros:** Higher BLEU, $\sim$5–10% improvement. **Cons:** $k\times$ slower; can favor generic answers.

**Question B (b):** Derive the **cross-entropy training objective** for the QA generator. Define the **BLEU metric** and compute $\mathrm{BLEU}_1$ for a toy example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Cross-entropy derivation (3 marks):** Maximize $P(Y \mid X) = \prod_t P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X)$. Take negative log and average over the dataset:
$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\sum_{t=1}^{T_y^{(n)}} \log \mathrm{softmax}\bigl(W_s s_t^{(n)} + b_s\bigr)_{y_t^{(n)}}$$
Optimized via **Adam** with learning rate $\eta \approx 10^{-3}$.

2. **BLEU definition (2 marks):**
$$\mathrm{BLEU}_N = \mathrm{BP} \cdot \exp\Bigl(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\Bigr)$$
with $w_n = 1/N$ (uniform), $p_n$ = clipped $n$-gram precision, and $\mathrm{BP}$ = brevity penalty.

3. **Toy computation (2 marks):**
   - Reference: *"the cat sat on the mat"* (6 tokens, 6 bigrams)
   - Candidate: *"the cat sat on a mat"* (6 tokens)
   - Unigram precision: $5/6$ (all words match except *a*).
   - $\mathrm{BLEU}_1 = \exp(\log(5/6)) \cdot \mathrm{BP} = 5/6 \approx 0.833$ (since $c = r = 6$, $\mathrm{BP}=1$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Encoder context with Attention context.** Vanilla $c$ is *static*; attention $c_t$ is *dynamic per step*. Examiners dock 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Always show the softmax normalization** in attention derivations — students frequently write $\alpha_{t,i} = \exp(e_{t,i})$ without the denominator.
> 3. **Forgetting the brevity penalty** in BLEU — a single mark is reserved for this in any BLEU-related question.
> 4. **Teacher Forcing ≠ Curriculum Learning** — they are different. Do not mix them up in long-answer questions.
> 5. **Mention exposure bias** when discussing teacher forcing for full marks on Part B.
> 6. **Label axes / blocks** in all diagrams — unmarked diagrams typically lose 1 mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Seq2Seq = Encoder + Decoder**; the bridge is the **context vector $c$**.
- ✅ Encoder is usually a **BiLSTM**; decoder is a **unidirectional LSTM/GRU**.
- ✅ $c$ is the final encoder state (or its bidirectional concat): $c = h_{T_x}^e$.
- ✅ Vanilla Seq2Seq suffers from the **information bottleneck** on long contexts.
- ✅ **Bahdanau attention** computes a scalar score $e_{t,i}$, normalizes via softmax to get $\alpha_{t,i}$, and forms a dynamic context $c_t = \sum_i \alpha_{t,i} h_i^e$.
- ✅ **Luong attention** uses a simpler dot/general/concat form of $e_{t,i}$.
- ✅ Training loss = **negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy)**; optimized with **Adam**.
- ✅ **Teacher forcing** uses gold tokens during training; **scheduled sampling** fixes the train–inference mismatch.
- ✅ **Greedy decoding** = fast but sub-optimal; **Beam search** = top-$k$ for higher BLEU.
- ✅ Evaluation: **BLEU** (precision-oriented, brevity penalty) and **ROUGE-L** (recall-oriented, LCS).
- ✅ Visualize attention as a **heatmap** for interpretability — a frequent viva question.
- ✅ Modern variants use **GRU** for speed, **BiLSTM** for accuracy, and **sub-word tokenization (BPE)** for vocabulary efficiency.
- ✅ Pre-Transformer baseline: **LSTM-Seq2Seq + Attention** is the canonical *before-Transformer* QA model.
- ✅ Total parameter count of an LSTM layer: $4 \times d_h \times (d_h + d_e)$ — useful for viva numerics.
- ✅ Always use **<BOS>** to start decoding and **<EOS>** to terminate; never use both interchangeably.

> **Recommended Study Order for KTU Exam Prep:**
> RNN → LSTM/GRU → Encoder-Decoder → Attention (Bahdanau) → Cross-Entropy Loss → Decoding Strategies → BLEU/ROUGE → Worked Example.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generative AI Architecture** refers to the structural blueprint of neural network systems designed to *learn the underlying probability distribution* of a training dataset and *sample novel, synthetic data points* from that learned distribution. The architecture typically comprises an **Encoder**, a **Latent Space (Bottleneck)**, and a **Decoder/Generator**, often paired with an auxiliary **Discriminator/Critic** network that guides training via adversarial or likelihood-based feedback signals.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework for **PEADT748 (Generative AI)**, the term **"Architecture"** in Module 3 encompasses the four foundational generative paradigms:

1. **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)** — the *Generator–Discriminator* dyad.
2. **Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)** — the *Encoder–Latent–Decoder* pipeline.
3. **Diffusion Models (DMs)** — the *Forward (Noising) and Reverse (Denoising)* Markov chain.
4. **Autoregressive / Transformer Generators** — the *Token-by-Token* prediction stack.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Art Forgery Detective" Story

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Generator as a Counterfeit Artist and a Discriminator as a Detective.**

Imagine a closed room where a **Counterfeit Artist (Generator, G)** tries to paint fake currency notes. Outside the room sits a **Detective (Discriminator, D)** who is shown both real currency (from the training dataset) and the fakes, and must classify each as *real* or *fake*. The Artist never sees the real notes — only feedback ("you fooled the detective" or "you got caught"). Over thousands of rounds:

- The **Artist upgrades his techniques** to produce fakes indistinguishable from real notes.
- The **Detective sharpens his forensic skills** to catch subtler flaws.

This adversarial tug-of-war is the **core engine** of GAN architecture. For VAEs, replace the Detective with a **Cartographer** who compresses the world into a compact map (the *latent space*), and the Artist redraws the world from that map. For Diffusion Models, imagine gradually sprinkling dust on a photograph until it becomes pure static, then training a model to **reverse** this corruption, dusting it off frame by frame.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants and metrics are universally referenced in Generative AI architecture literature and KTU 2024 evaluation:

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $z$ | Latent vector sampled from prior | $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ |
| $d_z$ | Dimensionality of latent space | 100, 256, 512 |
| $T$ | Number of diffusion timesteps | 1000 (DDPM) |
| $\beta_t$ | Noise schedule (variance) | linear, $\beta_1=10^{-4}$, $\beta_T=0.02$ |
| $\lambda$ | KL-divergence weight (VAE) | annealing 0 → 1 |
| $\theta_G$, $\theta_D$ | Generator & Discriminator parameters | learned |

The **standard normal prior** $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and the **binary cross-entropy** objective are the two most frequently tested items in KTU 2024 ESE for this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Architectural Paradigms — Structured Logic

#### 2.1.1 Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) Architecture

The GAN, introduced by *Goodfellow et al.*, formulates generation as a **two-player minimax game** between $G$ and $D$:

- **Generator $G(z;\theta_G)$** maps a random noise vector $z \in \mathbb{R}^{d_z}$ to a synthetic sample $x_{\text{fake}}$ in the data space $\mathcal{X}$.
- **Discriminator $D(x;\theta_D)$** outputs a probability $D(x) \in [0,1]$ indicating the likelihood that input $x$ is real.
- $G$ aims to **maximize** $D$'s error (fool it), while $D$ aims to **minimize** its classification error.

**Why this works:** Under ideal conditions (infinite capacity, perfect optimization), the generator's distribution $p_g$ converges to the true data distribution $p_{\text{data}}$, and the discriminator becomes unable to distinguish them ($D(x) = 0.5$ everywhere).

#### 2.1.2 Variational Autoencoder (VAE) Architecture

The VAE reframes generation through the lens of **Bayesian inference**:

- **Encoder $q_\phi(z \mid x)$** approximates the intractable posterior, mapping $x$ to a distribution over $z$ (parameterized as $\mu$ and $\sigma$).
- **Reparameterization trick:** $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$, where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ — this allows gradients to flow through the sampling operation.
- **Decoder $p_\theta(x \mid z)$** reconstructs $x$ from $z$.

**Why this works:** The latent space is **regularized** to resemble a standard normal prior, enabling smooth sampling and interpolation.

#### 2.1.3 Diffusion Model (DM) Architecture

Diffusion models operate over a **Markov chain of $T$ timesteps**:

- **Forward process $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$:** Gradually adds Gaussian noise, transforming any data point into pure noise.
- **Reverse process $p_\theta(x_{t-1} \mid x_t)$:** A neural network learns to denoise step-by-step, recovering structure from chaos.

**Why this works:** At each step, the model only needs to learn a small, well-conditioned denoising task, which is far easier than directly mapping noise to a complex image.

#### 2.1.4 Autoregressive Transformer Architecture

For sequential data (text, audio, code), the architecture is a stack of **Transformer decoder blocks**:

- **Input:** A sequence of tokens $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$.
- **Causal self-attention** ensures prediction of $x_t$ depends only on $x_{<t}$.
- **Output:** Probability distribution over the vocabulary, sampled to generate the next token.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the following table — it covers ~85% of KTU 2024 numerical and analytical questions for Module 3.**

| # | Formula | Equation | Purpose / Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | GAN Value Function | $\min_G \max_D V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | Defines the adversarial training objective. |
| 2 | Generator loss (non-saturating) | $\mathcal{L}_G = -\mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log D(G(z))]$ | Practical alternative to $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ to avoid vanishing gradients. |
| 3 | Discriminator loss | $\mathcal{L}_D = -[\mathbb{E}_{x}\log D(x) + \mathbb{E}_{z}\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | Binary cross-entropy over real vs. fake. |
| 4 | VAE ELBO | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] - D_{\text{KL}}(q_\phi(z \mid x) \,\vert\vert\, p(z))$ | Evidence Lower Bound; the VAE training objective. |
| 5 | Reconstruction term | $-\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] \approx \vert\vert x - \hat{x} \vert\vert^2$ | For Gaussian decoder, reduces to MSE. |
| 6 | KL regularization (closed form) | $D_{\text{KL}} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{d_z}\left(1 + \log\sigma_j^2 - \mu_j^2 - \sigma_j^2\right)$ | Closed form for diagonal Gaussian encoder. |
| 7 | Reparameterization | $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon,\ \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ | Enables backpropagation through sampling. |
| 8 | Forward diffusion (closed form) | $x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\,\epsilon,\ \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ | Sample any $x_t$ directly without iterating. |
| 9 | Simplified DM training loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{DM}} = \mathbb{E}_{t, x_0, \epsilon}\left[\vert\vert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t) \vert\vert^2\right]$ | Predict the noise, not the image. |
| 10 | Optimal discriminator | $D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$ | Theoretical optimum at convergence. |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GANs:** Image synthesis (StyleGAN, BigGAN), super-resolution, medical image augmentation, defect data generation for manufacturing quality control.
- **VAEs:** Anomaly detection in industrial IoT, drug discovery molecule generation, data compression.
- **Diffusion Models:** State-of-the-art text-to-image (Stable Diffusion, DALL·E 3), protein structure design, audio generation.
- **Autoregressive Transformers:** Code generation, conversational AI, scientific literature summarization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Discriminator of a GAN

We start from the GAN value function and find the discriminator $D^*$ that maximizes it for a *fixed* generator $G$:

$$
V(D, G) = \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x)\, dx + \int_z p_z(z) \log(1 - D(G(z)))\, dz
$$

Change of variable $x = G(z)$, and since $G$ is deterministic, $dz \rightarrow \frac{dx}{\vert G'(x) \vert}$ in the continuous case. For any fixed $G$, we get:

$$
V(D, G) = \int_x \left[ p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \right] dx
$$

**Step 1:** Take the functional derivative with respect to $D(x)$ and set to zero.

$$
\frac{\partial V}{\partial D(x)} = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $D(x)$.

$$
p_{\text{data}}(x) (1 - D(x)) = p_g(x) D(x)
$$

$$
p_{\text{data}}(x) - p_{\text{data}}(x) D(x) = p_g(x) D(x)
$$

$$
p_{\text{data}}(x) = D(x) \left[ p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x) \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Conclude the optimal discriminator.

$$
D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}
$$

> **Interpretation:** When $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$, we get $D^*(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}$ — the discriminator can no longer distinguish real from fake, marking the equilibrium of the minimax game.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — VAE Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)

The marginal log-likelihood of a data point is:

$$
\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int_z p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z)\, dz
$$

**Step 1:** Introduce an arbitrary variational distribution $q_\phi(z \mid x)$.

$$
\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int_z q_\phi(z \mid x) \frac{p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z)}{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\, dz
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Jensen's inequality ($\log$ is concave).

$$
\log p_\theta(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\!\left[\log \frac{p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z)}{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\right] = \text{ELBO}
$$

**Step 3:** Expand the ELBO into the standard two-term form.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ELBO} &= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] - \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\!\left[\log \frac{q_\phi(z \mid x)}{p(z)}\right] \\
&= \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)]}_{\text{Reconstruction}} - \underbrace{D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(q_\phi(z \mid x) \,\vert\vert\, p(z)\right)}_{\text{Regularization}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Closed-form KL for diagonal Gaussians with $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and $q_\phi(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2 I)$:

$$
D_{\text{KL}} = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{d_z}\left(1 + \log\sigma_j^2 - \mu_j^2 - \sigma_j^2\right)
$$

> **Why ELBO matters:** Maximizing the ELBO is equivalent to (a) reconstructing $x$ accurately via the decoder, and (b) keeping the latent distribution close to the prior so that sampling remains valid.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Forward Diffusion Closed-Form Distribution

The forward process is a Markov chain:

$$
q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(x_t;\ \sqrt{1 - \beta_t}\, x_{t-1},\ \beta_t I\right)
$$

Define $\alpha_t = 1 - \beta_t$ and $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t} \alpha_s$.

**Step 1:** Unroll two steps to identify a recursion.

$$
x_t = \sqrt{\alpha_t}\, x_{t-1} + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t}\,\epsilon_{t-1},\quad \epsilon_{t-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $x_{t-1} = \sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}}\, x_{t-2} + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_{t-1}}\,\epsilon_{t-2}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_t &= \sqrt{\alpha_t}\!\left(\sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}}\, x_{t-2} + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_{t-1}}\,\epsilon_{t-2}\right) + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t}\,\epsilon_{t-1} \\
&= \sqrt{\alpha_t \alpha_{t-1}}\, x_{t-2} + \sqrt{\alpha_t(1 - \alpha_{t-1})}\,\epsilon_{t-2} + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t}\,\epsilon_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** By induction, sum of independent Gaussians is Gaussian, and the two noise terms combine into a single Gaussian with variance $1 - \alpha_t \alpha_{t-1}$.

$$
x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\,\epsilon,\quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)
$$

> **Engineering significance:** This closed form lets us sample $x_t$ at *any* timestep in one shot, dramatically accelerating training.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Reparameterization Trick Justification

A naive sample $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2 I)$ is non-differentiable with respect to $\mu$ and $\sigma$. Reparameterize as:

$$
z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon,\quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)
$$

**Step 1:** $z$ is still Gaussian: $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2 I)$ since $\epsilon$ is centered.

**Step 2:** The gradient of any loss $\mathcal{L}$ w.r.t. $\mu$ becomes:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mu} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z}\cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial \mu} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} \cdot 1
$$

**Step 3:** Similarly, w.r.t. $\sigma$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \sigma} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} \cdot \epsilon
$$

> **Why this trick is essential:** Without it, stochastic sampling breaks the backpropagation chain, and VAEs cannot be trained end-to-end with gradient descent.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GAN Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Random Noise z ~ N(0,I)] --> B[Generator Network G]
    B --> C[Synthetic Sample x_fake]
    D[Real Data x_real ~ p_data] --> E[Discriminator Network D]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Probability: Real or Fake]
    F --> G{Loss Computation}
    G --> H[Update G parameters]
    G --> I[Update D parameters]
    H -.adversarial feedback.-> B
    I -.classification loss.-> E
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The Generator receives only random noise. The Discriminator receives *both* real data and synthetic outputs. Losses flow backward to update *both* networks in opposing directions.

---

### 4.2 VAE Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input x] --> B[Encoder q_phi]
    B --> C[Latent mean mu]
    B --> D[Latent std sigma]
    E[Sampled noise epsilon ~ N(0,I)] --> F[Reparameterization]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    F --> G[Latent z]
    G --> H[Decoder p_theta]
    H --> I[Reconstruction x_hat]
    I --> J{Reconstruction Loss MSE or BCE}
    C --> K[KL Divergence vs Prior]
    D --> K
    J --> L[Total ELBO Loss]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Backpropagate to Encoder and Decoder]
```

---

### 4.3 Diffusion Model Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Real Image x_0] --> B[Add Gaussian Noise beta_1]
    B --> C[Noisier x_1]
    C --> D[Add Gaussian Noise beta_2]
    D --> E[Noisier x_2]
    E --> F[Continue to x_T pure noise]
    F --> G[U-Net Denoiser epsilon_theta]
    G --> H[Predicted Noise at step t]
    H --> I[Subtract to get x_t-1]
    I --> J[Repeat T times]
    J --> K[Reconstructed x_0]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The *forward* path (top-down) progressively destroys information. The *reverse* path (right side) is what the neural network learns — a U-Net predicts the noise component at each step, which is subtracted to recover a slightly cleaner image.

---

### 4.4 Comparative Schematic of All Four Paradigms

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GAN["GAN Architecture"]
        GAN1[Noise z] --> GAN2[Generator G] --> GAN3[Fake x] --> GAN4[Discriminator D]
    end
    subgraph VAE["VAE Architecture"]
        VAE1[Input x] --> VAE2[Encoder] --> VAE3[mu sigma] --> VAE4[Reparameterize] --> VAE5[Latent z] --> VAE6[Decoder] --> VAE7[Reconstruction]
    end
    subgraph DM["Diffusion Architecture"]
        DM1[x_0] --> DM2[Forward Noise x_t] --> DM3[U-Net] --> DM4[Predicted epsilon] --> DM5[x_t-1]
    end
    subgraph AR["Autoregressive Transformer"]
        AR1[Tokens x_1 to x_t-1] --> AR2[Causal Self-Attention] --> AR3[Softmax over Vocabulary] --> AR4[Next Token x_t]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define the Generator–Discriminator architecture of a GAN. Why is the minimax formulation preferred over direct likelihood maximization?**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
- **Generator $G(z)$** maps noise $z \sim p_z$ to synthetic samples; **Discriminator $D(x)$** outputs the probability that $x$ is real (1 mark).
- Training is a two-player game: $G$ minimizes $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ and $D$ maximizes classification accuracy on real vs. fake (1 mark).
- **Why minimax:** Direct likelihood is intractable for high-dimensional data (e.g., images); the adversarial game implicitly learns a *distance* (Jensen–Shannon) between $p_g$ and $p_{\text{data}}$ without needing an explicit density (1 mark).

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Explain the reparameterization trick in a VAE. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
- Sample $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$ with $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, instead of $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2 I)$ directly (1 mark).
- It makes the sampling operation **differentiable** with respect to $\mu$ and $\sigma$, so gradients can flow through the stochastic node (1 mark).
- Without it, backpropagation is broken at the sampling step, and the VAE cannot be trained end-to-end using SGD (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Forgetting to write the **closed-form KL divergence** (Equation 6 above) in VAE questions typically costs **2–3 marks**.
> - In GAN derivations, students often **omit the change of variable $x = G(z)$** when converting the value function into the integral form. This is a **2-mark deduction** trap.
> - For diffusion models, **always state the closed-form $x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\epsilon$** — writing only the recursive form loses 1 mark.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a) (7 marks)** Derive the optimal discriminator $D^*(x)$ for a Generative Adversarial Network. Show all steps starting from the value function.

**(b) (7 marks)** Explain the architecture of a Variational Autoencoder with a neat block diagram. Derive the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) and state the closed-form KL divergence term for diagonal Gaussian encoder and standard normal prior.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Optimal Discriminator Derivation (7 marks):**
1. Write the GAN value function (1 mark):
$$V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$
2. Convert the second expectation to an integral over $x$ using $x = G(z)$ and $p_g(x)$ (1 mark).
3. Differentiate $V$ w.r.t. $D(x)$ and set to zero (2 marks).
4. Solve for $D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$ (2 marks).
5. State convergence condition: $p_g = p_{\text{data}} \Rightarrow D^* = 0.5$ (1 mark).

**(b) VAE Architecture & ELBO Derivation (7 marks):**
1. Block diagram of Encoder → $\mu, \sigma$ → Reparameterization → Latent $z$ → Decoder → $\hat{x}$ (2 marks).
2. Begin from $\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int_z p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z)\, dz$ (1 mark).
3. Introduce $q_\phi(z \mid x)$ and apply Jensen's inequality (2 marks).
4. Expand to obtain $\text{ELBO} = \mathbb{E}_q[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] - D_{\text{KL}}(q_\phi(z \mid x) \,\vert\vert\, p(z))$ (1 mark).
5. State closed-form KL (1 mark):
$$D_{\text{KL}} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{d_z}\left(1 + \log\sigma_j^2 - \mu_j^2 - \sigma_j^2\right)$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a) (7 marks)** Describe the architecture of a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM). Derive the closed-form expression for sampling $x_t$ directly from $x_0$.

**(b) (7 marks)** Compare and contrast the four major generative AI architectures (GAN, VAE, Diffusion Model, Autoregressive Transformer) in terms of training objective, sampling procedure, sample quality, and mode coverage.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DDPM Architecture & Closed-Form Sampling (7 marks):**
1. Block diagram: $x_0 \rightarrow$ forward noising chain ($T$ steps) $\rightarrow x_T$ (1 mark).
2. State the forward Markov transition $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\sqrt{1 - \beta_t} x_{t-1}, \beta_t I)$ (1 mark).
3. Define $\alpha_t = 1 - \beta_t$, $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^t \alpha_s$ (1 mark).
4. Unroll two timesteps and combine independent Gaussians (2 marks).
5. Conclude $x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\,\epsilon$ (1 mark).
6. Mention U-Net as the denoiser $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)$ and the simplified loss $\vert\vert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t) \vert\vert^2$ (1 mark).

**(b) Comparative Analysis (7 marks):**

| Aspect | GAN | VAE | Diffusion | Autoregressive |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Training objective | Adversarial min-max (2 marks) | ELBO (likelihood-based) | Noise prediction MSE | Token cross-entropy |
| Sampling | One forward pass (fast) | Encode-free, one decode | Iterative ($T$ steps, slow) | Sequential ($n$ steps) |
| Sample quality | Sharp, realistic | Blurry (over-regularized) | State-of-the-art | High (text/code) |
| Mode coverage | Mode collapse risk | Good coverage | Excellent coverage | Excellent |
| Use case | Images, super-resolution | Anomaly detection | Text-to-image, audio | Text, code, music |

Award **1 mark per filled row × 5 rows**, plus **2 marks** for the overall summary or extra architectural insight.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 3 — Architecture:**

- [x] **GANs** = Generator + Discriminator; training is a **minimax game**; optimal $D^* = \frac{p_{\text{data}}}{p_{\text{data}} + p_g}$.
- [x] **Mode collapse** is the most cited failure mode of GANs — generator produces limited variety.
- [x] **Non-saturating generator loss** $-\log D(G(z))$ is preferred over $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ to avoid vanishing gradients.
- [x] **VAE ELBO** has two terms: **Reconstruction** (decoder likelihood) and **KL Regularization** (latent prior).
- [x] **Reparameterization trick** is **mandatory** for VAE end-to-end training.
- [x] **Closed-form KL** (diagonal Gaussian vs. standard normal) is a high-yield KTU question.
- [x] **Diffusion models** add noise forward, learn to denoise reverse; training loss is **MSE on noise**, not on image.
- [x] **Closed-form $x_t$** via $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod \alpha_s$ is critical — remember to write it.
- [x] **Autoregressive Transformers** use **causal masking** so token $t$ only sees $x_{<t}$.
- [x] **Latent dimensionality** $d_z$ is a hyperparameter — too small = blurry outputs; too large = overfitting.
- [x] For KTU 2024: **always include a diagram** in any 7-or-14-mark question on architecture.
- [x] The **four architectures are not mutually exclusive** — e.g., **Latent Diffusion Models** combine VAE encoder with a diffusion process in latent space.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Attention Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Attention Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — ATTENTION MECHANISMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Attention Mechanism** is a computational framework in deep learning that enables a neural network to dynamically weight the relative importance of different elements within an input sequence (or between input and output sequences) when generating a representation or prediction. It computes a context vector as a **weighted sum** of value vectors, where the weights — called *attention scores* — are derived from a compatibility function between a *query* and a set of *keys*.

In the context of **Generative AI**, attention mechanisms form the foundational layer of Transformer architectures, which underpin modern Large Language Models (LLMs) such as **GPT, BERT, T5, and LLaMA**. It addresses the *bottleneck problem* of fixed-size context vectors in classical Seq2Seq models by allowing the decoder to "look back" at all encoder hidden states simultaneously, with learned, context-dependent emphasis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / The "Library Search" Intuition

Imagine you are a **researcher writing a thesis**. You have a long library of 1,000 books (encoder hidden states). When writing a particular sentence (decoder time step), you don't need to remember *every* book equally — you only need to focus on the **few most relevant books**.

- 🔍 Your **search query** = the current sentence being written
- 📚 The **library index** (book titles) = the *keys*
- 📖 The **actual content** of books = the *values*
- 🎯 The **relevance score** you assign to each book = *attention weight*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Generative AI:** In text generation, attention lets a model "focus" on semantically relevant prior tokens when predicting the next token, rather than compressing all history into a single fixed vector.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (KTU High-Yield)

- **d_model** = Embedding/hidden dimension (typically **512** in original Transformer)
- **d_k = d_v = d_model / h** = per-head dimension (typically **64**)
- **h** = Number of attention heads (typically **8** in base Transformer)
- **√d_k** = Scaling factor used in *Scaled Dot-Product Attention*
- **Sequence length** = n (input tokens)
- **Computational complexity per layer** = **O(n² · d)** for self-attention

> [!TIP]
> Memorize **d_k = 64** and **h = 8** — these are the canonical "base model" numbers from the original *Vaswani et al. (2017)* paper, and KTU questions frequently cite them.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Attention Was Introduced — The Motivation

The classical **Seq2Seq (Encoder-Decoder) RNN** architecture suffered from three critical limitations:

| # | Limitation | How Attention Solves It |
|---|------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **Information Bottleneck** — entire input compressed into one fixed-size vector | Decoder accesses **all** encoder states |
| 2 | **Long-range dependency loss** — vanishing gradients over long sequences | Direct connections between **any two positions** via attention weights |
| 3 | **Sequential processing** — RNNs cannot parallelize | Self-attention is **fully parallelizable** |

## 2.2 Types of Attention Mechanisms

### (a) Bahdanau Attention (Additive Attention, 2014)
- Learns a **feed-forward alignment** network jointly with the Seq2Seq model.
- Alignment score computed via a small neural network.

### (b) Luong Attention (Multiplicative Attention, 2015)
- Computes alignment score via simple **dot product** or **general** bilinear form.
- Faster than Bahdanau; precursor to scaled dot-product.

### (c) **Self-Attention (Intra-Attention)**
- Query, Key, and Value all come from the **same sequence**.
- Captures internal relationships between tokens of one sequence.

### (d) **Cross-Attention (Encoder-Decoder Attention)**
- Query comes from **decoder**; Keys and Values come from **encoder**.
- Used in machine translation, image captioning.

### (e) **Multi-Head Attention**
- Runs **h parallel attention operations** on projected versions of Q, K, V.
- Allows the model to attend to information from **different representation subspaces simultaneously**.

## 2.3 Operational Steps of Attention (Logic Flow)

1. **Input Projection:** Generate three vectors from each input embedding — **Query (Q)**, **Key (K)**, **Value (V)** — via learned linear projections.
2. **Score Computation:** Measure compatibility between query and each key using a score function (dot product, scaled dot product, additive, etc.).
3. **Scaling:** Divide raw scores by **√d_k** to prevent softmax saturation in high dimensions.
4. **Softmax Normalization:** Convert raw scores into a probability distribution summing to 1.
5. **Weighting:** Multiply each value vector by its corresponding attention weight.
6. **Aggregation:** Sum the weighted value vectors to obtain the **context vector (output)**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | General Attention Output | $c = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \cdot v_i$ | $\alpha_i$ = attention weight, $v_i$ = value vector |
| 2 | Softmax Weights | $\alpha_i = \dfrac{\exp(e_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \exp(e_j)}$ | $e_i$ = raw score (energy) |
| 3 | Dot-Product Score | $e_i = q^T \cdot k_i$ | Query–Key compatibility |
| 4 | Scaled Dot-Product Score | $e_i = \dfrac{q^T \cdot k_i}{\sqrt{d_k}}$ | Scaled for stable gradients |
| 5 | **Scaled Dot-Product Attention (Canonical)** | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | The Transformer core equation |
| 6 | Additive (Bahdanau) Score | $e_i = v_a^{\top} \tanh(W_a [q; k_i])$ | Concatenation of q and k |
| 7 | Multi-Head Concatenation | $\text{MHA}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\dots,\text{head}_h)\,W^O$ | $W^O$ = output projection |
| 8 | Single Head | $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V)$ | $W_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model}\times d_k}$ |
| 9 | Self-Attention Projection | $Q = X W^Q,\ \ K = X W^K,\ \ V = X W^V$ | $X$ = input embeddings |
| 10 | Positional Encoding (sin/cos) | $PE_{(pos,2i)} = \sin(pos / 10000^{2i/d_{model}})$ | Injects order information |
| 11 | Complexity (Self-Attention) | $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ | Quadratic in sequence length |
| 12 | Complexity (Recurrent) | $O(n \cdot d^2)$ | Linear in n but worse constants |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks the difference between **Bahdanau (additive)** and **Luong (dot-product / scaled dot-product)** attention. Memorize that **additive** uses a feed-forward network and handles variable dimensions, while **dot-product** is faster, parallelizable, and requires scaling by **√d_k** for high dimensions.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- 🗣️ **Machine Translation** (Google Translate, IndicTrans)
- 🖼️ **Image Captioning & Vision Transformers (ViT)**
- 🧬 **Protein Structure Prediction** (AlphaFold)
- 🎵 **Music & Audio Generation** (Jukebox, MusicGen)
- 🤖 **LLM-based Generative AI** (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini)
- 🎨 **Diffusion Models with Cross-Attention** (Stable Diffusion, DALL·E)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From RNN Context Vector to Attention-Weighted Context

### **Step 1 — Classical Seq2Seq Bottleneck**

In a vanilla encoder-decoder RNN, the entire input sentence $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ is encoded into a single fixed-size context vector:

$$
c = f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)
$$

**Problem:** $c$ is a *single* vector regardless of input length. For long sequences, the bottleneck causes information loss.

### **Step 2 — Introduce All Encoder Hidden States**

Let each encoder hidden state be $h_i$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$. Instead of using only the final state, we expose *all* states to the decoder.

### **Step 3 — Compute Alignment Scores**

For decoder hidden state $s_{t-1}$, compute a raw score (energy) against each encoder state:

$$
e_{t,i} = \text{score}(s_{t-1}, h_i)
$$

**Three common score variants:**

$$
\text{(a) Dot: } e_{t,i} = s_{t-1}^{\top} h_i
$$

$$
\text{(b) General: } e_{t,i} = s_{t-1}^{\top} W_a h_i
$$

$$
\text{(c) Additive (Bahdanau): } e_{t,i} = v_a^{\top} \tanh(W_a [s_{t-1}; h_i])
$$

### **Step 4 — Softmax Normalization**

Convert scores into a probability distribution (attention weights):

$$
\alpha_{t,i} = \frac{\exp(e_{t,i})}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \exp(e_{t,j})}
$$

**Why softmax?** It ensures:
- All weights are in $[0, 1]$
- $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_{t,i} = 1$
- Differentiable for backpropagation

### **Step 5 — Compute the Attention Context Vector**

The final context vector at decoder time step $t$ is the weighted sum:

$$
c_t = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_{t,i} \cdot h_i
$$

### **Step 6 — Feed into Decoder**

The decoder uses $c_t$ concatenated with the previous hidden state to predict the next token:

$$
\hat{y}_t = g(c_t, s_{t-1}, y_{t-1})
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Scaled Dot-Product Attention

The Transformer uses matrix form for efficiency. Given $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$, $K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$, $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$:

### **Step 1 — Compute raw dot products via matrix multiplication**

$$
S = Q K^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}
$$

Each entry $S_{ij} = q_i^{\top} k_j$ represents the similarity between query $i$ and key $j$.

### **Step 2 — Scale by √d_k**

$$
S' = \frac{S}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

**Why scale?** The dot product magnitude grows with $d_k$. If components of $q$ and $k$ are independent with mean 0 and variance 1, then $q^{\top}k$ has variance $d_k$. Large values push softmax into regions of tiny gradient, hindering learning.

### **Step 3 — (Optional) Apply Masking**

For causal/decoder attention, set masked positions to $-\infty$:

$$
S''_{ij} = \begin{cases} S'_{ij} & \text{if position } j \text{ is allowed} \\ -\infty & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### **Step 4 — Softmax along the key dimension (rows)**

$$
A = \text{softmax}(S'') \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}
$$

where $\sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{ij} = 1$ for each row $i$.

### **Step 5 — Weighted sum of values**

$$
\text{Output} = A V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}
$$

### **Final Compact Form:**

$$
\boxed{\ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V\ }
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Multi-Head Attention Output

### **Step 1 — Define h parallel projections**

For each head $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, h\}$:

$$
Q_i = Q W_i^Q, \quad K_i = K W_i^K, \quad V_i = V W_i^V
$$

where $W_i^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$, $W_i^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$, $W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_v}$.

### **Step 2 — Apply scaled dot-product attention per head**

$$
\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q_i, K_i, V_i) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q_i K_i^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V_i
$$

### **Step 3 — Concatenate all heads**

$$
\text{Concat} = [\text{head}_1; \text{head}_2; \dots; \text{head}_h] \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times h \cdot d_v}
$$

### **Step 4 — Final linear projection**

$$
\text{MHA}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat} \cdot W^O
$$

where $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{h d_v \times d_{model}}$.

### **Final Compact Form:**

$$
\boxed{\ \text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h) W^O\ }
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Self-Attention vs Cross-Attention vs Masked Attention

| Feature | Self-Attention | Cross-Attention | Masked Self-Attention |
|---------|----------------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Source of Q** | Same sequence | Decoder state | Same sequence |
| **Source of K, V** | Same sequence | Encoder output | Same sequence |
| **Used In** | Encoder, Decoder | Decoder blocks | Causal LMs (GPT) |
| **Mask Applied?** | No | No | Yes (causal mask) |
| **Purpose** | Capture internal dependencies | Align input & output tokens | Prevent future-token leakage |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(n^2 d)$ | $O(n \cdot m \cdot d)$ | $O(n^2 d)$ |
| **Example LLM Layer** | BERT encoder | Translation Transformer | GPT decoder block |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (For Exam Practice)

**Given:** A sequence of 3 tokens with embeddings $X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $W^Q = W^K = W^V = I_2$ (identity), and $d_k = 2$.

**Step 1:** $Q = K = V = X$.

**Step 2:** Compute $QK^{\top} = X X^{\top} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 3:** Scale by $\sqrt{d_k} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$:

$$
S' = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & 0 & 0.707 \\ 0 & 0.707 & 0.707 \\ 0.707 & 0.707 & 1.414 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply row-wise softmax (manually approximated):

Row 1: $e^{0.707} = 2.028$, $e^0 = 1$, $e^{0.707} = 2.028$ → sum $= 5.056$
Weights: $[0.401, 0.198, 0.401]$

Row 2: same as Row 1 by symmetry → $[0.198, 0.401, 0.401]$

Row 3: $e^{0.707} = 2.028$, $e^{0.707} = 2.028$, $e^{1.414} = 4.113$ → sum $= 8.169$
Weights: $[0.248, 0.248, 0.504]$

**Step 5:** Multiply attention matrix by $V$:

$$
\text{Output} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.401 & 0.198 & 0.401 \\ 0.198 & 0.401 & 0.401 \\ 0.248 & 0.248 & 0.504 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.802 & 0.599 \\ 0.599 & 0.802 \\ 0.752 & 0.752 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each row of the output is a context-aware representation of the corresponding token, weighted by its learned similarity to all tokens in the sequence.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Scaled Dot-Product Attention — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Embeddings X] --> B[Linear Projection WQ]
    A --> C[Linear Projection WK]
    A --> D[Linear Projection WV]
    B --> E[Query Matrix Q]
    C --> F[Key Matrix K]
    D --> G[Value Matrix V]
    E --> H[MatMul Q x KT]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Scale by 1 over sqrt dk]
    I --> J[Optional Mask]
    J --> K[Softmax Row-wise]
    K --> L[Attention Weights Matrix A]
    L --> M[MatMul A x V]
    G --> M
    M --> N[Output Context Vectors]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** From left to right, the input is projected into Q, K, V, their compatibility is measured (MatMul + Scale), normalized (Softmax), and used to weight the Values. The output is a set of context-aware vectors of the **same dimension** as the input.

---

## 4.2 Multi-Head Attention — Parallel Subspace Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputProj[Input Projections]
        I[Input X] --> QP[WQ]
        I --> KP[WK]
        I --> VP[WV]
    end
    QP --> H1[Head 1 Attention]
    KP --> H1
    VP --> H1
    QP --> H2[Head 2 Attention]
    KP --> H2
    VP --> H2
    QP --> H3[Head h Attention]
    KP --> H3
    VP --> H3
    H1 --> C[Concatenate]
    H2 --> C
    H3 --> C
    C --> WO[Linear WO]
    WO --> OUT[Final Output]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Each head learns to attend to *different* linguistic or semantic features — e.g., one head may track syntactic dependencies, another may track coreference, another may track positional proximity. Concatenation and $W^O$ fuse these diverse views into a single rich representation.

---

## 4.3 Self-Attention vs Cross-Attention — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENC[Encoder]
        E1[Token Embeddings] --> E2[Self Attention]
        E2 --> E3[Feed Forward]
    end
    subgraph DEC[Decoder]
        D1[Previous Output] --> D2[Masked Self Attention]
        D2 --> D3[Cross Attention]
        E3 --> D3
        D3 --> D4[Feed Forward]
        D4 --> D5[Next Token Prediction]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Visual Cue:** The arrows from `E3 → D3` represent **Cross-Attention** — the decoder's queries attend to the encoder's keys and values. Self-attention blocks (E2, D2) operate *within* their own sequence only.

---

## 4.4 Attention Mechanism — End-to-End Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Input Text or Image Patches] --> B[Tokenization and Embedding]
    B --> C[Add Positional Encoding]
    C --> D[Multi Head Self Attention]
    D --> E[Add and Norm Residual]
    E --> F[Feed Forward Network]
    F --> G[Add and Norm Residual]
    G --> H{Next Layer}
    H -->|Yes| D
    H -->|No| I[Task Head: Linear plus Softmax]
    I --> J[Output: Generated Token or Class]
```

> [!TIP]
> The **"Add & Norm"** blocks implement **residual connections** ($x + \text{Sublayer}(x)$) followed by **Layer Normalization** — these are critical for training deep Transformers (often 12, 96, or even 200+ layers).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the attention mechanism. Differentiate between self-attention and cross-attention.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Attention mechanism** is a deep learning technique that allows a model to dynamically focus on the most relevant parts of an input sequence by computing a weighted combination of value vectors, where weights are derived from the similarity between a query and a set of keys.

| Aspect | Self-Attention | Cross-Attention |
|--------|----------------|------------------|
| Q, K, V Source | All from **same** sequence | Q from decoder; K, V from encoder |
| Purpose | Capture **intra-sequence** dependencies | Capture **inter-sequence** (input–output) alignment |
| Example | BERT encoder block | Seq2Seq translation decoder |

**(3 marks — full marks require all three points: definition + comparison table covering both types.)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*Why is scaling by √d_k necessary in Scaled Dot-Product Attention?*

**Model Answer:**

1. In dot-product attention, the magnitude of $q^{\top}k$ grows with the dimension $d_k$. For unit-variance random vectors, $\text{Var}(q^{\top}k) = d_k$.
2. When $d_k$ is large, raw dot products become large in magnitude, pushing the softmax into regions of **near-one-hot outputs** with **extremely small gradients**.
3. This causes the **vanishing gradient problem** during training.
4. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ **normalizes the variance back to 1**, keeping the softmax in a sensitive, well-conditioned regime.
5. This allows the model to learn **meaningful attention distributions** during backpropagation.

**(3 marks — full marks require: variance reasoning, gradient problem, and explicit role of √d_k.)**

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Bahdanau (Additive) Attention** mechanism with its mathematical formulation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** formula used in Transformers and show its computational complexity. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Bahdanau Attention (Additive Attention):** Introduced by *Bahdanau, Cho, Bengio (2014)*, this was the first attention mechanism proposed for neural machine translation to overcome the encoder-decoder bottleneck.

**Step 1 — Encoder:** Produce hidden states $h_1, h_2, \dots, h_n$ from the input.

**Step 2 — Score function (alignment):** For decoder state $s_{t-1}$ at time $t$:

$$
e_{t,i} = v_a^{\top} \tanh\!\big(W_a [s_{t-1}; h_i]\big)
$$

where:
- $W_a \in \mathbb{R}^{d_a \times (d_s + d_h)}$ — trainable weight matrix
- $v_a \in \mathbb{R}^{d_a}$ — trainable vector
- $[s_{t-1}; h_i]$ — concatenation of decoder and encoder states
- $\tanh$ — non-linearity

**Step 3 — Softmax:**

$$
\alpha_{t,i} = \frac{\exp(e_{t,i})}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \exp(e_{t,j})}
$$

**Step 4 — Context vector:**

$$
c_t = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_{t,i} \cdot h_i
$$

**Step 5 — Decoder output:**

$$
\hat{y}_t = g(c_t, s_{t-1}, y_{t-1})
$$

**Key properties:** Learns alignment jointly with translation; can handle variable dimensions; not easily parallelizable due to the per-timestep dependency.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Scaled Dot-Product Attention** (the heart of the Transformer):

**Step 1 — Project inputs** into Queries, Keys, Values:

$$
Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V
$$

**Step 2 — Compute raw similarity matrix:**

$$
S = Q K^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}, \quad S_{ij} = q_i^{\top} k_j
$$

**Step 3 — Scale by √d_k:**

$$
S' = \frac{S}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

**Step 4 — Softmax:**

$$
A = \text{softmax}(S'), \quad A_{ij} = \frac{\exp(S'_{ij})}{\sum_{k} \exp(S'_{ik})}
$$

**Step 5 — Output:**

$$
\boxed{Z = A V = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V}
$$

**Computational Complexity Analysis:**

| Operation | Complexity | Notes |
|-----------|------------|-------|
| $Q K^{\top}$ | $O(n^2 \cdot d_k)$ | Dominant term |
| Softmax | $O(n^2)$ | Element-wise |
| $A \cdot V$ | $O(n^2 \cdot d_v)$ | Second dominant term |
| **Total** | $\mathbf{O(n^2 \cdot d)}$ | Quadratic in sequence length |

**Comparison with RNN:** RNN has $O(n \cdot d^2)$ — linear in $n$ but worse constants. Self-attention's $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ becomes cheaper than RNN for $n < d$ and supports full parallelism.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe the **Multi-Head Attention** mechanism. Why is it preferred over single-head attention? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the role of **self-attention in Generative AI models** with a suitable diagram. Discuss its complexity and limitations. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**Multi-Head Attention (MHA):** Instead of performing a single attention function on $d_{model}$-dimensional Q, K, V, the Transformer projects them into $h$ different learned subspaces and runs attention in parallel.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

**Step 1 — Define h parallel linear projections:**

$$
Q_i = Q W_i^Q,\quad K_i = K W_i^K,\quad V_i = V W_i^V,\quad \forall i \in \{1, \dots, h\}
$$

with $W_i^Q, W_i^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$ and $W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_v}$, where $d_k = d_v = d_{model} / h$.

**Step 2 — Apply scaled dot-product attention to each head:**

$$
\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q_i, K_i, V_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Concatenate heads:**

$$
\text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times h \cdot d_v}
$$

**Step 4 — Final projection:**

$$
\boxed{\text{MHA}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h) W^O}
$$

where $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{h d_v \times d_{model}}$.

**Why Multi-Head is Preferred:**

1. **Multiple Representation Subspaces:** Each head can attend to *different* types of relationships (syntactic, semantic, positional, coreferential).
2. **Increased Model Capacity:** More parameters learn richer features without increasing the per-head computational cost significantly.
3. **Ensemble Effect:** Multiple attention "experts" vote on context relevance, reducing the risk of a single head's bias dominating.
4. **Same Total Cost:** With $d_k = d_{model}/h$, the total compute is approximately equal to single-head attention with full dimension.

**In the base Transformer:** $d_{model} = 512$, $h = 8$, $d_k = d_v = 64$.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Role of Self-Attention in Generative AI:**

Self-attention is the **fundamental building block** of modern generative models. It allows every token in a sequence to "see" every other token, computing a contextualized representation.

**Process in a Generative Model (e.g., GPT-style):**

1. **Input:** Tokenized prompt $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$.
2. **Embedding + Positional Encoding:** $E = X + P$, where $P$ injects order.
3. **Stacked Self-Attention Blocks:** $L$ layers of (Masked Self-Attention → Add&Norm → FFN → Add&Norm).
4. **Output Head:** Linear projection + Softmax to predict next token probabilities.
5. **Sampling:** Token selected via temperature, top-k, or nucleus sampling.
6. **Autoregressive Loop:** Predicted token appended to input; process repeats.

**Complexity:**

- Time: $O(n^2 \cdot d_{model})$ per layer
- Space: $O(n^2)$ for the attention matrix
- For $L$ layers: $O(L \cdot n^2 \cdot d_{model})$

**Limitations:**

| Limitation | Consequence | Mitigation |
|------------|-------------|------------|
| **Quadratic in $n$** | Slow for long sequences (>4k tokens) | Sparse attention, Linformer, FlashAttention |
| **No inherent order** | Requires positional encoding | Sinusoidal, RoPE, ALiBi |
| **High memory** | $n^2$ attention matrix | Gradient checkpointing, low-rank approx. |
| **Causal masking** | Cannot use bidirectional context in LMs | Two-pass training (BERT vs GPT) |

**Impact on Generative AI:**
- Enabled **GPT, BERT, T5, LLaMA** — foundational to ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude.
- Extended to **Vision Transformers (ViT)**, **Stable Diffusion**, **Whisper (speech)**.
- Powers **multimodal generation** in DALL·E 3, GPT-4V, Gemini 1.5.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing the scaling factor:** Many students write $\frac{1}{d_k}$ instead of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_k}}$ — full 1 mark lost.
> 2. **Omitting the Q, K, V projection step:** You must show that Q, K, V are *learned linear projections* of the input, not the raw embeddings.
> 3. **Forgetting softmax is applied row-wise** across keys, not columns — wrong direction = 1 mark deducted.
> 4. **Conflating Bahdanau (additive) and Luong (dot-product):** They are NOT the same. Bahdanau uses a *feed-forward alignment network*; Luong uses *multiplicative* forms.
> 5. **Forgetting residual connections** when drawing a Transformer block — KTU expects `Add & Norm` after every sub-layer.
> 6. **Not mentioning masking** in decoder attention — causal masking is a compulsory part of the answer for any 14-mark question.
> 7. **Complexity analysis must compare** with RNN: showing $O(n^2 d)$ without comparing to $O(nd^2)$ is incomplete.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Core Equation:** $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$
- ✅ **Three Projections:** Every token becomes a Query, Key, and Value via learned matrices $W^Q, W^K, W^V$.
- ✅ **Why Scale by √d_k:** Keeps softmax gradients healthy when $d_k$ is large.
- ✅ **Multi-Head:** $h$ parallel attention heads, then Concat + $W^O$ projection.
- ✅ **Self vs Cross:** Self = same sequence; Cross = Q from decoder, K,V from encoder.
- ✅ **Masked Self-Attention:** Used in decoders/ causal LMs to prevent future-token peeking.
- ✅ **Bahdanau = Additive** (uses FFN); **Luong = Dot-Product** (faster, precursor to scaled).
- ✅ **Base Transformer Hyperparameters:** $d_{model}=512$, $h=8$, $d_k=d_v=64$, 6 encoder + 6 decoder layers.
- ✅ **Complexity:** Self-attention = $O(n^2 \cdot d)$; RNN = $O(n \cdot d^2)$.
- ✅ **Positional Encoding:** Required because attention is permutation-equivariant.
- ✅ **Residual + LayerNorm:** "Add & Norm" after every sub-layer; enables training deep Transformers.
- ✅ **Generative AI Link:** Self-attention is the **core mechanism** in GPT, BERT, T5, ViT, Stable Diffusion.
- ✅ **Limitations:** $O(n^2)$ memory; mitigated by FlashAttention, Linformer, sparse attention variants.

> **End of Module 3 — Attention Mechanisms Notes**
