> **Reading the diagrams:** Solid arrows show data flow, dashed arrows show residual connections, and dotted arrows show cross-attention (encoder → decoder). All node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed to comply with Mermaid syntax requirements.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]
**Define the self-attention mechanism in a Transformer. How does it differ from the attention used in classical encoder-decoder RNN models?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Self-attention is a mechanism where every position in a sequence computes its representation as a **weighted sum of all positions in the same sequence**, with the weights (attention scores) derived from pairwise compatibility between Query and Key vectors. Mathematically, $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$. (2 marks)

**Key difference from RNN attention:** In RNN-based seq2seq attention, the keys and values come from the *encoder hidden states* (a different sequence), while queries come from the *decoder*. In self-attention, **Q, K, and V all originate from the same input sequence**, allowing each token to attend to every other token in the same sequence. (1 mark)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]
**Why is positional encoding necessary in the Transformer architecture? State the sinusoidal formulas used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The self-attention operation is **permutation-invariant** — it treats the input as an unordered set of tokens. Without positional encoding, the model cannot distinguish "dog bites man" from "man bites dog." (1.5 marks)

Positional encoding injects order information by adding a position-dependent vector to each token embedding using fixed sinusoids: $PE_{(pos,\,2i)} = \sin(pos/10000^{2i/d_m})$ and $PE_{(pos,\,2i+1)} = \cos(pos/10000^{2i/d_m})$. These sinusoids allow the model to generalize to sequence lengths longer than those seen during training, since $PE(pos+k)$ can be expressed as a linear function of $PE(pos)$. (1.5 marks)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the scaled dot-product attention formula. Explain the role of the scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ in preventing softmax saturation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The attention function maps a query and a set of key-value pairs to an output. The output is computed as a weighted sum of the values, where the weight assigned to each value is determined by a compatibility function of the query with the corresponding key.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Using dot-product as the compatibility function, we compute the dot products of the query with all keys, divide each by $\sqrt{d_k}$, and apply a softmax to obtain the weights on the values:

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Why scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$? Assume the components of $q$ and $k$ are independent random variables with zero mean and unit variance. Their dot product $q \cdot k = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i$ has mean 0 and variance $d_k$. To keep the variance of the dot product at 1 (regardless of $d_k$), we divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Without scaling, large values of $d_k$ push the softmax into regions where it produces outputs extremely close to 0 or 1, resulting in vanishing gradients and slow learning. The $\sqrt{d_k}$ factor restores well-behaved gradients.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the multi-head attention mechanism. How does it improve over single-head attention in a translation task?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Multi-head attention projects $Q$, $K$, $V$ into $h$ different learned linear subspaces (using $h$ sets of projection matrices $W_i^Q, W_i^K, W_i^V$) and runs scaled dot-product attention independently in each subspace:

$$
\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q,\; KW_i^K,\; VW_i^V)
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The outputs of all heads are concatenated and linearly projected back to the model dimension using $W^O$:

$$
\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)\,W^O
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Why it helps in translation:**
- Different heads can learn to attend to **different linguistic phenomena** simultaneously — one head may focus on syntactic dependencies (subject–verb agreement), another on coreference (pronoun resolution), and another on positional locality.
- A single attention head must average over all these heterogeneous relationships, leading to a *blurred* attention distribution.
- The concatenation followed by $W^O$ allows the model to **jointly mix information** from all these parallel "perspectives."

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Empirically, the original paper used $h = 8$ heads with $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}}/h = 64$. This keeps the total computational cost comparable to single-head attention with full dimensionality, while drastically increasing the model's representational power.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the complete encoder block of a Transformer. Write the equations for self-attention, residual connection, and layer normalization.**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Input X (n × d_model)
        │
        ├──► Multi-Head Self-Attention ──► Add & LayerNorm ──► FFN ──► Add & LayerNorm ──► Output
        │              │                       ▲                            ▲
        └──────────────┘ Residual ─────────────┘ Residual ─────────────────┘
```

**Self-Attention Equation (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V
$$

**Residual Connection (1.5 marks):** Each sub-layer employs a residual connection to facilitate gradient flow in deep stacks:

$$
\tilde{X} = X + \text{Sublayer}(X)
$$

**Layer Normalization (1 mark):** LayerNorm is applied across the feature dimension for each token independently:

$$
\text{LayerNorm}(\tilde{X}) = \gamma \odot \frac{\tilde{X} - \mu}{\sigma} + \beta
$$

where $\mu, \sigma$ are the mean and standard deviation computed over the $d_{\text{model}}$ features of a single token, and $\gamma, \beta$ are learnable scale and shift parameters.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Differentiate between encoder-only, decoder-only, and encoder-decoder Transformers. Give one real-world generative AI model for each type.**

**Model Solution:**

| Architecture | Self-Attention Type | Cross-Attention | Use Case | Example Model |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Encoder-Only | Bidirectional (no mask) | None | Understanding / classification | **BERT** |
| Decoder-Only | Causal (masked) | None | Autoregressive generation | **GPT-4** |
| Encoder-Decoder | Both (encoder: bidirectional; decoder: causal + cross) | Yes | Seq2Seq generation | **T5 / BART** |

**Step-by-step explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Encoder-Only (2 marks):** The model uses **bidirectional self-attention** — every token can attend to every other token in both directions. This is ideal for *understanding* tasks (text classification, NER, embeddings) but cannot generate text autoregressively. **Example: BERT** (used in search engines, sentiment analysis).

2. **Decoder-Only (2.5 marks):** The model uses **masked (causal) self-attention** — each token can only attend to itself and previous tokens. This enables **autoregressive generation**: given a prompt, the model generates the next token, then the next, and so on. The stacked decoder blocks form the entire network. **Example: GPT-4, Llama 3, Claude** (used in ChatGPT, Copilot).

3. **Encoder-Decoder (2.5 marks):** The encoder produces a contextualized representation of the input, and the decoder attends to it via **cross-attention** while generating output tokens. The decoder uses masked self-attention over previously generated tokens plus cross-attention over encoder outputs. **Example: T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer), BART** (used in machine translation, summarization).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students frequently **omit the scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$** when writing the attention formula → lose **1 mark**.
> - Confusing **self-attention** with **cross-attention** is a common error. Remember: self-attention has $Q, K, V$ from the *same* sequence; cross-attention has $Q$ from the decoder and $K, V$ from the encoder.
> - Writing the positional encoding formula with $10000^{2i/d}$ as $10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}$ — examiners check for the correct base.
> - Many students write "Transformers use RNNs inside" — **WRONG**. The defining feature is that they **completely eliminate recurrence**.
> - Forgetting to mention **causal masking** when describing decoder-only models like GPT is a frequent deduction point.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Transformer = Attention Is All You Need.** No recurrence, no convolution — pure self-attention.
- [x] **Core formula:** $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$.
- [x] **Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$** to prevent softmax saturation in high dimensions.
- [x] **Multi-head attention** allows the model to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces.
- [x] **Positional encoding** (sinusoidal) is mandatory because self-attention is permutation-invariant.
- [x] **Residual + LayerNorm** stabilizes training of deep stacks.
- [x] **Encoder = bidirectional attention**; **Decoder = masked (causal) attention + cross-attention**.
- [x] **GPT, BERT, T5** are the three canonical variants of the Transformer.
- [x] **Complexity bottleneck:** $O(n^2)$ in sequence length → motivates efficient variants (FlashAttention, Linformer, etc.).
- [x] **FFN inner dimension = $4 \times d_{\text{model}}$** in the original paper; Gated FFN (SwiGLU) in modern LLMs.
- [x] **Original paper:** 6 encoder + 6 decoder layers, $d_{\text{model}} = 512$, $h = 8$ heads.
- [x] **Modern LLMs:** 96+ layers, $d_{\text{model}} = 12{,}288$, $h = 96$ heads, trillions of tokens.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BERT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** BERT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)** is a deep learning language representation model introduced by Google Research in 2018 (Devlin et al., in the paper *"BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*). It is a **pre-trained, multi-layer, bidirectional Transformer encoder** that learns contextual representations of tokens in a text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Precise Terminology):**
> BERT is a **Transformer-Encoder-only architecture** that employs **self-attention mechanisms** to generate **contextualized embeddings**. Unlike traditional unidirectional language models (e.g., GPT is left-to-right), BERT reads the entire sequence of tokens **simultaneously in both directions**, making it deeply bidirectional. It is pre-trained using two self-supervised objectives: **Masked Language Modeling (MLM)** and **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)**, and can be **fine-tuned** with just one additional output layer to achieve state-of-the-art performance on a wide range of downstream NLP tasks.

| Variant | Layers (L) | Hidden Size (H) | Attention Heads (A) | Total Parameters |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **BERT-Base** | **12** | **768** | **12** | **≈ 110 Million** |
| **BERT-Large** | **24** | **1024** | **16** | **≈ 340 Million** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reading Comprehension" Intuition

Imagine a student who only ever reads a book **from left to right**, one word at a time, never glancing ahead. This student (analogous to a **uni-directional** model like the traditional RNN or GPT) struggles to understand words whose meaning depends on *what comes after* them. For example, in the sentence:

*"The man went to the **bank** to deposit money, and later sat on the **river bank** to fish."*

A left-to-right reader would confuse the two meanings of *"bank"*. 

Now imagine a **careful proofreader with a highlighter** who, for every single word, reads the **entire sentence before and after it simultaneously**, weighing which surrounding words are most important. This proofreader is **bidirectional** and uses **contextual attention** — exactly how BERT works. BERT, for every token, "looks" at all other tokens in the sentence to figure out the most appropriate meaning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Bidirectional" is the Key Innovation:**
> Before BERT, models like **ELMo** used shallow concatenation of independent left-to-right and right-to-left LSTMs. BERT, in contrast, achieves **deep bidirectionality** — the bidirectional context is fused *inside* every Transformer layer, not merely concatenated at the top.

## 1.3 Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning — The Two-Phase Paradigm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Pre-train + Fine-tune" Transfer Learning Paradigm:**
> 1. **Pre-training Phase:** BERT is trained on a massive unlabeled corpus (Wikipedia **2.5B words** + BookCorpus **800M words**) using self-supervised tasks. The model learns universal language understanding.
> 2. **Fine-tuning Phase:** The pre-trained BERT is initialized with its learned weights, and a small task-specific output layer is added. The entire model is then trained on a small labeled dataset for the downstream task (e.g., question answering, sentiment analysis).

This two-phase approach allows a single pre-trained BERT to be **reused across dozens of NLP tasks**, saving enormous computational cost compared to training a task-specific model from scratch.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Transformer Encoder Backbone

BERT's core building block is the **Transformer encoder layer** (proposed by Vaswani et al., 2017). Each encoder layer consists of two sub-layers:

1. **Multi-Head Self-Attention (MHSA)**
2. **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)**

Each sub-layer is wrapped with a **residual connection** and **Layer Normalization**.

### Step 2.1.1 — Self-Attention Mechanism

Self-attention allows each token in the sequence to attend to **every other token**, computing a weighted sum of value vectors based on the compatibility between query and key vectors.

For an input sequence represented as a matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ (where $n$ = sequence length, $d$ = hidden size), the three learnable projection matrices are:

$$W^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}, \quad W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}, \quad W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_v}$$

The **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** is computed as:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

The scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ prevents the dot products from growing too large in magnitude, which would push the softmax into regions of extremely small gradients.

### Step 2.1.2 — Multi-Head Attention

Instead of performing a single attention function, BERT uses **$A$ parallel attention heads** (12 for BERT-Base, 16 for BERT-Large), each operating on a lower-dimensional subspace of size $d/A$. The outputs are concatenated and linearly projected.

$$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_A)\, W^O$$

$$\text{where} \quad \text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(X W^Q_i, X W^K_i, X W^V_i)$$

For BERT-Base: $d = 768$, $A = 12$, so each head operates on $d_k = d_v = 64$ dimensions.

### Step 2.1.3 — Position-wise Feed-Forward Network

A two-layer fully connected network applied **independently and identically** to each position:

$$\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0,\, x W_1 + b_1)\, W_2 + b_2$$

In BERT-Base, the inner dimension is **3072** (i.e., $4 \times d = 4 \times 768$).

### Step 2.1.4 — Residual Connection + Layer Normalization

Each sub-layer output is wrapped as:

$$\text{Output} = \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$$

LayerNorm stabilizes training by normalizing activations across the feature dimension, and the residual connection allows gradients to flow through the deep stack of $L$ layers.

## 2.2 BERT Input Representation

BERT uses **WordPiece tokenization** (a subword tokenization algorithm) with a vocabulary of **≈ 30,000 tokens**. Each input embedding is the **sum of three embeddings**:

$$E_{\text{input}} = E_{\text{token}} + E_{\text{segment}} + E_{\text{position}}$$

| Embedding Type | Purpose | Shape for BERT-Base |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| **Token Embeddings** | WordPiece token identity (uses **30,522** vocab) | $(n, 768)$ |
| **Segment Embeddings** | Distinguishes Sentence A vs Sentence B (0 or 1) | $(n, 768)$ |
| **Position Embeddings** | Absolute position index (max 512 tokens) | $(n, 768)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Tokens Used by BERT:**
> - **[CLS]** — inserted at the start of every input; its final hidden state is used as the aggregate sequence representation for classification tasks.
> - **[SEP]** — separates sentence pairs (Segment A from Segment B).
> - **[MASK]** — replaces selected tokens during pre-training for the MLM objective.

## 2.3 Pre-training Objectives (The Two Self-Supervised Tasks)

### Task 1: Masked Language Modeling (MLM)

Before feeding word sequences into BERT, **15% of the WordPiece tokens** are replaced at random. Of those selected tokens:
- **80%** are replaced with the `[MASK]` token,
- **10%** are replaced with a **random** token,
- **10%** are left **unchanged**.

The model then predicts the **original vocabulary ID** of the masked token based on the context provided by the surrounding unmasked tokens. The loss function is **cross-entropy** computed **only on the masked positions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the 80/10/10 split?**
> Pure masking (100% [MASK]) creates a mismatch between pre-training and fine-tuning, because `[MASK]` never appears during fine-tuning. Replacing 10% with random words and 10% with original words forces the model to maintain a **contextual distribution** over all tokens, not just `[MASK]`, mitigating this mismatch.

### Task 2: Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)

Given a pair of sentences (A, B), BERT must predict whether **B is the actual next sentence that follows A** in the original text. This is a **binary classification** task where:
- **50%** of the time, B is the actual next sentence → label = `IsNext`.
- **50%** of the time, B is a random sentence from the corpus → label = `NotNext`.

The prediction is made from the `[CLS]` token's final hidden state $C \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$, passed through a classification layer:

$$P(\text{IsNext} \mid A, B) = \text{softmax}(C W^{\text{NSP}})$$

The joint pre-training loss is the **sum of the mean MLM likelihood and the mean NSP likelihood**.

## 2.4 Fine-Tuning for Downstream Tasks

For each downstream task, we plug in the appropriate input/output structure and **fine-tune all parameters end-to-end**. The four canonical fine-tuning configurations are:

| Downstream Task | Input Format | Output Layer |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sentence Classification** (e.g., sentiment) | Single sentence | Use `[CLS]` hidden state $C$ → feed-forward → softmax |
| **Pair Classification** (e.g., NLI) | Sentence pair (A, B) | Use `[CLS]` → feed-forward → softmax |
| **Question Answering** (e.g., SQuAD) | (Question, Passage) | Predict start/end span indices |
| **Token Tagging** (e.g., NER, POS) | Single sentence | Per-token softmax over label set |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative relationship you will need to answer KTU exam questions on BERT.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables & Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$ | $Q,K,V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ |
| 2 | Multi-Head Attention | $\text{MHA}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(h_1,\ldots,h_A)\, W^O$ | $h_i$ is the $i$-th head |
| 3 | Feed-Forward Network | $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0,\, xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$ | Inner dim = $4d$ |
| 4 | Residual + Layer Norm | $y = \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$ | Applied around every sub-layer |
| 5 | Input Embedding Sum | $E = E_{\text{tok}} + E_{\text{seg}} + E_{\text{pos}}$ | All in $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ |
| 6 | Total BERT-Base Params | $L = 12,\; d = 768,\; A = 12,\; \text{params} \approx 110\text{M}$ | Sequence length cap = **512** |
| 7 | Total BERT-Large Params | $L = 24,\; d = 1024,\; A = 16,\; \text{params} \approx 340\text{M}$ | Sequence length cap = **512** |
| 8 | MLM Masking Rate | **15%** of tokens chosen; split **80/10/10** | Mask / random / unchanged |
| 9 | NSP Sampling Rate | **50%** IsNext, **50%** NotNext | Binary classification |
| 10 | NSP Probability | $P(\text{IsNext}) = \text{softmax}(CW^{\text{NSP}})$ | $C$ = `[CLS]` final state |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of BERT:**
> BERT is the foundational model for **search engines** (Google uses a BERT variant for query understanding), **chatbots**, **biomedical NLP** (BioBERT), **legal document analysis** (LegalBERT), **clinical text mining**, and **code search**. Its bidirectional contextual embeddings are also used as feature extractors in **multi-modal models**, **recommender systems**, and even **protein language modeling** (ProtBERT).

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Self-Attention Output (Complete Walkthrough)

We will derive the full self-attention computation for a sequence of $n$ tokens, step by step, in a manner that can be written directly in a KTU 14-mark answer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** Consider an input sequence represented as a matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ where $n$ is the number of tokens and $d$ is the embedding dimension. Each row $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the embedding of the $i$-th token.

**Step 1 — Linear Projections to Q, K, V**

We apply three learnable linear transformations:

$$Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V$$

where $W^Q, W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}$ and $W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_v}$. The resulting matrices have shape $Q, K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ and $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$.

**Step 2 — Compute Raw Compatibility Scores (QKᵀ)**

We compute the dot product between every query and every key:

$$S = Q K^{\top}$$

The result $S \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ where entry $S_{ij}$ measures how much token $i$ should attend to token $j$.

**Step 3 — Scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$**

To prevent the dot products from growing in magnitude with large $d_k$ (which would saturate the softmax), we scale:

$$\hat{S} = \frac{S}{\sqrt{d_k}} = \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

This keeps the variance of $\hat{S}_{ij}$ close to **1** when $Q$ and $K$ entries have zero mean and unit variance, ensuring well-conditioned gradients.

**Step 4 — Apply Softmax Row-wise**

Each row of $\hat{S}$ is passed through a softmax to produce a **probability distribution** over all positions:

$$A_{ij} = \text{softmax}(\hat{S}_i)_{j} = \frac{\exp(\hat{S}_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(\hat{S}_{ik})}$$

The resulting attention matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ satisfies $\sum_j A_{ij} = 1$ for each row $i$.

**Step 5 — Weighted Sum of Value Vectors**

Each output token representation $z_i$ is a weighted sum of the value vectors:

$$Z = A V$$

$$z_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{ij}\, v_j$$

This gives the final self-attention output $Z \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$. We can write the complete expression as:

$$\boxed{\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Toy Calculation)

Let us walk through a **3-token toy example** to make the math concrete.

> [!NOTE]
> Suppose $n = 3$, $d_k = 2$, and we have already computed:
> $$Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad K = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad V = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \\ 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Raw Scores**

We compute $Q K^{\top}$:

$$Q K^{\top} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Scale by $\sqrt{d_k} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$**

$$\hat{S} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & 0.000 & 0.707 \\ 0.000 & 0.707 & 0.707 \\ 0.707 & 0.707 & 1.414 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Row-wise Softmax**

Row 1: $e^{0.707} \approx 2.028$, $e^{0} = 1$, $e^{0.707} \approx 2.028$. Sum $\approx 5.056$. Softmax = $[0.401,\ 0.198,\ 0.401]$.

Row 2: identical structure (since row 2 of $\hat{S}$ is the same as row 1) → $[0.401,\ 0.198,\ 0.401]$.

Row 3: $e^{0.707} \approx 2.028$, $e^{0.707} \approx 2.028$, $e^{1.414} \approx 4.113$. Sum $\approx 8.169$. Softmax = $[0.248,\ 0.248,\ 0.503]$.

$$A \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.401 & 0.198 & 0.401 \\ 0.401 & 0.198 & 0.401 \\ 0.248 & 0.248 & 0.503 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Weighted Sum of Values**

$$Z = A V = \begin{bmatrix} 0.401 & 0.198 & 0.401 \\ 0.401 & 0.198 & 0.401 \\ 0.248 & 0.248 & 0.503 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \\ 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Row 1: $[0.401(1) + 0.198(3) + 0.401(5),\ 0.401(2) + 0.198(4) + 0.401(6)] = [2.997,\ 4.001]$.
Row 2 (same as row 1): $[2.997,\ 4.001]$.
Row 3: $[0.248(1) + 0.248(3) + 0.503(5),\ 0.248(2) + 0.248(4) + 0.503(6)] = [3.507,\ 4.502]$.

$$Z \approx \begin{bmatrix} 2.997 & 4.001 \\ 2.997 & 4.001 \\ 3.507 & 4.502 \end{bmatrix}$$

## 3.3 Multi-Head Attention — Combining $A$ Heads

For BERT-Base, $A = 12$ heads. Each head $i$ computes a separate $(Q_i, K_i, V_i)$ using distinct projection matrices $W^Q_i, W^K_i, W^V_i \in \mathbb{R}^{768 \times 64}$:

$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(X W^Q_i,\ X W^K_i,\ X W^V_i) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 64}$$

The 12 head outputs are concatenated horizontally:

$$\text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_{12}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 768}$$

A final output projection $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{768 \times 768}$ is applied:

$$\text{MHA}(X) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_{12})\, W^O$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multiple heads?** Different heads can learn to attend to **different linguistic relations** in parallel — one head might focus on syntactic dependencies, another on co-reference, another on positional proximity. The multi-head design lets the model **jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: BERT's Bidirectional Advantage over GPT

| Property | BERT | GPT (Traditional LM) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Architecture** | Transformer **Encoder** | Transformer **Decoder** |
| **Direction** | **Bidirectional** (deep) | Unidirectional (left-to-right) |
| **Pre-training** | MLM + NSP | Causal Language Modeling |
| **Token visibility** | All tokens visible to all others | Each token sees only previous tokens |
| **Best suited for** | Understanding tasks (classification, QA, NER) | Generation tasks |
| **[CLS] usage** | Used as sequence representation | Not typically used |
| **Token type** | Subword (WordPiece) | Subword (BPE) |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level BERT Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA["INPUT TOKENS"] --> tokenEmb["Token Embeddings"]
    inputA --> segEmb["Segment Embeddings"]
    inputA --> posEmb["Position Embeddings"]
    tokenEmb --> sumE["Element-wise Sum"]
    segEmb --> sumE
    posEmb --> sumE
    sumE --> enc1["Transformer Encoder Layer 1"]
    enc1 --> enc2["Transformer Encoder Layer 2"]
    enc2 --> encD["Transformer Encoder Layer L"]
    encD --> outCls["[CLS] Hidden State C"]
    encD --> outTok["Per-Token Hidden States T"]
    outCls --> mlm["MLM Head - Token Prediction"]
    outTok --> mlm
    outCls --> nsp["NSP Head - Binary Classifier"]
    outCls --> ft1["Fine-tune: Sentence Classification"]
    outCls --> ft2["Fine-tune: Pair Classification"]
    outTok --> ft3["Fine-tune: Token Tagging NER POS"]
    outTok --> ft4["Fine-tune: Question Answering"]
```

## 4.2 Inside a Single Transformer Encoder Layer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inX["Input x from previous layer"] --> mha["Multi-Head Self-Attention"]
    inX --> add1["Residual Add"]
    mha --> add1
    add1 --> ln1["Layer Normalization"]
    ln1 --> ffn["Feed-Forward Network FFN"]
    ln1 --> add2["Residual Add"]
    ffn --> add2
    add2 --> ln2["Layer Normalization"]
    ln2 --> outY["Output y to next layer"]
```

## 4.3 BERT Pre-training and Fine-tuning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph pretrain["PRE-TRAINING PHASE"]
        corpusA["Wikipedia 2.5B words"] --> tok1["WordPiece Tokenization"]
        corpusB["BookCorpus 800M words"] --> tok1
        tok1 --> mask["Mask 15 percent of tokens 80 MASK 10 Random 10 Unchanged"]
        mask --> enc1["Stack of L Transformer Encoders"]
        enc1 --> mlmLoss["MLM Loss Cross Entropy on Masked Positions"]
        enc1 --> nspLoss["NSP Loss Binary Cross Entropy from CLS"]
        mlmLoss --> total["Total Loss = MLM + NSP"]
        nspLoss --> total
        total --> saveW["Save Pre-trained Weights BERT-Base or Large"]
    end
    subgraph finetune["FINE-TUNING PHASE"]
        saveW --> initW["Initialize with Pre-trained Weights"]
        initW --> taskIn["Task-Specific Input e.g. Question + Passage"]
        taskIn --> taskHead["Add Task-Specific Output Layer"]
        taskHead --> ftTrain["Fine-tune all parameters on small labeled dataset"]
        ftTrain --> pred["Predictions for Downstream Task"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Self-Attention Score Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    xIn["Input Embeddings X n times d"] --> projQ["Multiply by WQ -> Q"]
    xIn --> projK["Multiply by WK -> K"]
    xIn --> projV["Multiply by WV -> V"]
    projQ --> qkT["Compute Q times K Transpose -> S n times n"]
    projK --> qkT
    qkT --> scale["Divide each entry by sqrt dk"]
    scale --> soft["Row-wise Softmax -> Attention Matrix A"]
    soft --> av["Multiply A by V -> Output Z"]
    projV --> av
    av --> outS["Self-Attention Output Z n times d"]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Fine-tuning Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph sentCls["SENTENCE CLASSIFICATION"]
        s1["Single Sentence Input"] --> s2["Add CLS and SEP"]
        s2 --> s3["BERT Encoder Stack"]
        s3 --> s4["CLS hidden state C"]
        s4 --> s5["Softmax Classifier"]
    end
    subgraph pairCls["PAIR CLASSIFICATION e.g. NLI"]
        p1["Sentence A + Sentence B"] --> p2["Add CLS and SEP and Segment IDs"]
        p2 --> p3["BERT Encoder Stack"]
        p3 --> p4["CLS hidden state C"]
        p4 --> p5["Softmax over 2 or 3 classes"]
    end
    subgraph qa["QUESTION ANSWERING e.g. SQuAD"]
        q1["Question + Passage"] --> q2["Add CLS and SEP and Segment IDs"]
        q2 --> q3["BERT Encoder Stack"]
        q3 --> q4["Per-token start vector S"]
        q3 --> q5["Per-token end vector E"]
        q4 --> q6["Softmax over all passage tokens -> start index"]
        q5 --> q7["Softmax over all passage tokens -> end index"]
    end
    subgraph tag["TOKEN TAGGING e.g. NER"]
        t1["Single Sentence"] --> t2["Add CLS and SEP"]
        t2 --> t3["BERT Encoder Stack"]
        t3 --> t4["Per-token hidden states"]
        t4 --> t5["Softmax over label set at each position"]
    end
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**Q: Define BERT. What are the two pre-training objectives used in BERT?**

**Model Answer:**
BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) is a **Transformer-encoder-only** deep learning model developed by Google (Devlin et al., 2018) that learns **deep bidirectional contextual representations** of text by conditioning on both left and right context simultaneously in every layer.

The **two pre-training objectives** are:
1. **Masked Language Modeling (MLM):** Randomly mask 15% of the input tokens and train the model to predict them from the surrounding context (using 80% `[MASK]`, 10% random, 10% original).
2. **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP):** Train the model to predict whether sentence B is the actual next sentence that follows sentence A in the original text (binary classification).

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**Q: Differentiate between BERT-Base and BERT-Large in terms of architecture.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | BERT-Base | BERT-Large |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Transformer Encoder Layers ($L$) | 12 | 24 |
| Hidden Size ($d$) | 768 | 1024 |
| Attention Heads ($A$) | 12 | 16 |
| Feed-Forward Inner Dimension | 3072 | 4096 |
| Total Parameters | **≈ 110 Million** | **≈ 340 Million** |

Both variants use a vocabulary of **≈ 30,522 WordPiece tokens** and a maximum sequence length of **512 tokens**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions of 14 marks have **internal choice**. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two completely independent alternatives — the student answers one. Each has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of BERT in detail. Discuss the role of the Multi-Head Self-Attention mechanism, the Feed-Forward Network, residual connections, and layer normalization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the **Masked Language Modeling (MLM)** and **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)** pre-training tasks. Justify why BERT uses the **80/10/10 splitting rule** for masked tokens. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A(a) (7 marks)**

**BERT Architecture Overview:**
BERT-Base consists of a stack of **$L = 12$** identical Transformer encoder layers. The input is a sequence of WordPiece tokens of maximum length **512**. Each input token is represented as the sum of three embeddings:

$$E_{\text{input}} = E_{\text{token}} + E_{\text{segment}} + E_{\text{position}}$$

where $E_{\text{token}}$ is the WordPiece token embedding, $E_{\text{segment}}$ distinguishes sentence A from B, and $E_{\text{position}}$ encodes absolute positions (0 to 511).

**Each Transformer Encoder Layer contains two sub-layers:**

**1. Multi-Head Self-Attention (MHSA) — 7 marks broken down:**
- Input $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ is projected into $A = 12$ sets of (Query, Key, Value) using learnable matrices $W^Q_i, W^K_i, W^V_i \in \mathbb{R}^{768 \times 64}$.
- Scaled dot-product attention is computed per head:
$$\text{head}_i = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{(XW^Q_i)(XW^K_i)^{\top}}{\sqrt{64}}\right)(XW^V_i)$$
- The 12 heads are concatenated and projected with $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{768 \times 768}$.
- Multi-head attention allows the model to **jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces** at different positions.

**2. Position-wise Feed-Forward Network:**
- A two-layer MLP applied independently to each position: $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0,\, xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$, with inner dimension **3072** and ReLU activation.
- This adds **non-linear representational capacity** to each token independently.

**3. Residual Connection + Layer Normalization:**
- Around each sub-layer: $y = \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$.
- Residuals **mitigate vanishing gradients** in the 12-layer stack; LayerNorm **stabilizes training** by normalizing activations.

**4. Output:**
- Each layer outputs a sequence of $d = 768$ dimensional contextual embeddings that become the input to the next layer.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A(b) (7 marks)**

**1. Masked Language Modeling (MLM):**
- Before feeding the input to BERT, **15%** of the WordPiece tokens in each sequence are selected at random for prediction.
- Of these selected tokens:
  - **80%** are replaced with the special `[MASK]` token (e.g., *"my dog is hairy"* → *"my dog is `[MASK]`"*).
  - **10%** are replaced with a **random** token (e.g., *"my dog is apple"*).
  - **10%** are left **unchanged** (e.g., *"my dog is hairy"*).
- The model is trained to predict the **original token ID** at every masked position using a **cross-entropy loss** computed only over the masked positions.
- The final hidden vector corresponding to the masked position is fed into a softmax over the vocabulary.

**2. Next Sentence Prediction (NSP):**
- For each pre-training example, a pair of sentences (A, B) is sampled.
- **50%** of the time, B is the **actual next sentence** that follows A in the corpus → label = `IsNext`.
- **50%** of the time, B is a **random sentence** from the corpus → label = `NotNext`.
- The `[CLS]` token's final hidden state $C \in \mathbb{R}^{768}$ is passed through a classification layer:
$$P(\text{IsNext}) = \text{softmax}(C W^{\text{NSP}})$$
- NSP trains BERT to model **sentence-level relationships**, which is critical for downstream tasks like Question Answering and Natural Language Inference.

**3. Justification of the 80/10/10 Split:**
- If 100% of selected tokens were replaced with `[MASK]`, then `[MASK]` would never appear during fine-tuning, creating a **train-test mismatch**.
- Replacing 10% with random tokens forces the model to maintain a **broad contextual distribution** over all tokens (so it cannot trivially memorize that masked positions are special).
- Leaving 10% unchanged gives the model **real contextual signal** for the original token, preserving language modeling ability.
- The combination produces representations that transfer better to downstream tasks where `[MASK]` is absent.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Transformer Encoder** used in BERT. Derive the **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** formula and explain why scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ is necessary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **fine-tuning procedure** of BERT for (i) Question Answering (SQuAD) and (ii) Token-level tasks like Named Entity Recognition (NER). Show the input/output format for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B(a) (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (Textual Representation for Exam):**
```
   Input X ──┬──► Multi-Head Self-Attention ──► Add & LayerNorm ──┬──► FFN ──► Add & LayerNorm ──► Output
             │                                                    │
             └──────────────── residual ──────────────────────────┘
```

**Derivation of Scaled Dot-Product Attention:**

Given an input matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ representing $n$ token embeddings each of dimension $d$:

**Step 1:** Project $X$ into queries, keys, and values using three learnable matrices:
$$Q = XW^Q, \quad K = XW^K, \quad V = XW^V$$

**Step 2:** Compute raw attention scores by taking the dot product of every query with every key:
$$S = QK^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

**Step 3:** Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to keep gradients well-conditioned:
$$\hat{S} = \frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

**Step 4:** Apply row-wise softmax to convert scores into a probability distribution:
$$A_{ij} = \frac{\exp(\hat{S}_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n}\exp(\hat{S}_{ik})}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the output as a weighted sum of value vectors:
$$Z = AV$$

Combining all steps, the final formula is:

$$\boxed{\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V}$$

**Why scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ is necessary:**
- If the entries of $Q$ and $K$ are independent random variables with mean 0 and variance 1, then the dot product $Q \cdot K$ has variance $d_k$ (sum of $d_k$ products of zero-mean unit-variance variables).
- For large $d_k$ (e.g., $d_k = 64$ in BERT-Base), the dot products grow large in magnitude, pushing the softmax into regions of **very small gradients** (saturated regime).
- Scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance back to ~1, keeping the softmax in a region where **gradients flow well** during back-propagation.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B(b) (7 marks)**

**Fine-tuning BERT: General Setup**
- Initialize model weights from the pre-trained BERT checkpoint.
- Add a small task-specific output layer (typically one or two feed-forward layers).
- Train all parameters end-to-end on the task's labeled data using a small learning rate (e.g., $2 \times 10^{-5}$ to $5 \times 10^{-5}$) for 2–4 epochs.

**(i) Fine-tuning for Question Answering (SQuAD-style Extractive QA):**

**Input format:**
- A single packed sequence: `[CLS] Question tokens [SEP] Passage tokens [SEP]`
- Segment IDs: `0` for question tokens, `1` for passage tokens.

**Output format:**
- The final hidden state of **every passage token** $T_i$ is scored using two learned vectors $S \in \mathbb{R}^{768}$ (start) and $E \in \mathbb{R}^{768}$ (end).
- Start score at position $i$: $P_i^{\text{start}} = \dfrac{e^{S \cdot T_i}}{\sum_j e^{S \cdot T_j}}$.
- End score at position $i$: $P_i^{\text{end}} = \dfrac{e^{E \cdot T_i}}{\sum_j e^{E \cdot T_j}}$.
- The answer span is the pair $(i^{\ast}, j^{\ast})$ that maximizes $P_i^{\text{start}} \cdot P_j^{\text{end}}$ subject to $i \le j$.
- Training loss = sum of cross-entropy losses for start and end positions.

**(ii) Fine-tuning for Token-level Tasks (NER):**

**Input format:**
- A single sentence: `[CLS] Token1 Token2 ... TokenN [SEP]`
- All segment IDs = 0.

**Output format:**
- The final hidden state of **every token** $T_i \in \mathbb{R}^{768}$ is fed into a classification layer that produces a **softmax distribution** over the NER label set (e.g., `B-PER`, `I-PER`, `B-LOC`, `I-LOC`, `O`, etc.).
$$P(\text{label}_i = c \mid T_i) = \text{softmax}(W^{\text{NER}} T_i + b^{\text{NER}})_c$$
- The `[CLS]` and `[SEP]` outputs are typically ignored (or used for auxiliary sentence-level signals).
- Training loss = **token-level cross-entropy** summed over all tokens in the sequence (often masking out `[CLS]` and `[SEP]`).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$** — The dot product alone (without scaling) is *not* the Scaled Dot-Product Attention. Marks are deducted if the formula is written as $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top})V$.
> 2. **Confusing BERT (encoder) with GPT (decoder)** — BERT uses only the **encoder**; it is **not autoregressive** and cannot be used directly for open-ended text generation without modification.
> 3. **Writing the MLM masking as 100% `[MASK]`** — This is wrong. BERT uses the 80/10/10 split to mitigate the pre-train/fine-tune mismatch.
> 4. **Omitting the 80/10/10 justification** — Examiners expect students to **explain why** the split is used, not just state the numbers.
> 5. **Confusing the role of `[CLS]`** — `[CLS]` is used for **sentence/pair-level classification** (and NSP during pre-training), not for per-token predictions.
> 6. **Forgetting the three embedding components** — Input representation is the **sum of three embeddings** (token + segment + position), not just the token embedding.
> 7. **Stating wrong parameter counts** — BERT-Base is **110M**, BERT-Large is **340M**. Mixing these up is a frequent error.
> 8. **Calling BERT "bidirectional LSTM" or "bidirectional RNN"** — BERT is purely a **Transformer encoder**. There are no recurrent components.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density BERT Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam:**

- **Full form:** Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers.
- **Paper:** Devlin et al., 2018, Google Research.
- **Architecture:** Transformer **encoder only** (no decoder).
- **Bidirectionality:** **Deep** — every layer sees left + right context simultaneously (unlike ELMo's shallow concatenation).
- **BERT-Base:** $L=12$, $d=768$, $A=12$ heads, **≈ 110M parameters**.
- **BERT-Large:** $L=24$, $d=1024$, $A=16$ heads, **≈ 340M parameters**.
- **Vocabulary:** **30,522** WordPiece tokens.
- **Max sequence length:** **512** tokens.
- **Three input embeddings summed:** Token + Segment + Position.
- **Special tokens:** `[CLS]` (classification), `[SEP]` (separator), `[MASK]` (pre-training).
- **Pre-training data:** Wikipedia (2.5B words) + BookCorpus (800M words).
- **Pre-training Task 1 — MLM:** Mask 15% of tokens; 80% `[MASK]`, 10% random, 10% original. Predict original ID via softmax over vocabulary; cross-entropy loss on masked positions only.
- **Pre-training Task 2 — NSP:** Predict if sentence B follows sentence A; 50/50 positive/negative; binary classification from `[CLS]` state.
- **Scaled Dot-Product Attention formula:** $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$.
- **Why $\sqrt{d_k}$:** Prevents softmax saturation; keeps gradient flow healthy.
- **Multi-Head Attention:** $A$ parallel heads (12 in Base, 16 in Large), each with $d/A$-dim subspaces, concatenated and projected.
- **FFN:** 2-layer MLP, inner dim = $4d$, ReLU activation, applied position-wise.
- **Residual + LayerNorm:** Around every sub-layer; $y = \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$.
- **Fine-tuning applications:** Sentence classification (`[CLS]`), Pair classification (`[CLS]`), QA (start/end span vectors), Token tagging (per-token softmax).
- **Typical fine-tuning hyperparams:** Learning rate $2 \times 10^{-5}$ to $5 \times 10^{-5}$, 2–4 epochs, batch size 16 or 32.
- **BERT vs. GPT:** BERT = encoder, bidirectional, understanding; GPT = decoder, left-to-right, generation.
- **Why BERT matters:** First model to achieve **deep bidirectionality** + **transfer learning** at scale for NLP; revolutionized 11+ downstream tasks.
- **Common follow-ups in exam:** Comparison with ELMo, comparison with GPT, derivations of attention, fine-tuning for SQuAD/NER.
- **Key numbers to memorize cold:** 110M, 340M, 30,522 vocab, 512 length, 15% mask, 80/10/10 split, 12 layers (Base), 768 dim (Base), 12 heads (Base).
- **Engineering applications:** Search ranking (Google), chatbots, biomedical NLP (BioBERT), legal NLP (LegalBERT), code search, protein modeling (ProtBERT).
- **Common pitfall answers to avoid:** Calling BERT "bidirectional RNN", writing $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top})V$ without $\sqrt{d_k}$, using 100% `[MASK]`, confusing `[CLS]`'s role.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GPT-2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** GPT-2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of GPT-2

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**GPT-2 (Generative Pre-Trained Transformer 2)** is a large-scale, unsupervised **autoregressive language model** released by OpenAI in February 2019. It belongs to the family of **Transformer-decoder-only** architectures and is designed to perform **zero-shot task transfer** — meaning it can generalize to new tasks it has never explicitly been trained on, purely through natural language prompts.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> GPT-2 is a multi-layer Transformer decoder trained on a massive corpus called **WebText** using a causal language modeling objective, predicting the next token $w_t$ given all previous tokens $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{t-1}$ in a sequence.

Formally, the joint probability over a token sequence is factorized as:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{t=1}^{n} P(w_t \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{t-1})$$

where each conditional probability is parameterized by a deep Transformer network with parameters $\theta$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hyper-Literate Autocomplete"

Imagine you are texting a friend. As you type each word, your phone suggests the next word. GPT-2 is essentially this autocomplete mechanism, but scaled up to **1.5 billion parameters**, trained on **8 million web pages**. It does not just "guess the next word" — it has internalized grammar, facts, reasoning patterns, and even translation ability, all by repeatedly performing one simple task: *predict the next word given everything before it*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** GPT-2's brilliance lies in the hypothesis *"if you train a big enough model on enough text to predict the next word, it will spontaneously learn a wide range of human-like language abilities — without being explicitly told to."*

## 1.3 Key Highlight Metrics of GPT-2

| Metric | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Release Date | February 2019 |
| Developer | OpenAI |
| Training Dataset | **WebText (40 GB of text)** |
| Vocabulary Size | **50,257 Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) tokens** |
| Context Window | **1024 tokens** |
| Largest Variant | **1.5 Billion parameters (GPT-2 XL)** |
| Training Objective | Causal Language Modeling (Next-token prediction) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why BPE Tokenization?** Byte-Pair Encoding handles rare words, common words, and even sub-words efficiently, allowing the model to represent any text in a compact 50,257-token vocabulary without an "unknown word" problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The GPT-2 Architecture — Layer-by-Layer Breakdown

GPT-2 is a **stack of identical Transformer decoder blocks**. Each block contains two sub-layers:

1. **Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention** — captures contextual relationships, but only looks at past tokens (causal mask).
2. **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)** — a two-layer MLP applied independently to each token.

Both sub-layers are wrapped with **residual connections** and **layer normalization**.

### Core Logical Flow:

- **Input Representation**: Each token is converted into a vector by summing a *token embedding* and a *positional embedding*.
- **Stacked Decoder Blocks (Nx times)**: For GPT-2 Small, $N_x = 12$; for GPT-2 XL, $N_x = 48$.
- **Final LayerNorm**: Stabilizes the output.
- **Linear Projection + Softmax**: Produces a probability distribution over the 50,257-token vocabulary for the next token.

## 2.2 The Four Released Model Variants

| Variant | Parameters $n_{params}$ | Layers $L$ | $d_{model}$ | Heads $h$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **GPT-2 Small** | 124 M | 12 | 768 | 12 |
| **GPT-2 Medium** | 355 M | 24 | 1024 | 16 |
| **GPT-2 Large** | 774 M | 36 | 1280 | 20 |
| **GPT-2 XL** | 1.5 B | 48 | 1600 | 25 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always remember the smallest variant is 124M and largest is 1.5B. The architecture is purely a *decoder* — there is **no encoder**, which distinguishes it from models like BERT.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes the foundational equations every KTU 2024 student must memorize for GPT-2 questions.

| Concept | Equation | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Causal Language Model** | $P(w) = \prod_{t} P(w_t \vert w_{<t}; \theta)$ | Autoregressive factorization of sequence probability |
| **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | Core attention operation with causal masking |
| **Multi-Head Attention** | $\text{MHA}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h)W^O$ | Parallel attention heads with output projection |
| **Single Attention Head** | $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V)$ | Linear projection per head |
| **FFN Sub-Layer** | $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0, xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$ | Position-wise two-layer feed-forward network |
| **GELU Activation** | $\text{GELU}(x) = 0.5x\!\left(1 + \tanh\!\left(\sqrt{2/\pi}(x + 0.044715x^3)\right)\right)$ | Non-linear activation used in GPT-2 FFN |
| **Layer Normalization** | $\text{LayerNorm}(x) = \gamma \cdot \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma} + \beta$ | Per-token normalization for training stability |
| **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t} \log P(w_t \vert w_{<t}; \theta)$ | Negative log-likelihood training objective |
| **Byte-Pair Encoding Merge** | $V_{\text{new}} = V \cup \{a\!+\!b\}$ | Iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent token pair |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\sqrt{d_k}$ in attention?** It prevents the dot products from growing too large in magnitude, which would push the softmax into saturated regions where gradients vanish.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

GPT-2 is the architectural ancestor of ChatGPT, GPT-3, GPT-4, and most modern LLMs. Its key engineering contributions include:

- **Text Generation** for content creation, code autocompletion, and creative writing.
- **Zero-Shot Learning** — solves tasks like translation, summarization, and question-answering without any task-specific training data.
- **Foundation for Downstream Models** — many domain-specific models (BioGPT, CodeGPT, LegalBERT variants) build on the GPT-2 backbone via fine-tuning.
- **Benchmark for NLP Research** — its release paper *"Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners"* (Radford et al., 2019) is a landmark in demonstrating emergent capabilities of scale.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Raw Text to Causal Language Model Loss

Let a training corpus contain a sequence of tokens $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$. We want to maximize the likelihood of the data under the model with parameters $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Express the joint probability using the chain rule of probability:**

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdots P(w_n \mid w_1, \dots, w_{n-1})$$

**Step 2 — Compact the chain rule using product notation:**

$$P(w_1, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{t=1}^{n} P(w_t \mid w_1, \dots, w_{t-1})$$

**Step 3 — Replace the conditional probability with the neural network's output (the softmax over vocabulary):**

$$P(w_t \mid w_{<t}; \theta) = \frac{\exp(f_\theta(w_{<t})[w_t])}{\sum_{v=1}^{V} \exp(f_\theta(w_{<t})[v])}$$

where $V = 50{,}257$ is the vocabulary size and $f_\theta(w_{<t})[v]$ is the logit for vocabulary token $v$.

**Step 4 — Take the log to convert products into sums (numerical stability):**

$$\log P(w_1, \dots, w_n) = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \log P(w_t \mid w_{<t}; \theta)$$

**Step 5 — Negate to convert maximization into minimization (loss):**

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\sum_{t=1}^{n} \log P(w_t \mid w_{<t}; \theta)$$

This is the **cross-entropy loss** minimized during GPT-2 training.

## 3.2 Derivation: Masked Self-Attention in GPT-2

GPT-2 uses *causal masking* so that position $t$ cannot attend to future positions.

**Step 1 — Compute raw attention scores as a dot product between Queries and Keys:**

$$S = Q K^{\top}$$

**Step 2 — Scale the scores by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to prevent gradient saturation:**

$$S_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply a causal mask $M$ that sets future positions to $-\infty$:**

$$S_{\text{masked}}[i, j] = \begin{cases} S_{\text{scaled}}[i, j] & \text{if } j \leq i \\ -\infty & \text{if } j > i \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Apply row-wise softmax to get attention weights:**

$$A_{ij} = \frac{\exp(S_{\text{masked}}[i,j])}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(S_{\text{masked}}[i,k])}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply the attention weights with the Values matrix to obtain the output:**

$$\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = A V$$

This ensures the model is **autoregressive** — predictions at time $t$ depend only on $t' \leq t$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Why the Decoder-Only Choice?

| Design Choice | Encoder-Decoder (BERT-style) | Decoder-Only (GPT-2) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Attention Direction | Bidirectional | **Unidirectional (causal)** |
| Pre-training Objective | Masked Language Modeling | **Causal Language Modeling** |
| Natural Fit For | Classification, Embeddings | **Text Generation** |
| Zero-Shot Generation | Weak | **Strong** |
| KTU Context | Understanding tasks | **Generative AI tasks** |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** If a question asks *"Why does GPT-2 use a decoder-only architecture and not an encoder?"* — answer: *"Because GPT-2's goal is generation, where future tokens must remain hidden during training. An encoder would leak future information via bidirectional attention."*

## 3.4 Parametric Analysis: From 124M to 1.5B

Let $L$ = number of layers, $d$ = embedding dimension, $V$ = 50257, $C$ = 1024 (context size).

**Total parameters can be approximated as:**

$$n_{params} \approx 2 \cdot V \cdot d + 2 \cdot L \cdot (4 d^2 + 3 d \cdot d_{ff}) + L \cdot 2 d$$

where $d_{ff} = 4d$ is the FFN hidden dimension.

**Plugging in for GPT-2 Small (L=12, d=768):**

$$n_{params} \approx 2 \cdot 50257 \cdot 768 + 12 \cdot (4 \cdot 768^2 + 3 \cdot 768 \cdot 3072)$$

$$n_{params} \approx 77.2M + 12 \cdot (2{,}359{,}296 + 7{,}077{,}888)$$

$$n_{params} \approx 77.2M + 12 \cdot 9{,}437{,}184 \approx 124M \text{ parameters}$$

This matches the published count, confirming the architectural details.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GPT-2 End-to-End Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete forward pass of GPT-2, from raw text input to the predicted next-token probability distribution.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Text Input] --> A1[Byte Pair Encoding Tokenizer]
    A1 --> A2[Token IDs Sequence]
    A2 --> A3[Token Embedding Lookup]
    A2 --> A4[Positional Embedding Lookup]
    A3 --> A5[Element Wise Sum]
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6[Dropout Layer]
    A6 --> B1[Decoder Block 1]
    B1 --> B2[Decoder Block 2]
    B2 --> B3[Decoder Block N]
    B3 --> B4[Final Layer Normalization]
    B4 --> B5[Linear Projection to Vocab Size]
    B5 --> B6[Softmax Activation]
    B6 --> C1[Next Token Probability Distribution]
    C1 --> C2[Sampled or Argmax Token]
    C2 --> C3[Append to Sequence and Repeat]
```

## 4.2 Internal Anatomy of a Single GPT-2 Decoder Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input Embeddings] --> NORM1[Layer Norm 1]
    NORM1 --> MHA[Masked Multi Head Attention]
    MHA --> ADD1[Residual Addition]
    IN --> ADD1
    ADD1 --> NORM2[Layer Norm 2]
    NORM2 --> FFN[Feed Forward Network with GELU]
    FFN --> ADD2[Residual Addition]
    ADD1 --> ADD2
    ADD2 --> OUT[Block Output]
```

## 4.3 GPT-2 Training and Zero-Shot Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[WebText Corpus 40GB] --> P2[Tokenization via BPE]
    P2 --> P3[Causal Language Modeling Objective]
    P3 --> P4[Train Transformer Decoder Stack]
    P4 --> P5[Frozen Pretrained GPT 2]
    P5 --> Q1[Task Prompt in Natural Language]
    Q1 --> Q2[Zero Shot Generation]
    Q2 --> Q3[Output Completion]
    P5 --> R1[Optional Downstream Fine Tuning]
    R1 --> Q2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice how the same trained model is reused for *all* downstream tasks in zero-shot mode — no architecture change, no weight update, just a different natural-language prompt. This is the central message of the GPT-2 paper.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]
**Define GPT-2. Mention any two architectural details that distinguish it from GPT-1.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** GPT-2 is a large-scale, unsupervised, autoregressive language model based on the Transformer-decoder architecture, developed by OpenAI in 2019, trained on the WebText corpus to perform next-token prediction.
- **Distinguishing Architectural Detail 1 (1 mark):** GPT-2 uses a much larger model size (up to 1.5B parameters vs GPT-1's 117M) and a deeper stack of decoder blocks.
- **Distinguishing Architectural Detail 2 (1 mark):** GPT-2 demonstrates *zero-shot task transfer* — it solves new tasks purely through natural-language prompts without any fine-tuning, whereas GPT-1 required supervised fine-tuning on each downstream task.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]
**What is the WebText dataset? Why was it created instead of using existing datasets like Wikipedia?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **WebText Definition (1 mark):** WebText is a large text corpus of approximately **40 GB**, scraped from **8 million web pages** linked from Reddit posts that received at least 3 upvotes.
- **Reason 1 (1 mark):** Existing datasets are dominated by Wikipedia, which is encyclopedic in nature and not representative of diverse natural language usage, dialogue, and conversational tone.
- **Reason 2 (1 mark):** WebText was curated to be a *natural, broad, and high-quality* source, capturing the long tail of human language, including slang, idioms, and varied writing styles.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the complete architecture of GPT-2 with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of masked self-attention and the feed-forward sub-layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the causal language modeling loss function and explain why BPE tokenization is preferred over word-level tokenization for large language models. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Architecture of GPT-2 (7 marks):**
- **Input Layer (1 mark):** The input text is tokenized into sub-word units using Byte-Pair Encoding, yielding a sequence of 1024 tokens. Each token is converted to a 768-dimensional vector (for GPT-2 Small) via a learned embedding table. A positional embedding of the same dimension is added to encode token order.
- **Stacked Decoder Blocks (3 marks):** GPT-2 contains N_x identical decoder blocks (12 for Small, 48 for XL). Each block has two sub-layers:
  - *Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention:* Computes scaled dot-product attention with a causal mask that prevents attending to future tokens. Multiple heads run in parallel, allowing the model to attend to different representation subspaces.
  - *Position-wise Feed-Forward Network:* A two-layer MLP with GELU activation, applied independently to each position, expanding the dimension to $4d$ and projecting back to $d$.
- **Residual Connections and LayerNorm (1 mark):** Each sub-layer uses a residual connection followed by layer normalization: $\text{output} = \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$, which stabilizes training of deep stacks.
- **Output Head (1 mark):** A final LayerNorm, a linear projection to the vocabulary size (50,257), and a softmax produce the next-token probability distribution.
- **Diagram (1 mark):** Draw the standard GPT-2 block diagram showing embeddings → N_x decoder blocks → output projection.

**(b) Causal LM Loss and BPE Tokenization (7 marks):**
- **Loss Derivation (4 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1, the joint probability factorizes as a product of conditional probabilities, the log-likelihood becomes a sum of log-conditional-probabilities, and the negative log-likelihood is the cross-entropy loss. Show all four steps.
- **Why Causal (1 mark):** Causal masking is essential during training to ensure the model never sees the token it is trying to predict, preserving the autoregressive property required at inference time.
- **BPE Advantages (2 marks):**
  - *No Out-of-Vocabulary Problem:* BPE can encode *any* string as a sequence of bytes, eliminating unseen tokens.
  - *Compact Vocabulary:* A 50,257-token BPE vocabulary is far smaller than a million-word English vocabulary, making the softmax computationally tractable.
  - *Handles Rare Words:* BPE breaks rare words into frequent sub-word units, allowing the model to learn representations even for infrequent terms.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast GPT-2 with BERT. Discuss the architectural and pre-training objective differences. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is zero-shot task transfer? Explain with two examples how GPT-2 performs translation and question-answering in a zero-shot setting. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) GPT-2 vs BERT (7 marks):**

| Criterion | GPT-2 | BERT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Architecture** (2 marks) | Decoder-only Transformer | Encoder-only Transformer |
| **Attention** (1 mark) | Unidirectional (causal mask) | Bidirectional |
| **Pre-training Objective** (2 marks) | Causal Language Modeling (predict next token) | Masked Language Modeling + Next Sentence Prediction |
| **Best Suited For** (1 mark) | Text generation, completion | Understanding, classification, embedding |
| **Zero-Shot Capability** (1 mark) | Strong — natural fit for prompting | Weak — needs fine-tuning |

- **Conclusion (mark integrated above):** GPT-2 generates text autoregressively, while BERT understands context bidirectionally. They are complementary, not competing.

**(b) Zero-Shot Task Transfer (7 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Zero-shot task transfer means the model performs a new task *without any task-specific training examples or weight updates* — only by formatting the task as a natural-language prompt.
- **Example 1 — Translation (2.5 marks):** Prompt: *"English: Hello, how are you? French:"*. GPT-2 has seen multilingual WebText pages and learned to map language patterns, so it continues the prompt with the French translation without ever being explicitly trained on translation pairs.
- **Example 2 — Question Answering (2.5 marks):** Prompt: *"Question: Who wrote the book 'Wings of Fire'? Answer:"*. The model completes the sentence using factual knowledge absorbed during pre-training, demonstrating question-answering without any QA-specific training data.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating GPT-2 with BERT:** Many students incorrectly state that GPT-2 is bidirectional. Always emphasize that GPT-2 is **causal (unidirectional)**.
> 2. **Wrong Tokenizer:** Do not say GPT-2 uses "word-level" tokenization. The correct answer is **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)** with a 50,257-token vocabulary.
> 3. **Ignoring Zero-Shot:** The single most important innovation of GPT-2 is zero-shot task transfer. Skipping this in a 14-mark answer will cost at least 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Confusing Variants:** GPT-2 Small is **124M**, not 117M (that is GPT-1's count). Memorize the four variants correctly.
> 5. **Forgetting the Mask:** When describing attention, students often omit the **causal mask** that makes GPT-2 autoregressive — this is a frequently tested point.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GPT-2 = Generative Pre-Trained Transformer 2**, released by OpenAI in February 2019.
- It is a **decoder-only Transformer** trained via **causal language modeling** (next-token prediction).
- Trained on the **WebText corpus** (~40 GB, 8 million Reddit-linked web pages).
- Uses **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)** with a vocabulary of **50,257** tokens.
- **Context window** is **1024 tokens**.
- Released in **four variants**: Small (124M), Medium (355M), Large (774M), **XL (1.5B)**.
- Core attention formula: $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ with a **causal mask**.
- Activation function inside the FFN is **GELU**, not ReLU.
- Uses **layer normalization** (not batch normalization) and **residual connections** around each sub-layer.
- Central innovation: **Zero-shot task transfer** — solve new tasks via natural-language prompts alone.
- Architectural difference from BERT: GPT-2 is **unidirectional**, BERT is **bidirectional**.
- Training objective difference: GPT-2 = **causal LM**, BERT = **masked LM**.
- GPT-2 is the **conceptual ancestor of GPT-3, GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and ChatGPT**.
- The paper *"Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners"* by Radford et al. is the seminal reference.
- GPT-2 does **not** use an encoder — it is **decoder-only**.
- Mask ensures the model cannot see future tokens during training, preserving the autoregressive property.
- Loss function is **cross-entropy** over the vocabulary at each time step.

> [!TIP]
> **Final One-Liner for Viva:** *"GPT-2 is a 1.5B-parameter decoder-only Transformer trained to predict the next word in 40GB of internet text — and that single objective, scaled up, gave it zero-shot translation, summarization, and question-answering abilities for free."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Large Language Models (LLM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Large Language Models (LLM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Large Language Model (LLM)** — A Large Language Model is a deep neural network, typically based on the **Transformer architecture**, trained on massive corpora of textual data (often hundreds of billions of tokens) using self-supervised learning objectives such as **next-token prediction** (causal/autoregressive) or **masked language modeling**. LLMs are characterized by their **parameter count (≥ 10⁹)**, **context window size**, and their ability to perform a wide spectrum of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** tasks via in-context learning, often without any task-specific gradient updates (a property called *zero-shot* or *few-shot generalization*).

In the strict terminology of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEADT748 – Generative AI, Module 3)**, an LLM is the *generative foundation model* of the language modality — the textual counterpart of image generators like DALL·E or Stable Diffusion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in the LLM Domain (Kotlin 2024 Scheme Highlights)**
> - **Parameters (N):** 10⁹ – 10¹² (e.g., GPT-3 has ~**175 B** parameters).
> - **Tokens:** Atomic sub-word units produced by tokenizers (BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece).
> - **Context Window (L):** Maximum input sequence length, e.g., 2K, 4K, 128K, 1M tokens.
> - **Embedding Dimension (d_model):** 768 – 12,288.
> - **Perplexity (PPL):** Standard intrinsic evaluation metric; lower is better.
> - **VRAM Footprint (Inference):** ~ **2 × N bytes** in FP16 precision.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Super-Powered Autocomplete"

Imagine a librarian who has read **every book, every Wikipedia page, every Reddit thread, and every legal document** in a country. This librarian is so well-read that when you start typing a sentence, they can predict — word by word — what would statistically *most likely* come next.

That is, in essence, what an LLM does.

| Real-World Analogy | LLM Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A child learning language by hearing parents | Pre-training on web-scale corpus |
| A doctor specializing in cardiology | Fine-tuning (SFT) on medical text |
| A student being corrected by a teacher | Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) |
| The librarian's "memory" of every book | The model's **weights (parameters)** |
| The librarian's attention span while reading | **Context window** |
| The librarian's fluency in a new slang | **Emergent ability** / in-context learning |

> [!TIP]
> **Think of an LLM as a "next-token predictor"** — *not* a knowledge database. It does not "know" facts the way a database does; it *learns the statistical structure* of language so deeply that factual recall emerges as a side effect of pattern completion.

---

## 1.3 Why LLMs Matter in Generative AI

LLMs are the **textual pillar** of modern Generative AI. They power:

- Conversational agents (ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, LLaMA).
- Code generation (GitHub Copilot, Code Llama).
- Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems.
- Agentic workflows where LLMs act as reasoning engines.
- Multimodal models (GPT-4V, Gemini 1.5) where text is fused with vision/audio.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of an LLM

An LLM, regardless of size, is built on three foundational pillars:

### 🔹 Pillar 1 — The Transformer Backbone
Introduced in the seminal paper **"Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et al., 2017)**, the Transformer replaced recurrence (RNNs/LSTMs) with a **self-attention mechanism**, enabling massive parallelization and long-range dependency modeling.

### 🔹 Pillar 2 — Self-Supervised Pre-training
Training objective differs by model family:
- **Autoregressive (Causal LM):** Predict next token — $\Pr(x_t \mid x_{<t})$. Examples: GPT, LLaMA, Mistral.
- **Masked LM:** Predict masked tokens — $\Pr(x_{\text{masked}} \mid x_{\text{context}})$. Example: BERT.
- **Encoder–Decoder (Seq2Seq):** Examples: T5, BART, FLAN-T5.

### 🔹 Pillar 3 — Scaling Laws
**Kaplan et al. (2020)** and **Hoffmann et al. (2022, Chinchilla)** empirically demonstrated that model performance scales as a **power-law** with compute, dataset size, and parameter count.

---

## 2.2 The Self-Attention Mechanism — The Heart of the LLM

For an input sequence of tokens, each token is projected into three vectors:
- **Query (Q)** — "What am I looking for?"
- **Key (K)** — "What do I contain?"
- **Value (V)** — "What information do I provide?"

The self-attention output is computed as:

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
$$

where $d_k$ is the dimensionality of the key vectors. The $\sqrt{d_k}$ term is the **scaling factor** that prevents the dot products from growing too large in high dimensions, which would push the softmax into saturated (near-zero gradient) regions.

### Multi-Head Attention (MHA)
Instead of performing one attention function, the model projects Q, K, V into $h$ different sub-spaces and runs attention in parallel:

$$
\text{MHA}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)\, W^{O}
$$

where each head is:
$$
\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^{Q},\, K W_i^{K},\, V W_i^{V})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why multiple heads?** Each head can attend to a *different semantic relationship* — one head may focus on syntactic dependencies (subject–verb), another on coreference (pronoun → noun), another on positional proximity. Concatenating them yields a richer representation.

---

## 2.3 Positional Encoding

Since self-attention is **permutation-invariant** (it treats the input as a *bag* of tokens), the model has no inherent sense of order. We inject positional information via **Positional Encodings (PE)**.

For the original Transformer, sinusoidal PE for position $pos$ and dimension $i$:

$$
PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right)
$$

$$
PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right)
$$

Modern LLMs (LLaMA, GPT-style) use **Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE)** which rotate Q and K vectors by position-dependent angles, enabling better length extrapolation.

---

## 2.4 Pre-training Objective — Cross-Entropy Loss

For an autoregressive LLM with vocabulary $V$, the training loss is the **negative log-likelihood** of the correct next token:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{LM}} = -\frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log \Pr_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t})
$$

with
$$
\Pr_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t}) = \text{softmax}(z_t)_{x_t}
$$

where $z_t \in \mathbb{R}^{|V|}$ is the logits vector at step $t$, and $|V|$ is the vocabulary size (typically 32K – 256K for modern LLMs).

---

## 2.5 RLHF — Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback

To align LLMs with human intent, **three stages** are applied:

1. **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT):** Fine-tune base model on human-written demonstrations.
2. **Reward Model (RM) Training:** Train a separate model to *score* outputs based on human preference rankings.
3. **PPO Optimization:** Use **Proximal Policy Optimization** to update the LLM to maximize the reward model's score while staying close to the SFT model (KL-divergence penalty).

$$
\max_\pi \; \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D},\, y \sim \pi(\cdot \mid x)}\!\left[ r_\phi(x, y) - \beta\, \mathrm{KL}\!\left(\pi(\cdot \mid x) \,\|\, \pi_{\text{SFT}}(\cdot \mid x)\right) \right]
$$

where $r_\phi$ is the reward model, $\pi$ is the policy (LLM), and $\beta$ controls deviation from SFT.

---

## 2.6 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$ | $d_k$ = key dim |
| 2 | Multi-Head Attention | $\text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\dots,\text{head}_h) W^{O}$ | $h$ = # heads |
| 3 | Sinusoidal PE (even) | $\sin\!\left(\dfrac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right)$ | position $pos$, dim $i$ |
| 4 | Sinusoidal PE (odd) | $\cos\!\left(\dfrac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right)$ | position $pos$, dim $i$ |
| 5 | LM Cross-Entropy Loss | $-\dfrac{1}{T}\sum_t \log \Pr_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ | lower = better |
| 6 | Perplexity | $\exp(\mathcal{L}_{\text{LM}})$ | intrinsic metric |
| 7 | RLHF Objective | $\mathbb{E}[r_\phi - \beta\,\mathrm{KL}(\pi \mid \pi_{\text{SFT}})]$ | $\beta$ = KL weight |
| 8 | Softmax | $\sigma(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | converts logits → probs |
| 9 | Layer Norm | $y = \gamma \odot \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}} + \beta$ | pre-norm in modern LLMs |
| 10 | FFN Block | $\text{FFN}(x) = W_2\,\sigma(W_1 x + b_1) + b_2$ | typically 4× d_model |
| 11 | KV-Cache Memory | $2 \cdot L \cdot h \cdot d_k \cdot N_{\text{batch}}$ bytes (FP16) | speeds up inference |
| 12 | Chinchilla Scaling | $N_{\text{opt}} \approx 20\, D$ | params ≈ 20× tokens |
| 13 | GELU Activation | $0.5\,x\!\left(1 + \tanh\!\left(\sqrt{2/\pi}(x + 0.044715 x^3)\right)\right)$ | used in GPT/BERT |
| 14 | Temperature Sampling | $\Pr(x) = \text{softmax}(z/T)$ | $T < 1$ sharper, $T > 1$ flatter |
| 15 | Top-p (Nucleus) Sampling | sample from smallest $V'$ s.t. $\sum \Pr \geq p$ | $p \in [0.9, 0.95]$ typical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas underpin every modern LLM stack — from Hugging Face Transformers and PyTorch implementations to vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, and production-scale serving infrastructure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Scaled Dot-Product Attention Output

We derive the attention output for a single sequence position step-by-step. Suppose we have a single query vector $q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_k}$ attending to a sequence of key vectors $\{k_1, k_2, k_3\}$, with $d_k = 4$ for simplicity.

**Step 1 — Compute raw similarity scores** (dot product of query with each key):

$$
s_1 = q^{\top} k_1, \quad s_2 = q^{\top} k_2, \quad s_3 = q^{\top} k_3
$$

**Step 2 — Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to stabilize gradients:**

$$
\tilde{s}_i = \frac{s_i}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

**Why scaling?** Under the assumption that $q$ and $k$ have zero mean and unit variance, $\text{Var}(q^{\top} k) = d_k$. Without scaling, large $d_k$ pushes softmax into saturation, killing gradients.

**Step 3 — Apply softmax to get attention weights $\alpha_i$:**

$$
\alpha_i = \frac{\exp(\tilde{s}_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{3} \exp(\tilde{s}_j)} \quad \text{with} \quad \sum_i \alpha_i = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Weighted sum of value vectors $v_i$:**
