Performance] M2 --> Safe M3 --> Safe M4 --> Safe M5 --> Safe ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]** *Define fine-tuning in the context of generative AI. Mention any two advantages of fine-tuning a pre-trained model over training from scratch.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Fine-tuning is the process of continuing the training of a large, pre-trained generative model on a smaller, task-specific dataset so that the model specialises for a particular domain, style, or instruction format while retaining the general knowledge learned during pre-training. **Two advantages over training from scratch:** 1. **Drastically reduced compute cost** — Fine-tuning requires orders of magnitude less FLOPs than pre-training, because the model has already learned low-level features (syntax, visual edges, etc.). 2. **Less labelled data required** — Because the model starts from a strong prior, only a few hundred to a few thousand examples are typically sufficient, compared to billions for pre-training. 3. *(Bonus 3rd point, if asked)* — Faster convergence and better final performance, thanks to the favourable initialisation provided by $\theta_{pre}$. *(Allocation: 1 mark definition + 2 × 1 mark = 3 marks)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]** *What is LoRA? State the LoRA weight update equation and explain the role of the rank parameter $r$.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** LoRA (**L**ow-**R**ank **A**daptation) is a Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) method that freezes the original pre-trained weight matrix $W_0$ and learns the *change* in weights as a low-rank decomposition. **Weight update equation:** $$\Delta W = \frac{\alpha}{r} \, B A, \quad B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r},\; A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$$ **Role of rank $r$:** - $r$ is the *intrinsic dimension* of the fine-tuning update. - It is chosen very small ($r = 4, 8, 16, 32, 64$) compared to $d$ and $k$. - **Smaller $r$** ⇒ fewer trainable parameters, more memory-efficient, but may under-fit complex tasks. - **Larger $r$** ⇒ more expressive, closer to full FT, but uses more memory. *(Allocation: 1 mark LoRA definition + 1 mark equation + 1 mark role of $r$ = 3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions in the 2024 scheme always have an **internal choice** (either-or) between two questions, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of LoRA. Derive the parameter compression ratio achieved by LoRA over full fine-tuning. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss in detail the different categories of Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods, and compare LoRA, QLoRA, and Adapter-based fine-tuning in terms of memory, speed, and quality. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution to Question A:** **(a) — LoRA Architecture and Compression Ratio (7 marks)** **Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the LoRA Architecture diagram in Section 4.2. The input tensor $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ passes through two parallel branches: - **Frozen branch:** $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$, the original pre-trained weight, which is *not* updated. - **Trainable branch:** A low-rank product $B \cdot A$, where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$ and $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$, with $r \ll \min(d,k)$. The outputs of both branches are summed, with the trainable branch scaled by $\alpha/r$. **Derivation (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Forward pass: } h &= W_0 x + \frac{\alpha}{r}\,B(Ax) \\ \text{Trainable parameters} &= d \cdot r + r \cdot k = r(d+k) \\ \text{Full FT parameters} &= d \cdot k \\ \text{Compression ratio } \rho &= \frac{r(d+k)}{d \cdot k} = \frac{r}{d} + \frac{r}{k} \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical Example (1 mark):** For a typical transformer layer with $d = k = 4096$ and $r = 8$: $$\rho = \frac{8}{4096} + \frac{8}{4096} = 0.00195 + 0.00195 = 0.0039 \approx 0.4\%$$ So only **0.4%** of the original parameters are trained, while 99.6% remain frozen. **Advantages (1 mark):** - Massive memory savings during training. - No additional inference latency (LoRA weights can be merged into $W_0$ post-training). - Multiple LoRA adapters can be swapped for different tasks on the same base model. --- **(b) — Categories of PEFT and Comparison (7 marks)** **Categories of PEFT (3 marks):** 1. **Additive methods** — Insert new trainable modules (Adapter layers, Prompt Tokens, Prefix Tokens) into a frozen model. 2. **Selective methods** — Fine-tune only a subset of existing parameters (e.g., BitFit: only bias terms). 3. **Reparametrised methods** — Express weight updates as a low-rank decomposition (LoRA, QLoRA, IA³). 4. **Hybrid methods** — Combine the above (e.g., LoRA + adapters). **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Aspect | LoRA | QLoRA | Adapters (Houlsby) | |---|---|---|---| | **Base model quantisation** | 16-bit / FP16 | 4-bit (NormalFloat) | 16-bit / FP16 | | **Trainable param fraction** | 0.1–5% | 0.1–5% | ~3–5% | | **GPU memory** | Moderate | Very Low (fits 65B on 48 GB) | Moderate | | **Training speed** | Fast | Slower (dequantisation) | Moderate | | **Inference latency** | None (merging) | None (merging) | Small (sequential) | | **Quality vs Full FT** | ~95–99% | ~93–97% | ~94–98% | | **Multi-task serving** | Easy (swap LoRAs) | Easy (swap LoRAs) | Hard (sequential) | **Conclusion (1 mark):** QLoRA is the *most memory-efficient* and democratises fine-tuning of very large models on consumer hardware. LoRA is the *most balanced* choice for production. Adapters are best when *modular, multi-task* serving is required and inference latency is not critical. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the concept of catastrophic forgetting in fine-tuning. With the help of the Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) loss function, derive how EWC mitigates this problem. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Differentiate between Instruction Tuning, RLHF, and DPO as alignment techniques for generative models. Why has DPO become a popular alternative to RLHF in 2024? *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution to Question B:** **(a) — Catastrophic Forgetting and EWC (7 marks)** **Definition of Catastrophic Forgetting (2 marks):** Catastrophic forgetting is the phenomenon where a neural network, after being fine-tuned on a new task (Task B), significantly loses its previously learned ability on the original task (Task A). In generative models, this manifests as: a model fine-tuned on medical text forgets how to write poetry; a Stable Diffusion model fine-tuned on a single subject loses its general image generation ability. **Mathematical Setup — Bayesian View (2 marks):** The optimal parameters for both tasks A and B maximise the joint posterior: $$\log p(\theta \mid D_A, D_B) = \log p(D_B \mid \theta) + \log p(\theta \mid D_A) - \log p(D_B \mid D_A)$$ The term $\log p(\theta \mid D_A)$ is the "memory" of task A. Applying a Laplace approximation around $\theta_A^{*}$ gives: $$\log p(\theta \mid D_A) \approx \text{const} - \frac{1}{2}\sum_j F_j (\theta_j - \theta_{A,j}^{*})^2$$ **EWC Loss Derivation (2 marks):** Combining the new-task loss with the quadratic penalty: $$\mathcal{L}_{EWC}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}_{task_B}(\theta) + \frac{\lambda}{2}\sum_j F_j (\theta_j - \theta_{A,j}^{*})^2$$ where $F_j$ is the diagonal of the Fisher Information Matrix (measuring how important weight $j$ is for task A), and $\lambda$ is a hyperparameter. **Interpretation (1 mark):** - Weights with **high** $F_j$ (critical for A) are *heavily* penalised for changing → preserved. - Weights with **low** $F_j$ (unimportant for A) can move freely → learn B. - This is essentially a *soft, learned* form of freezing. --- **(b) — Instruction Tuning, RLHF, and DPO (7 marks)** **1. Instruction Tuning (1.5 marks):** - **What:** Supervised fine-tuning on a dataset of (instruction, ideal response) pairs. - **How:** Standard cross-entropy loss, $\mathcal{L} = -\sum \log P_\theta(\text{response} \mid \text{instruction})$. - **Strength:** Cheap, simple, single-stage. - **Limitation:** Cannot capture *preference* nuances (e.g., "response A is better than B because it's more helpful"). **2. RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) (2.5 marks):** - **What:** Three-stage pipeline — (i) SFT, (ii) train a *reward model* $r_\phi(x,y)$ from human preference rankings, (iii) fine-tune the LM via PPO to maximise reward while staying close to the SFT model. - **Objective:** $$\max_\pi \; \mathbb{E}_{x,y \sim \pi}\big[r_\phi(x,y)\big] - \beta \, D_{KL}\big(\pi \,\Vert\, \pi_{SFT}\big)$$ - **Strength:** State-of-the-art alignment quality (used in ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini). - **Limitation:** Instability of PPO, need to train a separate reward model, hyperparameter-sensitive, expensive. **3. DPO (Direct Preference Optimisation) (2 marks):** - **What:** Closed-form solution that *bypasses* the reward model and PPO entirely. Directly optimises a binary-classification loss on preference pairs $(y_w, y_l)$ (winner vs loser). - **Loss (conceptual):** $$\mathcal{L}_{DPO} = -\log \sigma\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_w \mid x)}{\pi_{ref}(y_w \mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_l \mid x)}{\pi_{ref}(y_l \mid x)}\right)$$ - **Strength:** Stable, no reward model, single stage, comparable quality to RLHF. - **Why DPO became popular in 2024 (1 mark):** 1. **No reward model** → saves ~30% of the alignment compute. 2. **No PPO** → no actor-critic instability. 3. **Closed-form derivation** → mathematically elegant and reproducible. 4. Used in **Llama 3, Mistral, and many open-source 2024 releases**. **Comparative Summary (0 marks extra, just for reference):** | Aspect | Instruction Tuning | RLHF | DPO | |---|---|---|---| | Stages | 1 | 3 | 1 | | Reward model | No | Yes | No | | PPO required | No | Yes | No | | Stability | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | | Quality (helpfulness) | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | | Cost | Low | High | Medium | --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing PEFT with FFT** — Many students write "PEFT updates all weights" — **wrong**. PEFT explicitly *freezes* most of the model. (–1 to –2 marks) > 2. **Omitting the rank $r$ in LoRA equations** — Always specify $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$ and $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$. Writing $B \cdot A$ without dimensions loses at least 1 mark. > 3. **Treating RLHF and DPO as identical** — They are fundamentally different: RLHF needs a reward model and PPO; DPO directly optimises preferences. Examiners will deduct 2+ marks for conflating them. > 4. **Forgetting the catastrophic forgetting caveat** — Any fine-tuning answer that does *not* mention catastrophic forgetting and at least one mitigation (LoRA, EWC, KD, rehearsal) is considered incomplete. > 5. **No numerical example in compression-ratio questions** — Always plug in numbers (e.g., $d=4096$, $r=8$, $\rho \approx 0.4\%$) for full marks. > 6. **Spelling "LoRA" as "Lora" or "LORA" in some texts** — Use the canonical form **LoRA** (capital L, lower-case o-r-a), referring to the 2021 Hu et al. paper. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This on Your Wall Before ESE.** - [x] **Fine-tuning = continued training of a pre-trained model on task-specific data**, starting from $\theta_{pre}$. - [x] **Two main flavours:** Full Fine-Tuning (FFT, 100% params) and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT, < 5% params). - [x] **PEFT categories:** Additive (Adapters, Prompt Tuning), Selective (BitFit), Reparametrised (LoRA, QLoRA, IA³). - [x] **LoRA equation:** $\Delta W = (\alpha/r) B A$, with $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$, $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$, $r \ll \min(d,k)$. - [x] **LoRA compression ratio:** $\rho = r(d+k)/(d \cdot k) \approx 2r/\max(d,k)$; typically 0.1 – 5%. - [x] **QLoRA = LoRA on a 4-bit quantised base model** — fits 65B models on 48 GB GPUs. - [x] **Catastrophic forgetting** = loss of pre-trained knowledge during FT; mitigated by LoRA (freeze $W_0$), EWC (Fisher penalty), KD (KL term), or rehearsal. - [x] **EWC loss:** $\mathcal{L}_{EWC} = \mathcal{L}_{B} + (\lambda/2)\sum_j F_j (\theta_j - \theta_{A,j}^{*})^2$. - [x] **Instruction tuning** = SFT on (instruction, response) pairs; the *first* alignment stage. - [x] **RLHF** = SFT + Reward Model + PPO; three stages, used in ChatGPT. - [x] **DPO** = direct preference optimisation; no reward model, no PPO; the 2024 alternative to RLHF. - [x] **Customisability hierarchy (low → high effort):** Prompt engineering → RAG → Prompt tuning → LoRA/QLoRA → Full fine-tuning → Training from scratch. - [x] **Small learning rate ($\eta \approx 1\mathrm{e}^{-5}$ for FFT)** is non-negotiable to prevent catastrophic forgetting. - [x] **AdamW** is the default optimiser for fine-tuning transformers; it decouples weight decay from the gradient. - [x] **Quality > Quantity** in fine-tuning data: 1,000 curated samples > 100,000 noisy ones. - [x] **Evaluation must include both** task-specific metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, FID) *and* general-capability metrics (MMLU, HumanEval) to detect regression. - [x] **Production reality (2024–25):** > 85% of enterprise GenAI deployments use LoRA/QLoRA, not full FT. ---

topics.title: Parameter Efficient Fine Tuning Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Parameter Efficient Fine Tuning Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)** is a class of transfer learning techniques in deep learning and Large Language Model (LLM) adaptation that seeks to adapt a massive pre-trained model to a downstream task by updating only a **small subset of parameters** (typically **0.1% – 5%** of total model weights), while keeping the vast majority of the original pre-trained parameters **frozen**. 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** PEFT addresses the computational, memory, and storage bottlenecks of **Full Fine-Tuning (FFT)** of billion-parameter models such as GPT, LLaMA, and PaLM, by introducing *trainable auxiliary structures* or *low-rank decompositions* that capture task-specific knowledge without modifying the base model weights.

### Formal Mathematical Statement

Given a pre-trained transformer with weight matrix $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$, traditional fine-tuning updates:

$$W = W_0 + \Delta W$$

PEFT instead constrains the update $\Delta W$ such that:

$$\vert \theta_{\text{trainable}} \vert \ll \vert W_0 \vert$$

where $\theta_{\text{trainable}}$ represents the *small* set of newly introduced or selectively updated parameters.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Specialist Surgeon vs. The General Practitioner**

Imagine a **highly experienced general surgeon** (the pre-trained LLM with billions of parameters learned from internet-scale data) who can perform almost any operation. Now, a hospital needs this surgeon to specialize in **cardiac micro-surgery**.

- **Full Fine-Tuning (FFT)** = Sending the surgeon back to medical school for 10 more years, re-training *every* surgical skill. Costly, slow, and risks *catastrophic forgetting* (losing the general skills).
- **PEFT** = Attaching a **specialized micro-lens** and giving the surgeon a **custom surgical glove** trained specifically for cardiac work. The surgeon's brain (frozen base model) remains untouched, but the *attachments* (small trainable parameters) enable expert-level cardiac surgery.

This is exactly what PEFT does — it adds tiny, task-specific "lenses" or "gloves" to the frozen base model.

---

## 1.3 Why PEFT? The Core Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A 7-billion parameter model in full fine-tuning requires:
> - **GPU Memory:** ~112 GB just for weights (FP16) + ~448 GB for optimizer states (Adam: 2x weights in FP32) + ~112 GB for gradients
> - **Total ≈ 672 GB VRAM** (4× A100 80GB GPUs)
> 
> With **LoRA** (r=8), the same model can be fine-tuned in **< 24 GB VRAM** on a *single* consumer GPU.

### Key Drivers
1. **Storage Efficiency** — One base model + thousands of tiny PEFT checkpoints (each < 50 MB) vs. full model copies (each ~14 GB for a 7B model).
2. **Catastrophic Forgetting Mitigation** — Base knowledge is preserved because $W_0$ is never overwritten.
3. **Multi-Task Serving** — Switch between tasks by simply swapping a small adapter.
4. **Democratization** — Enables researchers with limited compute to fine-tune frontier models.

---

## 1.4 Taxonomy of PEFT Methods (High-Level Overview)

| Family | Core Idea | Trainable Params | Memory |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Additive** | Insert new trainable modules | $0.1\% - 5\%$ | Low |
| **Selective** | Update only specific existing params | $0.01\% - 1\%$ | Very Low |
| **Reparameterization-Based** | Reparameterize $\Delta W$ via low-rank | $0.1\% - 1\%$ | Low |
| **Prompt-Based** | Learn soft prompts in input space | $0.001\% - 0.1\%$ | Ultra Low |

> [!NOTE]
> Each family is explored in detail in **Section 2**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Family 1: Additive PEFT Methods (Adapter-Based)

### 2.1.1 Adapter Layers (Houlsby et al., 2019)

Adapter modules are **small bottleneck feed-forward networks** inserted *inside* each transformer layer (after the attention and FFN sub-blocks).

**Architecture of an Adapter Block:**

$$\text{Adapter}(x) = x + W_{\text{up}} \cdot \sigma(W_{\text{down}} \cdot x)$$

where:
- $W_{\text{down}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$ — projects down to bottleneck dimension $r$
- $W_{\text{up}} \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d}$ — projects back to original dimension $d$
- $\sigma$ — non-linearity (typically GELU or ReLU)
- $r \ll d$ (bottleneck)

**Why it works:** The residual connection $x + \cdot$ ensures identity initialization (i.e., the adapter starts as a no-op, preserving pre-trained behavior).

**Parameter count per adapter:**

$$P_{\text{adapter}} = 2dr + d + r \approx 2dr \quad \text{(when } d \gg r\text{)}$$

For $d = 768$, $r = 8$: $P \approx 12{,}288$ parameters per layer — tiny compared to the millions in the base transformer.

---

### 2.1.2 Houlsby vs. Pfeiffer (Parallel vs. Sequential)

| Variant | Placement | Performance |
|---|---|---|
| **Houlsby Adapter** | After Attention **AND** after FFN | Slightly better |
| **Pfeiffer Adapter** | Only after FFN (Bottleneck) | More parameter-efficient |
| **Parallel Adapter** | In parallel with attention/FFN | Faster inference |

---

## 2.2 Family 2: Reparameterization-Based (LoRA Family)

### 2.2.1 LoRA — Low-Rank Adaptation (Hu et al., 2021)

The **most influential** PEFT method. Core hypothesis: *the change in weights during fine-tuning has a low intrinsic rank*.

**Core Equation:**

$$W' = W_0 + \Delta W = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} \cdot B \cdot A$$

where:
- $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$ — **frozen** pre-trained weight matrix
- $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$ — trainable, initialized via Kaiming/He initialization
- $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$ — trainable, initialized to **zero** matrix
- $r$ — rank of the low-rank decomposition (hyperparameter, typically 4, 8, 16, 32, 64)
- $\alpha$ — scaling hyperparameter (often $\alpha = 2r$ or $\alpha = r$)

**Parameter Count:**

$$P_{\text{LoRA}} = r \cdot (d + k) \quad \text{vs.} \quad P_{\text{FFT}} = d \cdot k$$

**Compression ratio:**

$$\rho = \frac{P_{\text{LoRA}}}{P_{\text{FFT}}} = \frac{r(d+k)}{d \cdot k} \approx \frac{r}{\min(d,k)}$$

For a $4096 \times 4096$ matrix with $r = 8$:

$$\rho = \frac{8 \times 8192}{16{,}777{,}216} \approx 0.0004 = 0.04\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Initialization trick:** $B = 0$ ensures that at the start of training, $\Delta W = B \cdot A = 0$, so the model output is *identical* to the pre-trained model. This is mathematically equivalent to the adapter's identity initialization.

---

### 2.2.2 LoRA Variants (KTU High-Yield)

| Variant | Innovation | Key Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **QLoRA** | Quantize $W_0$ to 4-bit (NF4) | $W_0 \rightarrow \text{NF4}(W_0)$ |
| **AdaLoRA** | Adaptive rank allocation per layer | $r_i$ varies per layer |
| **DoRA** | Decompose magnitude & direction | $W' = m \cdot \frac{V + \Delta V}{\Vert V + \Delta V \Vert}$ |
| **LoRA+** | Different learning rates for A & B | $\eta_A \neq \eta_B$ |
| **VeRA** | Shared frozen matrices, learned scaling | $W' = W_0 + \Lambda_b B \Lambda_a A$ |
| **GaLore** | Low-rank *gradient* projection | Project $\nabla W$ to low-rank space |

---

## 2.3 Family 3: Prompt-Based PEFT Methods

### 2.3.1 Prefix Tuning (Li & Liang, 2021)

Prepends **trainable continuous key-value pairs** to the attention mechanism of every transformer layer.

**Modified Attention:**

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K_{\text{concat}}, V_{\text{concat}})$$

where:

$$K_{\text{concat}} = [K_{\text{prefix}}; \, K_{\text{input}}], \quad V_{\text{concat}} = [V_{\text{prefix}}; \, V_{\text{input}}]$$

- Prefix length $L_p$ is a hyperparameter (typically 10–200 tokens)
- Only $P_{\text{prefix}} = 2 \cdot L_p \cdot d \cdot N_{\text{layers}}$ parameters are trained

> [!NOTE]
> **Reparameterization Trick:** To stabilize training, the prefix is parameterized as $P_{\text{prefix}} = \text{MLP}(P_{\text{learned}})$, where MLP is discarded at inference. This is the *reparameterization encoder*.

---

### 2.3.2 Prompt Tuning (Lester et al., 2021)

The **simplest** PEFT method. Prepends trainable soft tokens to the **input embedding only** (not every layer).

$$h_{\text{input}} = [P_{\text{soft}}; \, \text{Embed}(x_{\text{real}})]$$

where $P_{\text{soft}} \in \mathbb{R}^{L_p \times d}$ is the *only* trainable parameter.

- **No reparameterization** needed for large models (T5-XXL works well)
- Trained prompt is model-specific (not human-readable)

---

### 2.3.3 P-Tuning v2 (Deep Prompt Tuning)

Applies prefix-tuning-style prompts to **every layer**, but **without** the reparameterization MLP. Essentially, prompt tuning applied at every layer depth.

$$h^{(l)} = [P^{(l)}; \, h^{(l-1)}] \quad \text{for each layer } l$$

---

## 2.4 Family 4: Selective PEFT Methods

### 2.4.1 BitFit (Zaken et al., 2021)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extreme Parameter Efficiency:** Only updates the **bias terms** of the network. All weights and LayerNorm parameters are frozen.

$$P_{\text{BitFit}} = \sum_{l=1}^{L} \vert b^{(l)} \vert$$

For a BERT-base model, this is approximately **~0.08% of total parameters** — yet achieves competitive performance on GLUE benchmarks.

### 2.4.2 Diff Pruning

Learns a *sparse* update $\Delta W$ by adding a penalty that drives most entries to zero:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{diff}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}} + \lambda \Vert \Delta W \Vert_1$$

---

## 2.5 Family 5: Hybrid & Unified Methods

### 2.5.1 IA³ (T-Few, 2022)

Multiplies activations by **learned scaling vectors** $\ell \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$:

$$x' = \ell \odot x$$

- $P_{\text{IA}^3} = 3 \cdot d$ per layer (Q, K, V, FFN)
- Even more parameter-efficient than LoRA

### 2.5.2 MAM Adapter

Combines parallel adapter + soft prompt in a unified design. Used in the **UniPELT** framework, which learns a *gating function* to dynamically select which PEFT methods to combine.

### 2.5.3 UniPELT Gating

For each transformer layer $l$, gate $g_l \in [0,1]$ controls which adapter branch is active:

$$h^{(l)} = g_l^{(A)} \cdot \text{Adapter}(h^{(l-1)}) + g_l^{(L)} \cdot \text{LoRA}(h^{(l-1)}) + g_l^{(P)} \cdot \text{Prompt}(h^{(l-1)})$$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Adapter Params** | $P = 2dr + d + r$ | $r = 8, d = 768 \Rightarrow P \approx 12\text{k}$ |
| 2 | **LoRA Update** | $W' = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} BA$ | $r = 8, \alpha = 16$ |
| 3 | **LoRA Compression** | $\rho = \frac{r}{\min(d,k)}$ | $0.01\% - 1\%$ |
| 4 | **Prefix-Tuning Params** | $P = 2 L_p d N_L$ | $L_p = 100, d = 512, N_L = 12$ |
| 5 | **Prompt-Tuning Params** | $P = L_p \cdot d$ | $L_p = 20, d = 4096 \Rightarrow P = 81\text{k}$ |
| 6 | **BitFit Params** | $P = \sum_l \vert b^{(l)} \vert$ | $\approx 0.08\%$ of total |
| 7 | **IA³ Params** | $P = 3d$ per layer | $d = 4096 \Rightarrow 12\text{k per layer}$ |
| 8 | **Compression Ratio (LoRA)** | $\text{CR} = \frac{dk}{r(d+k)}$ | $\approx 250\times$ for $r=8$ |
| 9 | **QLoRA Memory Savings** | $\text{Mem} \approx \frac{W_0}{4} + W_{\text{LoRA}} + \text{Opt}$ | $\sim 4\times$ reduction |
| 10 | **UniPELT Gating** | $h = g_A \cdot A + g_L \cdot L + g_P \cdot P$ | $\sum g = 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** In production LLM serving (e.g., multi-tenant chatbots), one base model + N LoRA adapters enables serving 100+ tasks with marginal additional memory, compared to deploying 100 full model copies — a 100× storage reduction.

---

## 2.7 Comparative Trade-off Analysis

```
                Parameter Efficiency
                        ▲
                        │
        BitFit ●        │        ● IA³
                        │
        Prompt-Tune ●   │   ● Prefix-Tuning
                        │
        Adapter ●       │       ● LoRA
                        │
        QLoRA ●         │           ● DoRA
                        │
                        └─────────────────────────►
                              Task Performance
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Trade-off:** Methods in the *upper-left* (BitFit, IA³) use the fewest parameters but may underperform on complex reasoning tasks. Methods in the *lower-right* (LoRA, DoRA) use more parameters but achieve near-full-fine-tuning accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: LoRA Forward Pass Mathematics

### 3.1.1 Setup

We derive the forward pass through a LoRA-augmented linear layer with **frozen base weight** $W_0$ and **trainable low-rank update** $BA$.

**Given:**
- Input: $x \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times 1}$ (column vector of dimension $k$)
- Frozen base weight: $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$
- Trainable matrix A: $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$
- Trainable matrix B: $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$
- Scaling factor: $s = \dfrac{\alpha}{r}$

### 3.1.2 Derivation Steps

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{\text{out}} &= W' \cdot x \\[4pt]
&= \left(W_0 + s \cdot B \cdot A\right) \cdot x \\[4pt]
&= W_0 \cdot x + s \cdot B \cdot A \cdot x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Expansion:** Apply distributivity of matrix multiplication over addition.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } z &= A \cdot x \quad \text{where } z \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times 1} \\[4pt]
\text{Let } y &= B \cdot z \quad \text{where } y \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times 1} \\[4pt]
\text{Let } u &= W_0 \cdot x \quad \text{where } u \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Decomposition:** The forward pass is split into two parallel paths.

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{\text{out}} &= W_0 \cdot x + s \cdot B \cdot A \cdot x \\[4pt]
&= \underbrace{W_0 \cdot x}_{\text{frozen path}} + s \cdot \underbrace{B \cdot (A \cdot x)}_{\text{LoRA path}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Initialization verification:** At training step $t = 0$, $B = 0$ (zero-initialized).

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_0 &= 0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r} \\[4pt]
\Delta W_0 &= s \cdot B_0 \cdot A_0 = s \cdot 0 \cdot A_0 = 0 \\[4pt]
\therefore h_{\text{out}}^{(0)} &= W_0 \cdot x + 0 = W_0 \cdot x
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the **identity initialization property** — the model output at step 0 is identical to the pre-trained model.

### 3.1.3 Backward Pass (Gradient Flow)

For loss $\mathcal{L}$, we compute gradients w.r.t. $A$ and $B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial A} &= s \cdot B^{\top} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_{\text{out}}} \cdot x^{\top} \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial B} &= \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_{\text{out}}} \cdot (s \cdot A \cdot x)^{\top} \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_0} &= \text{NOT COMPUTED (frozen)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Implication:** Because $W_0$ is frozen, we do **not** store gradients for it. This is the primary memory savings mechanism — gradient memory drops from $\mathcal{O}(dk)$ to $\mathcal{O}(r(d+k))$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Adapter Layer Forward Pass

### 3.2.1 Architecture Recap

The adapter for input $h \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_{\text{down}} &= W_{\text{down}} \cdot h \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{r} \\[4pt]
z_{\text{mid}} &= \sigma(z_{\text{down}}) \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{r} \\[4pt]
z_{\text{up}} &= W_{\text{up}} \cdot z_{\text{mid}} \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{d} \\[4pt]
h_{\text{out}} &= h + z_{\text{up}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Parameter Count Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{adapter}} &= \underbrace{d \cdot r}_{W_{\text{down}}} + \underbrace{r \cdot d}_{W_{\text{up}}} + \underbrace{r}_{b_{\text{down}}} + \underbrace{d}_{b_{\text{up}}} \\[4pt]
&= 2dr + d + r
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example (BERT-base, $d=768$, $r=8$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 2(768)(8) + 768 + 8 \\[4pt]
&= 12{,}288 + 768 + 8 \\[4pt]
&= 13{,}064 \text{ parameters per adapter}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For 12 BERT layers with Houlsby placement (2 adapters/layer):**

$$
P_{\text{total}} = 12 \times 2 \times 13{,}064 = 313{,}536 \approx 0.3 \text{ M params}
$$

Base BERT-base: $110$ M params.

$$
\text{Parameter Ratio} = \frac{0.3\text{M}}{110\text{M}} \approx 0.27\%
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Prefix-Tuning Effective Parameter Count

### 3.3.1 Setup

For each transformer layer $\ell \in \{1, 2, \ldots, N_L\}$ and each attention head $h$, we prepend a learnable prefix of length $L_p$.

**Per-layer trainable parameters:**

$$
P_{\text{prefix}}^{(\ell)} = 2 \cdot L_p \cdot d_{\text{model}}
$$

The factor of **2** accounts for both the **Key** and **Value** matrices.

### 3.3.2 Total Across Network

$$
P_{\text{total}} = N_L \cdot 2 \cdot L_p \cdot d_{\text{model}}
$$

**Numerical Example (BART-base: $N_L=12$, $d_{\text{model}}=768$, $L_p=100$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= 12 \cdot 2 \cdot 100 \cdot 768 \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}843{,}200 \text{ parameters} \\[4pt]
&\approx 1.84 \text{ M params}
\end{aligned}
$$

**BART-base has 140 M parameters**, so:

$$
\text{Parameter Ratio} = \frac{1.84\text{M}}{140\text{M}} \approx 1.3\%
$$

### 3.3.3 With Reparameterization MLP

If a 2-layer MLP with hidden dim $d_h = 800$ reparameterizes the prefix:

$$
P_{\text{reparam}} = N_L \cdot 2 \cdot L_p \cdot d_{\text{model}} + L_p \cdot d_h + d_h \cdot (2 \cdot d_{\text{model}} \cdot N_L)
$$

But only $P_{\text{reparam}}$ is trained; the *effective* (inference-time) prefix is just the original $P_{\text{total}}$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Method-by-Method Comparison

| Method | Trainable Params | Memory Savings | Inference Latency Overhead | Task Performance vs FFT | Multi-Task Switching | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full FT** | 100% | 0% | None | 100% (baseline) | ❌ Heavy | Unlimited compute |
| **Adapter (Houlsby)** | 0.5% - 5% | ~95% | +5-10% (extra FFN) | 95-99% | ✅ Easy | Vision & NLP |
| **LoRA** | 0.1% - 1% | ~75% (no extra FFN) | **Zero** (mergeable) | 97-100% | ✅ Easy | LLM adaptation |
| **QLoRA** | 0.1% - 1% | ~93% (4-bit base) | Zero | 95-99% | ✅ Easy | Consumer GPUs |
| **Prefix-Tuning** | 0.1% - 1% | ~95% | **Zero** (no extra params) | 90-95% | ✅ Easy | Generation tasks |
| **Prompt-Tuning** | 0.001% - 0.1% | ~99% | Zero | 85-95% | ✅ Very easy | Large models (>10B) |
| **BitFit** | 0.05% - 0.1% | ~99% | Zero | 90-95% | ✅ Easy | Quick baselines |
| **IA³** | 0.01% - 0.05% | ~99% | Zero | 92-96% | ✅ Easy | T-Few, very efficient |
| **DoRA** | 0.1% - 1% | ~75% | Zero | **100%+** vs FFT | ✅ Easy | Top accuracy |
| **UniPELT** | 1% - 3% | ~90% | +5% | 98-100% | ✅ Easy | Hybrid scenarios |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Engineering Insight:** LoRA's key advantage over Adapter methods is **zero inference overhead** because $BA$ can be *merged* with $W_0$ at deployment: $W_{\text{merged}} = W_0 + s \cdot BA$. The model architecture remains unchanged.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: LoRA Merging at Inference

### 3.5.1 Step-by-Step Merge

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{deployed}} &= W_0 + s \cdot B \cdot A \\[4pt]
\text{Since } A, B \text{ are constant at inference, merge them:} \\[4pt]
W_{\text{deployed}} &= W_0 + s \cdot (B \cdot A) \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k} \\[4pt]
\text{Matrix multiply is associative, so the merged weight is a single dense matrix.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5.2 Numerical Verification

For $d = k = 4096$, $r = 8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LoRA params: } & 8 \times (4096 + 4096) = 65{,}536 \\[4pt]
\text{Merged weight: } & 4096 \times 4096 = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ (same as base)} \\[4pt]
\text{Storage: } & 16.7\text{ M floats} \times 2 \text{ bytes (FP16)} = 32 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:**
- **LoRA adapter storage:** $65{,}536 \times 2 = 128$ KB
- **Storage savings:** $32 \text{ MB} / 128 \text{ KB} = 256\times$ per task
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy: PEFT Methods Classification

```mermaid
graph TB
    ROOT["PEFT METHODS"]:::root
    ADD["Additive Methods"]:::cat
    SEL["Selective Methods"]:::cat
    REPR["Reparameterization-Based"]:::cat
    PROMPT["Prompt-Based Methods"]:::cat
    HYB["Hybrid / Unified"]:::cat

    ROOT --> ADD
    ROOT --> SEL
    ROOT --> REPR
    ROOT --> PROMPT
    ROOT --> HYB

    ADD --> A1["Adapter Layers Houlsby 2019"]:::node
    ADD --> A2["Pfeiffer Adapter"]:::node
    ADD --> A3["Parallel Adapter"]:::node
    ADD --> A4["Compactor"]:::node

    SEL --> S1["BitFit Bias-Only"]:::node
    SEL --> S2["Diff Pruning"]:::node
    SEL --> S3["FishMask"]:::node

    REPR --> R1["LoRA Low-Rank Adaptation"]:::node
    REPR --> R2["QLoRA 4-bit Quantized"]:::node
    REPR --> R3["AdaLoRA Adaptive Rank"]:::node
    REPR --> R4["DoRA Weight-Decomposed"]:::node
    REPR --> R5["VeRA Shared Matrices"]:::node
    REPR --> R6["GaLore Gradient Low-Rank"]:::node

    PROMPT --> P1["Prefix Tuning"]:::node
    PROMPT --> P2["Prompt Tuning Lester 2021"]:::node
    PROMPT --> P3["P-Tuning v2 Deep Prompt"]:::node
    PROMPT --> P4["Soft Prompt Search"]:::node

    HYB --> H1["MAM Adapter"]:::node
    HYB --> H2["UniPELT Gated"]:::node
    HYB --> H3["IA3 Learned Scaling"]:::node

    classDef cat fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef node fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#0f172a,stroke-width:1px
```

---

## 4.2 LoRA Architecture: Internal Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    X["Input x<br/>dim k"]:::input
    W0["Frozen W0<br/>d x k<br/>NOT TRAINED"]:::frozen
    A["Trainable A<br/>r x k<br/>He Init"]:::trainable
    B["Trainable B<br/>d x r<br/>Zero Init"]:::trainable
    S["Scale s = alpha / r"]:::scale
    MUL["Matrix Multiply"]:::op
    ADD["Element-wise Add"]:::op
    OUT["Output h"]:::output

    X --> W0
    X --> A
    A --> MUL
    B --> MUL
    MUL --> S
    S --> ADD
    W0 --> ADD
    ADD --> OUT

    classDef input fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#78350f,color:#0f172a
    classDef frozen fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#374151,color:#0f172a,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    classDef trainable fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534,color:#0f172a
    classDef scale fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#0f172a
    classDef op fill:#c4b5fd,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#0f172a
    classDef output fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#0f172a
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The frozen base path (gray) is *always* computed. The LoRA path (green) starts as a no-op (since $B = 0$) and gradually learns the task-specific update.

---

## 4.3 Adapter Layer Placement in Transformer Block

```mermaid
graph TB
    EMB["Input Embedding"]:::stage
    ATT["Multi-Head Self-Attention<br/>FROZEN"]:::frozen
    A1["Adapter 1<br/>Houlsby<br/>TRAINABLE"]:::trainable
    NORM1["LayerNorm"]:::frozen
    FFN["Feed-Forward Network<br/>FROZEN"]:::frozen
    A2["Adapter 2<br/>Houlsby<br/>TRAINABLE"]:::trainable
    NORM2["LayerNorm"]:::frozen
    OUT["Output to next layer"]:::stage

    EMB --> ATT
    ATT --> A1
    A1 --> NORM1
    NORM1 --> FFN
    FFN --> A2
    A2 --> NORM2
    NORM2 --> OUT

    classDef stage fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    classDef frozen fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#374151,color:#0f172a,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    classDef trainable fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534,color:#0f172a
```

```mermaid

    BASE["Shared Base Model<br/>LLaMA-7B<br/>FROZEN<br/>13 GB"]:::base
    DISK["PEFT Adapter Library"]:::storage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Production Insight:** A 13 GB base model + 100 LoRA adapters (8 MB each) = 13.8 GB total — *less* than deploying 2 full model copies (26 GB). The marginal cost per new task is just **8 MB**.

---

## 4.5 LoRA Forward Pass: Computational Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    START["Input Tensor x<br/>Shape batch x seq x k"]:::in
    P1["Step 1 Compute W0 * x<br/>Shape batch x seq x d"]:::frozen
    P2["Step 2 Compute A * x<br/>Shape batch x seq x r"]:::train
    P3["Step 3 Compute B * A_x<br/>Shape batch x seq x d"]:::train
    P4["Step 4 Scale by alpha / r"]:::scale
    P5["Step 5 Add to W0 path<br/>h_out = W0_x + scaled BAx"]:::op
    END["Output h_out"]:::out

    START --> P1
    START --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P1 --> P5
    P5 --> END

    classDef frozen fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#374151,color:#0f172a,stroke-dasharray: 5 5

    classDef scale fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#0f172a

    classDef out fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#0f172a
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT). Mention any two advantages over Full Fine-Tuning.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 mark):** Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning is a class of transfer learning techniques that adapts a large pre-trained model to a downstream task by training only a **small subset of parameters** (typically 0.1% – 5%) while keeping the original pre-trained weights **frozen**.

**Advantages over Full Fine-Tuning (2 marks — any two):**

1. **Storage Efficiency:** A full 7B model checkpoint requires ~14 GB in FP16, whereas a LoRA adapter requires only ~8 MB — a **~1750× reduction** in storage per task. This allows storing thousands of task-specific adaptations on a single disk.
2. **Catastrophic Forgetting Mitigation:** Since the base weights $W_0$ are never overwritten, the general-purpose pre-trained knowledge is preserved, preventing degradation on out-of-distribution inputs.
3. **Compute & Memory Efficiency:** Optimizer states and gradients are stored only for trainable parameters, reducing VRAM consumption by 3–4× (no Adam state for frozen params).
4. **Multi-Task Deployment:** A single base model can serve hundreds of tasks by hot-swapping small PEFT adapters, drastically reducing serving infrastructure costs.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the working of LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) with the help of its core equation.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Working Principle (2 marks):** LoRA injects **trainable low-rank decomposition matrices** $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$ and $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$ alongside the frozen pre-trained weight $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$, where $r \ll \min(d, k)$. The forward pass is computed as a parallel sum of the frozen base path and the trainable low-rank path.

**Core Equation (1 mark):**

$$W' = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} \cdot B \cdot A$$

where $B$ is initialized to zero (ensuring the model starts as the pre-trained model), $A$ is initialized via Kaiming/He initialization, and $\alpha$ is a scaling hyperparameter (typically $\alpha = 2r$). At deployment, $W' = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} BA$ can be merged into a single dense matrix, adding **zero inference latency**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **Adapter-based PEFT** approach in detail. Explain the architecture of an adapter module, derive the parameter count, and comment on the **Houlsby vs. Pfeiffer** placement strategies. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Prefix Tuning** and **Prompt Tuning** in detail. Compare them on the basis of (i) parameter count, (ii) training stability, and (iii) task performance. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

##### Part (a) — Adapter-Based PEFT (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Adapter-based PEFT introduces **small bottleneck feed-forward networks** (adapters) into each transformer layer while keeping all original model weights frozen.

**Architecture of an Adapter Module (3 marks):**

For an input $h \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$, the adapter performs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_{\text{down}} &= W_{\text{down}} \cdot h \in \mathbb{R}^{r} \\[4pt]
z_{\text{mid}} &= \sigma(z_{\text{down}}) \in \mathbb{R}^{r} \\[4pt]
z_{\text{up}} &= W_{\text{up}} \cdot z_{\text{mid}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d} \\[4pt]
h_{\text{out}} &= h + z_{\text{up}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $W_{\text{down}} \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d}$ (down-projection), $W_{\text{up}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$ (up-projection), $\sigma$ is a non-linearity (GELU/ReLU), and $r \ll d$ is the bottleneck dimension.

**Parameter Count Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{adapter}} &= \underbrace{d \cdot r + r}_{W_{\text{down}} + b_{\text{down}}} + \underbrace{r \cdot d + d}_{W_{\text{up}} + b_{\text{up}}} \\[4pt]
&= 2dr + d + r
\end{aligned}
$$

For BERT-base ($d = 768$, $r = 8$): $P = 2(768)(8) + 768 + 8 = 13{,}064$ parameters per adapter.

**Houlsby vs. Pfeiffer Placement (1 mark):**
- **Houlsby** places adapters **after both** the attention and FFN sub-layers (2 adapters per transformer layer) — slightly better performance, more parameters.
- **Pfeiffer** places a **single adapter only after the FFN** sub-layer (1 adapter per layer) — more parameter-efficient, marginally lower accuracy.

##### Part (b) — Prefix Tuning vs. Prompt Tuning (7 Marks)

**Prefix Tuning Explanation (2.5 marks):** Prefix Tuning prepends **trainable continuous key-value vectors** to the Keys and Values at every attention layer. The model attention becomes:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, [K_{\text{prefix}}; K_{\text{input}}], [V_{\text{prefix}}; V_{\text{input}}])$$

Total parameters: $P = 2 \cdot L_p \cdot d_{\text{model}} \cdot N_L$, where $L_p$ is prefix length. A **reparameterization MLP** (with hidden dim 800) is used during training for stability and discarded at inference.

**Prompt Tuning Explanation (2.5 marks):** Prompt Tuning prepends learnable **soft tokens** $P_{\text{soft}} \in \mathbb{R}^{L_p \times d}$ to the *input embedding only* (not every layer). Total parameters: $P = L_p \cdot d$. No reparameterization is needed for large models.

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Prefix Tuning | Prompt Tuning |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Parameters** | $2 L_p d N_L$ (per layer) — moderate | $L_p d$ (input only) — minimal |
| **(ii) Training Stability** | Needs reparam MLP for small models; stable | Stable for large models (>10B) |
| **(iii) Performance** | Strong across model sizes | Matches FFT only for very large models |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the **forward pass equations** for a LoRA-augmented linear layer. Show that the model output at initialization is identical to the pre-trained model. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **QLoRA**, **DoRA**, and **IA³** variants of PEFT. Discuss their memory savings and inference characteristics. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

##### Part (a) — LoRA Forward Pass Derivation (7 Marks)

**Setup (1 mark):** Given input $x \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$, frozen base weight $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$, trainable matrices $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$ and $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$, and scaling factor $s = \frac{\alpha}{r}$.

**Forward Pass Derivation (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{\text{out}} &= W' \cdot x \\[4pt]
&= \left(W_0 + s \cdot B \cdot A\right) \cdot x \\[4pt]
&= W_0 \cdot x + s \cdot (B \cdot A) \cdot x \\[4pt]
&= W_0 \cdot x + s \cdot B \cdot (A \cdot x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computational Steps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & z = A \cdot x \in \mathbb{R}^{r} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: } & y = B \cdot z \in \mathbb{R}^{d} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: } & h_{\text{out}} = W_0 \cdot x + s \cdot y
\end{aligned}
$$

The frozen path and LoRA path are computed **in parallel** and summed.

**Initialization Verification (2 marks):** At training step $t = 0$, $B$ is initialized to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_0 &= 0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r} \\[4pt]
\therefore \Delta W_0 &= s \cdot B_0 \cdot A_0 = s \cdot 0 \cdot A_0 = 0_{d \times k} \\[4pt]
\therefore h_{\text{out}}^{(0)} &= W_0 \cdot x + 0 = W_0 \cdot x
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves that **at initialization, the LoRA-augmented model produces identical outputs to the pre-trained model**, ensuring a stable training starting point. The reparameterization $\frac{\alpha}{r}$ is applied only to the LoRA path.

##### Part (b) — Advanced PEFT Variants (7 Marks)

**QLoRA (2.5 marks):** Combines **4-bit NormalFloat (NF4) quantization** of the base model with LoRA adapters. The frozen weights are stored in NF4 (4 bits/param), while LoRA matrices remain in FP16/BF16. Memory formula:

$$M_{\text{QLoRA}} \approx \frac{W_0}{2} \text{ (NF4)} + W_{\text{LoRA}} \text{ (FP16)} + \text{Optimizer (FP32)}$$

This enables fine-tuning a 65B model on a **single 48 GB GPU** — a ~4× memory reduction over standard LoRA. Inference: zero overhead (LoRA can be merged).

**DoRA — Weight-Decomposed Low-Rank Adaptation (2.5 marks):** Decomposes pre-trained weight into **magnitude** $m \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times k}$ and **direction** $V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$:

$$W' = m \cdot \frac{V + \Delta V}{\Vert V + \Delta V \Vert}$$

Where $\Delta V$ is the LoRA-style update. DoRA consistently **outperforms** LoRA and even full fine-tuning on several benchmarks while using the same parameter count as LoRA.

**IA³ — Infused Adapter by Inhibiting and Amplifying Activations (2 marks):** Multiplies the Key, Value, and FFN intermediate activations by **learned scaling vectors** $\ell \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$:

$$x' = \ell \odot x$$

Total parameters: $P = 3d$ per layer (Q, K, V, FFN). For a 7B model, IA³ trains only **~0.01%** of parameters — the most efficient method in the family. Used in the **T-Few** framework for few-shot learning. Inference: zero overhead.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deduction Points:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing LoRA matrices A and B dimensions** (1–2 mark loss). Remember: $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$ (input projection) and $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$ (output projection). The product $BA$ must recover the shape of $W_0$.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the scaling factor $\frac{\alpha}{r}$** (1 mark loss). Many students write $W' = W_0 + BA$ but omit the scaling, which is essential for controlling update magnitude.
> 
> 3. **Missing the initialization trick** ($B = 0$) (1–2 mark loss). This is the *key insight* that makes LoRA safe to apply — examiners explicitly test this.
> 
> 4. **Confusing Prefix Tuning with Prompt Tuning** (2 mark loss). Prefix = every layer; Prompt = input embedding only.
> 
> 5. **Not stating inference-time benefits** of LoRA (1 mark loss). The *zero-overhead merging* is a critical advantage over Adapter methods.
> 
> 6. **Incorrect parameter count formula** (1 mark loss). For LoRA: $P = r(d + k)$, NOT $P = r \cdot d \cdot k$ (the latter is the full matrix size, not the trainable count).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — PEFT Methods**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- **PEFT** = train tiny subset, freeze the rest of a large pre-trained model.
- **Catastrophic Forgetting** is mitigated by freezing $W_0$.
- **Storage savings** of 100×–1000× per task compared to full fine-tuning.

### 🔢 LoRA Essentials
- **Core equation:** $W' = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} BA$ where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$, $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$.
- **Initialization:** $B = 0$ (output side), $A \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ (Kaiming).
- **Typical rank:** $r \in \{4, 8, 16, 32, 64\}$; common $\alpha = 2r$.
- **Parameter count:** $P = r(d + k)$.
- **Zero inference overhead** — merge $W' = W_0 + s \cdot BA$ at deployment.
- **LoRA typically targets only $W_q$ and $W_v$** (query and value projection matrices).

### 🧩 Adapter Essentials
- **Bottleneck structure:** down-project → non-linearity → up-project → residual.
- **Formula:** $P = 2dr + d + r$.
- **Houlsby** = 2 adapters/layer; **Pfeiffer** = 1 adapter/layer.
- **Drawback:** Adds *inference latency* (extra FFN per layer).

### 💬 Prompt-Based Essentials
- **Prefix Tuning:** trainable $K, V$ at every attention layer; needs reparameterization for small models.
- **Prompt Tuning:** trainable soft tokens at input embedding only; works best for **very large models** (>10B).
- **P-Tuning v2:** prompt tuning applied at every layer (no reparameterization MLP).

### ⚡ Selective Methods
- **BitFit:** update only bias terms; $\sim 0.08\%$ params; surprisingly competitive.
- **Diff Pruning:** learn a sparse update via $L_1$ regularization.

### 🚀 Advanced Variants
- **QLoRA:** 4-bit base + LoRA; trains 65B model on single 48GB GPU.
- **DoRA:** decomposes magnitude and direction; often *exceeds* full FT.
- **AdaLoRA:** adaptive rank per layer (more rank for important layers).
- **VeRA:** shared frozen matrices with learned scaling vectors.
- **GaLore:** projects gradients to low-rank space (different paradigm).
- **IA³:** learned scaling vectors on Q, K, V, FFN; ultra-efficient.

### 📊 Selection Decision Rules
| Constraint | Best Method |
|---|---|
| Limited GPU (< 24 GB) | **QLoRA** or **LoRA r=8** |
| Need zero inference overhead | **LoRA, DoRA, IA³** |
| Very small dataset | **IA³, BitFit** |
| Multi-task serving | **LoRA** (easy merge/swap) |
| Maximum accuracy | **DoRA** or **LoRA r=64** |
| Edge devices / mobile | **BitFit, IA³** |
| Few-shot learning | **IA³ (T-Few)** |
| Hybrid scenario | **UniPELT** |

### 🧠 Key Numbers to Memorize for KTU Exam
- LoRA compression for $r=8$ on $4096 \times 4096$: **~0.04%** of FFT params.
- BitFit: **~0.08%** of BERT-base params.
- Adapter bottleneck with $r=8, d=768$: **13,064** params per adapter.
- QLoRA: trains 65B model on **48 GB** GPU.
- Adapter inference overhead: **+5–10% latency** vs base model.
- LoRA inference overhead: **0%** (mergeable).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prompt Tuning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Prompt Tuning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Prompt Tuning** is a *parameter-efficient fine-tuning* (PEFT) technique in which a small set of **continuous, learnable embedding vectors** (called *soft prompts*) is prepended to the input of a large pre-trained language model, while the **entire body of the model's parameters remains frozen**. Only the soft prompt embeddings are updated by gradient descent to adapt the frozen model to a downstream task.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Prompt Tuning is a lightweight adaptation method for Generative AI models where, instead of updating all model weights $\theta$, only a small set of task-specific prompt parameters $P \in \mathbb{R}^{n_p \times d}$ are learned, where $n_p$ is the prompt length and $d$ is the hidden dimension of the model.

## 1.2 Variants of Prompting (Hard vs Soft Prompts)

| Aspect | Hard Prompts (Prompt Engineering) | Soft Prompts (Prompt Tuning) |
|---|---|---|
| Representation | Discrete natural language tokens | Continuous embedding vectors |
| Trainable? | No (manually crafted) | Yes (learned via gradient descent) |
| Search space | Vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$ | Continuous $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| Human readable? | Yes | No |
| Performance ceiling | Lower | Comparable to full fine-tuning at scale |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Frozen Expert with Cue Cards**
>
> Imagine a world-class chef (the **frozen pre-trained LLM**) who already knows 10,000 recipes but refuses to learn any new dishes. He will, however, happily cook anything you describe. **Prompt Engineering** is like telling him in plain English: *"Make me a spicy Kerala fish curry with coconut milk."* The words are fixed and he interprets them literally. **Prompt Tuning**, on the other hand, is like handing him a stack of small cue cards printed in his own private shorthand (continuous embeddings) that he has agreed to silently *read first* before cooking. The cue cards are updated by a manager (the training loop) until the chef produces the right dish — but the chef's brain (model weights $\theta$) is **never touched**.

## 1.4 Why Prompt Tuning Matters in Generative AI

- **Storage efficiency:** One base model + many tiny prompt files (a few KB each) instead of one full model copy per task.
- **Multi-task serving:** Switch tasks instantly by swapping prompt embeddings.
- **Cheap training:** Backpropagation touches only $\sim 0.001\%$ of the parameters.
- **Catastrophic forgetting is avoided** because the original model weights are frozen.
- Scales remarkably well: at $\geq 10$B parameters, prompt tuning matches full fine-tuning.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Fact for KTU Exams:** The number of trainable parameters in prompt tuning is $n_p \cdot d$ (prompt length $\times$ hidden dimension), independent of model size $N$. For a $7$B-parameter model with $n_p=20$, $d=4096$, you train only $81{,}920$ parameters — roughly **5 orders of magnitude fewer**.

## 1.5 Place in the PEFT Family

$$
\text{PEFT} \;\supset\; \begin{cases} \text{Adapter Tuning} \\ \text{LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)} \\ \text{Prefix Tuning} \\ \text{P-Tuning} \\ \mathbf{\text{Prompt Tuning (this module)}} \end{cases}
$$

Prompt Tuning is the **simplest** member of this family — it is essentially *Prefix Tuning* restricted to the input embedding layer only.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow (Structured Logic)

1. **Freeze the base model.** All original parameters $\theta$ of the pre-trained transformer are set as non-trainable: $\nabla_\theta = 0$.
2. **Initialize the soft prompt.** A learnable tensor $P \in \mathbb{R}^{n_p \times d}$ is created (either random init, or init from vocabulary token embeddings).
3. **Construct the augmented input.** The prompt embeddings are *concatenated* with the input token embeddings of the actual query $x$.
4. **Forward pass through frozen model.** The combined sequence flows through every frozen transformer block.
5. **Compute task loss.** Standard cross-entropy between predicted output and ground-truth label $y$.
6. **Backward pass — partial.** Gradients flow back only into $P$; $\theta$ receives no updates.
7. **Update only $P$.** Apply gradient descent (typically Adam) to the prompt parameters.
8. **Deploy.** Store only the tiny $P$ tensor per task; the base model is shared.

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation

Let the frozen language model be $f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$, parameterized by $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$.

Let the soft prompt be a tensor:

$$
P = \begin{bmatrix} p_1 & p_2 & \cdots & p_{n_p} \end{bmatrix}^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{n_p \times d}
$$

For an input token sequence $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{n_x})$, embed it via the frozen embedding matrix $E \in \mathbb{R}^{|\mathcal{V}| \times d}$:

$$
X_e = E(x) \in \mathbb{R}^{n_x \times d}
$$

The augmented input sequence (in embedding space) is:

$$
H_0 = \begin{bmatrix} P \\ X_e \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{(n_p + n_x) \times d}
$$

The hidden states evolve through $L$ frozen transformer layers:

$$
H_l = \text{TransformerLayer}_l(H_{l-1}), \quad l = 1, 2, \dots, L
$$

The output prediction is:

$$
\hat{y} = \text{Head}(H_L[n_p + n_x, :])
$$

The optimization objective is:

$$
P^{*} = \arg\min_{P} \; \mathcal{L}\!\left(\hat{y}, y\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works — the deep reason:** The soft prompt $P$ effectively *steers* the model into a particular region of its pre-learned representation space, activating task-relevant circuits that already exist inside the frozen transformer. This is sometimes called "prompting as a reparametrization of the task."

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Equation | Symbol | Definition / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Trainable parameter count | $\vert P \vert$ | $n_p \cdot d$ |
| 2 | Frozen parameter count | $\vert \theta \vert$ | $N$ (e.g., $7 \times 10^{9}$) |
| 3 | Trainable ratio | $r$ | $\dfrac{n_p \cdot d}{N}$ (typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$) |
| 4 | Augmented input | $H_0$ | $[P;\ X_e] \in \mathbb{R}^{(n_p+n_x) \times d}$ |
| 5 | Loss | $\mathcal{L}$ | $-\sum_{i} y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ (cross-entropy) |
| 6 | Gradient update | $P_{t+1}$ | $P_t - \eta \nabla_{P} \mathcal{L}$ |
| 7 | Prefix-Tuning extension | $P_k^{(l)}, P_v^{(l)}$ | Prepended to Keys and Values at *every* layer $l$ |
| 8 | Memory for one task | $M_{\text{task}}$ | $n_p \cdot d \cdot 4$ bytes (FP32) |
| 9 | Effective context length | $n_{\text{eff}}$ | $n_p + n_x$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Customer-support chatbots** serving 1000 companies from one LLM (e.g., Google's Flan-PT, OpenAI enterprise deployments).
- **Personalized AI assistants** on edge devices — only the prompt ships with the app.
- **Multi-lingual adaptation** — one prompt per language, one shared backbone.
- **A/B testing of model behaviour** — swap prompts to compare steering effects without retraining.

## 2.5 Prompt Tuning vs Prefix Tuning vs P-Tuning

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Prompt Tuning:}   &\quad \text{Learn } P \in \mathbb{R}^{n_p \times d} \text{ at input only.} \\
\textbf{Prefix Tuning:}   &\quad \text{Learn } P_k^{(l)}, P_v^{(l)} \text{ at every layer } l \in \{1, \dots, L\}. \\
\textbf{P-Tuning:}        &\quad \text{Learn } P \text{ at input + a small LSTM/MLP encoder for prompts.} \\
\textbf{Full Fine-Tune:}  &\quad \text{Learn all } \theta \in \mathbb{R}^{N}.
\end{aligned}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Trainable Parameter Count

The prompt tensor is a matrix of $n_p$ rows (one per virtual prompt token) and $d$ columns (one per hidden dimension). Every entry is a learnable scalar.

**Step 1.** Count entries in the matrix.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{dim}(P) &= n_p \times d \\
\Rightarrow \vert P \vert &= n_p \cdot d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Convert to memory in bytes (single-precision, 4 bytes/float).

$$
M_{\text{prompt}} = 4 \cdot n_p \cdot d \;\; \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 3.** Numerical example (typical values): $n_p = 20$, $d = 4096$ (matches LLaMA-7B).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert P \vert &= 20 \times 4096 = 81{,}920 \text{ parameters} \\
M_{\text{prompt}} &= 4 \times 81{,}920 \approx 327 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** Trainable ratio for a $7$B-parameter model ($N = 7 \times 10^9$).

$$
r = \frac{n_p \cdot d}{N} = \frac{8.192 \times 10^{4}}{7 \times 10^{9}} \approx 1.17 \times 10^{-5}
$$

That is, only **0.00117 %** of the model is trained.

## 3.2 Derivation: Forward Pass Through Frozen Transformer

Let $H_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{(n_p + n_x) \times d}$ be the augmented embedding sequence. Each transformer layer applies self-attention followed by a feed-forward network.

**Step 1.** Compute Query, Key, Value projections (all frozen).

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{(l)} &= H_{l-1} W_Q^{(l)} \\
K^{(l)} &= H_{l-1} W_K^{(l)} \\
V^{(l)} &= H_{l-1} W_V^{(l)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $W_Q^{(l)}, W_K^{(l)}, W_V^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ are **frozen**, they produce no gradient updates.

**Step 2.** Scaled dot-product attention.

$$
\text{Attn}^{(l)} = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q^{(l)} (K^{(l)})^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V^{(l)}
$$

**Step 3.** Residual + LayerNorm + FFN block.

$$
H_l = \text{LayerNorm}\!\left(H_{l-1} + \text{Attn}^{(l)}\right)
$$

$$
H_l = \text{LayerNorm}\!\left(H_l + \text{FFN}^{(l)}(H_l)\right)
$$

All parameters inside these blocks belong to $\theta$ and are frozen.

**Step 4.** Take the hidden state of the *last real input token* (position $n_p + n_x$) and project through the language-model head.

$$
\hat{y} = \text{softmax}\!\left(W_{\text{LM}} \, H_L[n_p + n_x,\, :]\right)
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Loss and Gradient Flow

**Step 1.** Cross-entropy loss over vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$ for a true token $y$:

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{|\mathcal{V}|} y_i \log \hat{y}_i = -\log \hat{y}_{y}
$$

**Step 2.** Apply the chain rule from the head all the way back to the prompt.

$$
\nabla_{P} \mathcal{L} \;=\; \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial H_L} \cdot \frac{\partial H_L}{\partial H_{L-1}} \cdots \frac{\partial H_1}{\partial H_0} \cdot \frac{\partial H_0}{\partial P}
$$

**Step 3.** Since the head, attention, and FFN weights are frozen, all intermediate Jacobians $\partial H_l / \partial H_{l-1}$ are *constants* (no parameter dependence). Only $\partial H_0 / \partial P = I$ contributes a non-trivial term, giving:

$$
\nabla_{P} \mathcal{L} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P}
$$

**Step 4.** Update rule (Adam shown for completeness):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_t &= \beta_1 m_{t-1} + (1 - \beta_1) \nabla_{P} \mathcal{L} \\
v_t &= \beta_2 v_{t-1} + (1 - \beta_2) (\nabla_{P} \mathcal{L})^2 \\
\hat{m}_t &= \dfrac{m_t}{1 - \beta_1^{t}}, \qquad \hat{v}_t = \dfrac{v_t}{1 - \beta_2^{t}} \\
P_{t+1} &= P_t - \eta \cdot \dfrac{\hat{m}_t}{\sqrt{\hat{v}_t} + \epsilon}
\end{aligned}
$$

Typical hyperparameters: $\eta = 10^{-3}$, $\beta_1 = 0.9$, $\beta_2 = 0.999$, $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Prompt Tuning vs Full Fine-Tuning

| Criterion | Prompt Tuning | Full Fine-Tuning |
|---|---|---|
| Trainable parameters | $n_p \cdot d$ ($\sim 10^4$–$10^5$) | $N$ ($\sim 10^9$–$10^{11}$) |
| Storage per task | $\sim 100$ KB | $\sim 14$ GB (for 7B in FP16) |
| Training time per step | Low | High |
| GPU memory | $\sim$ inference memory | $\sim 3\times$ inference (optimizer states) |
| Risk of catastrophic forgetting | **None** (frozen) | High |
| Performance on small models | Lower gap to full FT | Best |
| Performance on large models ($\geq 10$B) | **Matches** full FT | Best |
| Multi-task deployment | Easy (swap prompts) | Hard (separate model copies) |
| Inference latency overhead | Negligible (concatenation) | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Insight:** Lester, Al-Rfou & Constant (2021) — the original *"The Power of Scale for Parameter-Efficient Prompt Tuning"* paper — showed that as model size grows, the performance gap between Prompt Tuning and Full Fine-Tuning *vanishes*. This is a commonly-tested KTU fact.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Exam-Ready)

**Problem:** A research team uses prompt tuning on a $1.3$B-parameter language model with hidden dimension $d = 2048$ and uses $n_p = 50$ prompt tokens. Calculate (a) the number of trainable parameters, (b) the trainable ratio, (c) the prompt storage in MB for FP16 (2 bytes).

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a)} \quad \vert P \vert &= n_p \cdot d = 50 \times 2048 = 102{,}400 \text{ parameters} \\[4pt]
\text{(b)} \quad r &= \frac{102{,}400}{1.3 \times 10^9} = 7.88 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.00788\% \\[4pt]
\text{(c)} \quad M_{\text{prompt}} &= 2 \times 102{,}400 = 204{,}800 \text{ bytes} \approx 0.195 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Inference:** One full LLM checkpoint is $\sim 2.6$ GB; the prompt occupies $\sim 0.195$ MB — about **13,000 times smaller**. You could fit ~13,000 task-specialized models in the storage of one fine-tuned model.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture: Prompt Tuning vs Full Fine-Tuning

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PT["PROMPT TUNING APPROACH"]
        direction TB
        A1["Input tokens x"] --> A2["Frozen Embedding E"]
        A3["Soft Prompt P (TRAINABLE)"] --> A4["Concatenate"]
        A2 --> A4
        A4 --> A5["Frozen Transformer Layers 1 to L"]
        A5 --> A6["Task Head (often frozen)"]
        A6 --> A7["Output y-hat"]
        A3 -. "gradients flow back" .-> A5
    end

    subgraph FT["FULL FINE-TUNING APPROACH"]
        direction TB
        B1["Input tokens x"] --> B2["Trainable Embedding E"]
        B2 --> B3["Trainable Transformer Layers 1 to L"]
        B3 --> B4["Trainable Task Head"]
        B4 --> B5["Output y-hat"]
        B3 -. "all parameters updated" .-> B2
        B3 -. "all parameters updated" .-> B4
    end
```

## 4.2 Detailed Data-Flow of the Augmented Forward Pass

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Soft Prompt Tensor P, shape n_p by d"] --> CAT["Concatenation along token axis"]
    X["Input Embedding X_e, shape n_x by d"] --> CAT
    CAT --> H0["H_0 shape n_p + n_x by d"]
    H0 --> L1["Transformer Layer 1 frozen"]
    L1 --> L2["Transformer Layer 2 frozen"]
    L2 --> LD["Transformer Layer L frozen"]
    LD --> LAST["Pick hidden state at position n_p + n_x"]
    LAST --> HEAD["LM Head frozen"]
    HEAD --> OUT["Token probabilities y-hat"]
    OUT --> LOSS["Cross-Entropy Loss L"]
    LOSS -. "grad_P only" .-> P
```

## 4.3 Multi-Task Deployment with a Single Frozen Backbone

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    USERS["Incoming Requests"] --> ROUTER["Task Router"]
    ROUTER --> P1["Prompt P1: Sentiment Analysis"]
    ROUTER --> P2["Prompt P2: Translation EN to FR"]
    ROUTER --> P3["Prompt P3: Code Generation"]
    ROUTER --> P4["Prompt Pn: Custom Task k"]
    P1 --> BASE["Single Frozen Base LLM theta"]
    P2 --> BASE
    P3 --> BASE
    P4 --> BASE
    BASE --> RESP["Response y-hat"]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: Memory Footprint

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FT2["FULL FINE-TUNE: 14 GB per task"]
        F1["Embedding: 0.2 GB"]
        F2["Transformer: 13 GB"]
        F3["Head: 0.2 GB"]
        F4["Optimizer States: 28 GB"]
    end

    subgraph PT2["PROMPT TUNE: 0.2 MB per task"]
        Q1["Soft Prompt P: 0.2 MB"]
    end

    BASE2["Shared Frozen Backbone: 14 GB, ONE copy, ONE GPU"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual takeaway (Board-mark favourite):** In Prompt Tuning, only the *arrow from $P$ to the model* is trainable (dashed line); every other arrow is frozen (solid line). Students are expected to draw a similar diagram in the 14-mark questions and label the trainable vs frozen arrows explicitly.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between Hard Prompts and Soft Prompts in the context of Generative AI.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Hard Prompts** are human-readable natural language strings crafted manually (e.g., *"Translate to French: Hello"*) and live in the discrete vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$. They are **not trainable** and rely entirely on prompt-engineering skill. (1.5 marks)
- **Soft Prompts** are continuous embedding vectors $P \in \mathbb{R}^{n_p \times d}$ that exist *inside* the model's embedding space. They are **trainable via back-propagation**, not human-readable, and can be optimized to achieve higher task accuracy. (1.5 marks)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**List any three advantages of Prompt Tuning over full fine-tuning of large language models.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Storage efficiency** — only $n_p \cdot d$ trainable parameters (KBs) per task versus GBs for full fine-tuning. (1 mark)
2. **Mitigates catastrophic forgetting** — original model weights are frozen, so previously learned capabilities are preserved. (1 mark)
3. **Cheap and fast training** — gradients flow through $\sim 0.001\%$ of the model, drastically reducing compute and memory cost. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Prompt Tuning** in large language models. With a neat block diagram, describe how it differs from full model fine-tuning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the number of trainable parameters in Prompt Tuning. A team fine-tunes a $7$B-parameter model with $d = 4096$ and $n_p = 30$. Compute the trainable ratio and the FP32 storage in MB for one task's prompt. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) (7 marks)

1. **Definition (2 marks):** Prompt Tuning is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning technique that prepends a small set of *learnable continuous embeddings* (soft prompt $P \in \mathbb{R}^{n_p \times d}$) to the input of a *frozen* pre-trained LLM. Only $P$ is updated by gradient descent; the model weights $\theta$ remain unchanged.

2. **Working (3 marks):**
   - Initialize $P$ randomly (or from vocabulary embeddings).
   - Concatenate $P$ with input embeddings: $H_0 = [P; X_e]$.
   - Pass through all $L$ frozen transformer layers to obtain $\hat{y}$.
   - Compute cross-entropy loss $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$.
   - Back-propagate **only** into $P$; update via Adam.

3. **Block diagram (2 marks):** See **Section 4.1** above. Crucial visual elements to be drawn:
   - Dashed (trainable) line from $P$ → frozen transformer.
   - Solid (frozen) line for all other components.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) (7 marks)

**Derivation (3 marks):**

The soft prompt is a matrix of $n_p$ rows (one per virtual token) and $d$ columns (one per hidden dimension).

$$
|P| = n_p \times d
$$

**Numerical computation (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
|P| &= 30 \times 4096 = 122{,}880 \text{ parameters} \\[4pt]
r &= \dfrac{|P|}{N} = \dfrac{1.2288 \times 10^{5}}{7 \times 10^{9}} = 1.756 \times 10^{-5} \\[4pt]
M_{\text{prompt}}^{\text{FP32}} &= 4 \times 122{,}880 = 491{,}520 \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
M_{\text{prompt}}^{\text{MB}} &= \dfrac{491{,}520}{1024 \times 1024} \approx 0.469 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (0 marks — implicit):** The prompt occupies less than 0.5 MB, while the base model is $\sim 28$ GB in FP32 — a ratio of nearly 1 : 60,000.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare **Prompt Tuning, Prefix Tuning, and Full Fine-Tuning** with respect to (i) where the trainable parameters are added, (ii) trainable parameter count, and (iii) typical use-case scenarios. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **multi-task serving architecture** enabled by Prompt Tuning. Why is this attractive for enterprise-scale LLM deployment? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) (7 marks)

| Aspect | Prompt Tuning | Prefix Tuning | Full Fine-Tuning |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Trainable location | Input embedding layer only | Keys & Values of *every* transformer layer | All layers (embedding, attention, FFN, head) |
| (ii) Parameter count | $n_p \cdot d$ (small) | $L \cdot 2 \cdot n_p \cdot d$ (medium) | $N$ (large) |
| (iii) Typical use case | Massive LLMs ($>$ 10 B), many tasks | Mid-size LLMs, few tasks, tighter adaptation | Small models, maximum task accuracy |

(5 marks for the table + 1 mark explanation + 1 mark for naming the original papers: Lester et al. 2021 for Prompt Tuning; Li & Liang 2021 for Prefix Tuning.)

---

#### Model Solution — (b) (7 marks)

1. **Architecture (4 marks):** A single frozen base LLM $\theta$ is hosted on one GPU cluster. For each task $k$, a tiny prompt file $P_k$ is loaded into the model context at inference time. A *task router* selects which $P_k$ to apply based on the incoming user request. See **Section 4.3** above.
2. **Enterprise advantages (3 marks):**
   - **Cost:** One copy of the model serves all customers — no per-tenant model duplication.
   - **Latency:** Prompts add only $n_p$ tokens of overhead, negligible compared to context length.
   - **Privacy / Customization:** Each tenant's data is reflected only in their private prompt, never in the base weights — easing compliance.
   - **Versioning & rollback:** Replacing one $P_k$ file instantly updates that customer's behaviour.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Prefix Tuning with Prompt Tuning** (–2 marks): Prefix Tuning adds trainable vectors at *every* layer's K/V projections; Prompt Tuning adds them *only* at the input embedding layer. Many students draw them identically — don't.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify the frozen model size** (–1 mark): When asked "compute trainable parameters," always state $d$ and $N$ explicitly before substituting.
> 3. **Writing "soft prompt = text"** (–2 marks): Soft prompts are **continuous vectors**, not text. Markers deduct heavily for this conceptual error.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram in 14-mark answers** (–2 marks): The KTU rubric for 7-mark sub-questions explicitly allocates marks for a "neat labelled diagram."
> 5. **Mixing up LoRA and Prompt Tuning**: LoRA adds *low-rank matrices* to attention weights; Prompt Tuning adds *soft prompts* to inputs. They are different PEFT methods.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This**

- **Definition:** Prompt Tuning learns a small continuous prompt $P \in \mathbb{R}^{n_p \times d}$; the LLM's weights $\theta$ are frozen.
- **Trainable count:** $|P| = n_p \cdot d$ (e.g., $20 \times 4096 = 81{,}920$).
- **Hard prompt ≠ Soft prompt:** Hard = text, Soft = continuous embedding.
- **Forward pass:** $H_0 = [P;\ X_e] \rightarrow$ frozen transformer $\rightarrow$ LM head $\rightarrow$ $\hat{y}$.
- **Loss:** Cross-entropy $\mathcal{L} = -\log \hat{y}_y$.
- **Update rule:** $P_{t+1} = P_t - \eta \nabla_P \mathcal{L}$ — **only $P$ changes**.
- **Storage:** $\sim 0.1$–$1$ MB per task in FP32 (KB-range in FP16).
- **Trainable ratio $r$:** Typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$.
- **Key empirical fact:** At model size $\geq 10$B, Prompt Tuning *matches* full fine-tuning accuracy.
- **Variants to remember:**
  - **Prefix Tuning:** Trains $P_k^{(l)}, P_v^{(l)}$ at every layer.
  - **P-Tuning:** Trains input prompts + a small LSTM/MLP prompt encoder.
- **Why it works:** The soft prompt *steers* the frozen model into a task-specific region of its existing representation space.
- **Multi-task deployment:** One base model + many prompt files, switched at inference by a router.
- **Avoids:** Catastrophic forgetting, large GPU memory, expensive optimizer states.
- **Enables:** Edge deployment, personalized AI, cheap A/B testing, regulatory-friendly customization.
- **Original reference (no fabrication):** Lester, Al-Rfou & Constant, *"The Power of Scale for Parameter-Efficient Prompt Tuning,"* EMNLP 2021.
- **Engineering one-liner:** *"Update the cue cards, not the chef."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
