topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Prefix Tuning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Prefix Tuning** is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) technique for large pre-trained language models, formally introduced in the seminal paper *"Prefix-Tuning: Optimizing Continuous Prompts for Generation"* by **Xiang Lisa Li and Percy Liang (ACL 2021)**. Instead of updating all parameters of a massive language model for a downstream task, Prefix Tuning prepends a sequence of trainable, **continuous task-specific vectors** (collectively called the *prefix*) to the keys and values at every transformer self-attention layer. The base model weights remain **completely frozen**, and only the parameters of the prefix vectors are optimized via gradient descent.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Exam Gold-Standard Phrasing):**  
> *"Prefix Tuning is a lightweight, parameter-efficient adaptation method for frozen language models in which a small set of continuous, trainable prefix vectors — prepended to the keys and values of every attention layer — is optimized, while all original model parameters remain unchanged."*

### Mathematical Abstraction

Let the frozen language model be parameterized by $\theta$. Prefix Tuning introduces a new, small set of parameters $\phi$ that parameterize the prefix $P_{\phi}$ (a matrix of size $\vert P_{\vert} \times \dim(K)$). The training objective becomes:

$$\max_{\phi} \log P_{\theta}(y \mid x, P_{\phi}) = \sum_{t} \log P_{\theta}(y_t \mid x, P_{\phi}, y_{<t})$$

Only $\phi$ is updated via backpropagation; $\theta$ remains static.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **The "Sticky-Note on a Refrigerator" Analogy**

Imagine you rent a **giant, fully-stocked industrial refrigerator** (the frozen pre-trained LLM with billions of parameters). You cannot modify the refrigerator itself — it belongs to the landlord, and rewiring its compressor (full fine-tuning) would be ruinously expensive and would destroy its general-purpose utility for other tenants.

Instead, you are allowed to stick **a few personalized sticky notes** (the trainable prefix vectors) on the door of the refrigerator. Every time you ask the refrigerator a question, it first **reads your sticky notes**, then uses them as context to give you a customized answer tailored to your specific task (e.g., summarization, translation, or medical QA).

- The sticky notes are **continuous** (real-valued vectors, not human-readable text).
- The notes are stuck at **every shelf** (every transformer layer), not just the front door.
- Only the sticky notes are edited when you "train" — the refrigerator's internal mechanics (the base model) are untouched.
- When a different student comes along with a different task, you simply **peel off your sticky notes and replace them with theirs** — the refrigerator serves everyone.

This is precisely what Prefix Tuning achieves: **task-specific adaptation via a tiny, swappable, continuous "control signal" inserted directly into the attention mechanism of every layer of an otherwise-untouched, massive frozen model**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & High-Yield Vocabulary

| **Term** | **Symbol** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| Prefix Length | $\vert P_{\vert}$ | Number of trainable tokens prepended (typically **10–200**) |
| Hidden Dimension | $d$ | Dimensionality of prefix vectors (e.g., 768, 1024, 4096) |
| Trainable Parameters | $\phi$ | Only the prefix; in millions, often **< 0.1%** of model size |
| Frozen Parameters | $\theta$ | The entire base model, kept static during training |
| Reparameterization Size | $d_{\text{MLP}}$ | Hidden width of the auxiliary MLP used during training |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why Prefix Tuning is "Parameter-Efficient":** For a model with $\sim 10^9$ parameters, the prefix may contain only $\sim 10^5$ trainable parameters — a **three-to-four order-of-magnitude reduction** in storage, memory, and gradient computation cost per downstream task.

---

## 1.4 Distinguishing Prefix Tuning from Neighboring Techniques

> [!NOTE]  
> **Family of Prompt-Based PEFT Methods** — commonly confused in exams.

- **Prompt Tuning (Lester et al., 2021):** Only the **input embeddings** of the transformer (layer 0) are prepended. No modification of deeper layers.
- **Prefix Tuning (Li & Liang, 2021):** Prefix vectors are prepended to the **keys and values of every attention layer** (all $L$ layers).
- **P-Tuning v2 (Liu et al., 2021):** Adds continuous prompts to **multiple layers** (similar depth to Prefix Tuning but uses different positioning logic).
- **Adapter Tuning (Houlsby et al., 2019):** Inserts small trainable **MLP bottleneck modules** inside each transformer block, not into attention keys/values.

**Key differentiator:** Prefix Tuning is the only one of the above that directly **modifies the key/value matrices of self-attention** at every layer, effectively steering the attention distribution itself.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

The transformer self-attention layer is the natural site of intervention. Let us dissect the mechanism layer by layer.

### Step 1: Standard Self-Attention (Baseline)

In a vanilla multi-head attention layer, given input $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$, we compute queries, keys, and values via three learned projection matrices $W_Q, W_K, W_V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$:

$$Q = X W_Q, \quad K = X W_K, \quad V = X W_V$$

The scaled dot-product attention then yields:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

### Step 2: Prefix Prepending — The Core Intervention

Instead of feeding only $X$ into the layer, Prefix Tuning constructs an **extended key matrix and value matrix** by concatenating trainable prefix vectors to the left (prepending) of the projected $K$ and $V$:

$$\tilde{K} = \bigl[ P_K ;\, K \bigr] \in \mathbb{R}^{(\vert P_{\vert} + n) \times d}, \quad \tilde{V} = \bigl[ P_V ;\, V \bigr] \in \mathbb{R}^{(\vert P_{\vert} + n) \times d}$$

Here $P_K, P_V \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert P_{\vert} \times d}$ are the **trainable prefix matrices**, and the semicolon denotes row-wise concatenation. The query matrix is left **unchanged**:

$$Q = X W_Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$$

The modified attention computation is then:

$$\text{Attention}_{\text{prefix}}(Q, \tilde{K}, \tilde{V}) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q \tilde{K}^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) \tilde{V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why prepend to K and V, not Q?**  
> Prepending to queries would require re-running attention against all prior context for the prefix slots, complicating autoregressive decoding. Prepending to keys and values allows the original queries to **attend to the prefix** as if it were additional, freely-controllable context — the prefix slots effectively become "virtual memory addresses" the model can read from at inference time.

### Step 3: Layer-wise Application

For a transformer with $L$ layers, the prefix is applied at **every** layer $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, L\}$. A separate independent prefix is learned for each layer:

$$P^{(i)} = \bigl( P_K^{(i)}, P_V^{(i)} \bigr), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, L$$

The total number of trainable parameters is therefore:

$$\vert \phi \vert = 2 \cdot L \cdot \vert P_{\vert} \cdot d$$

(For a 12-layer model with $\vert P_{\vert} = 10$ and $d = 768$: $\vert \phi \vert = 2 \cdot 12 \cdot 10 \cdot 768 = \mathbf{184{,}320}$ parameters — vanishingly small compared to $\sim 100\text{M+}$ of the base model.)

### Step 4: Reparameterization Trick (Training Stability)

Directly optimizing $P_K$ and $P_V$ from random initialization leads to unstable training and degraded performance. Li & Liang's solution is to **first learn the prefix through a small auxiliary MLP**, then discard the MLP at inference and store only the reparameterized prefix.

$$P_K = \text{MLP}_K(P_K'), \quad P_V = \text{MLP}_V(P_V')$$

where $P_K', P_V' \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert P_{\vert} \times d_{\text{MLP}}}$ are the actual trainable parameters, and $\text{MLP}_K, \text{MLP}_V : \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{MLP}}} \to \mathbb{R}^{d}$ are two-layer feedforward networks with a non-linearity (e.g., tanh). After training, only the reparameterized $P_K, P_V$ are saved.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Intuition for Reparameterization:**  
> The MLP acts as a **low-pass filter / smoother** on the optimization trajectory. Random initialization of raw prefix vectors places them in a high-curvature region of the loss landscape; the MLP lifts them into a smoother, lower-dimensional manifold from which gradient descent is far more effective. At inference, the smoother manifold collapses back to the final vectors.

### Step 5: Autoregressive Decoding Adjustment

For **left-to-right language models** (e.g., GPT-2), an additional masking is applied: the prefix slots at layer $i$ are allowed to attend to **all** earlier prefix slots across **all** earlier layers (the "previous layers' prefix" trick). This preserves the autoregressive property of the base model and significantly improves generation quality. The notation is:

$$\text{Mask}_{\text{autoregressive}}(i, j) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } j \le i \text{ and } j \text{ is a prefix slot at layer } j \\ -\infty & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

---

## 2.2 Why Prefix Tuning Works — The "Why" Behind the Method

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Three Core Theoretical Justifications**

1. **Expressivity beyond Discrete Prompts:** Continuous vectors live in the full $d$-dimensional embedding space, so the prefix can encode vastly richer task-specific instructions than any finite set of natural-language tokens could ever express.

2. **Direct Steering of Attention Distribution:** Because $K$ and $V$ are the very quantities attention distributions are computed from, modifying them directly reshapes *what the model attends to* at every layer — a high-leverage intervention point.

3. **Inductive Bias Preservation:** The frozen base model retains all of its pre-trained world knowledge, linguistic structure, and reasoning ability. The prefix only nudges this existing capability toward a narrower task distribution, rather than risking catastrophic forgetting through full gradient updates.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> **Mandatory High-Yield Formula Table** — reproduce this verbatim in revision.

| **#** | **Equation / Quantity** | **Formula** | **Notes / Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Modified Key Matrix | $\tilde{K} = \bigl[ P_K ;\, K \bigr]$ | Row-wise concat; shape $(\vert P_{\vert} + n) \times d$ |
| 2 | Modified Value Matrix | $\tilde{V} = \bigl[ P_V ;\, V \bigr]$ | Row-wise concat; shape $(\vert P_{\vert} + n) \times d$ |
| 3 | Modified Attention | $\text{Attn} = \text{softmax}\!\left(Q \tilde{K}^{\top} / \sqrt{d_k}\right) \tilde{V}$ | Query unchanged |
| 4 | Trainable Parameter Count | $\vert \phi \vert = 2 L \vert P_{\vert} d$ | $L$ = layers, $d$ = hidden dim |
| 5 | Storage per Task | $2 L \vert P_{\vert} d \times 4$ bytes (FP32) | Plus optional reparam. overhead |
| 6 | Reparameterization | $P_K = \text{MLP}(P_K')$, $P_V = \text{MLP}(P_V')$ | Auxiliary MLP discarded post-training |
| 7 | Efficiency Ratio | $\rho = \vert \phi \vert \,/\, \vert \theta \vert$ | Typically $\rho \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-3}]$ |
| 8 | Scaling Factor | $1 / \sqrt{d_k}$ | Standard softmax temperature |
| 9 | Loss (Next-token CE) | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t} \log P_{\theta}(y_t \mid x, P_{\phi}, y_{<t})$ | Standard cross-entropy |
| 10 | Gradient Flow | $\nabla_{\phi} \mathcal{L}$ only; $\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L} = 0$ | $\theta$ is frozen |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]  
> **Where Prefix Tuning is Deployed in Practice**

- **Multi-Tenant LLM Serving:** A single frozen 7B/13B/70B model serves thousands of users, each with their own tiny per-user prefix (kilobytes) instead of a per-user fine-tuned copy (gigabytes).
- **Domain Adaptation:** News summarization, legal document QA, biomedical text mining, code generation — all adapted from one base checkpoint.
- **Edge/On-Device Deployment:** Only the prefix needs to be downloaded and swapped; the base model can live in read-only flash memory.
- **Continual Learning Pipelines:** New tasks can be added incrementally as new prefixes, avoiding the catastrophic forgetting endemic to full fine-tuning.
- **A/B Testing at Scale:** Two prefixes differing by a single vector index can be deployed side-by-side for causal evaluation of prompt-design hypotheses.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Trainable Parameter Count

**Problem Statement:**  
A 12-layer transformer with hidden dimension $d = 768$ uses Prefix Tuning with prefix length $\vert P_{\vert} = 10$. Compute the total number of trainable parameters, assuming prefixes are added to both keys and values at every layer.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the contribution of one layer.

At a single transformer layer, the prefix consists of two matrices: one for keys and one for values. Each matrix has $\vert P_{\vert}$ rows and $d$ columns.

$$\text{Params per matrix} = \vert P_{\vert} \times d = 10 \times 768 = 7{,}680$$

**Step 2:** Account for both key and value prefixes at one layer.

$$\text{Params per layer} = 2 \times \vert P_{\vert} \times d = 2 \times 7{,}680 = 15{,}360$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by the number of layers $L = 12$.

$$\vert \phi \vert = L \times 2 \times \vert P_{\vert} \times d = 12 \times 15{,}360$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the product.

$$\vert \phi \vert = 12 \times 15{,}360 = 184{,}320 \text{ parameters}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to megabytes under FP32 (4 bytes per parameter).

$$\text{Storage} = 184{,}320 \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 737{,}280 \text{ bytes} \approx 0.703 \text{ MB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Conclusion:** A complete, deployable task-specific Prefix Tuning checkpoint weighs **less than 1 MB**, while a full fine-tuning checkpoint of the same 12-layer model weighs **$\sim 350$ MB** (12 layers $\times$ $\sim$30M params). This is a **$\sim 500\times$ compression** in storage.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Attention Score Derivation with Prefix

**Problem Statement:**  
Show the explicit form of the attention score matrix when a prefix of length $\vert P_{\vert}$ is prepended to the key matrix, and demonstrate that the prefix slots act as additional "memory addresses" the original query positions can attend to.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the original key matrix.

$$K = X W_K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}, \quad \text{where } n = \vert x \vert \text{ is the input sequence length.}$$

**Step 2:** Prepend the trainable prefix.

$$\tilde{K} = \begin{bmatrix} P_K \\ K \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{(\vert P_{\vert} + n) \times d}$$

**Step 3:** Form the full attention score matrix.

The score matrix $S \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (\vert P_{\vert} + n)}$ is:

$$S = \frac{Q \tilde{K}^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_k}} \begin{bmatrix} Q_{[1]} P_K^{\top} & Q_{[1]} K^{\top} \\ Q_{[2]} P_K^{\top} & Q_{[2]} K^{\top} \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ Q_{[n]} P_K^{\top} & Q_{[n]} K^{\top} \end{bmatrix}$$

We can partition this block-wise:

$$S = \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_k}} \begin{bmatrix} S_{QP} & S_{QK} \end{bmatrix}$$

where

$$S_{QP} = \frac{Q P_K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times \vert P_{\vert}}, \quad S_{QK} = \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

**Step 4:** Apply softmax row-wise to obtain attention weights.

$$A = \text{softmax}(S) = \begin{bmatrix} A_{QP} & A_{QK} \end{bmatrix}$$

with $A_{QP} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times \vert P_{\vert}}$ and $A_{QK} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$.

**Step 5:** Compute the final attended output.

$$\text{Output} = A \tilde{V} = A_{QP} P_V + A_{QK} V$$

**Step 6:** Interpret the result.

The output is a **weighted sum of two contributions**:

- $A_{QK} V$ — the original attention over the input sequence.
- $A_{QP} P_V$ — a learned, task-specific "global bias" term that all query positions contribute to, parameterised by $P_V$ and gated by the soft-attention weights $A_{QP}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Insight:** The prefix slots function as a **shared, learned, non-textual memory** that every token in the input can selectively draw upon. This is functionally analogous to a **soft, learned bias vector** added to the attention output, but with the critical difference that it is *content-dependent* (it depends on $Q$) rather than a fixed additive constant.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reparameterization Effective Parameter Count

**Problem Statement:**  
During training, an auxiliary two-layer MLP is used to reparameterize each prefix. The MLP has architecture $d_{\text{MLP}} \to d_{\text{hid}} \to d$, with ReLU activation. If $d_{\text{MLP}} = d$ and $d_{\text{hid}} = d$, compute the total *training-time* parameters for a 12-layer model with $\vert P_{\vert} = 10$ and $d = 768$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Compute the parameters of one MLP for keys.

The MLP structure is:

$$P_K = W_2 \cdot \tanh(W_1 \cdot P_K' + b_1) + b_2$$

With $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$, $W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$, $b_1, b_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$:

$$\text{MLP params} = d^2 + d^2 + d + d = 2d^2 + 2d$$

**Step 2:** Plug in $d = 768$.

$$2(768)^2 + 2(768) = 2 \times 589{,}824 + 1{,}536 = 1{,}181{,}184 \text{ parameters per MLP}$$

**Step 3:** Account for both $K$ and $V$ MLPs at one layer.

$$\text{Per layer (training time)} = 2 \times 1{,}181{,}184 = 2{,}362{,}368$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by 12 layers.

$$\text{Total training-time} = 12 \times 2{,}362{,}368 = 28{,}348{,}416 \approx 28.3 \text{ M parameters}$$

**Step 5:** Compare to the inference-time (post-reparam) count.

$$\frac{28{,}348{,}416}{184{,}320} \approx 153.7$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Conclusion:** Although training uses $\sim 28.3\text{M}$ parameters, the **deployable, inference-time model only retains the 184K reparameterized prefix vectors**. The factor of $\sim 150\times$ in training-time overhead buys **optimization stability and final performance gains**; it does not bloat the deployed artifact.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Prefix Tuning vs Related Methods

> [!NOTE]  
> **Exam-Ready Comparison Table** — frequently appears in KTU 14-mark questions.

| **Criterion** | **Full Fine-Tuning** | **Prefix Tuning** | **Prompt Tuning** | **Adapter Tuning** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Trainable Params | 100% ($\sim 10^8$–$10^{10}$) | $\sim 0.1\%$ ($\sim 10^5$) | $\sim 0.01\%$ ($\sim 10^4$) | $\sim 1$–$5\%$ ($\sim 10^6$–$10^7$) |
| Frozen Base Model | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Where is intervention? | All weights | K, V at every layer | Input embedding only | Inside MLP block |
| Storage per task | GBs | **< 1 MB** | **< 100 KB** | $\sim 10$–50 MB |
| Inference latency overhead | None | Negligible | Negligible | Small (extra MLP) |
| Performance vs full FT | 100% (baseline) | 95–99% on most tasks | 85–95% | 95–99% |
| Training stability | High | Medium (improved by reparam.) | High | High |
| Original Reference | — | Li & Liang, ACL 2021 | Lester et al., EMNLP 2021 | Houlsby et al., ICML 2019 |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Prefix Tuning in a Transformer

The following Mermaid diagram maps the flow of tensors through a single transformer block with Prefix Tuning enabled. Each numbered node corresponds to a discrete computational step. This block is replicated $L$ times (one per transformer layer), with **independent** prefix parameters $P_K^{(i)}, P_V^{(i)}$ at every layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input1["Input Embeddings X (n x d)"]
    qproj["Linear Projection: W_Q"]
    kproj["Linear Projection: W_K"]
    vproj["Linear Projection: W_V"]
    qnode["Query Matrix Q (n x d)"]
    knode["Original Key Matrix K (n x d)"]
    vnode["Original Value Matrix V (n x d)"]
    prefk["Trainable Prefix P_K (L_p x d)"]
    prefv["Trainable Prefix P_V (L_p x d)"]
    reparamk["Optional: MLP_K Reparameterization"]
    reparamv["Optional: MLP_V Reparameterization"]
    concatk["Row Concat: tilde K (L_p + n) x d"]
    concatv["Row Concat: tilde V (L_p + n) x d"]
    scoremat["Scaled Score Mat: Q tilde K_T / sqrt d_k"]
    softmaxnode["Row Softmax over (L_p + n) cols"]
    attnout["Weighted Sum: A tilde V (n x d)"]
    outproj["Output Projection: W_O"]
    nextlayer["Output to Next Layer i + 1"]
    lossflow["Cross Entropy Loss against ground truth y"]

    input1 --> qproj --> qnode
    input1 --> kproj --> knode
    input1 --> vproj --> vnode
    preparam1[ ]:::dummy
    preparam2[ ]:::dummy
    reparamk --> prefk
    reparamv --> prefv
    reparamk -. "Optimized during training" .-> prefk
    reparamv -. "Optimized during training" .-> prefv
    prefk --> concatk
    knode --> concatk
    prefv --> concatv
    vnode --> concatv
    qnode --> scoremat
    concatk --> scoremat
    scoremat --> softmaxnode --> attnout
    concatv --> attnout
    attnout --> outproj --> nextlayer
    nextlayer --> lossflow

    classDef dummy height:0px,width:0px,fill:none,stroke:none;
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- All **frozen** elements ($W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O$, base embeddings) are depicted in the main forward path; gradients **do not** flow back into them.
- The **blue-highlighted** prefix tensors $P_K, P_V$ are the sole trainable quantities per layer.
- The **dashed** arrows from `reparamk` / `reparamv` indicate that during training the MLP is optimized, but at inference only the resulting reparameterized prefix vectors are used.

---

## 4.2 Layer-Wise Prefix Propagation Across the Full Transformer

The following diagram shows the **vertical cascade** of prefixes through a multi-layer transformer, emphasizing that each layer has its **own independent** prefix.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    emb["Token + Position Embeddings"]
    layer1["Transformer Layer 1 (frozen W_Q, W_K, W_V) + Prefix 1: P_K1, P_V1"]
    layer2["Transformer Layer 2 (frozen W_Q, W_K, W_V) + Prefix 2: P_K2, P_V2"]
    layer3["Transformer Layer 3 (frozen W_Q, W_K, W_V) + Prefix 3: P_K3, P_V3"]
    layerL["Transformer Layer L (frozen W_Q, W_K, W_V) + Prefix L: P_KL, P_VL"]
    head["Task Head (LM Head for generation)"]
    out["Output Token Distribution"]

    emb --> layer1 --> layer2 --> layer3 --> layerL --> head --> out

    p1[ ]:::space
    p2[ ]:::space
    p3[ ]:::space
    pL[ ]:::space
    layer1 -.- p1
    layer2 -.- p2
    layer3 -.- p3
    layerL -.- pL
    p1:::space
    p2:::space
    p3:::space
    pL:::space

    classDef space width:0px,height:0px,fill:none,stroke:none;
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Visual Takeaway:** The trainable prefix parameters form a **vertical "ribbon"** running parallel to the frozen transformer stack. This ribbon is the *only* mutable part of the model. Swapping the ribbon swaps the entire task behavior of the model — analogous to changing the cassette tape in a vintage Walkman while the player mechanism stays put.

---

## 4.3 Inference-Time Deployment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    basemodel["Frozen Base LLM (e.g., GPT-2 Large, LLaMA-7B) loaded once into VRAM"]
    prefixstore1["Prefix A: Summarization (184 KB)"]
    prefixstore2["Prefix B: Translation (184 KB)"]
    prefixstore3["Prefix C: Medical QA (184 KB)"]
    router["Task Router / API Gateway"]
    user1["User Request: Summarize this news article"]
    user2["User Request: Translate EN to FR"]
    user3["User Request: Diagnose from symptoms"]
    response1["Generated Summary"]
    response2["Generated Translation"]
    response3["Generated Diagnosis"]

    router --> user1
    router --> user2
    router --> user3
    user1 --> prefixstore1 --> basemodel --> response1
    user2 --> prefixstore2 --> basemodel --> response2
    user3 --> prefixstore3 --> basemodel --> response3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Deployment Insight:** A single base model in VRAM ($\sim 14$ GB for a 7B model in FP16) is shared across **all** tasks. Per-task state is reduced from gigabytes to **sub-megabyte** prefix files, enabling thousands of concurrent task adaptations on commodity GPU hardware.

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 3 marks]**

**Q: Define Prefix Tuning. How does it differ from full fine-tuning of a language model?**

**Model Answer:**

Prefix Tuning is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning technique for pre-trained language models in which a small set of trainable continuous vectors (the *prefix*) is prepended to the keys and values at every attention layer, while the entire base model remains frozen.

| **Aspect** | **Full Fine-Tuning** | **Prefix Tuning** |
|---|---|---|
| Trainable Parameters | 100% of model weights ($\sim 10^8$–$10^{10}$) | $\sim 0.1\%$ ($\sim 10^5$, only the prefix) |
| Storage per Task | Gigabytes | Sub-megabyte |
| Catastrophic Forgetting Risk | High | Negligible |
| Multi-Task Deployment | Expensive (one copy per task) | Cheap (one base + many small prefixes) |

**(3 marks — definitions, contrast table, and parameter count comparison.)**

---

### Question A2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 3 marks]**

**Q: What is the reparameterization trick used during Prefix Tuning? Why is it needed?**

**Model Answer:**

The reparameterization trick expresses the trainable prefix vectors $P_K, P_V$ as the output of a small auxiliary two-layer MLP whose input $P_K', P_V' \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert P_{\vert} \times d}$ is the actual trainable parameter:

$$P_K = W_2 \cdot \tanh(W_1 P_K' + b_1) + b_2, \quad P_V = W_2' \cdot \tanh(W_1' P_V' + b_1') + b_2'$$

**Why it is needed:** Direct optimization of randomly initialized $P_K, P_V$ leads to unstable training and degraded final performance because the raw initialization places them in high-curvature regions of the loss landscape. The MLP lifts the optimization into a smoother, lower-dimensional manifold, after which the final reparameterized prefix vectors are extracted. The MLP itself is **discarded at inference time**, so the deployment cost is unaffected.

**(3 marks — full equation, justification of need, mention of MLP discardal.)**

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Choose either A or B*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 14 marks with internal choice]**

**Question A: (a)** Explain the architecture of Prefix Tuning in detail. Describe how the prefix vectors are integrated into the self-attention mechanism of a transformer layer. Support your answer with the relevant mathematical formulation. **(7 marks)**

**Question A: (b)** A 24-layer transformer-based language model with hidden dimension $d = 1024$ is being adapted via Prefix Tuning with prefix length $\vert P_{\vert} = 20$. Compute the total number of trainable parameters, the storage requirement in MB (FP32), and the efficiency ratio $\rho$ relative to a base model with $\vert \theta \vert = 1.5 \times 10^9$ parameters. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Architecture and Mathematical Formulation

**Step 1 — Baseline Self-Attention:**

In a standard transformer self-attention layer, the input $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ is projected into queries, keys, and values:

$$Q = X W_Q, \quad K = X W_K, \quad V = X W_V$$

where $W_Q, W_K, W_V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ are the (frozen) projection matrices and $n$ is the input sequence length.

**Step 2 — Prefix Construction:**

Two trainable prefix matrices are introduced, one for keys and one for values:

$$P_K \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert P_{\vert} \times d}, \quad P_V \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert P_{\vert} \times d}$$

**Step 3 — Modified K and V via Prepending:**

$$\tilde{K} = \bigl[ P_K ;\, K \bigr] \in \mathbb{R}^{(\vert P_{\vert} + n) \times d}, \quad \tilde{V} = \bigl[ P_V ;\, V \bigr] \in \mathbb{R}^{(\vert P_{\vert} + n) \times d}$$

**Step 4 — Modified Attention Computation:**

$$\text{Attention}_{\text{prefix}} = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q \tilde{K}^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) \tilde{V}$$

**Step 5 — Layer-wise Cascade:**

This intervention is applied at every transformer layer $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, L\}$ with **independent** prefix parameters $P_K^{(i)}, P_V^{(i)}$.

**Step 6 — Reparameterization for Stability:**

$$P_K^{(i)} = \text{MLP}\!\left({P_K^{(i)}}'\right), \quad P_V^{(i)} = \text{MLP}\!\left({P_V^{(i)}}'\right)$$

The MLP is used only during training and discarded at inference.

**Step 7 — Autoregressive Variant:**

For left-to-right language models, the prefix slots are allowed to attend bidirectionally among themselves, but each query position attends to all prefix slots in a left-to-right manner. This preserves the autoregressive property.

**Architectural Advantages:**
- Only prefix parameters are stored per task.
- Base model is fully shared and reusable.
- Attention distribution is directly steered by the prefix.

---

#### Part (b) — Parameter Count, Storage, and Efficiency Ratio

**Step 1 — Trainable Parameter Count:**

$$\vert \phi \vert = 2 \times L \times \vert P_{\vert} \times d = 2 \times 24 \times 20 \times 1024$$

$$\vert \phi \vert = 2 \times 24 \times 20{,}480 = 2 \times 491{,}520 = 983{,}040 \text{ parameters}$$

**Step 2 — Storage in MB (FP32, 4 bytes/param):**

$$\text{Storage} = 983{,}040 \times 4 = 3{,}932{,}160 \text{ bytes} \approx 3.75 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency Ratio:**

$$\rho = \frac{\vert \phi \vert}{\vert \theta \vert} = \frac{983{,}040}{1.5 \times 10^9} = 6.55 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.0655\%$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with Full Fine-Tuning Storage:**

Full fine-tuning would require $1.5 \times 10^9 \times 4 = 6 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes} = 6 \text{ GB}$. Prefix Tuning's $3.75 \text{ MB}$ is approximately:

$$\frac{6 \text{ GB}}{3.75 \text{ MB}} = \frac{6000 \text{ MB}}{3.75 \text{ MB}} = 1600 \times \text{ smaller}$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes on This Problem:**
> 1. Forgetting the factor of **2** (both keys *and* values are prefixed). Many students compute only $L \cdot \vert P_{\vert} \cdot d$ and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. Confusing **hidden dimension** $d$ with **head dimension** $d_k$ in the scaling factor. The prefix width equals the full hidden dimension $d$, not the per-head dimension.
> 3. Reporting the efficiency ratio as a percentage without multiplying by 100. $\rho = 6.55 \times 10^{-4}$ is **0.0655%**, not just $6.55 \times 10^{-4}$.
> 4. Forgetting to convert bytes to MB. Divide by $1024^2$ for true MB or $10^6$ for "Mega-bytes" depending on convention used in the question.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 14 marks with internal choice]**

**Question B: (a)** Compare and contrast Prefix Tuning, Prompt Tuning, and Adapter Tuning as parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods. Use a comparison matrix and discuss the trade-offs in terms of trainable parameter count, inference latency, storage, and task performance. **(7 marks)**

**Question B: (b)** Derive the explicit form of the attention score matrix when a prefix of length $\vert P_{\vert}$ is prepended to the key matrix in a transformer layer. Show that the output is a sum of two terms: one over the original input and one over the prefix. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Comparison of PEFT Methods

| **Criterion** | **Prefix Tuning** | **Prompt Tuning** | **Adapter Tuning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trainable Params** | $\sim 0.1\%$ of model | $\sim 0.01\%$ of model | $\sim 1$–$5\%$ of model |
| **Location of Intervention** | Keys and values at every attention layer | Input embedding layer (layer 0) only | Inside MLP block of each transformer layer |
| **Storage per Task** | Sub-megabyte | Kilobytes | 10–50 MB |
| **Inference Latency** | Negligible overhead | None | Small overhead (extra MLP forward pass) |
| **Catastrophic Forgetting** | Minimal | Minimal | Minimal |
| **Task Performance** | 95–99% of full FT | 85–95% of full FT | 95–99% of full FT |
| **Hyperparameter Sensitivity** | Medium (prefix length, reparam) | Low | Low |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Medium | Low | Medium |
| **Multi-Task Scalability** | Excellent | Excellent | Good |

**Trade-off Discussion:**

- **Prefix Tuning vs Prompt Tuning:** Prefix Tuning modifies all $L$ layers, giving it more expressive power and better downstream performance, at the cost of slightly more trainable parameters.
- **Prefix Tuning vs Adapter Tuning:** Both achieve similar task performance, but Prefix Tuning has **lower inference latency** (no extra MLP in the forward pass) and **smaller per-task storage**, making it preferable for latency-sensitive multi-tenant serving.
- **Prefix Tuning vs Full Fine-Tuning:** Full FT achieves marginally higher peak performance (1–5% on hard tasks) but is $\sim 1000\times$ more expensive in storage and is prone to catastrophic forgetting.

---

#### Part (b) — Attention Score Matrix Derivation with Prefix

**Step 1:** Write the original key matrix.

$$K = X W_K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$$

**Step 2:** Define the trainable prefix.

$$P_K \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert P_{\vert} \times d}$$

**Step 3:** Form the augmented key matrix via row-wise concatenation.

$$\tilde{K} = \begin{bmatrix} P_K \\ K \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{(\vert P_{\vert} + n) \times d}$$

**Step 4:** Form the augmented value matrix similarly.

$$\tilde{V} = \begin{bmatrix} P_V \\ V \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{(\vert P_{\vert} + n) \times d}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the attention scores.

$$S = \frac{Q \tilde{K}^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_k}} \begin{bmatrix} Q P_K^{\top} & Q K^{\top} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6:** Apply softmax row-wise.

$$A = \text{softmax}(S) = \begin{bmatrix} A_{QP} & A_{QK} \end{bmatrix}$$

where $A_{QP} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times \vert P_{\vert}}$ and $A_{QK} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ are the attention weights over the prefix and the original sequence respectively.

**Step 7:** Compute the final output.

$$\text{Output} = A \tilde{V} = A_{QP} P_V + A_{QK} V$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation:**

The output is a **sum of two terms**:

$$\boxed{\text{Output} = \underbrace{A_{QP} P_V}_{\text{prefix contribution}} + \underbrace{A_{QK} V}_{\text{original input contribution}}}$$

- $A_{QP} P_V$ encodes a **task-specific, query-conditioned bias** drawn from the trainable prefix.
- $A_{QK} V$ is the **standard attention output** over the input.

This decomposition reveals that Prefix Tuning functions as a **learned, content-dependent, additive memory** grafted onto standard attention.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost on 14-Mark Questions:**
> 1. **Skipping the reparameterization discussion:** Failing to mention the MLP reparameterization trick costs 1–2 marks. It is a *core* component of the original paper and examiners expect it.
> 2. **Confusing prefix-tuning with prompt-tuning:** Examiners specifically test whether students know that Prefix Tuning modifies *all $L$ layers*, not just the input embeddings.
> 3. **Not drawing the architecture diagram:** A 14-mark question without a labeled diagram loses at least 1–2 marks. Always include a self-attention block schematic showing the prefix prepended to K and V.
> 4. **Forgetting inference-time behavior:** The MLP is discarded post-training. Many students mistakenly say the MLP is part of the deployed model.
> 5. **Unit conversion errors:** Always double-check whether storage should be reported in bytes, KB, or MB — examiners are strict.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Print this section. Memorize verbatim. Appear in the exam hall confident.**

- **Definition:** Prefix Tuning = frozen base LLM + trainable continuous vectors prepended to K and V of every attention layer.
- **Trainable Params:** $2 L \vert P_{\vert} d$ — typically < 0.1% of model.
- **Storage per Task:** Sub-megabyte, vs. GB-scale for full fine-tuning.
- **Why prepend to K and V (not Q):** Avoids disrupting autoregressive masking; the prefix acts as additional context tokens the original queries can attend to.
- **Reparameterization Trick:** Auxiliary MLP $P_K = \text{MLP}(P_K')$ during training, discarded at inference. Improves optimization stability.
- **Layer-wise Independence:** Each layer has its *own* independent prefix $P_K^{(i)}, P_V^{(i)}$.
- **vs. Prompt Tuning:** Prefix Tuning intervenes at *all* $L$ layers; Prompt Tuning intervenes only at the input embedding layer.
- **vs. Adapter Tuning:** Prefix Tuning has lower inference latency (no extra MLP forward pass).
- **vs. Full Fine-Tuning:** Prefix Tuning preserves base model knowledge (no catastrophic forgetting) and is $\sim 1000\times$ more storage-efficient.
- **Typical Hyperparameters:** Prefix length 10–200, reparam MLP hidden size = $d$, base model = GPT-2 / BART / LLaMA family.
- **Key Mathematical Decomposition:** $\text{Output} = A_{QP} P_V + A_{QK} V$ — sum of prefix contribution and original input contribution.
- **Loss Function:** Standard cross-entropy $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_t \log P_\theta(y_t \mid x, P_\phi, y_{<t})$; gradient taken w.r.t. $\phi$ only.
- **Autoregressive Variant:** Prefix slots attend bidirectionally among themselves; each query attends to all prefix slots in causal order.
- **Original Reference (no fabrication):** Li & Liang, *"Prefix-Tuning: Optimizing Continuous Prompts for Generation"*, ACL 2021.
- **Engineering Utility:** Multi-tenant LLM serving, edge deployment, continual learning, A/B testing of prompt hypotheses.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: P-Tuning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT748 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** P-Tuning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **P-Tuning (Prompt Tuning with a Prompt Encoder)** is a **parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT)** methodology introduced by Liu et al. (2021) in the seminal paper *"GPT Understands, Too"*. It is designed to automatically search and optimize **continuous prompt embeddings** (often called "soft prompts") that are prepended to the input layer of a frozen pre-trained language model, enabling the LLM to perform downstream Natural Language Understanding (NLU) and Natural Language Generation (NLG) tasks without modifying the original model weights.

The core innovation of P-Tuning lies in the introduction of a **trainable Prompt Encoder** network (typically an **LSTM** or **MLP**) that transforms a sequence of pseudo-token indices into continuous embedding vectors. These continuous embeddings, when prepended to the input of a frozen language model, effectively "steer" the model's behavior on a specific task.

$$P = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$$

Where:
- $P$ represents the sequence of **continuous prompt embeddings** of dimension $d$
- $n$ is the prompt length (a hyperparameter)
- The embeddings are **not tied to real vocabulary tokens** — they exist purely in the continuous embedding space

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Whispered Instructions" Metaphor:**
> 
> Imagine a highly knowledgeable but rigid professor (the **frozen pre-trained LLM**) who only responds to exact, precisely-worded instructions. Traditionally, you (the engineer) had to spend hours hand-crafting the perfect sentence to elicit the right answer — this is **manual prompt engineering**.
> 
> Now, imagine giving the professor a small **earpiece** through which a translator whispers carefully-tuned, fluid instructions in real-time. You can train *just the translator* (the **Prompt Encoder**) without ever changing the professor's brain. The translator learns to whisper exactly the right "mood words" that prime the professor's mind for the specific task at hand.
> 
> The professor's brain = **frozen LLM weights**
> The whispered fluid instructions = **continuous prompt embeddings**
> The trainable translator = **Prompt Encoder (LSTM/MLP)**

## 1.3 P-Tuning vs. Related Paradigms

| Method | What is Trained? | Prompt Type | Parameter Cost |
|--------|------------------|-------------|----------------|
| **Full Fine-Tuning** | All model weights | N/A (no prompts) | 100% (~110M–175B params) |
| **Manual Prompt Engineering** | Nothing (human-designed) | Discrete (text) | 0% |
| **Prompt Tuning (Lester et al., 2021)** | Free embeddings directly | Continuous (free) | ~0.001% |
| **P-Tuning (Liu et al., 2021)** | **Prompt Encoder** that produces embeddings | Continuous (encoded) | **~0.001%** |
| **Prefix-Tuning** | Prefix key-value pairs at every layer | Continuous (prefix) | ~0.1% |

## 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary

> [!NOTE]
> - **Soft Prompt**: A sequence of continuous-valued vectors (real numbers) inserted as virtual tokens at the input of an LLM. They have no real-world word mapping.
> - **Hard Prompt / Discrete Prompt**: Natural language text tokens (e.g., "Translate the following English sentence to French:").
> - **Prompt Encoder**: The small trainable neural network (LSTM/MLP) in P-Tuning that maps pseudo-token indices to continuous embeddings.
> - **Pseudo-Token Index**: An integer placeholder (e.g., index `[V]`, `[V+1]`) used as input to the prompt encoder. They do not correspond to real vocabulary words.
> - **Frozen Backbone**: The pre-trained LLM whose weights are kept constant during P-Tuning training.
> - **Reparameterization**: The technique of using an auxiliary network (encoder) to generate the soft prompts, which improves training stability compared to direct embedding tuning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

The P-Tuning methodology operates through a carefully orchestrated pipeline. The following bulleted logic breaks down the entire flow from input text to trained model:

1. **Step 1 — Input Formulation**: Take the raw task input $x$ (e.g., a question, sentence, or query). This is a discrete sequence of real vocabulary tokens.
   - $x = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m]$ where each $x_i \in V$ (vocabulary)

2. **Step 2 — Pseudo-Token Index Initialization**: Create a sequence of placeholder indices $[h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_n]$ that will be replaced by continuous embeddings. These indices are **not real tokens** — they are integer handles for the prompt encoder.
   - $h_i \in \{V, V+1, \ldots, V+n-1\}$ (indices past the vocabulary)

3. **Step 3 — Prompt Encoding (Reparameterization)**: Pass the pseudo-token indices through the **trainable Prompt Encoder** $f_\theta$ (LSTM or MLP) to obtain continuous prompt embeddings.
   - This is the **"Why"** of P-Tuning: directly optimizing free embeddings (as in vanilla Prompt Tuning) leads to unstable gradients and poor convergence. Using an encoder provides smoother optimization.

4. **Step 4 — Embedding Concatenation**: Concatenate the generated prompt embeddings $P$ with the embeddings of the input $x$ (obtained via the frozen embedding matrix $E$).

5. **Step 5 — Frozen LLM Forward Pass**: Feed the concatenated sequence into the frozen pre-trained language model. The model processes both prompt and input jointly.

6. **Step 6 — Loss Computation**: Compute the task-specific loss (e.g., cross-entropy for classification, log-likelihood for generation) between the model's output and the ground truth label.

7. **Step 7 — Backpropagation Through Prompt Encoder Only**: Compute gradients $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$ with respect to the **Prompt Encoder parameters $\theta$ only**. The LLM weights are completely frozen, so no gradient flows into them.

8. **Step 8 — Parameter Update**: Update only the prompt encoder parameters $\theta$ using gradient descent. The LLM remains untouched.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of Reparameterization:**
> Empirically, directly training free embeddings (Prompt Tuning) suffers from:
> - High variance in gradient updates
> - Sensitivity to initialization
> - Difficulty in optimizing the discrete-to-continuous bridge
> 
> The Prompt Encoder (LSTM/MLP) acts as a **low-rank bottleneck** that:
> - Smooths the optimization landscape
> - Couples the prompt tokens via sequential dependencies (LSTM)
> - Provides better gradient flow due to its non-linear transformation

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all essential mathematical formulations for P-Tuning, critical for both theoretical and exam-oriented preparation.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description | Key Parameters |
|---|-------------------|-------------------------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | Continuous Prompt Sequence | $P = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ | Sequence of $n$ continuous embeddings of dim $d$ | $n$ = prompt length, $d$ = hidden dim |
| 2 | Prompt Encoder Mapping | $P = f_\theta(h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_n)$ | Maps pseudo-indices to embeddings | $f_\theta$ = LSTM/MLP with params $\theta$ |
| 3 | LSTM Prompt Encoder (per step) | $h_i, c_i = \text{LSTM}_\theta([\text{emb}(h_i); h_{i-1}], c_{i-1})$ | Sequential dependency modeling | Hidden states $h_i, c_i$ |
| 4 | MLP Prompt Encoder | $p_i = \text{MLP}_\theta(e(h_i)) = W_2 \cdot \sigma(W_1 \cdot e(h_i) + b_1) + b_2$ | Non-linear projection of embeddings | $W_1, W_2, b_1, b_2$ |
| 5 | Concatenated Input | $X_{\text{full}} = [p_1, \ldots, p_n, E(x_1), \ldots, E(x_m)]$ | Prompt prepended to input embeddings | $E$ = frozen embedding matrix |
| 6 | Frozen LLM Output | $Y = \text{LLM}_{\text{frozen}}(X_{\text{full}})$ | Forward pass through frozen backbone | No gradient to LLM |
| 7 | Task Loss (Classification) | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Cross-entropy between prediction & label | $y_i$ = ground truth, $\hat{y}_i$ = predicted prob |
| 8 | Gradient Computation | $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$ | Gradients w.r.t. prompt encoder params only | $\theta$ = prompt encoder weights |
| 9 | Parameter Count (Trainable) | $\vert\theta\vert \ll \vert\Phi_{\text{LLM}}\vert$ | Encoder params << LLM params | Typically ~0.001% of LLM size |
| 10 | Discretization (Optional Inference) | $p_i^{\text{disc}} = \arg\max_{v \in V} \text{sim}(p_i, E(v))$ | Map continuous prompts back to nearest tokens | $E(v)$ = embedding of token $v$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

P-Tuning and its descendants have critical applications across the AI industry:

- **Low-Resource Deployment**: Multi-tenant LLM serving where thousands of task-specific adapters must be stored alongside a single shared frozen LLM, drastically reducing storage costs.
- **Privacy-Preserving Customization**: Healthcare and finance organizations can customize LLMs on sensitive data without ever exposing or modifying the original model.
- **Rapid Task Adaptation**: Few-shot and zero-shot task adaptation in production NLP pipelines.
- **Multi-Task Learning**: Training separate prompt encoders for different tasks while sharing one backbone.
- **Continual Learning**: Avoiding catastrophic forgetting by keeping the LLM frozen and only updating prompts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Mathematical Walkthrough: From Input to Loss

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivation provides an **exhaustive, line-by-line mathematical treatment** of the P-Tuning forward and backward passes. Every algebraic transition is explained.

### 3.1.1 Setup & Notation

Let us define the components of the P-Tuning system rigorously:

- Pre-trained LLM with frozen parameters $\Phi_{\text{LLM}}$
- Vocabulary $V$ of size $\vert V \vert$
- Input sequence $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m)$ where each $x_i \in V$
- Pseudo-token indices $h = (h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_n)$ where $h_i \in \{V, V+1, \ldots, V+n-1\}$
- Prompt Encoder $f_\theta$ with trainable parameters $\theta$
- Embedding dimension $d$ (matches LLM's input dimension)
- True label $y$ for the task

### 3.1.2 Forward Pass Derivation

**Step 1 — Embedding Lookup for Pseudo-Indices**

First, we look up initial embeddings for the pseudo-indices from the frozen embedding matrix $E \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times d}$. However, since $h_i$ are indices *outside* the vocabulary, we cannot use $E$ directly. Instead, we use a **trainable embedding matrix** $E' \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ initialized randomly for the prompt encoder.

$$e(h_i) = E'_{h_i - V} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$$

This produces a sequence of initial pseudo-embeddings $e(h) = (e(h_1), e(h_2), \ldots, e(h_n))$.

**Step 2 — Prompt Encoding via LSTM**

For an LSTM-based prompt encoder, each step $i$ computes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_i &= \sigma(W_{ii} \cdot e(h_i) + b_{ii} + W_{hi} \cdot h_{i-1} + b_{hi}) \\
f_i &= \sigma(W_{if} \cdot e(h_i) + b_{if} + W_{hf} \cdot h_{i-1} + b_{hf}) \\
g_i &= \tanh(W_{ig} \cdot e(h_i) + b_{ig} + W_{hg} \cdot h_{i-1} + b_{hg}) \\
o_i &= \sigma(W_{io} \cdot e(h_i) + b_{io} + W_{ho} \cdot h_{i-1} + b_{ho}) \\
c_i &= f_i \odot c_{i-1} + i_i \odot g_i \\
h_i &= o_i \odot \tanh(c_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $i_i, f_i, g_i, o_i$ are the input, forget, cell, and output gates respectively
- $c_i$ is the cell state, $h_i$ is the hidden state
- $\sigma$ is the sigmoid activation, $\tanh$ is the hyperbolic tangent
- $W$ and $b$ matrices are part of $\theta$ (trainable)
- $\odot$ denotes element-wise (Hadamard) multiplication

The final prompt embeddings are extracted from the hidden states of the LSTM:

$$p_i = h_i^{\text{LSTM}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Step 3 — Alternative: MLP-Based Prompt Encoder**

For an MLP-based encoder, each pseudo-embedding is processed independently:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_i^{(1)} &= \sigma(W_1 \cdot e(h_i) + b_1) \\
p_i &= W_2 \cdot z_i^{(1)} + b_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d}$, $W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_h}$, and $d_h$ is the hidden dimension of the MLP (typically $d_h = 2d$).

**Step 4 — Concatenation of Prompt and Input Embeddings**

The frozen LLM's embedding matrix $E$ converts real input tokens to embeddings:

$$E(x_j) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

The full input sequence to the frozen LLM is:

$$X_{\text{full}} = [p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n, E(x_1), E(x_2), \ldots, E(x_m)] \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+m) \times d}$$

**Step 5 — Frozen LLM Forward Pass**

The frozen LLM processes the concatenated sequence. For a transformer-based LLM with $L$ layers:

$$H^{(0)} = X_{\text{full}}$$

For each layer $\ell = 1, 2, \ldots, L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{(\ell)} = H^{(\ell-1)} W_Q^{(\ell)}, \quad K^{(\ell)} = H^{(\ell-1)} W_K^{(\ell)}, \quad V^{(\ell)} = H^{(\ell-1)} W_V^{(\ell)} \\
A^{(\ell)} = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{Q^{(\ell)} (K^{(\ell)})^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) \\
\text{head}^{(\ell)} = A^{(\ell)} V^{(\ell)} \\
H^{(\ell)} = \text{LayerNorm}\left(H^{(\ell-1)} + \text{FFN}^{(\ell)}(\text{head}^{(\ell)})\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Crucially**: All $W_Q^{(\ell)}, W_K^{(\ell)}, W_V^{(\ell)}, W_O^{(\ell)}, W_{\text{FFN}}^{(\ell)}$ remain **frozen** (no gradient updates).

**Step 6 — Output Projection & Loss**

The final hidden state corresponding to the last input token $x_m$ is used for prediction:

$$h_{\text{cls}} = H^{(L)}[n + m] \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$$

The classification head (which can be trainable or frozen depending on the task) produces logits:

$$\hat{y} = W_{\text{head}} \cdot h_{\text{cls}} + b_{\text{head}}$$

The cross-entropy loss for classification is:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log(\text{softmax}(\hat{y})_c)$$

### 3.1.3 Backward Pass — Gradient Flow

The chain rule for backpropagation through the frozen LLM is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta} &= \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial h_{\text{cls}}} \cdot \frac{\partial h_{\text{cls}}}{\partial X_{\text{full}}} \cdot \frac{\partial X_{\text{full}}}{\partial P} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial \theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical observation**: The term $\frac{\partial h_{\text{cls}}}{\partial X_{\text{full}}}$ is computed via the frozen LLM, but **the LLM weights are not updated**. The gradient flows *through* the frozen LLM (as a function) but only updates $\theta$.

The gradient $\frac{\partial P}{\partial \theta}$ depends on the encoder type:
- For LSTM: involves backpropagation through time (BPTT)
- For MLP: standard backpropagation

### 3.1.4 Parameter Efficiency Calculation

For a BERT-base model with $\vert\Phi_{\text{BERT}}\vert \approx 110\text{M}$ parameters and an LSTM prompt encoder with hidden size $d = 768$ and prompt length $n = 20$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert\theta_{\text{LSTM}}\vert &= 4 \times (d \times d + d) \times n \\
&\approx 4 \times (768^2 + 768) \times 1 \quad \text{(per-step params)} \\
&\approx 2.36\text{M parameters (shared across all } n \text{ steps)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The trainable parameter ratio is:

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{\vert\theta\vert}{\vert\Phi_{\text{LLM}}\vert} \approx \frac{2.36\text{M}}{110\text{M}} \approx 0.021 \text{ or } 2.1\%$$

For larger models (e.g., GPT-3 175B), this ratio drops to less than **0.001%**.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following table provides a deep analytical comparison of P-Tuning against closely related PEFT methods — essential for KTU exam questions that ask for comparative analysis.

| Dimension | Full Fine-Tuning | Prompt Tuning | **P-Tuning** | Prefix-Tuning | LoRA |
|-----------|------------------|---------------|--------------|---------------|------|
| **Trainable Params** | 100% | ~0.001% | ~0.001–0.1% | ~0.1% | ~0.1–1% |
| **Trainable Location** | All layers | Input only | **Input only (via encoder)** | All layers (prefix) | Attention weights |
| **Reparameterization** | N/A | None | **LSTM/MLP encoder** | MLP (optional) | Low-rank matrices |
| **Discreteness** | N/A | Continuous | **Continuous** | Continuous | Continuous |
| **Storage per Task** | Full model copy (~110M+) | Soft prompt (~10K) | **Encoder + prompt (~2M)** | Prefix (~1M) | LoRA matrices (~1M) |
| **Training Stability** | High (overfitting risk) | Low (unstable) | **Medium-High (encoder stabilizes)** | Medium | High |
| **Performance vs Full FT** | 100% (baseline) | 90–95% (small models), ~99% (large) | **Comparable to full FT on NLU** | Comparable | Comparable |
| **Infer-time Latency Overhead** | None | None | **None** (prompts computed once) | Small (prefix processing) | Small (matmul) |
| **Discretization Possible?** | N/A | Yes (via projection) | **Yes (semantic mapping)** | No | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 P-Tuning End-to-End Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete P-Tuning pipeline, from pseudo-token initialization through the frozen LLM to the loss computation and gradient flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Input x: x1, x2, ..., xm] --> B[Token Embedding Lookup via Frozen Matrix E]
    B --> C[Input Embeddings: E_x1, E_x2, ..., E_xm]
    
    D[Pseudo-Token Indices: h1, h2, ..., hn] --> E[Trainable Pseudo Embedding E_prime]
    E --> F[Prompt Encoder f_theta: LSTM or MLP]
    F --> G[Continuous Soft Prompts: p1, p2, ..., pn]
    
    G --> H[Concatenation Layer]
    C --> H
    H --> I[Full Sequence X_full: p1...pn, E_x1...E_xm]
    
    I --> J[Frozen Pre-trained LLM Phi_LLM]
    J --> K[Output Hidden States H_L]
    K --> L[Classification Head W_head]
    L --> M[Prediction y_hat]
    M --> N[Cross-Entropy Loss L]
    
    N --> O[Backpropagation: Gradient w.r.t. theta]
    O --> P[Update Prompt Encoder Parameters theta]
    
    Q[Frozen LLM Weights Phi_LLM: NO UPDATE] -.-> J
    
    style F fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style Q fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#666666,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    style P fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Color Coding Legend:**
> - 🟥 **Red (Prompt Encoder)**: The only component whose parameters are trained.
> - 🟦 **Blue (Frozen LLM)**: The massive pre-trained model that remains untouched.
> - ⬜ **Gray (Dashed)**: Reinforces the frozen nature of the LLM weights.
> - 🟩 **Green (Update)**: Indicates where the gradient-based parameter update occurs.

## 4.2 P-Tuning vs. Prompt Tuning — Comparative Block Architecture

This block diagram highlights the **critical architectural difference** between P-Tuning and vanilla Prompt Tuning: the presence of the Prompt Encoder.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PromptTuning[Vanilla Prompt Tuning]
        PT1[Free Embeddings p1...pn] --> PT2[Direct Concatenation]
        PT2 --> PT3[Frozen LLM]
    end
    
    subgraph PTuning[P-Tuning with Encoder]
        PE1[Pseudo Indices h1...hn] --> PE2[Trainable Embedding E_prime]
        PE2 --> PE3[LSTM or MLP Encoder f_theta]
        PE3 --> PE4[Generated Soft Prompts p1...pn]
        PE4 --> PE5[Concatenation]
        PE5 --> PE6[Frozen LLM]
    end
    
    style PE3 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style PT1 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#cccc00,stroke-width:2px
    style PT3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style PT6 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Training & Inference Phase Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TrainingPhase[Training Phase]
        T1[Initialize Prompt Encoder theta randomly] --> T2[Freeze LLM weights Phi]
        T2 --> T3[Forward pass: encode prompts, concat, pass through LLM]
        T3 --> T4[Compute task loss L]
        T4 --> T5[Backprop gradient to theta only]
        T5 --> T6[Update theta via Adam/SGD]
        T6 --> T3
    end
    
    subgraph InferencePhase[Inference Phase]
        I1[Load trained prompt encoder theta] --> I2[Encode pseudo-tokens to soft prompts]
        I2 --> I3[Prepend prompts to input embeddings]
        I3 --> I4[Single forward pass through frozen LLM]
        I4 --> I5[Output prediction]
    end
    
    T6 -.->|Trained Encoder| I1
    
    style T1 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style I1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style I5 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
```

## 4.4 Gradient Flow Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Loss L] --> G1[Grad w.r.t. y_hat]
    G1 --> G2[Grad w.r.t. h_cls]
    G2 --> G3[Grad flows through Frozen LLM as function]
    G3 --> G4[Grad w.r.t. Soft Prompts P]
    G4 --> G5[Grad w.r.t. Prompt Encoder theta]
    G5 --> U[Update theta ONLY]
    
    G3 -.->|NO UPDATE TO Phi| Freeze[Frozen LLM Weights Phi: CONSTANT]
    
    style U fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px
    style Freeze fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#666666,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```
